stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her GP. She had a Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 4 years and 11 months ago for contraception and heavy menstrual bleeding. Insertion was via a private clinic that printed a removal-reminder card stating 'replace at 5 years'. She has had no bleeding for 18 months, no pain, threads visible at the routine 4-yearly check. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, has no plans to conceive for at least 5 more years and would prefer to keep this device as long as possible. She specifically asks whether the device must be replaced this month or whether she can defer. Cycles, partner, BMI and screening history are all unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 24. Speculum: Mirena threads visible at cervical os, no discharge.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test normal 12 months ago. No further investigations performed today.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding her current Mirena device?

options.text: Replace the device this month because the heavy-menstrual-bleeding indication caps duration at 5 years
options.rationale: The HMB indication is licensed for 5 years, but if her current need is contraception (HMB now resolved with amenorrhoea), the contraception licence governs duration. The device does not need replacement at 5 years for contraception (TGA PI Mirena 2023).

options.text: Replace the device this month because all LNG-IUS devices share a 5-year contraceptive licence in Australia
options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS was extended to 8 years for contraception by the TGA in 2023; Kyleena 19.5 mg remains 5 years. The licences are not shared (TGA PI Mirena; AMH 2024).

options.text: Continue the current device for contraception until 8 years from insertion, then replace
options.rationale: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is now TGA-licensed in Australia for up to 8 years of contraception. Her HMB has resolved with amenorrhoea so the operative licence is contraception. She may safely defer replacement to the 8-year mark (TGA PI Mirena 2023; AMH 2024; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Continue indefinitely as LNG-IUS devices are effective until menopause once inserted after age 25
options.rationale: There is no Australian licence for indefinite LNG-IUS use. A Mirena inserted at or after age 45 may be used until age 55 for endometrial protection on MHT, but that is a different scenario; this patient is 31 (Family Planning Australia; AMH).

options.text: Replace with a copper IUD now to take advantage of its 10-year licence
options.rationale: Switching to copper offers a longer interval but exposes her to heavier bleeding and removes the HMB benefit she may want preserved. There is no clinical indication to switch when the current LNG-IUS remains licensed for a further 3 years (Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: The Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS is licensed in Australia for 8 years of contraception (extended from 5 years in 2023). It remains licensed for 5 years when used for endometrial protection in MHT and for treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding.
key_points: Mirena 52 mg LNG-IUS: 8 years for contraception (TGA 2023)
Mirena: 5 years for HMB and for endometrial protection on MHT
Kyleena 19.5 mg LNG-IUS: 5 years contraception
Implanon NXT etonogestrel implant: 3 years
Copper IUD (TT380): 10 years
explanation.pearls: Different indications carry different licensed durations for the same device. The contraceptive extension to 8 years does not automatically apply to HMB or MHT endometrial protection.

stem: A 24-year-old woman attends a sexual-health clinic 96 hours after a single episode of condom failure. Her cycles are regular at 28 days; today is day 16 (intercourse occurred close to her predicted ovulation). She has a BMI of 33 and uses no regular medications. She declines an intrauterine device today because she is needle-phobic about the speculum/insertion. She has no allergies. She wants the oral option with the lowest failure rate given the 96-hour interval and her BMI, and she accepts that she will need to wait 5 days before resuming hormonal contraception if started on this method.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 23. Abdomen soft, no tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral emergency contraceptive in this scenario?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single dose now
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel is licensed only up to 72 hours; she presents at 96 hours, so it is outside its window. Efficacy also declines with BMI above 26 and around ovulation (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 3 mg orally as a single dose now, doubled for BMI
options.rationale: Although double-dose levonorgestrel is sometimes used off-label for higher BMI, it remains licensed only to 72 hours; at 96 hours it is outside the evidence-based window regardless of dose (Cochrane; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single dose now
options.rationale: Ulipristal is licensed up to 120 hours, is more effective than levonorgestrel around ovulation, and has less BMI-related fall-off below 35. With the IUD declined, ulipristal is the best oral option at 96 hours (Family Planning Australia; Cochrane).

options.text: Yuzpe regimen using combined oral contraceptive pills now
options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is less effective than levonorgestrel or ulipristal and causes more nausea; it is now reserved for settings where dedicated emergency contraceptives are not available (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill today and abstain for 7 days
options.rationale: Starting the COCP today is for ongoing contraception, not emergency contraception; it provides no rescue effect for the unprotected intercourse 96 hours ago (Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: Ranking of emergency contraception efficacy (most to least): copper IUD > ulipristal acetate > levonorgestrel > Yuzpe. The copper IUD has a failure rate under 1% and also provides ongoing contraception for up to 10 years.
key_points: Cu-IUD: most effective EC, within 120 hours, ongoing contraception
Ulipristal 30 mg: up to 120 hours, single dose
Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg: up to 96 hours (licensed 72 h)
Avoid concurrent ulipristal and progestogen contraception for 5 days
explanation.pearls: Offer the copper IUD first to any woman who would accept it. BMI > 26 reduces oral EC efficacy and shifts the balance further toward Cu-IUD.

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks presents with three weeks of progressive nausea and vomiting, six to ten episodes daily, unable to keep down most fluids, 3.5 kg weight loss (5%), PUQE score 14. She has tried small frequent meals, ginger and oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily without benefit. BP 102/68 with a 15 mmHg postural drop, HR 104, dry mucous membranes, ketones 3+ on urinalysis. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8. TSH normal. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy. She has been admitted, IV fluids with thiamine have been commenced and the team is choosing the next antiemetic step.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/68 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 36.7 degC. Postural drop 15 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, abdomen soft and non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: ketones 3+, SG 1.030. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8 mmol/L. Beta-hCG 78,000 IU/L. TSH 0.8 mIU/L. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next antiemetic step?

options.text: Add oral doxylamine 12.5 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg four times daily as second-line therapy
options.rationale: Pyridoxine monotherapy has failed. RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend stepping up to doxylamine-pyridoxine combination as the next-line therapy before broader antiemetics, since safety data in pregnancy are best established (RANZCOG NVP/HG; eTG Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Switch directly to oral ondansetron 8 mg three times daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for cases unresponsive to first- and second-line agents because of small first-trimester risks (orofacial clefts) and maternal QT effects; doxylamine-pyridoxine has not yet been trialled (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 16 mg three times daily today
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are last-line in refractory hyperemesis after failure of multiple antiemetics; using them now skips established earlier rungs of the ladder (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Discharge home on oral ginger and reassure that symptoms typically improve by 12 weeks
options.rationale: She has dehydration, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and >=5% weight loss, meeting criteria for hyperemesis admission; discharge without escalation is unsafe (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Commence intramuscular prochlorperazine 12.5 mg every 6 hours as monotherapy
options.rationale: Phenothiazines may be used adjunctively but doxylamine-pyridoxine is preferred next-line per RANZCOG given the better pregnancy safety profile; IM prochlorperazine monotherapy is not the next step (RANZCOG NVP/HG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

explanation.summary: First-line antiemetic for hyperemesis after pyridoxine alone has failed is doxylamine-pyridoxine. Ondansetron, metoclopramide and prochlorperazine are second-line. Steroids are reserved for refractory cases.
key_points: PUQE score quantifies severity
Hyperemesis: dehydration, ketonuria, >5% weight loss
Always give thiamine before glucose-containing fluids
Check TSH (gestational thyrotoxicosis often resolves)
explanation.pearls: If symptoms began after 10 weeks or there is abdominal pain, fever, headache or focal neurology, look for an alternative diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman is at 13 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain LMP confirmed on first-trimester ultrasound. She presents with a 48-hour history of fresh red vaginal bleeding which has now settled, and has passed several small clots at home. She is haemodynamically stable. At booking she was confirmed Rh(D) negative with a negative antibody screen, and the screen remains negative today. The previous pregnancy ended in a term vaginal birth without complications and she did not receive routine antenatal anti-D in that pregnancy (declined at the time). TVUS today shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy of 13 weeks 5 days with appropriate growth. The decision is to manage expectantly with follow-up in one week.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Speculum: small amount of dark blood, cervical os closed.
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O Rh(D) negative, antibody screen negative at booking and today. Beta-hCG 42,000 IU/L.

lead_in: What is the appropriate anti-D immunoglobulin regimen for this presentation in Australian practice?

options.text: No anti-D is required as bleeding has settled and antibody screen is negative
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage beyond 12 weeks in an RhD-negative non-sensitised woman is an indication for anti-D regardless of whether bleeding has settled (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
options.rationale: 250 IU is the dose for sensitising events before 12 weeks. From 13 weeks onwards, including threatened miscarriage, the recommended dose is 625 IU IM (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Anti-D 625 IU IM as a single dose now
options.rationale: From 13 weeks gestation onwards, sensitising events including bleeding in early pregnancy require 625 IU IM anti-D in non-sensitised RhD-negative women. Routine 1500 IU prophylaxis is given at 28 and 34 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Anti-D 1500 IU IM as a single dose now
options.rationale: 1500 IU is the routine antenatal prophylaxis dose given at 28 and 34 weeks; it is not the dose for a sensitising event at 13 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Withhold anti-D until Kleihauer testing confirms feto-maternal haemorrhage
options.rationale: Kleihauer testing is not required for sensitising events before 20 weeks; anti-D must be given within 72 hours regardless. Kleihauer is reserved for events from 20 weeks onwards to guide additional dosing (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

explanation.summary: Australian anti-D dosing differs from the UK. Before 12 weeks: 250 IU for sensitising events. From 12 weeks: 625 IU for sensitising events and for routine prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks.
key_points: AU first trimester sensitising event: 250 IU IM
AU >=12 weeks sensitising event or routine RAADP at 28 and 34 weeks: 625 IU IM
Kleihauer required after 20 weeks to size FMH and guide additional anti-D
Anti-D must be given within 72 hours of the event
explanation.pearls: Memorise 250/625 IU as the Australian first/second-trimester pair. UK uses 250/1500 IU - different country, different dose.

sources.label: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 woman attends her midwife at 35 weeks and 4 days. Her two prior births were uncomplicated term vaginal deliveries; her second neonate developed early-onset GBS sepsis confirmed on blood culture at 18 hours of life and recovered fully after IV benzylpenicillin. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated with normal antenatal serology and OGTT. There has been no preterm labour, no PROM, no fever and no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy. The local maternity service offers either universal culture-based screening at 35-37 weeks or a risk-factor pathway, and her midwife is planning intrapartum management.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 34 cm. Cephalic presentation. Foetal heart rate 142 bpm.
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal bloods unremarkable. OGTT normal. Anatomy USS at 20 weeks normal. No urine GBS reported during this pregnancy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding GBS screening and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for this woman?

options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab now and only give intrapartum prophylaxis if positive
options.rationale: A previous infant with invasive GBS disease is a stand-alone indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regardless of swab result; screening is not required for this woman (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

options.text: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36-37 weeks and decide prophylaxis on the result
options.rationale: Even with a swab inside the recommended 35-37 week window, a previous infant with invasive GBS disease still mandates intrapartum prophylaxis irrespective of the result (RANZCOG; CDC).

options.text: Do not screen; give intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin in labour regardless of swab status
options.rationale: A previous neonate with invasive early-onset GBS disease is an automatic indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis; screening adds no value and could falsely reassure if negative (RANZCOG; CDC).
source_refs: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

options.text: Do not screen and do not give intrapartum prophylaxis as she had no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy
options.rationale: GBS bacteriuria in the index pregnancy is one indication but is not the only one. A prior infant with invasive GBS is an independent indication that overrides absence of bacteriuria (RANZCOG; CDC).

options.text: Screen on admission in labour using a rapid intrapartum PCR and treat if positive
options.rationale: Intrapartum PCR is not the standard pathway in Australia, and again, a previous infant with invasive GBS mandates prophylaxis regardless of the test (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Under a universal culture-based screening pathway, a combined low vaginal-anorectal swab is collected at 35-37 weeks (CDC now 36-0 to 37-6). Positive swab triggers intrapartum benzylpenicillin prophylaxis. Risk-factor pathways are an alternative used in some Australian services.
key_points: Universal screening swab: 35-37 weeks, low vaginal + anorectal
Result valid approximately 5 weeks
IAP indicated for GBS-positive swab, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy, previous GBS-affected baby, or risk factors (preterm, ROM >18 h, fever >=38 degC)
First-line IAP: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV then 1.8 g 4-hourly until delivery
explanation.pearls: GBS bacteriuria at any time in pregnancy means treat now AND give intrapartum prophylaxis.

sources.label: CDC: Prevention of perinatal group B streptococcal disease

subtopic_detail: GBS screening - timing of universal swab

stem: A 34-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her GP using clinician-collected liquid-based cytology and HPV. She has had two previous CSTs, both negative for oncogenic HPV. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14. She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no pelvic pain. Today's result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is not pregnant.

data_table.Examination: Speculum not performed today (self-collected swab pathway).
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: HPV 16/18 detected. Reflex LBC: not performed.

lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 12 months
options.rationale: The 12-month repeat pathway applies to HPV non-16/18 with negative or pLSIL reflex cytology, not pHSIL or HSIL (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 5 years as her prior history is reassuring
options.rationale: A positive HPV result with high-grade cytology requires escalation not a return-to-routine interval, regardless of prior screening or vaccination history (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Refer directly for colposcopy
options.rationale: Under the NCSP, HPV non-16/18 with pHSIL or HSIL on reflex cytology mandates direct colposcopy referral, the same as any HPV 16/18 result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Refer directly for diagnostic cone biopsy without colposcopy
options.rationale: Cone biopsy is a treatment after colposcopic diagnosis, not a primary diagnostic step from a screening result (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab in 12 months to confirm the result
options.rationale: Self-collection is for routine screening, not for follow-up of a high-grade result; colposcopy is required now (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP, HPV 16/18 detected always means colposcopy referral. Other oncogenic HPV types are triaged by reflex LBC. Self-collection has equivalent sensitivity for high-grade disease.
key_points: HPV 16/18 positive: direct colposcopy
Other oncogenic HPV positive: reflex LBC -> if negative/pLSIL/LSIL, 12-month repeat HPV; if HSIL+/possible HSIL, colposcopy
HPV negative: 5-yearly recall (or earlier if symptomatic)
Routine screening: every 5 years from age 25 to 74
explanation.pearls: Self-collection became universally available in July 2022 in Australia and has equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected samples for CIN2+.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines
Australian Government Department of Health: NCSP

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her child-health-nurse appointment 6 weeks after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. She describes 4 weeks of low mood, poor sleep beyond infant needs, anhedonia and intrusive thoughts she is a 'bad mother'. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to the baby. She has a supportive partner and is breastfeeding well. There is no history of bipolar disorder, prior psychiatric admissions or postpartum psychosis. The nurse administers the COPE-recommended screening instrument: she scores 16 out of 30 with item 10 (self-harm thoughts) scored 0. There are no language or literacy barriers.

data_table.Examination: Affect flat. No psychotic features. Infant thriving on breastfeeds.
data_table.Investigations: TSH normal 4 weeks ago. Hb 116 g/L. No drug or alcohol use.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the EPDS in 2-4 weeks without GP referral as the score is below threshold
options.rationale: An EPDS score of 13 or more in the postnatal period meets the threshold for further clinical assessment under COPE guidance; 16 with persistent symptoms is not below threshold (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Refer to her GP for clinical assessment for postnatal depression within 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: An EPDS score >=13 with item-10 zero (no acute self-harm risk) indicates probable depression requiring GP clinical assessment within a short window. Acute escalation is not needed; routine perinatal mental-health follow-up is not sufficient (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Refer to the emergency department for acute psychiatric assessment today
options.rationale: ED referral is reserved for active suicidality, infanticidal ideation, psychotic features or rapid decline; item-10 zero with no risk features does not warrant ED (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily today without GP review as it is safe in breastfeeding
options.rationale: Antidepressant initiation is a medical decision after diagnostic assessment and informed consent; the child-health nurse should not prescribe without GP review (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Reassure that this represents normal 'baby blues' and review at the 8-week immunisation visit
options.rationale: Baby blues resolve by 2 weeks postpartum; symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks with EPDS 16 indicate probable postnatal depression, not blues (COPE 2023).
source_refs: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

explanation.summary: EPDS is the recommended perinatal depression screen in Australia, used at booking, 6-12 weeks postnatal, and as clinically indicated. Item 10 (self-harm) is a flagged safety question requiring same-day follow-up if positive.
key_points: EPDS >=13 = probable major depression; >=10 = possible depression
Always review item 10 (self-harm) regardless of total score
Combine with psychosocial assessment (e.g. ANRQ)
Postpartum psychosis is a different condition requiring emergency psychiatric review
explanation.pearls: Postpartum blues peak at days 3-5 and resolve by 2 weeks; persistent symptoms beyond 2 weeks warrant EPDS screening and clinical assessment.

sources.label: COPE Australian Clinical Practice Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period
RANZCOG: Mental health care in the perinatal period

stem: A 52-year-old woman attends her general practitioner with 14 months of severe vasomotor symptoms (multiple hot flushes daily, drenching night sweats, sleep disruption). She has had no menstrual period for 18 months. She has an intact uterus. She has no history of breast cancer, no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, no migraine with aura, BMI 26, BP 124/78, is a non-smoker, and has minimal alcohol intake. Her FBC, LFTs, lipids and TSH are normal. Mammogram and cervical screening are up to date. She has tried non-pharmacological measures without benefit and would like to commence menopausal hormone therapy. She has read about transdermal oestrogen and asks which regimen is recommended.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, BMI 26.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable. No breast lumps.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, LFTs, lipids, TSH normal. Mammogram normal. Cervical screening up to date.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line MHT regimen?

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen alone, daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus risks endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma; progestogen must be added (AMS; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Oral oestradiol with cyclical oral medroxyprogesterone, daily regimen
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen has a higher VTE risk than transdermal and is not preferred first-line when transdermal is available; cyclical progestogen is used in perimenopause but this woman is postmenopausal (>12 months amenorrhoea) (AMS).
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch with continuous combined micronised progesterone
options.rationale: Transdermal oestradiol (lower VTE risk than oral) plus continuous micronised progesterone (favourable breast and metabolic profile) is the preferred first-line MHT for a postmenopausal woman with intact uterus (AMS; NICE NG23).
source_refs: NICE NG23: Menopause diagnosis and management

options.text: Oral tibolone 2.5 mg daily as continuous monotherapy
options.rationale: Tibolone is a reasonable option but not first-line; it has a higher stroke risk in older women and is generally reserved when standard oestrogen-progestogen MHT is unsuitable (AMS).
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Vaginal oestriol cream 0.5 mg twice weekly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms but does not relieve systemic vasomotor symptoms (AMS).
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

explanation.summary: First-line MHT for systemic vasomotor symptoms in a postmenopausal woman with intact uterus: transdermal oestradiol plus continuous combined micronised progesterone. Transdermal route avoids first-pass effect and reduces VTE/stroke risk; micronised progesterone has a more favourable breast cancer signal than synthetic progestogens.
key_points: Intact uterus = progestogen always
Transdermal oestradiol preferred (lower VTE risk)
Continuous combined regimen once postmenopausal (>12 months amenorrhoea)
Vaginal oestrogen for isolated GSM only
explanation.pearls: Window of opportunity: starting MHT within 10 years of menopause and before age 60 has the most favourable cardiovascular profile.

sources.label: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement
NICE NG23: Menopause diagnosis and management

subtopic_detail: Menopause MHT - first-line for vasomotor symptoms

stem: A 27-year-old G2P0 (one prior tubal ectopic managed by salpingostomy 18 months ago) presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at an estimated 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by LMP with 36 hours of crampy left lower quadrant pain and intermittent dark vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable. The current pregnancy was conceived spontaneously and she did not use contraception. She has no shoulder tip pain. Her partner has accompanied her.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, focal left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism. Speculum: minimal dark blood, os closed. Bimanual: tender left adnexa.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 2,400 IU/L today (1,800 IU/L 48 hours ago). Hb 124 g/L, platelets 240. Blood group A Rh negative, antibody negative. TVUS: empty uterus, no intrauterine gestational sac or pseudosac, 2.1 cm complex left adnexal mass separate from ovary with surrounding ring of vascularity, small amount of free fluid in pouch of Douglas.

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Threatened miscarriage of viable intrauterine pregnancy with subchorionic haematoma
options.rationale: Beta-hCG is above the discriminatory zone yet there is no intrauterine gestational sac visible on TVUS, making a viable intrauterine pregnancy unlikely (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

options.text: Complete miscarriage with retained corpus luteum on adnexa
options.rationale: Complete miscarriage typically shows falling beta-hCG, not the suboptimal rise seen here, and would not produce a complex adnexal mass with peripheral vascularity (KEMH).

options.text: Pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) requiring serial beta-hCG monitoring
options.rationale: PUL is a working label only when TVUS shows no intrauterine or extrauterine pregnancy; here a complex adnexal mass with ring of vascularity is identified, which is sonographically diagnostic of ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

options.text: Tubal ectopic pregnancy in the right ampulla
options.rationale: Beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone with empty uterus, an adnexal mass separate from the ovary showing the 'ring of fire' peripheral vascularity, free fluid, and a suboptimal beta-hCG trajectory in a woman with previous ectopic is essentially diagnostic of ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

options.text: Haemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst with concurrent early intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: A haemorrhagic corpus luteum is intra-ovarian; the described mass is separate from the ovary, and no intrauterine sac is seen despite hCG above the discriminatory zone (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

explanation.summary: Diagnostic features here: hCG above discriminatory zone (>=1,500-2,000 IU/L) with empty uterus, complex adnexal mass separate from the ovary with peripheral ring of vascularity, free fluid, and a suboptimal hCG rise (<63% in 48 h) in a woman with previous tubal ectopic.
key_points: Discriminatory zone: 1,500-2,000 IU/L
Adnexal mass separate from ovary with ring of fire = ectopic
Suboptimal hCG rise <63% in 48 h is abnormal
Previous ectopic increases recurrence risk 10-20%
Anti-D 250 IU IM if Rh negative in first trimester
explanation.pearls: An empty uterus with hCG above the discriminatory zone is ectopic until proven otherwise. Heterotopic pregnancy is rare in spontaneous conception.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of early pregnancy problems

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman attends for booking ultrasound at an estimated 10 weeks and 2 days gestation by certain LMP. She has had no bleeding or abdominal pain. Her early pregnancy symptoms (nausea, breast tenderness) settled approximately 7 days ago. She has no significant medical history, is on folate 500 mcg and iodine 150 mcg daily, and has no allergies. She has not had a previous miscarriage, ectopic, or IVF cycle, and the dating by LMP correlates with sonographic measurements within the expected range. The transvaginal ultrasound is performed by an accredited sonographer with appropriate views obtained on a high-resolution probe in a tertiary unit.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Speculum not performed.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 38,000 IU/L. TVUS findings: single gestational sac in uterine fundus, mean sac diameter 26 mm; foetal pole present with crown-rump length 9 mm; no foetal cardiac activity observed after 5 minutes of careful observation by two operators; no subchorionic haematoma.

lead_in: Which finding most definitively confirms early pregnancy loss in this scan?

options.text: Crown-rump length >=7 mm with no foetal cardiac activity on TVUS
options.rationale: Established TVUS criteria for early pregnancy loss are CRL >=7 mm with no foetal cardiac activity, or mean sac diameter >=25 mm with no visible embryo; once an embryo is present the CRL criterion applies (NICE NG126; RANZCOG).
source_refs: NICE NG126: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage

options.text: Mean gestational sac diameter >=25 mm with no embryo on TVUS
options.rationale: A valid criterion but does not apply here because an embryo is clearly visible with measurable CRL (NICE NG126).
source_refs: NICE NG126: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage

options.text: Beta-hCG plateau over 48 hours with empty uterus on TVUS
options.rationale: Suggestive of non-viable pregnancy but never diagnostic alone; TVUS criteria are required to confirm loss (RANZCOG).

options.text: Loss of pregnancy symptoms during the first trimester with closed cervical os
options.rationale: Non-specific clinical sign that does not establish the diagnosis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Mean sac diameter to CRL ratio less than 5 mm on TVUS
options.rationale: Small sac size relative to CRL is a marker of higher risk of loss but is not a definitive diagnostic criterion in current guidelines (NICE NG126).
source_refs: NICE NG126: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage

explanation.summary: Strict TVUS criteria are used to diagnose early pregnancy loss because of the consequences of acting on a viable pregnancy. CRL >=7 mm with absent cardiac activity, or MSD >=25 mm with no embryo, are the two main criteria. Borderline measurements warrant repeat scan in 7-14 days.
key_points: CRL >=7 mm + no foetal heart = early pregnancy loss
MSD >=25 mm + no embryo = early pregnancy loss
If uncertain, repeat in 7-14 days (or use a second operator)
Management options: expectant, medical (misoprostol +/- mifepristone), surgical
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, wait and repeat. Acting on borderline measurements risks terminating a viable pregnancy.

sources.label: NICE NG126: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 36 weeks and 4 days gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 90 seconds. She had been complaining of a 24-hour frontal headache and epigastric pain. There is no known history of epilepsy, head injury, illicit drug use or hyponatraemia. She had documented systolic readings of 150-160 mmHg at her two most recent antenatal visits with proteinuria 2+ on dipstick. On arrival she is post-ictal, drowsy but maintaining her airway, and there is no further seizure activity. The foetal heart rate is 138 bpm with a reassuring CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, T 37.0.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, GCS 13, brisk reflexes with sustained clonus. Abdomen soft, fundus 36 cm, cephalic presentation.
data_table.Investigations: Urine protein:creatinine 145 mg/mmol. Hb 118, platelets 92, AST 88, ALT 76, urate 0.42, creatinine 96, LDH 480. CTG: baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line drug to administer now to prevent further seizures?

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 5 mg slow push, repeat at 5 minutes
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines may be used to terminate an active prolonged seizure but are not first-line for seizure prevention in eclampsia and carry neonatal respiratory depression risk (SOMANZ; Cochrane).

options.text: Intravenous phenytoin loading 1 g over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium sulphate for both treatment and prevention of eclamptic seizures (Cochrane Eclampsia Trial).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour maintenance
options.rationale: Magnesium sulphate is the agent of choice for eclampsia and severe pre-eclampsia, reducing recurrent seizures by approximately 50% versus diazepam or phenytoin; SOMANZ and RANZCOG recommend 4 g IV over 5-20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours (SOMANZ 2023; Cochrane).

options.text: Intravenous levetiracetam 1.5 g over 5 minutes as load
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is not a recommended first-line agent in eclampsia; magnesium sulphate has the strongest evidence (SOMANZ).

options.text: Immediate Caesarean section under general anaesthesia without anticonvulsant therapy
options.rationale: Delivery is required after maternal stabilisation but is not the immediate anticonvulsant; stabilise mother first with magnesium, then control BP, then plan birth (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Magnesium sulphate is first-line for prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures. Typical regimen: 4 g IV over 5-20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour for 24 hours, with monitoring for toxicity (reflexes, respiratory rate, urine output).
key_points: MgSO4 reduces recurrent seizures by ~50% vs diazepam/phenytoin (Cochrane)
Monitor: patellar reflexes, RR (>=12), UO (>=100 mL/4 h), SpO2
Toxicity antidote: 10% calcium gluconate 10 mL IV slow
Antihypertensive (labetalol, hydralazine or nifedipine) for systolic >=160 or diastolic >=110
Plan birth after maternal stabilisation
explanation.pearls: Stabilise then deliver. Birth is the only definitive treatment but premature delivery before maternal stabilisation worsens outcomes.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 31 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding while resting on the couch. The bleeding has filled one sanitary pad in 30 minutes. She has had no abdominal pain, no contractions, no rupture of membranes and reports normal foetal movements. Her previous two births were by lower-segment Caesarean section for breech and for failure to progress. At her 20-week morphology scan the placenta was reported as anterior, fundal and well clear of the internal os. She is a non-smoker, denies cocaine or other substance use, and there has been no recent intercourse or trauma. She is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.7, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender, uterus relaxed, fundal height 32 cm. Cephalic presentation. Speculum (no digital VE): moderate fresh blood pooling in vagina, cervix appears closed.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116, platelets 240, coags normal. Group A Rh positive. CTG reactive. Bedside USS now: posterior placenta with leading edge 8 mm from internal os.

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of her antepartum haemorrhage?

options.text: Placental abruption with concealed retroplacental haematoma
options.rationale: Abruption typically presents with painful bleeding, tonically contracted uterus, and possible foetal compromise; this woman has painless bleeding with a soft non-tender uterus and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG APH).

options.text: Low-lying placenta or placenta praevia covering internal os
options.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the late second or third trimester with a placental edge within 20 mm of the internal os on USS defines a low-lying placenta and is the most likely cause here (RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: KEMH: Placenta praevia and accreta spectrum

options.text: Vasa praevia with foetal vessels traversing the os
options.rationale: Vasa praevia bleeds at the time of membrane rupture and is typically associated with rapid foetal compromise (sinusoidal CTG, bradycardia); membranes here are intact and CTG is reassuring (RANZCOG).

options.text: Uterine rupture along a prior LSCS scar
options.rationale: Rupture typically presents with severe abdominal pain, loss of station, foetal bradycardia and maternal compromise during labour; this patient is not labouring and is stable (RANZCOG).

options.text: Cervical ectropion bleed from friable columnar epithelium
options.rationale: Cervical lesions cause minor postcoital spotting; volume of bleeding here (full pad in 30 min) is excessive for an ectropion and pooling of fresh blood with a closed cervix points elsewhere (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Painless bright red bleeding after 20 weeks with a low-lying placenta on USS is placenta praevia (low-lying placenta in updated nomenclature: placental edge <20 mm from internal os). Avoid digital vaginal examination until praevia excluded.
key_points: Updated terminology (RANZCOG 2018): low-lying placenta if edge within 20 mm of internal os; placenta praevia if covering os
Painless bleeding + soft uterus + abnormal placental location = praevia
Confirm with TVUS (safe)
Anti-D if Rh negative; admit, IV access, group and hold, CTG, plan steroids if <34/40 and considering preterm birth
20-week scan may relocate as the lower segment forms - repeat at 32 weeks if low-lying
explanation.pearls: Painful + tender uterus = think abruption. Painless + soft uterus = think praevia. ROM-triggered foetal bradycardia = think vasa praevia.

sources.label: KEMH: Placenta praevia and accreta spectrum

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 38 weeks and 6 days gestation is admitted in early labour. The foetus is in transverse lie despite an ECV attempt one week prior, and the obstetric team is considering Caesarean section. Spontaneous rupture of membranes occurs during the admission examination with a moderate gush of clear liquor. Immediately afterward the midwife identifies a pulsating loop of umbilical cord protruding through the cervix into the vagina. The CTG shows an abrupt foetal heart rate deceleration to 80 bpm. The obstetric team is being called and the emergency theatre is being readied for Category 1 Caesarean section.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 96 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Cord palpable in vagina, cervix 3 cm dilated, transverse lie, foetal back inferior. Membranes ruptured (clear liquor).
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged foetal bradycardia 80 bpm. FBC, group and hold sent.

lead_in: What is the most important immediate manoeuvre while the theatre is being prepared?

options.text: Replace cord gently into uterus through cervix, observe maternal vitals while preparing for delivery
options.rationale: Cord replacement is not recommended; it may cause vasospasm or compression. Hands-off the cord, or minimal handling only if needed to elevate the presenting part (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and place in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (vaginal hand pushing the presenting part upwards) and maternal positioning (knee-chest or Trendelenburg) until delivery is the immediate action to relieve cord compression while preparing for Category 1 CS (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Start IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite vaginal birth at 3 cm cervical dilatation in current transverse lie
options.rationale: Augmentation will worsen cord compression and is contraindicated; this patient also has a transverse lie and is at 3 cm, so vaginal birth is not feasible (RANZCOG).

options.text: Fill the bladder with 500 mL warmed saline via Foley catheter only and avoid manual elevation of presenting part
options.rationale: Bladder filling can elevate the presenting part and is a useful adjunct if transfer is prolonged but is not the most immediate manoeuvre; manual elevation and maternal positioning come first (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 mcg SC to reduce contractions and observe the cord pulsation before further intervention
options.rationale: Acute tocolysis may be considered as an adjunct if uterine activity is impairing perfusion or transfer is delayed, but the immediate priority is manual elevation and positioning (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency. Immediate priorities: call for help, manual elevation of presenting part off the cord, maternal positioning (knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg), Category 1 Caesarean section if not at full dilatation. Bladder filling and tocolysis are adjuncts if transfer is delayed.
key_points: Risk factors: malpresentation, polyhydramnios, prematurity, ARM with high station
Minimal handling of the cord; keep moist and warm
Cat 1 CS unless full dilatation with imminent vaginal birth
Continuous CTG until delivery
Document call-to-delivery interval; target <30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Knee-chest position uses gravity to displace the presenting part off the cord and is especially useful during transfer.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman attends the birth suite at 39 weeks and 1 day gestation reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid 4 hours ago with ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, normal foetal movements, and no per-vaginal bleeding. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated: booking bloods normal, OGTT normal at 28 weeks, group B streptococcus low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36 weeks was negative, and her 20-week anatomy scan was normal. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is a non-smoker.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7, RR 16.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, fundus 39 cm, cephalic, longitudinal lie. Sterile speculum: clear fluid pooling in posterior fornix, cervix appears favourable, no obvious cord. No digital VE performed.
data_table.Investigations: Maternal observations unremarkable. CTG reactive, baseline 140, variability 12, accelerations present, no decelerations. Bedside USS: AFI 9 cm, cephalic, normal Doppler.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Discharge home with daily community midwife review and induction if not labouring by 96 hours after rupture
options.rationale: Expectant management for that long at term exceeds Australian guidance; chorioamnionitis risk rises beyond ~24 hours (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Offer induction of labour now or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with shared decision-making
options.rationale: At term with confirmed PROM, RANZCOG recommends offering either immediate induction or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with informed consent; immediate induction reduces chorioamnionitis risk slightly without increasing CS rates (RANZCOG; TERMPROM trial).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Proceed to immediate Category 2 Caesarean section regardless of patient preference or GBS status today
options.rationale: PROM at term is not an indication for CS; vaginal birth is appropriate where there is no other contraindication (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics now regardless of GBS status, temperature, or duration of rupture
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in afebrile term PROM with negative GBS; reserve antibiotics for clinical chorioamnionitis or GBS-positive status (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Tocolysis with oral nifedipine and IM corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation before any delivery plan
options.rationale: Tocolysis and steroids are not indicated at 39+1 weeks; both are for preterm gestations (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: Term PROM: offer induction of labour (oxytocin or prostaglandin if unfavourable cervix) or expectant management up to 24 hours with informed consent. The TERMPROM trial showed lower chorioamnionitis and neonatal infection with immediate induction; CS rates were similar.
key_points: Confirm PROM by sterile speculum (pooling) - avoid digital VE
Continuous CTG; assess for chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foetal tachycardia, malodorous discharge)
Group B Streptococcus status guides intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
Anti-D not required at term gestation for PROM alone
explanation.pearls: Document the time of membrane rupture - the 18-hour threshold for IAP in some GBS pathways starts from rupture, not from labour.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes

stem: A 24-year-old nulligravid woman attends her general practice with a 3-year history of progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain. Pain begins 1-2 days before menses, peaks on day 1-2, and is associated with deep dyspareunia and occasional dyschezia. She has no urinary symptoms, no postcoital bleeding and a normal cervical screening test 18 months ago. She has tried regular ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily during menses with partial benefit. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, is sexually active and is not currently planning pregnancy in the next 2 years; she is open to hormonal therapy and has no contraindications to oestrogen.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile, mildly tender, no adnexal masses, no palpable nodularity in the pouch of Douglas (this can be normal in early endometriosis).
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal ovaries, no endometriomas, no deep infiltrating lesions identified.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before initiating any empirical medical therapy in clinic
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy is recommended before laparoscopy; surgery is offered if empirical treatment fails or imaging suggests deep disease or endometrioma (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin) with hormonal add-back therapy from the outset
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are second-line after failure of COCP or progestogens due to cost, side effects and bone density concerns (ESHRE).

options.text: Trial of continuous combined oral contraceptive pill plus NSAID as needed for pain
options.rationale: First-line empirical therapy for suspected endometriosis without red flags is hormonal suppression with COCP (continuous preferable for symptom control) or progestogen, plus NSAID for breakthrough pain; effective in 70-80% (RANZCOG; ESHRE 2022).

options.text: Refer for laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Definitive surgery is inappropriate in a 24-year-old who has not completed her family and has not trialled medical therapy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Serum CA-125 testing to confirm endometriosis diagnosis before any therapy is initiated
options.rationale: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis and is not recommended as a diagnostic test (ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Empirical hormonal suppression (COCP, progestogens or LNG-IUS) plus NSAIDs is first-line for suspected endometriosis. Imaging may be normal in superficial peritoneal disease; absence of findings on USS does not exclude endometriosis. Laparoscopy is reserved for failed medical therapy, suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis or fertility concerns.
key_points: Empirical hormonal therapy before laparoscopy
Continuous COCP reduces menstruation and pain
LNG-IUS, dienogest 2 mg daily and DMPA are alternatives
Pelvic USS may be normal in superficial endometriosis
Refer to gynaecologist if no response in 3-6 months or red flags
explanation.pearls: Dyspareunia + dyschezia + cyclical pelvic pain + relief with menses ending is the classical history; consider endometriosis even with a normal pelvic ultrasound.

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 18 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 45-90 days), facial and chest hair growth requiring weekly waxing, weight gain of 6 kg over 12 months, and acanthosis nigricans. BMI 29, modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12. Total testosterone is at the upper limit of normal, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated, 17-hydroxyprogesterone normal, TSH and prolactin normal, beta-hCG negative. Pelvic ultrasound is technically suboptimal due to bowel gas: each ovary appears to contain 'multiple small follicles' but follicle count cannot be quantified and ovarian volumes are not reported. Anti-Mullerian hormone is 58 pmol/L (markedly elevated).

data_table.Examination: BMI 29. Modified Ferriman-Gallwey score 12 (hirsutism). Acanthosis nigricans at nape. No virilisation. No goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Total testosterone mildly elevated, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated. 17-OHP normal. TSH normal. Prolactin normal. Beta-hCG negative. Day-of-test LH:FSH 2.5. Pelvic USS: each ovary contains 22 follicles 2-9 mm, ovarian volume 11 mL each. Fasting glucose 5.4, HbA1c 5.7%.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step under the 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline?

options.text: Diagnose PCOS now on the basis of oligo-anovulation plus clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism
options.rationale: The 2023 guideline confirms that any two of (oligo-anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS or elevated AMH) meet criteria in adults; this patient meets the first two so the diagnosis is established without USS or AMH (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

options.text: Defer diagnosis until a repeat pelvic ultrasound quantifies follicle count and ovarian volume
options.rationale: USS is not required once two other Rotterdam features are present, and the 2023 guideline accepts AMH as an alternative to ultrasound; further imaging adds no diagnostic value here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

options.text: Defer diagnosis as elevated AMH alone is sufficient and other criteria are not required
options.rationale: AMH or polycystic morphology in isolation is not diagnostic; two of three Rotterdam-derived features remain required in adults (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

options.text: Defer diagnosis until LH:FSH ratio confirms elevated LH consistent with PCOS
options.rationale: LH:FSH ratio is not part of the Rotterdam-derived criteria and is not required for diagnosis (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

options.text: Defer diagnosis until adrenal hyperplasia is excluded with an ACTH stimulation test
options.rationale: 17-hydroxyprogesterone is already normal which adequately excludes non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia; ACTH stimulation is unnecessary here (Teede 2023).
source_refs: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

explanation.summary: PCOS diagnosis (Rotterdam, endorsed by 2023 international guideline): 2 of 3 of oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology (>=20 follicles per ovary on high-resolution TVUS or ovarian volume >=10 mL, or elevated AMH), after exclusion of other causes (thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia, non-classical CAH, Cushing's, androgen-secreting tumour).
key_points: Rotterdam: 2/3 features
Exclude mimics: TFTs, prolactin, 17-OHP +/- DHEAS
Within 8 years of menarche: hyperandrogenism + oligo-anovulation (USS unreliable)
Long-term: screen for IGT/T2DM, dyslipidaemia, endometrial protection, mental health
Lifestyle is first-line; metformin and COCP common adjuncts
explanation.pearls: USS appearance of polycystic ovaries alone, without clinical or biochemical features, does not make PCOS.

sources.label: Teede HJ et al. 2023 International PCOS Guideline

stem: A 44-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practitioner with 8 months of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Her cycles remain regular at 28 days but periods now last 9-10 days with passage of clots and she requires double protection (tampon and pad) on the heaviest days. She has needed to take time off work and has felt increasingly fatigued. There is no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. She is not on hormonal therapy or anticoagulants. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. Cervical screening is up to date and negative. She had a normal pelvic ultrasound 18 months ago for unrelated symptoms.

data_table.Examination: BMI 26. Speculum: cervix appears normal. Bimanual: anteverted uterus, irregularly enlarged to approximately 12-week size, non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (microcytic), ferritin 9 ng/mL, TSH 1.6, beta-hCG negative, coagulation profile normal, von Willebrand screen normal. TVUS today: uterus 11 x 7 x 6 cm with two intramural fibroids (4.5 cm and 3.2 cm) distorting the cavity; endometrial thickness 9 mm and homogeneous; ovaries normal.

lead_in: Using the FIGO PALM-COEIN classification, which is the most likely cause of her abnormal uterine bleeding?

options.text: AUB-P (endometrial polyp on TVUS)
options.rationale: No endometrial polyp is seen on TVUS; endometrium is homogeneous (FIGO PALM-COEIN).

options.text: AUB-A (adenomyosis on TVUS/MRI)
options.rationale: USS features of adenomyosis (globular uterus, asymmetric myometrium, sub-endometrial linear striations) are not described; intramural fibroids are the dominant finding (FIGO).

options.text: AUB-L (leiomyoma/fibroid on TVUS)
options.rationale: Two intramural fibroids distorting the cavity (FIGO type 2-5 depending on location) most plausibly explain heavy regular menses and an enlarged irregular uterus; classification is AUB-L (FIGO PALM-COEIN).

options.text: AUB-O (ovulatory dysfunction on history)
options.rationale: Ovulatory dysfunction is typically associated with irregular cycles; her cycles remain regular at 28 days (FIGO).

options.text: AUB-C (coagulopathy on bleeding history)
options.rationale: Coagulation studies and von Willebrand screen are normal, and there is no personal or family bleeding history (FIGO).

explanation.summary: FIGO PALM-COEIN classifies AUB into structural (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia) and non-structural (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified). Heavy regular menses + enlarged irregular uterus + intramural fibroids on USS = AUB-L.
key_points: PALM = structural, COEIN = non-structural
Leiomyomas sub-classified 0-8 by location (FIGO)
Investigations for HMB: FBC, ferritin, TFTs, beta-hCG, TVUS
Endometrial sampling if >=45 years or risk factors (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, persistent IMB)
Treat iron deficiency anaemia in parallel
explanation.pearls: Persistent intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding always needs further investigation regardless of imaging (consider hysteroscopy).

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman (one prior uncomplicated term vaginal birth 3 years ago, birth weight 3,650 g) attends for her routine 28-week 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She has a BMI of 30 (pre-pregnancy 28), a sister with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at age 38, and identifies as South Asian. Her booking HbA1c was 5.5% and booking fasting glucose was 4.7 mmol/L. The pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated: BP averaging 110-118/68-74, fundal height appropriate, foetal movements normal, 20-week morphology scan normal. She is on pregnancy multivitamin only, no allergies, non-smoker, social drinker (ceased), and has had her influenza and pertussis vaccinations. She follows a primarily vegetarian diet and walks 30 minutes most days.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 78, BMI 30.
data_table.Examination: Fundus 28 cm, cephalic. No glycosuria.
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT today (after 8-hour fast): fasting venous plasma glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.4 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.7 mmol/L. Booking HbA1c 5.5% (37 mmol/mol). Urine ACR normal. TSH 1.4.

lead_in: Using ADIPS 2014 (RANZCOG-endorsed) 75 g OGTT thresholds, which interpretation is correct?

options.text: Normal OGTT; reassure and continue routine antenatal care without insulin or metformin
options.rationale: Fasting 5.3 (>=5.1) and 2-hour 8.7 (>=8.5) both meet ADIPS GDM thresholds; this is not a normal result (ADIPS 2014).
source_refs: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

options.text: Impaired fasting glucose only - repeat OGTT in 4 weeks before commencing therapy
options.rationale: ADIPS does not use 'impaired fasting glucose' for GDM diagnosis; any one threshold breach (fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5) diagnoses GDM (ADIPS).
source_refs: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

options.text: GDM diagnosed - fasting and 2-hour values both at or above ADIPS thresholds
options.rationale: ADIPS 2014 thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; meeting any single threshold diagnoses GDM. Her fasting (5.3) and 2-hour (8.7) both meet criteria (ADIPS; RANZCOG GDM).
source_refs: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

options.text: Overt diabetes in pregnancy - commence subcutaneous insulin immediately at booking visit
options.rationale: Overt diabetes requires fasting >=7.0, random >=11.1 or HbA1c >=6.5%; her values are below these thresholds (ADIPS).
source_refs: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

options.text: GDM diagnosed - the 1-hour value alone is diagnostic per ADIPS criteria
options.rationale: Her 1-hour value (9.4) is below the 10.0 threshold; diagnosis here is based on fasting and 2-hour, not 1-hour (ADIPS).
source_refs: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

explanation.summary: ADIPS 2014 (RANZCOG-endorsed) 75 g OGTT diagnostic thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L. Any one value meeting threshold diagnoses GDM. Overt diabetes thresholds: fasting >=7.0, random >=11.1, HbA1c >=6.5%.
key_points: Universal screening at 24-28 weeks (earlier if risk factors)
GDM management: lifestyle, BGL monitoring, metformin and/or insulin
Targets: fasting <5.0-5.3, 1-h <7.4, 2-h <6.7 (varies slightly by service)
Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks to detect persistent T2DM
Lifelong T2DM screening (yearly fasting glucose or HbA1c)
explanation.pearls: ADIPS thresholds (5.1/10.0/8.5) differ from older NDDG and Carpenter-Coustan criteria - know the Australian numbers cold for the exam.

sources.label: ADIPS: Consensus guidelines for diagnosis of GDM 2014

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman is in second-stage labour at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation in a public hospital birth suite. Her two previous births were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries at 3,750 g and 3,920 g. This pregnancy was complicated by gestational diabetes managed on metformin with reasonable glycaemic control (most fasting BGLs 5.0-5.4, postprandial 6.5-7.2). The 36-week growth scan suggested an EFW of 4,250 g (95th centile). She has been pushing for 25 minutes. The foetal head delivers in OA position but appears to retract tightly against the perineum (turtle sign). Routine downward traction does not deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife calls the obstetric emergency.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 132/82, HR 96, T 36.8. Foetal HR was reassuring prior to delivery of head.
data_table.Examination: Turtle sign positive. Head delivered, no restitution, anterior shoulder impacted behind symphysis. No cord around neck.
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks negative. Hb 118. CTG: prior to delivery, normal baseline.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line manoeuvre after calling for help?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to dislodge the anterior shoulder from behind the symphysis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated; it worsens impaction and may cause uterine rupture or brachial plexus injury (RANZCOG; PROMPT).

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre and apply suprapubic pressure as first manoeuvres
options.rationale: First-line manoeuvre in the HELPERR sequence after calling for help is McRoberts (hyperflexion of maternal hips on abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure, resolving up to 50-70% of cases (RANZCOG; PROMPT; ACOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to Zavanelli manoeuvre and emergency Caesarean section now
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort procedure attempted after exhaustion of all other manoeuvres; it is not first-line (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform symphysiotomy at the bedside before any other manoeuvre is attempted
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is rarely used in high-resource settings and only as a last resort with appropriate skills (RANZCOG).

options.text: Cut the umbilical cord at the perineum and proceed to emergency Caesarean
options.rationale: The cord cannot be cut while the foetus is still impacted; this would cause severe hypoxia. Shoulder dystocia is managed vaginally with the HELPERR sequence (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia management uses the HELPERR mnemonic: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotation: Rubin II, Wood's screw), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin all-fours). McRoberts and suprapubic pressure together resolve most cases.
key_points: Call for help: obstetrician, anaesthetist, paediatrician, additional midwife, scribe
Avoid fundal pressure (worsens impaction)
Avoid excessive head traction (brachial plexus injury)
Document time intervals, manoeuvres, foetal position, staff present
After delivery: check for PPH, perineal trauma, neonatal injury (Erb's palsy, fractures, hypoxia)
explanation.pearls: Macrosomia, GDM, prior shoulder dystocia, prolonged second stage and operative vaginal birth are risk factors but most shoulder dystocia is unpredictable.

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends the sexual health service with 5 days of progressive lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and abnormal vaginal discharge. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past 2 months, has not used condoms consistently, and is on the COCP. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no past surgical history, no allergies and is otherwise well. There is no urinary frequency, dysuria, fever, vomiting or rectal bleeding. She is haemodynamically stable and not pregnant. She has no IUD and no history of ectopic pregnancy. She has not yet had any antibiotics.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen: bilateral lower quadrant tenderness, no peritonism, no mass. Speculum: mucopurulent cervical discharge, friable cervix. Bimanual: marked cervical motion tenderness, bilateral adnexal tenderness, no palpable mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Self-collected vaginal swab NAAT for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, Mycoplasma genitalium pending. HIV/syphilis/HBV/HCV sent. WCC 13.2, CRP 42. TVUS: no tubo-ovarian abscess, free fluid in pouch of Douglas. Urinalysis negative.

lead_in: Which empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen is recommended by Therapeutic Guidelines for mild-moderate PID?

options.text: Amoxicillin 1 g PO three times daily for 7 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone does not cover gonorrhoea, chlamydia or anaerobes and is inadequate for PID (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days
options.rationale: Quinolone resistance in Neisseria gonorrhoeae is high in Australia; ciprofloxacin monotherapy is not recommended (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy without gonorrhoea or anaerobe cover
options.rationale: Doxycycline alone misses gonorrhoea (requires ceftriaxone) and anaerobes (requires metronidazole) (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat, plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, plus metronidazole 400 mg BD
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines recommends combined cover of gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone), chlamydia/Mycoplasma genitalium (doxycycline), and anaerobes (metronidazole) for outpatient PID; treat empirically before NAAT results (TG; AusSTI).

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose alone without ceftriaxone, doxycycline or metronidazole cover today
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is insufficient for PID and increases macrolide resistance in Mycoplasma genitalium; combination 14-day therapy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

explanation.summary: Outpatient PID (mild-moderate): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat + doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg PO BD for 14 days. Inpatient (severe, tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, IUD with PID, failed outpatient): IV ceftriaxone + IV/PO doxycycline + IV/PO metronidazole, with surgical drainage for TOA.
key_points: Treat empirically; do not wait for NAAT
Trace and treat sexual partners
Test for HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B/C
Mycoplasma genitalium increasingly implicated; consider moxifloxacin if M. gen confirmed and doxycycline failure
Counsel about increased risk of ectopic, infertility, chronic pelvic pain
explanation.pearls: PID diagnosis is primarily clinical: pelvic/lower abdominal pain plus cervical motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness in a sexually active woman.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Pelvic inflammatory disease

stem: A 38-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practice for contraceptive counselling. Her youngest child is 4 months old and exclusively breastfed. Her BMI is 33. She has migraine with aura (typical scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral headache, average twice per month) since age 19. She had a left ankle ORIF 6 weeks ago for a complex fracture and remains in a removable boot, mobilising with crutches; her physiotherapist plans to discontinue the boot in 2 weeks. Her mother had a provoked DVT on the combined oral contraceptive at age 25. She is planning a long-haul flight (16 hours) in two weeks, which already imposed by her reduced mobility post-fracture. She is normotensive (118/74) and a non-smoker. She has no liver disease, no breast cancer history, no current pelvic infection and no abnormal uterine bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76. BMI 33.
data_table.Examination: Well. Left ankle in boot. No breast lumps. No pelvic tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date negative. No recent FBC.

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate at this consultation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: COCP is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for migraine with aura at any age, and additionally UKMEC 3-4 with recent immobilisation post-orthopaedic surgery, BMI >=35 (here 33, but elevated baseline VTE), family history of VTE, age >=35 with multiple risk factors, and breastfeeding before 6 weeks postpartum (now 4 months so less restrictive but cumulative risk is high) (FSRH UKMEC 2016 + 2024 updates).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Combined transdermal contraceptive patch - weekly application regimen
options.rationale: Same systemic VTE/arterial risks as COCP; UKMEC 4 for migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) - clinic insertion
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1-2 in all of her stated risk categories (migraine with aura UKMEC 2, postpartum >=4 weeks UKMEC 1, VTE risk UKMEC 2, BMI 33 UKMEC 1) and provides highly effective long-term contraception suitable for breastfeeding and immobilisation; this is the most appropriate choice (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)
Family Planning Australia: Contraception in special circumstances

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) - IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 2 for migraine with aura and for breastfeeding but is UKMEC 3 in women with multiple VTE risk factors (recent surgery, immobilisation); LARC options with no VTE signal (IUDs, implant) are preferable (FSRH UKMEC).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel) - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: POP is safe (UKMEC 1-2) in her circumstances but is less effective (typical-use failure approximately 9%) than LNG-IUS and depends heavily on adherence; in a woman with this risk profile a LARC is preferable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)

explanation.summary: This woman has multiple UKMEC 3/4 considerations for oestrogen-containing contraception: migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 absolute), recent orthopaedic surgery with reduced mobility (UKMEC 3), family history of VTE (UKMEC 3 if first-degree relative <45), planned long-haul flight (additional risk), age 38, BMI 33. The safest and most effective option is the LNG-IUS, which is UKMEC 1-2 across all her risk factors and offers 8 years of contraception.
key_points: Migraine with aura = UKMEC 4 for any combined hormonal contraception (any age)
Recent surgery with immobilisation = UKMEC 3-4 for CHC
BMI >=35 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
First-degree relative VTE <45 = UKMEC 3 for CHC
LNG-IUS and Cu-IUD are UKMEC 1-2 in nearly all medical contexts; insertion postpartum after 4 weeks is straightforward
explanation.pearls: Stack the UKMEC scores. Any one '4' rules out a method; combinations of '3's should push you to a different category entirely.

sources.label: FSRH: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use 2016 (amended)
Family Planning Australia: Contraception in special circumstances

subtopic_detail: STEMI ECG criteria - posterior MI recognition
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with nausea and diaphoresis. He has a history of hypertension and is an ex-smoker. He took two of his wife's GTN sprays without relief. The nearest primary PCI centre is 45 minutes away by road. Vital signs show HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% on room air, RR 18. He is in distress but mentating well. The initial 12-lead ECG shows 1.5 mm ST depression in V1 to V3 with prominent R waves in V2, upright T waves in V1 to V3, and 1 mm ST depression in II, III and aVF. There is no anterior ST elevation. A right-sided and posterior lead ECG is performed and shows 1 mm ST elevation in V7, V8 and V9. High-sensitivity troponin returns at 320 ng/L (reference <14). He has no contraindications to fibrinolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1.5 mm V1 to V3, dominant R in V2, upright T V1 to V3. Posterior leads V7 to V9 show 1 mm ST elevation. Inferior leads show 1 mm depression.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 320 ng/L (<14). FBC, UEC, glucose, lipids pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Conservative medical management with aspirin, ticagrelor and enoxaparin only, with serial troponins
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI is a STEMI equivalent and mandates emergent reperfusion. Treating as NSTE-ACS is the classic anti-pattern of missing posterior MI because there is no anterior ST elevation; this option would only be correct if anterior changes were purely reciprocal in a confirmed non-STEMI presentation with negative posterior leads (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Transfer for primary PCI without giving fibrinolysis, accepting the 45 minute transfer delay plus catheter-lab activation time, with full anticoagulation en route
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred only when first medical contact to balloon is achievable within 120 minutes for transferred patients. With a 45 minute road transfer plus catheter-lab activation, that target will be breached. This would be the correct strategy if the PCI centre were <60 minutes away or onsite, in which case fibrinolysis is avoided (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now, plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin, then transfer for pharmaco-invasive PCI within 3 to 24 hours
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI confirmed by V7 to V9 ST elevation is a STEMI equivalent requiring immediate reperfusion. When primary PCI cannot occur within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway is fibrinolysis (tenecteplase weight-banded), antiplatelets (aspirin + clopidogrel preferred with lysis), and anticoagulation (enoxaparin), followed by transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours regardless of clinical course.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Treat the changes as non-ST-elevation, give aspirin, ticagrelor and fondaparinux, and refer for angiography within 72 hours
options.rationale: The 72 hour invasive window is the NSTE-ACS pathway and does not apply to STEMI equivalents. This would be correct only if posterior leads were negative and the troponin/ECG profile fitted intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS rather than transmural posterior infarction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Repeat ECG in 30 minutes and reassess; if persistent changes, then consider fibrinolysis
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established by V7 to V9 ST elevation and positive troponin; further serial ECGs add no information. The classic anti-pattern is treating posterior STEMI as an evolving NSTEMI; deliberate delay costs myocardium (time-is-muscle). Serial ECGs are appropriate only when initial ECG is non-diagnostic and troponin is pending (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Posterior STEMI presents with ST depression and tall R waves in V1 to V3 and is confirmed with posterior leads V7 to V9 showing >=0.5 mm ST elevation. It is a STEMI equivalent. When primary PCI is not achievable within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway (fibrinolysis then transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours) is indicated.
key_points: Posterior STEMI = ST depression V1 to V3 with dominant R, confirmed by V7 to V9 elevation >=0.5 mm
STEMI equivalents (posterior, de Winter, Wellens evolution, LBBB with Sgarbossa) need urgent reperfusion
Primary PCI within 120 min FMC-to-balloon; otherwise lyse then transfer 3 to 24 h
Clopidogrel (not ticagrelor) is preferred with fibrinolysis to limit bleeding
