explanation.pearls: Always do posterior and right-sided leads when you see V1 to V3 ST depression with upright T waves and dominant R in V2 (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: NSTE-ACS - timing of invasive strategy and GRACE risk
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to a tertiary metropolitan emergency department with three episodes of central chest pain over the last 12 hours, each lasting around 15 minutes, the last occurring 90 minutes ago at rest while she was reading on her couch. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%), longstanding hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline stable), and was admitted 18 months ago with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction managed conservatively at that admission. Her current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg. She is an ex-smoker (quit 8 years ago, 25 pack-years prior) and has a sister with CABG at 64. Vital signs at triage are HR 88 regular, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% on room air, RR 18, afebrile. She is mildly distressed but well perfused with warm peripheries. Examination is unremarkable apart from a grade 2 soft systolic murmur at the apex without radiation. The 12-lead ECG shows 1 mm horizontal ST depression in V4 to V6 with biphasic T waves in V5 and V6; there is no ST elevation in any territory and no posterior or right-sided changes on additional leads. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 78 ng/L (reference <14) drawn at 1 hour after symptom resolution. There is no pulmonary oedema clinically or radiographically and no haemodynamic instability. The cardiology registrar has calculated a GRACE score of 158.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1 mm V4 to V6, biphasic T waves V5 to V6. No ST elevation.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 78 ng/L (<14) at 1 h post-symptoms. eGFR 52. HbA1c 8.2%.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate timing of coronary angiography?

options.text: Within 24 hours of presentation, as part of an early invasive strategy
options.rationale: Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS recommends invasive angiography within 24 hours for high-risk NSTE-ACS: GRACE >140, dynamic ST or T-wave changes, or troponin rise consistent with MI. She meets all three criteria. The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for very-high-risk features (refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic/electrical instability, mechanical complication), which she does not have.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 2 hours of presentation, as part of an immediate invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic or electrical instability, or mechanical complications. None apply here. This would be correct if she developed ongoing pain despite therapy, VT/VF, cardiogenic shock or acute mitral regurgitation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 72 hours of presentation, as part of a delayed invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 72 hour window applies to intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS without dynamic changes or significant troponin rise. GRACE 158 plus dynamic ECG changes plus positive troponin places her in the high-risk group requiring intervention within 24 hours. This would be correct only if GRACE were 109 to 140 with no dynamic changes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Selective invasive strategy guided by non-invasive ischaemia testing after discharge
options.rationale: Selective/ischaemia-guided strategy is reserved for low-risk NSTE-ACS (GRACE <109, no dynamic changes, negative troponin). The anti-pattern is downgrading risk because of frailty or CKD; high-risk biomarker and ECG features override (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Defer angiography given CKD stage 3a; manage medically and reassess at 6 weeks
options.rationale: CKD stage 3a is not a contraindication to angiography; high-risk NSTE-ACS with CKD has greater absolute benefit from early invasive management with contrast minimisation and peri-procedural hydration. KHA-CARI explicitly endorses angiography in this setting. This would only be correct in stage 5 CKD on conservative management with limited life expectancy (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; KHA-CARI).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Heart Foundation/CSANZ stratifies NSTE-ACS into four invasive timing categories: immediate (<2 h) for instability; early (<24 h) for high-risk; delayed (<72 h) for intermediate-risk; selective for low-risk. High-risk criteria include GRACE >140, dynamic ECG changes, or rise/fall of troponin meeting MI criteria.
key_points: GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTE-ACS, angio within 24 h
Refractory ischaemia, instability, mechanical complication = <2 h
Dynamic ECG and positive troponin push to early invasive
CKD is not a barrier; mitigate contrast risk instead
explanation.pearls: Treat 'NSTE-ACS' as a risk-stratified pathway not a single diagnosis; the timing decision is the assessable step (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 61-year-old man underwent successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD 6 weeks ago for anterior STEMI. He has been compliant with aspirin 100 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no atrial fibrillation, no prior stroke, eGFR 78, and no prior bleeding. He attends cardiology review and asks about antiplatelet duration. Two weeks ago he was diagnosed with a 3 cm right colonic adenocarcinoma on screening colonoscopy and is scheduled for elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy in 4 weeks. He has had no chest pain since the procedure.

data_table.Procedure: Primary PCI LAD, 1 DES, 6 weeks ago.
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg, ticagrelor 90 mg BD, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metoprolol 50 mg BD.
data_table.Risk factors: No AF, no prior bleed/stroke, eGFR 78, no upcoming surgery.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiplatelet management plan around the upcoming hemicolectomy?

options.text: Continue aspirin perioperatively, stop ticagrelor 5 days before surgery, and restart ticagrelor as soon as surgical haemostasis allows (typically 24 to 72 hours postoperatively)
options.rationale: Within the first 6 months after DES PCI for ACS, stent thrombosis risk is highest; CSANZ and NHFA guidance recommend continuing aspirin perioperatively for most non-cardiac surgery and pausing the P2Y12 inhibitor for the shortest possible window (ticagrelor 5 days), then restarting promptly once haemostasis allows. Delaying elective surgery is preferable to lengthening the off-DAPT window when possible (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS 2016; eTG).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Stop both aspirin and ticagrelor 5 days before surgery and resume both after discharge
options.rationale: Stopping both antiplatelets within 6 months of DES PCI for ACS substantially raises stent thrombosis risk; aspirin should generally be continued perioperatively if surgical bleeding risk is acceptable (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Continue both aspirin and ticagrelor throughout the perioperative period without interruption
options.rationale: Continuing both antiplatelets through colorectal surgery markedly raises bleeding risk; the recommended strategy is single antiplatelet (aspirin) during surgery (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Bridge ticagrelor with prophylactic low molecular weight heparin from 5 days pre-op until restart of ticagrelor
options.rationale: Heparin bridging does not protect against stent thrombosis (a platelet-driven event) and adds bleeding risk; bridging is for atrial fibrillation or mechanical valves on warfarin, not antiplatelets (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Proceed with surgery now without changing antiplatelet therapy because the stent was placed more than a month ago
options.rationale: Six weeks post-DES for ACS is still within the very high-risk window; the planned management must explicitly address P2Y12 inhibitor timing rather than ignore it (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Default DAPT post-ACS PCI is 12 months (aspirin + ticagrelor preferred), then lifelong aspirin. Shorter durations (1, 3, 6 months) are tailored to bleeding risk or stable-disease PCI; extension beyond 12 months requires individualised risk/benefit (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
key_points: Post-ACS PCI: 12 months DAPT then aspirin lifelong
Ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel in ACS (not with lysis)
Shorter DAPT considered when bleeding risk is high (e.g., HAS-BLED, prior bleed, frailty)
Stable CAD PCI default is 6 months DAPT
explanation.pearls: Remember the indication (ACS vs stable) drives DAPT duration, not the stent type (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: An 82-year-old woman attends her general practice for review of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation found on a 24 hour Holter for palpitations. Her history includes essential hypertension on perindopril-indapamide, ischaemic stroke 3 years ago with full recovery, and no diabetes or heart failure. She weighs 56 kg. Current medications: perindopril-indapamide 5/1.25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol as required. BP 132/80, HR 78 irregularly irregular. Bloods today: creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, HCO3 24. She has no prior bleeding and her HAS-BLED score is 2. She prefers an oral medication strategy without routine INR monitoring.

data_table.Risk factors: Age 67, female, HTN treated, prior TIA, no DM, no HF, no vascular disease.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril/indapamide 5/1.25 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol PRN.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate stroke-prevention prescription?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as standard non-valvular AF dose
options.rationale: Apixaban dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily is mandated when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are present; she meets all three, so standard dosing would over-anticoagulate (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily based on age, weight, and creatinine criteria
options.rationale: Per AMH and NHFA/CSANZ, apixaban is reduced to 2.5 mg BD when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are met; she fulfils all three, so the reduced dose is required to balance stroke prevention against bleeding (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 with point-of-care monitoring
options.rationale: Warfarin remains effective but DOACs are first-line for non-valvular AF in Australia, especially when the patient prefers monitoring-free therapy; warfarin would only be preferred for mechanical valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily with the main meal of the day
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban requires dose reduction to 15 mg daily at CrCl 15 to 49 mL/min; her eGFR of 38 mandates the reduced dose, so 20 mg is incorrect (AMH).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as sole long-term antithrombotic therapy
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; it has minimal protective effect against cardioembolic stroke with comparable bleeding risk to anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: Non-valvular AF stroke prevention: calculate CHA2DS2-VASc, anticoagulate when >=1 in men or >=2 in women (excluding sex). DOACs are first-line over warfarin. Apixaban dose reduction (2.5 mg BD) requires at least 2 of: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VASc: CHF, HTN, Age >=75 (2), DM, Stroke/TIA (2), Vasc disease, Age 65 to 74, female
Anticoagulate AF if >=1 (men) or >=2 (women, excluding sex)
DOACs preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF
Aspirin and DAPT are not stroke-prevention strategies in AF
explanation.pearls: Female sex contributes a point but is not an independent indication for anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

subtopic_detail: Rate vs rhythm control in AF with heart failure
stem: A 58-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 32% on TTE 4 weeks ago) and persistent atrial fibrillation diagnosed 7 months ago is reviewed in the dedicated heart failure clinic of a metropolitan tertiary hospital. He is on sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily and apixaban 5 mg twice daily, all at maximum tolerated doses with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records. Despite these guideline-directed therapies, he reports NYHA class III symptoms with breathlessness when flat-walking 50 metres on level ground and orthopnoea requiring two pillows at night, with one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea in the past week. Resting heart rate is 78 in AF on the clinic ECG; 6 minute walk test today is 280 metres (down from 360 m at his last review 3 months ago). He had one direct current cardioversion 4 months ago, which restored sinus rhythm initially with relapse to AF within 6 weeks despite continuation of beta-blocker therapy. He has no significant comorbidities (no diabetes, no CKD, no prior stroke) and lives 30 minutes from a metropolitan electrophysiology centre with established AF ablation programme. Symptoms have not improved despite optimal heart failure medical therapy for 3 months and adequate rate control.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 32%, mild LA dilatation, no significant valve disease.
data_table.Medications: Sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 BD, bisoprolol 7.5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, dapagliflozin 10 mg, apixaban 5 mg BD.
data_table.Functional: NYHA III, 6MWT 280 m, resting HR 78 in AF, BP 118/72.
data_table.Procedural history: DCCV 4 months ago, AF relapse at 6 weeks.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to improve symptoms and outcomes?

options.text: Increase bisoprolol to 10 mg daily for tighter rate control and continue current management
options.rationale: Rate is already controlled at 78 with adequate beta-blockade and symptoms persist; tighter rate control has no mortality benefit and risks worsening fatigue/hypotension. This would be correct only if HR were >110 at rest with symptoms attributable to tachycardia, in lone AF without HFrEF (NHFA/CSANZ AF; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: Add amiodarone 200 mg daily after loading, for pharmacological rhythm control
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reasonable in HFrEF when flecainide/sotalol are contraindicated, but catheter ablation has superior survival, HF hospitalisation and quality-of-life outcomes in HFrEF + AF (CASTLE-AF). The anti-pattern is reflex amiodarone in HFrEF when ablation is feasible. Amiodarone would be the preferred rhythm option only if ablation is unavailable or patient declines (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: Repeat DCCV and load amiodarone for maintenance
options.rationale: Repeat DCCV without substrate modification is unlikely to give durable rhythm control after early relapse. This would be reasonable as a one-off bridge while planning ablation, or if a clearly reversible trigger (sepsis, thyrotoxicosis) has been corrected since the first DCCV (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: AV node ablation with biventricular pacing
options.rationale: AV node ablation plus pacing is a salvage strategy for refractory rate-control failure when rhythm control is not pursued, and commits to lifelong pacing without restoring sinus rhythm. It would be correct only after failed AF ablation and failed antiarrhythmics with persistent intolerable symptoms, particularly with pacing-dependent CRT indication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for catheter ablation of atrial fibrillation as the rhythm-control strategy of choice in his HFrEF
options.rationale: In symptomatic AF with HFrEF despite optimal GDMT and adequate rate control, catheter ablation reduces all-cause mortality and HF hospitalisation and improves LVEF, symptoms and quality of life (CASTLE-AF, CABANA HF subgroup, CASTLE-HTx). NHFA/CSANZ recommends catheter ablation for symptomatic AF where rhythm control is desired and is the preferred rhythm strategy in HFrEF over antiarrhythmic drugs. This patient has failed DCCV, has ongoing NYHA III symptoms, and is geographically accessible to an EP centre.

explanation.summary: In symptomatic AF + HFrEF despite optimal GDMT and rate control, catheter ablation is the preferred rhythm strategy (mortality and HF hospitalisation benefit). Amiodarone is reserved for patients who are not ablation candidates. AV node ablation + pacing is a salvage option only.
key_points: HFrEF + symptomatic AF: catheter ablation > drug rhythm control (CASTLE-AF)
Optimise GDMT before declaring rhythm-control failure
Flecainide and sotalol are contraindicated in HFrEF
AV node ablation + CRT is salvage, not first-line
explanation.pearls: When AF is the perpetuating driver of HFrEF, restoring sinus rhythm can reverse the cardiomyopathy (NHFA/CSANZ).

subtopic_detail: HFrEF - four pillars guideline-directed medical therapy
stem: A 62-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (LVEF 28% on transthoracic echocardiogram performed during admission) presents to the heart failure clinic at a metropolitan tertiary centre 2 weeks after discharge from his index admission with acute pulmonary oedema. The admission echocardiogram showed global hypokinesis without focal wall motion abnormality, mild left atrial dilatation and no significant valvular disease. Cardiac MRI during admission excluded an infiltrative cardiomyopathy and showed no late gadolinium enhancement suggestive of myocarditis. Coronary angiography demonstrated non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He is in sinus rhythm. Current medications post-discharge are ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and frusemide 40 mg daily. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.4%, on metformin 1 g twice daily), no atrial fibrillation, no significant valve disease, eGFR 64, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, BP 122/76 supine and 118/74 standing, HR 64 sinus rhythm, and NYHA class II symptoms when walking up two flights of stairs. He has no history of angio-oedema, no symptomatic hypotension or dizziness, and no recent gout flare. His weight is 88 kg (down 4 kg since admission with diuresis) and he is keen to optimise medical therapy following written education from the heart failure nurse practitioner.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 28%, no significant valve disease, mild LA dilatation.
data_table.Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 64 sinus rhythm, NYHA II.
data_table.Current Rx: Ramipril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metformin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition or modification to his heart failure regimen?

options.text: Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan and add spironolactone 12.5 to 25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily over subsequent visits
options.rationale: NHFA/CSANZ 2018 (with 2022 update) recommends the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi if ARNI not tolerated/available), evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor. Each agent independently reduces mortality in HFrEF. Switch ACEi to ARNI (washout 36 hours), add MRA when K <5.0 and eGFR >30, and add SGLT2i regardless of diabetes status. Bisoprolol can be uptitrated. This sequenced approach matches the current quadruple therapy paradigm.

options.text: Add digoxin 125 micrograms daily for symptom relief
options.rationale: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is not foundational therapy; the anti-pattern is reaching for digoxin before completing the four pillars. Digoxin would be reasonable for persistent symptoms despite full GDMT, or for rate control in HFrEF with AF when beta-blocker is maximally tolerated (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with resting HR >=70 despite the maximally tolerated beta-blocker, plus optimised baseline GDMT. He is HR 64 with un-uptitrated bisoprolol. This would be correct only after bisoprolol uptitration plateaus with persistent HR >=70 in sinus rhythm and persistent NYHA II to IV symptoms (NHFA/CSANZ; SHIFT).

options.text: Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add metolazone 2.5 mg as required
options.rationale: Diuretics treat congestion but do not modify mortality; he is euvolaemic and the priority is mortality-reducing GDMT. The anti-pattern is escalating diuretics in a euvolaemic HFrEF patient because LVEF is poor. Diuretic escalation is correct only when there is congestion (rising weight, peripheral oedema, raised JVP) (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer immediately for cardiac resynchronisation therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) without further uptitration of oral guideline-directed medical therapy
options.rationale: CRT/ICD is considered after >=3 months of optimal GDMT with persistent LVEF <=35% and appropriate QRS criteria (LBBB >=130 ms for CRT, structural cardiomyopathy meeting primary prevention ICD criteria). He has been on partial therapy for 2 weeks. Device referral now skips the reverse-remodelling chance from quadruple therapy (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: The four pillars of HFrEF (LVEF <=40%) GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), evidence-based beta-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol-XL, nebivolol), MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Each reduces mortality; aim to initiate all four within weeks then uptitrate.
key_points: ARNI > ACEi when tolerated; 36 h washout from ACEi
MRA needs K <5.0 and eGFR >30
SGLT2i used regardless of diabetes status
Devices (ICD/CRT) considered only after >=3 months optimal GDMT
explanation.pearls: Sequence matters less than getting all four pillars on board within weeks at low doses, then uptitrate (NHFA/CSANZ 2022 update).

subtopic_detail: HFpEF - SGLT2 inhibitor as first-line disease-modifying therapy
stem: A 73-year-old woman presents to a community cardiology clinic with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 6 months, now limiting her ability to walk to her letterbox without stopping. She has longstanding essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.6%, on metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg daily), obesity (BMI 34, weight 92 kg), and moderate obstructive sleep apnoea well-controlled on nightly CPAP at 9 cmH2O with good compliance documented. She had a hospital admission 3 months ago for fluid overload requiring intravenous diuresis and was discharged on oral frusemide as needed. Her examination today shows BP 138/82, HR 76 sinus, JVP not elevated, mild bibasal end-inspiratory crackles, and no peripheral oedema. Transthoracic echocardiogram performed last week shows preserved LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, left atrial volume index 38 mL/m2 (dilated), septal e' velocity 6 cm/s, E/e' ratio of 14, and no significant valvular disease. NT-proBNP is 612 pg/mL (elevated for her age and BMI, after BMI correction). Her current medications are perindopril 10 mg daily, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. Renal function: eGFR 58, K 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. There is no history of gout, recurrent urinary infection, or genital fungal infection. She is keen on therapies that will reduce hospitalisation and would prefer to avoid additional injectable medications.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, LAVI 38, E/e' 14.
data_table.Current Rx: Perindopril 10, indapamide 2.5, metformin, gliclazide MR, atorvastatin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate addition to her management for heart failure outcomes?

options.text: Add sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily
options.rationale: PARAGON-HF missed its primary endpoint in HFpEF; sacubitril/valsartan has only a Class IIb role and is positioned behind SGLT2 inhibitors. This would be a reasonable add-on if LVEF were in the lower-normal range (HFmrEF) or after SGLT2 inhibitor is established and symptoms persist (NHFA/CSANZ HF).

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily
options.rationale: TOPCAT showed regional-subgroup-only benefit; spironolactone is a reasonable add-on but not first-line and SGLT2 inhibitors have stronger HFpEF evidence (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER). MRA would be the next step after SGLT2 inhibitor in symptomatic HFpEF, watching K and eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
options.rationale: Beta-blockers do not have robust HFpEF mortality benefit and can worsen chronotropic incompetence (a key driver of exertional symptoms in HFpEF). They would be correct only for an independent indication (AF rate control, post-MI, symptomatic ischaemia) (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add empagliflozin 10 mg daily as first-line disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF
options.rationale: EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin) and DELIVER (dapagliflozin) both demonstrated reduction in the composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFpEF, driven primarily by reduction in HF hospitalisation. NHFA/CSANZ 2022 guideline update recommends SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line disease-modifying therapy in HFpEF irrespective of diabetes status. She also has type 2 diabetes, providing dual benefit. eGFR 58 is well above the threshold for initiation.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

options.text: Increase frusemide as required and emphasise weight loss and CPAP adherence only
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures (weight loss, CPAP, BP control) are essential adjuncts but withholding SGLT2 inhibitor misses a Class I-equivalent mortality/hospitalisation benefit. This combination alone would be correct only if SGLT2 inhibitors were contraindicated (recurrent DKA, severe genitourinary infection, eGFR <20 for initiation) (NHFA/CSANZ; EMPEROR-Preserved).

explanation.summary: HFpEF (LVEF >=50%) management: BP control, comorbidity treatment (sleep apnoea, obesity, diabetes), diuretics for congestion. SGLT2 inhibitors are first-line disease-modifying therapy with Class I evidence based on EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER. ARNI and MRA have a more limited role.
key_points: EMPEROR-Preserved + DELIVER: SGLT2i reduce HF hospitalisation in HFpEF
SGLT2i benefit is independent of diabetes status
ARNI (PARAGON-HF) and MRA (TOPCAT) have weaker HFpEF evidence
Aggressively manage comorbidities: HTN, AF, obesity, OSA, CKD
explanation.pearls: The HFpEF treatment landscape changed dramatically post-2021; the SGLT2 inhibitor is no longer optional in suitable HFpEF patients (NHFA/CSANZ).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

subtopic_detail: Cardiac vs vasovagal syncope - high-risk features
stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to ED after collapsing without warning while shopping. Bystanders report he became briefly stiff with a few arm jerks; he was unresponsive for around 30 seconds and recovered immediately on the floor with no confusion. He had two similar episodes in the past 2 months, one while sitting watching television. Examination: BP 142/82 lying, 140/82 standing, HR 60 sinus, no postural drop, normal cardiovascular and neurological exam. ECG: sinus rhythm 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block. Bloods within reference. He drives a private car daily for his work as a courier.

data_table.ECG: Sinus 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB + LAFB (bifascicular block).
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, glucose, hs-troponin, TSH all within reference.

lead_in: Which combination is most appropriate as his next steps?

options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE, advise him not to drive a private vehicle until cause is identified and treated, and notify him of his obligation to inform the licensing authority
options.rationale: High-risk cardiac syncope features (age over 60, no prodrome, syncope while sitting, recurrent episodes, bifascicular block) mandate admission with monitoring and echocardiography per ESC 2018. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 imposes a 4-week non-driving period for unexplained syncope at minimum, longer if cardiac, and the driver bears the duty to inform the licensing authority of any condition affecting fitness to drive (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE but allow private driving once telemetry is normal for 48 hours
options.rationale: Austroads requires defined non-driving periods after unexplained or cardiac syncope based on aetiology and treatment, not on a brief in-hospital telemetry result; this would breach the standard (Austroads).

options.text: Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for vasovagal syncope, and continue driving
options.rationale: His features are atypical for vasovagal (no prodrome, sitting, recurrent, abnormal ECG) and high-risk for cardiac syncope; discharge and continued driving are unsafe (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Arrange outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected new-onset seizure disorder and advise no driving for 6 months under epilepsy rules
options.rationale: Brief myoclonic jerks during cerebral hypoperfusion (convulsive syncope) are common and do not indicate epilepsy; rapid recovery without confusion argues against seizure, so epilepsy driving rules are misapplied (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Discharge home for outpatient 24-hour Holter and tilt-table testing within 4 weeks; continue driving in the interim
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral and continued driving with high-risk features and bifascicular block exposes him to in-vehicle syncope and third-party harm; admission with monitoring is the standard (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

explanation.summary: Cardiac syncope red flags: age >60, structural heart disease, exertional syncope, syncope while supine/sitting, absent prodrome, family history of sudden death, abnormal ECG (bifascicular block, prolonged QT, Brugada pattern). These mandate admission with telemetry and TTE. Convulsive syncope (brief jerks during cerebral hypoperfusion) does not indicate epilepsy.
key_points: Cardiac syncope: abrupt, no prodrome, supine/exertional, abnormal ECG
Vasovagal: prodrome (warmth, nausea, blurred vision), upright trigger
Bifascicular block + syncope suggests intermittent CHB until proven otherwise
Austroads imposes non-driving periods after cardiac/unexplained syncope
explanation.pearls: Always ask about driving and apply Austroads non-driving periods (BMJ Best Practice; Austroads 2022).

sources.label: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

stem: A 46-year-old woman is referred to a metropolitan hypertension clinic with progressively worsening hypertension that has resisted intensification over 18 months. Her home blood pressure log, taken with a validated upper-arm device twice daily, averages 162/96 mmHg despite perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg taken daily with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records over the past 12 months. She has no obstructive sleep apnoea symptoms (no snoring, no witnessed apnoeas, Epworth 4), no flushing or palpitations, no abdominal bruit on examination, and no family history of phaeochromocytoma, paraganglioma or thyroid medullary carcinoma. She has had intermittent hypokalaemia (lowest documented potassium 3.1 mmol/L) attributed by her referring GP to indapamide. Her most recent UEC shows: Na 142, K 3.4, Cl 102, HCO3 28 mmol/L, urea 5.6, creatinine 72 micromol/L, eGFR 89. Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.7%. TSH and free T4 within reference range. Urinary catecholamine and metanephrine screen normal on two separate 24-hour collections. Renal artery Doppler ultrasonography shows no evidence of renal artery stenosis bilaterally. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon positive). She is keen to investigate a potentially reversible secondary cause given her young age and target-organ damage on ECG.

data_table.BP: Home average 162/96 on perindopril 10, amlodipine 10, indapamide 2.5.
data_table.Bloods: Na 142, K 3.4 (low end), HCO3 28, Cr 72, eGFR 89, HbA1c 5.7%, TSH normal.
data_table.Other workup: Urinary catecholamines normal, renal Doppler no RAS, ECG LVH.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: 24-hour urinary metanephrines (repeat)
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma screening has been done with normal urinary catecholamines and no paroxysmal symptoms; repeating is low-yield. Repeat metanephrines would be correct if she developed new paroxysmal headaches/palpitations/diaphoresis or an adrenal incidentaloma with imaging features suggestive of phaeo (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: MR angiography of renal arteries
options.rationale: Renovascular hypertension has been excluded by Doppler and she lacks supportive features (abdominal bruit, flash pulmonary oedema, ACEi-induced AKI, asymmetric kidney sizes). MRA would be correct only if Doppler were equivocal in a high pre-test probability patient or fibromuscular dysplasia were suspected (younger woman) (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: Cushing screening is justified by clinical features of hypercortisolism (central obesity, striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy, glucose intolerance) - none described and HbA1c is normal. Dexamethasone suppression would be correct in resistant HTN plus Cushingoid features or recent rapid weight gain with new diabetes (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio after appropriate medication adjustment
options.rationale: Primary aldosteronism prevalence in resistant hypertension is 15 to 20% and is the most common reversible cause. The Endocrine Society and BMJ Best Practice recommend ARR screening in resistant HTN, spontaneous or diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN with adrenal incidentaloma, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Pre-test preparation: replace potassium, normalise sodium, and ideally substitute interfering drugs (MRAs stopped for 4 to 6 weeks; ACEi/ARB/diuretics may falsely lower ratio but ARR is generally still interpretable with knowledge of medications). She has resistant HTN and diuretic-associated hypokalaemia: a textbook ARR indication.
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg empirically and reassess BP at 12 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical spironolactone is reasonable as fourth-line add-on in resistant HTN, but starting it before ARR will suppress the test for 4 to 6 weeks and miss a diagnosis with potentially curative implications (adrenalectomy in unilateral disease). This would be correct only after a primary aldosteronism workup is complete or declined (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

explanation.summary: Screen for primary aldosteronism (ARR) in resistant HTN, spontaneous/diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN + adrenal incidentaloma, HTN with family history of early CVA/PA, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Confirm with saline suppression or fludrocortisone suppression, then adrenal CT and adrenal vein sampling if surgery considered.
key_points: PA causes 15 to 20% of resistant HTN
Screen with morning ARR after K replacement
Stop MRAs 4 to 6 weeks before testing
Lateralised disease (Conn adenoma) is surgically curable
explanation.pearls: Resistant HTN + low K = Conn's until proven otherwise; do the ARR before adding spironolactone (Endocrine Society).

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

subtopic_detail: NHFA hypertension - lifestyle as first-line for grade 1 low-risk
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his GP for a workplace medical. He is asymptomatic, exercises twice a week, drinks 8 standard drinks weekly, has BMI 28, does not smoke, has no diabetes, and no family history of premature CVD. Three separate clinic BP readings over 6 weeks average 156/96, and home BP averaged over 7 days is 152/94. Bloods: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, triglycerides 1.2, fasting glucose 5.1, eGFR over 90, normal urea/electrolytes and urine ACR. ECG normal. His Australian absolute 5-year CV risk (NVDPA calculator) is 4 percent. He has tried 12 weeks of structured lifestyle change with his GP including reduced salt, DASH-style diet, alcohol reduction to 8 standard drinks per week, and increased exercise; BP today remains 154/94.

data_table.Bloods: Chol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, TG 1.2, FBG 5.1, eGFR >90, ACR normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence perindopril 5 mg daily and continue ongoing lifestyle measures, with home BP review at 6 weeks
options.rationale: After an adequate trial of lifestyle change (3 to 6 months) without BP control, NHFA 2016 recommends adding pharmacotherapy in grade 1 to 2 hypertension even at low absolute CV risk. First-line agents are ACE inhibitor, ARB, dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, or thiazide; perindopril is a reasonable first choice in a younger patient without contraindications (NHFA; eTG).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Continue lifestyle modification for another 6 months before considering pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: An adequate 3 to 6 month lifestyle trial has already failed at sustained BP above 140/90; further deferral risks ongoing end-organ exposure without proportional benefit (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Refer for 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to exclude white-coat hypertension before any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Clinic and home readings are concordant for sustained hypertension; ABPM is unnecessary when these two agree, and adds delay (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Commence atorvastatin 20 mg daily for global CV risk reduction in addition to lifestyle
options.rationale: His absolute CV risk is low (4 percent) and lipids are unremarkable; statin is not indicated and does not address his uncontrolled BP (NVDPA; NHFA).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Investigate for secondary hypertension with plasma aldosterone-renin ratio, renal artery imaging and 24-hour urinary metanephrines today
options.rationale: Secondary hypertension workup is reserved for resistant hypertension (BP uncontrolled on three agents including a diuretic), young-onset severe hypertension with red flags (hypokalaemia, episodic symptoms), or clinical clues; not first-line at this stage (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

explanation.summary: Grade 1 HTN (140 to 159/90 to 99) at low absolute CV risk: 3 to 6 months of lifestyle (DASH-style diet, salt <5 g/day, alcohol limits, exercise, weight reduction) then add pharmacotherapy if uncontrolled. Higher risk or higher BP grade triggers earlier or immediate medication.
key_points: Use absolute CV risk (NVDPA calculator) to guide pharmacotherapy threshold
DASH diet, exercise, weight loss, salt and alcohol limits
Reassess at 3 to 6 months for low-risk
Pharmacotherapy first-line: ACEi/ARB, dihydropyridine CCB, or thiazide
explanation.pearls: Absolute CV risk - not BP number alone - drives the decision to start drugs (NHFA 2016).

sources.label: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

subtopic_detail: Mobitz II AV block - indication for permanent pacemaker
stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidaemia is admitted to a regional hospital with two episodes of presyncope over 48 hours. She has no chest pain. Her medications are perindopril 5 mg and atorvastatin 40 mg; she takes no AV-blocking agents. On telemetry she has predominantly sinus rhythm at 62 bpm with intermittent dropped QRS complexes; the preceding PR intervals are constant at 200 ms before each non-conducted P wave. The QRS during conducted beats is 140 ms with a right bundle branch block morphology. Her BP is 132/76 between episodes and drops to 84/52 during prolonged pauses (longest documented pause 4.2 seconds). Echocardiogram is structurally normal with LVEF 60%. Bloods: TSH 2.1, electrolytes normal, troponin negative, no infection on screen, no Lyme exposure. She has no reversible cause identified (no recent cardiac surgery, no AV-blocking medications, no acute MI, no electrolyte derangement).

data_table.Telemetry: Sinus 62 bpm with intermittent dropped QRS; constant PR 200 ms preceding non-conducted P. Longest pause 4.2 s.
data_table.ECG: RBBB morphology QRS 140 ms during conducted beats.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation under Class I indication for symptomatic Mobitz II AV block
options.rationale: Mobitz II second-degree AV block (constant PR with sudden non-conducted P) is a Class I indication for permanent pacing regardless of symptoms because the block is below the AV node (His-Purkinje), unpredictable, and prone to progress to complete heart block with unreliable escape rhythm. Wide QRS (RBBB) supports infranodal block. ESC 2021 Pacing and CSANZ position support this indication. Reversible causes have been excluded.
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Atropine 600 microgram IV bolus and observe
options.rationale: Atropine acts on the AV node and frequently fails or paradoxically worsens infra-nodal (Mobitz II, wide-QRS) block by speeding atrial rate without improving His-Purkinje conduction. Atropine is a temporising bridge, not definitive. It would be correct only as initial pharmacotherapy for nodal (Mobitz I, narrow-QRS) bradycardia with reversible vagal trigger (ANZCOR ALS bradycardia algorithm; ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Isoprenaline infusion and discharge with outpatient cardiology follow-up
options.rationale: Isoprenaline is an inpatient bridge to definitive pacing; discharging on chronotropic infusion is unsafe and ignores the Class I pacemaker indication. Isoprenaline use would be correct only as a short-term inpatient measure pending transvenous or permanent pacing (ANZCOR; ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Reduce perindopril and observe with daily telemetry on the ward
options.rationale: Perindopril does not cause AV block; no reversible cause has been identified. Watchful waiting misses the Class I indication for permanent pacing and risks progression to complete heart block with unreliable wide escape. Drug review would be the correct first step only if a culprit (e.g., beta-blocker, non-DHP calcium channel blocker, digoxin) was present (ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Electrophysiology study with potential AV node ablation as a definitive procedure to control her bradyarrhythmia and presyncope episodes
options.rationale: AV node ablation deliberately CREATES complete heart block to control refractory tachyarrhythmias - the opposite of what is needed here. EP study is occasionally useful to localise block in fascicular disease with syncope, but the surface ECG is diagnostic. This option is a deliberate red herring exploiting the word ablation (ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

explanation.summary: Permanent pacing is Class I indicated for: third-degree AV block, Mobitz II, advanced AV block (>=2:1), symptomatic sinus node dysfunction, alternating bundle branch block, and acquired complete heart block irrespective of symptoms. Mobitz I (Wenckebach) with narrow QRS is usually intra-nodal and does not need pacing unless symptomatic.
key_points: Mobitz I (narrow QRS) = AV nodal, usually no pacing unless symptomatic
Mobitz II = infra-nodal, Class I pacing regardless of symptoms
Wide QRS during AV block suggests infra-nodal pathology
Always exclude reversible causes: ischaemia, drugs, hyperkalaemia, hypothyroidism, Lyme
explanation.pearls: PR-constant-then-dropped = Mobitz II; PR-lengthening-then-dropped = Mobitz I (ESC 2021).

sources.label: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a rural hospital ED with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left jaw, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history, dyslipidaemia on atorvastatin 40 mg, and untreated hypertension. He took two GTN sprays at home with minimal relief. He has no prior bleeding history, no recent surgery, no stroke, and is not on anticoagulation. Initial ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in V1 to V4 with reciprocal inferior ST depression. The nearest PCI-capable centre is 180 minutes away by road, and the local rural transfer team estimates first-medical-contact-to-balloon time of 200 minutes if transferred immediately. The local ED can administer fibrinolysis within 20 minutes. Troponin I has been sent; bedside echocardiogram shows anteroseptal hypokinesis. The team specifically asks whether transfer for primary PCI is still preferred even with predicted door-to-balloon exceeding 120 minutes, whether to delay reperfusion pending troponin confirmation, whether IV unfractionated heparin alone with next-day angiography would be safe, and whether conservative anti-ischaemic therapy with high-dose nitrates and beta-blockade is acceptable given only minor reciprocal changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 148/92 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 20, SpO2 95% RA, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, GCS 15, chest clear, S4 gallop, no murmur, calves soft.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: 3 mm ST elevation V1 to V4 with reciprocal inferior ST depression. CXR clear.
data_table.Medications: Atorvastatin 40 mg nocte. GTN spray PRN.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Transfer immediately for primary PCI even though first-medical-contact-to-balloon time will exceed 120 minutes
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred only when first-medical-contact-to-balloon is within 120 minutes; beyond this window fibrinolysis with rescue PCI capability gives superior outcomes. This would be correct only if the PCI centre were reachable inside the 120 minute window from FMC (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS 2016 update).

options.text: Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now and arrange transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours
options.rationale: When PCI cannot be delivered within 120 minutes of first medical contact and there are no contraindications, fibrinolysis should be given within 30 minutes of arrival followed by routine transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours later (pharmaco-invasive strategy), as per the Heart Foundation/CSANZ STEMI pathway.

options.text: Withhold reperfusion and load with ticagrelor and aspirin while awaiting troponin result
options.rationale: STEMI diagnosis on ECG does not require troponin confirmation before reperfusion. The anti-pattern is waiting for biomarker confirmation in a clear STEMI; delaying therapy increases myocardial loss and mortality. Awaiting troponin is appropriate only when ECG is non-diagnostic and clinical picture is uncertain (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Give intravenous unfractionated heparin alone and transfer for elective angiography next day
options.rationale: Anticoagulation alone is not a reperfusion strategy; STEMI requires fibrinolysis or PPCI. This is the classic anti-pattern of treating STEMI as if it were unstable angina. Heparin alone would be correct in NSTE-ACS while invasive management is being arranged within 24 to 72 hours (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Treat conservatively with high-dose nitrates and beta-blockade given the small reciprocal change
options.rationale: Anti-ischaemic therapy without reperfusion is inadequate for STEMI; the small reciprocal change distraction is the anti-pattern (any reciprocal change confirms the STEMI). Beta-blockers acutely are also relatively contraindicated with incipient cardiogenic shock. Conservative therapy is only appropriate when patient is past the 12-hour window and is asymptomatic with no ongoing ischaemia (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

explanation.summary: In STEMI, the choice of reperfusion is driven by the time interval from first medical contact to balloon inflation. If PPCI cannot be delivered within 120 minutes, fibrinolysis within 30 minutes of arrival followed by routine transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours later (pharmaco-invasive strategy) is the recommended Australian approach (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
key_points: PPCI preferred if FMC-to-balloon less than 120 minutes
Fibrinolysis within 30 minutes of arrival if PPCI delayed
Pharmaco-invasive: routine angiography 3 to 24 hours post-lysis
Absolute contraindications to lysis: prior ICH, ischaemic stroke less than 3 months, active bleeding, suspected dissection
Adjunctive therapy: aspirin 300 mg + clopidogrel (or ticagrelor if PPCI) + anticoagulation
explanation.pearls: Rural Australia: 120-minute rule trumps the theoretical superiority of PPCI - lyse and transfer.

subtopic_detail: Acute coronary syndrome - reperfusion choice and timing in STEMI

stem: A 67-year-old man presents with 2 hours of severe central chest pain radiating to the back, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. He has a history of hypertension on amlodipine and a 40 pack-year smoking history. He has no prior cardiac history. On arrival he is in pain, clammy and pale. The 12-lead ECG shows horizontal ST depression of 2 mm in V1, V2 and V3 with prominent upright R-waves in V1 and V2 (R/S greater than 1), tall upright T waves in V1 to V3, and no ST elevation or depression in any other lead. There is no Q-wave. A posterior 15-lead ECG with leads V7, V8 and V9 placed below the left scapula shows 1 mm ST elevation in V8 and V9. Troponin I is pending. Bedside echocardiogram shows inferolateral wall hypokinesis. The ED registrar asks whether to manage as NSTEMI with conservative pathway and 24-hour angiography, whether the ECG could represent Wellens syndrome, right bundle branch block, or digoxin effect, and whether emergency cath lab activation is justified for a STEMI equivalent in the absence of overt ST elevation on the standard 12-lead.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale, diaphoretic, JVP not elevated, chest clear, dual heart sounds no murmur.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: 2 mm horizontal ST depression V1 to V3, dominant R in V1/V2, upright T waves. Posterior leads V8, V9: 1 mm ST elevation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Manage as NSTEMI with conservative pathway and angiography within 24 hours
options.rationale: The 24-hour NSTEMI window does not apply when posterior leads confirm STEMI equivalent. This is the most common posterior STEMI miss - treating it as NSTEMI because there is only ST depression anteriorly. This pathway would be correct only with negative posterior leads and rise/fall biomarker pattern consistent with NSTEMI (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; ANZCOR).

options.text: Activate the cath lab for emergency primary PCI as a STEMI equivalent
options.rationale: Isolated posterior STEMI presents with ST depression in V1 to V3 (reciprocal), dominant R in V1, and is confirmed by ST elevation greater than 0.5 mm in posterior leads V7 to V9. It carries equivalent prognosis to anterior STEMI and mandates emergency reperfusion within the standard PPCI window (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS Guideline; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Treat as Wellens syndrome with urgent outpatient cardiology referral
options.rationale: Wellens syndrome shows biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in V2 to V3 in a pain-free patient with prior chest pain (a critical proximal LAD lesion sign); this ECG shows reciprocal ST depression with upright T and posterior ST elevation, not Wellens morphology. Wellens management is urgent inpatient angiography, not emergency PCI activation (LITFL; Heart Foundation).

options.text: Diagnose right bundle branch block and arrange echocardiogram in the morning
options.rationale: RBBB shows an rSR pattern in V1 with a broad QRS (>=120 ms) and discordant T-wave inversion; this ECG has a narrow QRS with dominant R and upright T - the wrong morphology. The anti-pattern is anchoring on dominant R in V1 without checking QRS width (Heart Foundation; LITFL).

options.text: Diagnose digoxin effect and check digoxin level urgently
options.rationale: Digoxin effect shows scooped (Salvador Dali moustache) ST depression with shortened QT, typically in lateral leads, in patients on digoxin. He is not on digoxin and the pattern is acute ischaemic. This would only be correct in a digoxin-treated patient with the classic scooped morphology (AMH).

explanation.summary: Isolated posterior STEMI is missed on the standard 12-lead because the infarct vector points away from the anterior precordial leads. Recognising horizontal ST depression in V1 to V3 with dominant upright R-waves and upright T-waves should prompt a posterior 15-lead ECG; ST elevation greater than 0.5 mm in V7 to V9 confirms posterior STEMI, which is a STEMI equivalent requiring emergency PPCI.
key_points: Posterior STEMI: ST depression V1-V3, dominant R V1/V2, upright T V1-V3
Confirm with posterior leads V7, V8, V9 (less than 1 mm threshold: 0.5 mm)
Most commonly from circumflex or dominant RCA occlusion
STEMI-equivalent: emergency PPCI within standard window
Other STEMI equivalents: LBBB with Sgarbossa criteria, aVR elevation with diffuse ST depression
explanation.pearls: Anterior ST depression with dominant R-wave is posterior STEMI until proven otherwise - flip the ECG upside down and backwards to see the elevation.

subtopic_detail: Posterior STEMI - ECG recognition with isolated ST depression in V1 to V3

stem: A 62-year-old woman undergoes successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD for an anterior STEMI. She has hypertension on perindopril, dyslipidaemia, no diabetes, and no prior bleeding. Pre-procedure haemoglobin was 138 g/L and platelets 240 x 10^9/L. She was loaded with aspirin 300 mg and ticagrelor 180 mg in the ED. Post-procedurally she is haemodynamically stable, has no ongoing chest pain, and an echocardiogram shows an LVEF of 42 percent with anteroseptal hypokinesis. She has no history of stroke, intracranial haemorrhage, peptic ulcer disease, or anticoagulant use. The cardiology team plans discharge on day 3. She has no history of stroke, no bleeding ulcers, no prior peptic disease and asks specifically about ticagrelor versus clopidogrel versus prasugrel, the role of aspirin alone, and whether apixaban should be added given her stent. She has private cover and no PBS concerns about device or drug choice.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Chest clear, dual heart sounds, no signs of heart failure.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 132 g/L, platelets 232 x 10^9/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L, LVEF 42 percent.
data_table.Medications: Loaded with aspirin 300 mg and ticagrelor 180 mg; ramipril 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, bisoprolol 2.5 mg started inpatient.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing antiplatelet regimen?

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily for 12 months, then aspirin alone indefinitely
options.rationale: Default DAPT post-ACS-PCI is aspirin 100 mg daily plus ticagrelor 90 mg BD for 12 months (or prasugrel as an alternative), then aspirin alone indefinitely, unless bleeding risk dictates otherwise (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS Guideline; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus clopidogrel 75 mg daily for 12 months, then aspirin alone
options.rationale: Clopidogrel is the alternative P2Y12 when ticagrelor and prasugrel are contraindicated (high bleeding risk, prior intracranial bleed, on long-term anticoagulation, ticagrelor dyspnoea intolerance). It is less potent than ticagrelor (PLATO showed superior outcomes for ticagrelor). This would be correct as default only when ticagrelor/prasugrel are contraindicated (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily alone, with clopidogrel added only if recurrent ischaemia occurs
options.rationale: Single antiplatelet post-ACS-PCI substantially increases stent thrombosis risk; DAPT for at least 12 months is standard. This would be correct only after the planned DAPT duration is completed, or earlier if a major bleeding event mandates de-escalation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus prasugrel 10 mg daily for 6 months, then aspirin alone
options.rationale: Prasugrel is an alternative to ticagrelor but is contraindicated with prior stroke/TIA, not recommended over age 75, and at reduced dose if under 60 kg. Default ACS-PCI duration is 12 months, not 6 months. Six-month DAPT would be correct only with high bleeding risk after careful weighing (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus apixaban 5 mg twice daily for 12 months
options.rationale: DOAC plus aspirin is not standard post-ACS-PCI in sinus rhythm; this would be correct only if the patient had concurrent AF requiring anticoagulation, in which case triple therapy (DOAC + aspirin + clopidogrel) is given for the shortest possible period (typically 1 week to 1 month) then dual therapy with DOAC + clopidogrel (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

explanation.summary: Default DAPT after ACS with PCI in Australia is aspirin 100 mg daily indefinitely plus a P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor 90 mg BD or prasugrel) for 12 months. Clopidogrel substitutes when more potent agents are contraindicated. Shorter or longer DAPT can be tailored to bleeding/ischaemic risk using validated scores (DAPT, PRECISE-DAPT).
key_points: Aspirin + ticagrelor (or prasugrel) x 12 months post-ACS-PCI
Clopidogrel if high bleeding risk, prior ICH, or on oral anticoagulant
Prasugrel: avoid if prior stroke/TIA, age greater than 75, weight less than 60 kg
Lifelong aspirin after DAPT period unless contraindicated
Tailor with DAPT/PRECISE-DAPT scores
explanation.pearls: Ticagrelor causes dyspnoea in about 1 in 7 patients - reassure but do not stop unless severe.

subtopic_detail: ACS - DAPT choice and duration post-PCI

stem: A 74-year-old man with known HFrEF (LVEF 32 percent) presents to the ED with 2 days of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and 3 kg weight gain. He admits to non-adherence with his frusemide for 1 week. He has a background of ischaemic cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and CKD stage 3 (baseline creatinine 140 micromol/L). On arrival he is sitting upright, working hard to breathe, and unable to complete sentences. He has bibasal crackles to the mid-zones and elevated JVP. He has no chest pain and his ECG shows old anterior Q-waves without acute ischaemic changes. His usual medications are frusemide 40 mg BD, perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, and dapagliflozin 10 mg. The team specifically asks whether IV fluid bolus and IV metoprolol for the tachycardia would help, whether IV morphine with high-flow nasal cannula at 60 L/min is appropriate, whether to withhold his ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker indefinitely while starting dobutamine, and whether oral diuretics would suffice.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/94 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, RR 32, SpO2 88% RA improving to 94% on 6 L Hudson, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, accessory muscle use, bibasal crackles to mid-zones, JVP 8 cm, peripheral oedema to mid-shins.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: SR with old anterior Q-waves. Troponin I 28 ng/L (cut-off less than 16). NT-proBNP 4200 ng/L. Creatinine 175 micromol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L.
data_table.Medications: Frusemide 40 mg BD, perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, dapagliflozin 10 mg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus, GTN infusion, and non-invasive ventilation with CPAP
options.rationale: Acute pulmonary oedema with hypertensive blood pressure and severe respiratory distress is managed with IV loop diuretic (typically 1 to 2.5 times the home oral dose), IV nitrate to reduce preload/afterload, and non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP) to reduce work of breathing and improve oxygenation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF Guideline 2018; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: IV fluid bolus 500 mL Hartmann's and IV metoprolol 5 mg for the tachycardia
options.rationale: Fluid loading worsens pulmonary oedema in an already congested patient; IV beta-blockade in acute decompensation precipitates cardiogenic shock by removing residual sympathetic compensation. This combination would never be correct in ADHF; it would be appropriate only if the diagnosis were misread as septic tachyarrhythmia without congestion (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: IV morphine 5 mg with high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 60 L/min and observation
options.rationale: Routine opioid use in ADHF is associated with worse outcomes (increased mortality, ICU admission, intubation) and is no longer recommended - a key legacy anti-pattern. HFNO has not been shown superior to NIV in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. Morphine is reserved at most for terminal palliative dyspnoea (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Withhold his ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker indefinitely and start a dobutamine infusion
options.rationale: Inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension and end-organ hypoperfusion; this patient is hypertensive and not in shock. Chronic disease-modifying therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker) should generally be continued during decompensation unless hypotension or AKI dictates otherwise. Indefinite withdrawal worsens long-term outcomes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Oral frusemide 80 mg, oral metolazone 5 mg, and admit to a monitored bed
options.rationale: Oral diuretic onset is too slow for acute severe pulmonary oedema with respiratory failure (gut oedema impairs absorption); IV diuretic is required. Oral diuresis is appropriate only in stable congestion managed in clinic or after acute response, not as initial management in respiratory distress (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

explanation.summary: Acute pulmonary oedema with respiratory failure and elevated BP is managed with IV loop diuretic, IV nitrate, and non-invasive ventilation. Inotropes are restricted to cardiogenic shock. Routine morphine is now avoided. Chronic disease-modifying therapy (beta-blocker, RAAS inhibitor) is generally continued at reduced dose where tolerated.
key_points: IV frusemide dose: 1 to 2.5 x oral home dose
GTN reduces preload and afterload - useful if BP is preserved
NIV (CPAP/BiPAP) reduces intubation rate in cardiogenic APO
Inotropes only if hypotensive with end-organ hypoperfusion
Avoid routine morphine - increased mortality signal
explanation.pearls: Hypertensive flash pulmonary oedema needs nitrates more than it needs diuretics - venous redistribution buys minutes.

subtopic_detail: Acute decompensated heart failure - initial management priorities

stem: A 66-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 28 percent, ischaemic aetiology) attends the heart failure clinic. She is NYHA class II to III with mild exertional dyspnoea climbing one flight. She has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.2 percent), hypertension, and CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline). She has been on stable doses for 3 months of perindopril 8 mg daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily, and atorvastatin 80 mg. Her BP is 118/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L. She has no symptomatic hypotension. She is keen to optimise prognosis-modifying therapy and has private cover for medication cost. She specifically asks whether to add digoxin for prognosis, whether to increase her frusemide and add amlodipine for blood pressure, whether to refer for cardiac resynchronisation now, or whether to switch to candesartan with ivabradine instead of the ARNI and MRA approach. She has no contraindication to MRA, ARNI or SGLT2 inhibitor and has tolerated her ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker without symptomatic hypotension over the past 3 months. She has private cover and PBS access to all four pillars.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: JVP not elevated, chest clear, dual heart sounds with soft S3, trace ankle oedema.
data_table.Investigations: LVEF 28 percent. eGFR 52, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, HbA1c 7.2 percent, NT-proBNP 980 ng/L.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 7.5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to optimise prognosis?

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, and plan switch of perindopril to sacubitril/valsartan after a 36-hour washout
options.rationale: The four pillars of HFrEF GDMT are ACEi/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor; she is already on ACEi and beta-blocker and needs the remaining two pillars added, with ACEi-to-ARNI switch (after 36-hour washout to avoid angioedema) for additional mortality benefit (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF Guideline; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Add digoxin 125 microgram daily for prognostic benefit and to address ongoing exertional dyspnoea despite optimised first-line guideline-directed medical therapy
options.rationale: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is not a prognosis pillar; the anti-pattern is reaching for digoxin before completing four-pillar GDMT. Digoxin would be reasonable for persistent symptoms on full GDMT, particularly with concurrent AF requiring additional rate control (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add amlodipine 5 mg daily for tighter blood pressure control without adding any additional prognosis-modifying agent
options.rationale: Loop diuretics are symptom-controlling not prognosis-modifying; amlodipine is neutral in HFrEF but does not improve survival. The anti-pattern is escalating non-prognostic therapy while skipping the mortality-reducing pillars. Diuretic escalation is correct for symptomatic congestion, not asymptomatic LV dysfunction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for cardiac resynchronisation therapy without further medical optimisation given the persistent NYHA class II to III symptoms despite current triple therapy regimen
options.rationale: CRT is indicated for LVEF <=35% with LBBB and QRS >=130 ms after at least 3 months of optimised GDMT; she has not had GDMT optimised first. CRT before four-pillar optimisation misses the reverse-remodelling benefit and risks inappropriate device implantation if LVEF recovers (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Switch perindopril to candesartan 8 mg daily and add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily for additional symptomatic and prognostic benefit in HFrEF management
options.rationale: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with HR >=77 bpm on maximally tolerated beta-blocker (SHIFT trial threshold); her HR is 64 bpm so she does not qualify. ARB substitutes for ACEi only when ACEi-intolerant (cough, angioedema), not as a routine switch. This option mixes two off-target choices (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; SHIFT).

explanation.summary: Modern HFrEF GDMT comprises four prognosis pillars: ACE inhibitor or ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor. All four should be initiated and titrated in parallel where tolerated. ARNI switch from ACEi requires a 36-hour washout to reduce angioedema risk. Diuretics are symptom-controlling and do not improve survival.
key_points: Four pillars: ACEi/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i
ARNI: 36 h washout from ACEi to avoid angioedema
SGLT2i benefit independent of diabetes status
Monitor potassium and renal function with MRA + ACEi/ARNI
CRT after at least 3 months of optimised GDMT if QRS criteria met
explanation.pearls: Start the SGLT2 inhibitor early - the survival curves separate within weeks, not years.

subtopic_detail: Chronic HFrEF - guideline-directed medical therapy escalation

stem: A 68-year-old woman is referred for symptomatic palpitations and exertional dyspnoea over the past 3 weeks. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 118 bpm at rest. She has hypertension on perindopril, no diabetes, no prior stroke, no vascular disease, and no heart failure. Her LVEF on echo done last week is 58 percent with a mildly dilated left atrium (44 mm) and no significant valvular disease. She is otherwise active and reports the palpitations are interfering with her hiking. She has no contraindications to anticoagulation, antiarrhythmics, or cardioversion. Her current symptom burden is EHRA class IIb. Her TSH and electrolytes are normal. She specifically asks whether rate control alone is acceptable, whether early electrical cardioversion is preferred, whether anticoagulation alone is sufficient pending spontaneous reversion, whether digoxin monotherapy without anticoagulation would be appropriate given her age, or whether amiodarone loading without anticoagulation is reasonable in someone with such low overall stroke risk.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 118 bpm irregular, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, dual heart sounds no murmur, chest clear, no peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: AF with rate 118. Echo: LVEF 58 percent, LA 44 mm. TSH 2.1 mIU/L, K 4.0 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management strategy for her atrial fibrillation?

options.text: Rate control only with bisoprolol; defer rhythm control until two recurrences despite optimal rate control
options.rationale: In symptomatic AF of recent onset (<1 year), early rhythm control reduces cardiovascular events compared with rate control alone (EAST-AFNET 4). The 'wait for recurrences' approach is the pre-EAST-AFNET legacy practice. Rate-control-only would be correct in long-standing persistent AF (>1 year), asymptomatic patients, or patients who decline rhythm interventions (CSANZ AF Guideline 2018; EAST-AFNET 4).

options.text: Commence apixaban and a rate-controlling beta-blocker, then refer for early electrical cardioversion with consideration of catheter ablation
options.rationale: Symptomatic AF within 12 months of diagnosis benefits from an early rhythm-control strategy (cardioversion plus antiarrhythmic or ablation), with appropriate anticoagulation based on CHA2DS2-VA score; she meets criteria (symptomatic, recent onset, suitable candidate) (CSANZ AF Guideline; eTG; EAST-AFNET 4).

options.text: Anticoagulation alone with apixaban; AF often spontaneously reverts without further intervention
options.rationale: She is symptomatic (EHRA IIb) and tachycardic at 118; anticoagulation alone is insufficient. Spontaneous reversion happens but should not be relied on. Anticoagulation alone would be appropriate only in asymptomatic permanent AF with rate already controlled (CSANZ AF).

options.text: Digoxin monotherapy for rate control without anticoagulation as her CHA2DS2-VA score is 1
options.rationale: Digoxin monotherapy is poor for exertional rate control (vagal action diminished by sympathetic activity) and her CHA2DS2-VA is 2 (age 65 to 74 = 1, female = 1, hypertension = 1 - actually 3), meeting the threshold for anticoagulation. The combined errors make this never correct (CSANZ AF).

options.text: Amiodarone loading orally for 4 weeks followed by maintenance, with no anticoagulation given low stroke risk
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is required regardless of antiarrhythmic strategy when CHA2DS2-VA reaches threshold; rhythm control does not abolish stroke risk. Amiodarone is generally reserved for structural heart disease or failed first-line antiarrhythmics due to long-term toxicity (thyroid, lung, hepatic, ocular). This would only be appropriate in structural heart disease with failed flecainide/sotalol AND with anticoagulation (CSANZ AF; AMH).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic AF diagnosed within the past 12 months should be considered for early rhythm control (cardioversion, antiarrhythmics, or catheter ablation) alongside anticoagulation, as this reduces cardiovascular events compared with rate control alone (EAST-AFNET 4 incorporated into CSANZ guidance). Anticoagulation is governed by CHA2DS2-VA, not by rhythm vs rate strategy.
key_points: Early rhythm control improves outcomes in recent-onset symptomatic AF
Anticoagulation is independent of rate vs rhythm strategy
CHA2DS2-VA replaces CHA2DS2-VASc in Australia (drops sex modifier)
Catheter ablation first-line for paroxysmal AF in suitable candidates
Amiodarone reserved for structural heart disease or refractory cases
explanation.pearls: EAST-AFNET 4 shifted the paradigm - rhythm-control benefits accrue when started within the first year, not after years of failed rate control.

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation - rate vs rhythm control strategy

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with sudden palpitations for 50 minutes. She has had similar self-terminating episodes since her teens. She is haemodynamically stable. Her past history includes moderate persistent asthma managed with budesonide-formoterol; she had a hospital admission with status asthmaticus 3 years ago and reports wheeze with viral illnesses. She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill and drinks 2 to 3 coffees daily. On arrival she is anxious but well perfused. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 184 bpm with no clearly visible P waves and a normal QRS axis. A modified Valsalva manoeuvre with leg elevation has been attempted twice without success.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 184 bpm regular, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Warm well-perfused peripheries, GCS 15, chest clear, no signs of heart failure.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: narrow-complex regular tachycardia 184 bpm, no clear P waves, normal axis. K 4.2 mmol/L, TSH normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push followed by 20 mL saline flush with continuous ECG recording
options.rationale: Adenosine is normally first-line after failed vagal manoeuvres in stable narrow-complex SVT, but it can precipitate severe bronchospasm and is relatively contraindicated in patients with significant active asthma (eTG Cardiovascular; AMH).

options.text: IV verapamil 5 mg slowly over 2 minutes with continuous ECG and blood pressure monitoring
options.rationale: In stable narrow-complex SVT where adenosine is relatively contraindicated by active asthma, IV verapamil (or diltiazem) is the preferred alternative provided LV function is preserved and the rhythm is genuinely narrow-complex (eTG Cardiovascular; AMH).

options.text: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 50 joules under procedural sedation with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: Synchronised cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamically unstable SVT (hypotension, shock, ischaemia, severe heart failure); she remains stable so pharmacological termination is preferred (ANZCOR 11.10).

options.text: Oral metoprolol 25 mg with discharge home and cardiology outpatient follow-up if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Oral beta-blockade does not acutely terminate SVT and discharge in active tachyarrhythmia is unsafe; non-selective beta-blockade also risks bronchospasm in asthma (ANZCOR; eTG).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose over 60 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reserved for wide-complex tachycardia or refractory arrhythmias; it is not first-line for stable narrow-complex SVT and carries significant proarrhythmic and toxicity profile (ANZCOR; eTG).

explanation.summary: Stable narrow-complex SVT is treated with vagal manoeuvres (modified Valsalva preferred) followed by IV adenosine 6 mg, then 12 mg, then 12 mg if needed. Unstable SVT requires synchronised DC cardioversion. Verapamil or beta-blockers are alternatives in patients without contraindications.
key_points: Modified Valsalva (REVERT trial position) more effective than standard
Adenosine: 6 mg, 12 mg, 12 mg rapid push with saline flush
Warn patient about transient flushing/chest discomfort/sense of impending doom
Avoid adenosine in asthma (bronchospasm) and prefer alternatives
Unstable SVT: synchronised cardioversion 50 to 100 J
explanation.pearls: Always run a rhythm strip during adenosine - the brief AV block can unmask the underlying atrial activity (flutter waves, P-wave morphology).

stem: A 68-year-old man with a 50 pack-year smoking history attends his GP for review. Spirometry 4 weeks ago confirmed COPD with post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC 0.58 and FEV1 62 percent predicted (GOLD 2). Over the past year he has had 2 exacerbations managed in the community with prednisolone and amoxicillin, and no hospital admissions. His mMRC dyspnoea score is 2 (stops for breath after about 100 m on the flat) and CAT score is 18. He is currently on as-needed salbutamol inhaler only. He stopped smoking 6 months ago, is up to date with influenza and pneumococcal vaccines, and is enrolled in a pulmonary rehabilitation program. He has no diagnosis of asthma, no significant atopy, and a blood eosinophil count of 120 cells/microL. His GP specifically asks whether to add a LAMA alone, to add a fixed-dose LAMA/LABA combination, to add a LABA/ICS combination, to commence triple therapy with LAMA/LABA/ICS, or to add oral theophylline.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 95% RA.
data_table.Examination: Mild barrel chest, reduced air entry bilaterally, prolonged expiration, no peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Post-bronchodilator FEV1 62 percent predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.58. Eosinophils 120 cells/microL. CXR hyperinflation without infiltrate.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next inhaled therapy?

options.text: Add tiotropium 18 microg daily (LAMA)
