options.rationale: Single long-acting bronchodilator (LAMA or LABA) is appropriate for low-symptom, low-exacerbation patients (mMRC <2, CAT <10, <1 exacerbation/year); he has both high symptom burden (mMRC 2, CAT 18) and 2 exacerbations in 12 months, mandating dual bronchodilation. This would be correct only as initial therapy in a less symptomatic, infrequent-exacerbator patient (Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X; GOLD).

options.text: Add a LAMA/LABA combination inhaler
options.rationale: Symptomatic COPD patients with exacerbations (greater than or equal to 2 moderate/year or any hospitalisation) and CAT greater than or equal to 10 should escalate from SABA to dual long-acting bronchodilator (LAMA/LABA) per Australian COPD-X; ICS is added only if eosinophils greater than or equal to 300 cells/microL or asthma overlap, and his eosinophils are 120 (Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X; eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Add a fixed-dose LABA/ICS combination
options.rationale: ICS adds pneumonia risk and should be reserved for patients with blood eosinophils >=300 cells/microL, asthma-COPD overlap, or persistent exacerbations on dual bronchodilator therapy. His eosinophils are 120. This option is the classic 'reach for ICS too early' anti-pattern. LABA/ICS would be appropriate only with eosinophilic phenotype or asthma overlap (COPD-X; GOLD).

options.text: Triple therapy with LAMA/LABA/ICS in a single inhaler
options.rationale: Triple therapy is escalation FROM dual bronchodilation when exacerbations persist; he has not trialled dual bronchodilator yet and lacks ICS-favouring features (eos <300, no asthma overlap). Triple therapy would be correct after dual bronchodilator failure with rising eosinophils or persistent exacerbations (COPD-X; GOLD).

options.text: Add oral theophylline 200 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Theophylline has a narrow therapeutic index, significant drug interactions (cytochrome P450), and limited efficacy; it is a last-line agent in stable COPD. Theophylline would only be considered after maximal inhaler therapy in patients with persistent symptoms and no other options (AMH; COPD-X).

explanation.summary: Australian COPD-X escalation is: SABA PRN, then LAMA or LABA monotherapy for low-symptom/low-exacerbation patients, then LAMA/LABA dual for higher symptom burden or exacerbators, then triple therapy (LAMA/LABA/ICS) for persistent exacerbators or those with eosinophilia or asthma overlap. Blood eosinophil count greater than or equal to 300 cells/microL favours ICS addition.
key_points: SABA PRN at all stages
LAMA or LABA monotherapy for mild symptomatic COPD
LAMA/LABA dual for greater than or equal to 2 exacerbations/year or high symptom burden
ICS adds pneumonia risk; eosinophil-guided (greater than or equal to 300) or ACO
Smoking cessation and pulmonary rehab provide the largest mortality benefits
explanation.pearls: Australian COPD-X is regularly updated by Lung Foundation Australia - check the latest version before deciding ICS escalation.

subtopic_detail: COPD - GOLD group classification and inhaler escalation

stem: A 71-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 38 percent predicted) is brought to ED by ambulance with worsening dyspnoea, productive cough with greenish sputum, and confusion over the past 24 hours. She is a current smoker. On arrival she was placed on 8 L/min Hudson mask oxygen by paramedics; SpO2 is now 98 percent. She is drowsy with bounding peripheral pulses, flapping tremor, and warm sweaty palms. She is using salbutamol/ipratropium nebulisers continuously since arrival. An arterial blood gas is taken on the current oxygen flow rate. Her usual inhalers are tiotropium and salmeterol/fluticasone, and she has had 3 prior admissions for COPD exacerbations in the past year, two requiring non-invasive ventilation. The ED registrar specifically asks whether to maintain current oxygen and proceed straight to intubation, to reduce oxygen to nasal prongs 2 L/min without ventilatory support, to administer IV doxapram to stimulate respiration, or to switch to nasal high-flow oxygen at 60 L/min and continue nebulisers as definitive management before any non-invasive ventilation trial is attempted. A BiPAP machine is available now in resus. ICU has been notified of likely admission.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 148/86 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 8 L Hudson mask, T 37.4 degC, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, flapping tremor, warm peripheries, accessory muscle use, widespread polyphonic wheeze, prolonged expiration.
data_table.Investigations: ABG (on 8 L Hudson): pH 7.21, pCO2 88 mmHg, pO2 110 mmHg, HCO3 32 mmol/L, BE +3. CXR: hyperinflation, no consolidation or pneumothorax.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Titrate oxygen down to a target SpO2 of 88 to 92 percent and initiate non-invasive ventilation with BiPAP
options.rationale: She has acute on chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure with respiratory acidosis (pH 7.21, pCO2 88) precipitated by hyperoxia; controlled oxygen to a target SpO2 88 to 92 percent and BiPAP (e.g. IPAP 12 to 15, EPAP 4 to 5) is the standard management for COPD with pH less than 7.35 despite optimal medical therapy (Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X; eTG Respiratory; BTS NIV).

options.text: Maintain the current 8 L/min Hudson mask oxygen flow rate and proceed straight to endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation
options.rationale: Intubation is for failure of NIV, severe agitation precluding mask tolerance, copious secretions, or cardiopulmonary arrest. Maintaining excessive oxygen (8 L Hudson mask drives the acidosis higher via Haldane effect and loss of hypoxic drive) compounds the harm. Intubation is correct only after NIV trial fails or contraindications to NIV exist (COPD-X; BTS NIV).

options.text: Reduce oxygen to nasal prongs at 2 L/min and observe in resus without ventilatory support pending repeat arterial blood gas in 1 hour
options.rationale: Reducing oxygen alone will not correct the respiratory acidosis quickly enough in a drowsy patient with pH 7.21 and pCO2 88; NIV is needed to actively unload CO2. Observation alone would be correct only with milder hypercapnia (pH >7.35) and adequate respiratory effort (COPD-X; BTS NIV).

options.text: Administer intravenous doxapram infusion to stimulate respiratory drive and continue current Hudson mask oxygen flow rate while awaiting ICU review
options.rationale: Doxapram is rarely used now and has been superseded by NIV; it causes agitation, arrhythmia, and seizure. Continued excessive oxygen compounds the problem. Doxapram has essentially no current role in COPD exacerbation management (AMH; BTS NIV).

options.text: Switch to nasal high-flow oxygen at 60 L/min, FiO2 0.6, and continue back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium nebulisers without BiPAP
options.rationale: Nasal high-flow oxygen can be used in selected COPD patients (HOT-AIR trial) but NIV (BiPAP) has stronger evidence and is first-line in hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidosis (pH <7.35). HFNO would be a reasonable second-line option if NIV is not tolerated, not the first-line choice with pH 7.21 (COPD-X; BTS NIV).

explanation.summary: Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (Type 2) in COPD with pH less than 7.35 despite optimal pharmacological therapy is treated with BiPAP. Oxygen is titrated to target SpO2 88 to 92 percent to avoid worsening hypercapnia from loss of hypoxic drive and Haldane effect. Standard COPD exacerbation bundle: controlled oxygen, salbutamol + ipratropium nebs, prednisolone 30 to 50 mg daily for 5 days, antibiotics if increased purulence/dyspnoea/sputum volume.
key_points: Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD (avoid hyperoxia-induced CO2 retention)
NIV (BiPAP) for pH less than 7.35 with hypercapnia despite optimal therapy
Steroids: prednisolone 30-50 mg PO for 5 days
Antibiotics if Anthonisen criteria met: increased dyspnoea + sputum volume + sputum purulence
Intubation if NIV fails, GCS rapidly falling, or cardiopulmonary arrest
explanation.pearls: Bounding pulses, flap, and warm sweaty palms are the bedside triad of hypercapnia - they predict the ABG before it returns.

subtopic_detail: COPD exacerbation - acute management and ABG interpretation

stem: A 24-year-old woman with known moderate persistent asthma presents to ED with 6 hours of progressively worsening dyspnoea, wheeze, and dry cough. She had a viral URTI 3 days ago. She has been using her salbutamol inhaler every 30 minutes for the last 2 hours with minimal relief. She has a background of eczema and rhinitis, no other medical history, and her usual inhaler is budesonide/formoterol 200/6 maintenance and reliever. Her best-ever PEF is 460 L/min. On arrival she can only speak in short phrases, is sitting upright, and is using accessory muscles. There is widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze without focal crackles. There is no chest pain, no haemoptysis, and no calf swelling. The ED registrar asks specifically whether the exacerbation is mild-moderate, severe, or life-threatening, and which initial treatment bundle applies under the Australian Asthma Handbook for this patient today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, RR 30, SpO2 91% RA, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, accessory muscle use, short-phrase speech, widespread polyphonic wheeze, prolonged expiration.
data_table.Investigations: PEF 180 L/min (39 percent of personal best). CXR: hyperinflation, no consolidation/pneumothorax. K 3.6 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate classification of severity and corresponding initial treatment?

options.text: Mild to moderate exacerbation - salbutamol pMDI 4 to 12 puffs via spacer and reassess in 20 minutes for ongoing wheeze
options.rationale: Mild to moderate asthma has PEF >60% of best, speaks in sentences, no accessory muscle use. Her PEF 39%, short-phrase speech, RR 30, SpO2 91% all meet severe criteria. The anti-pattern is under-classifying severity from a single number (e.g., looking only at SpO2). This management would only be appropriate in true mild-moderate exacerbation (Australian Asthma Handbook).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Severe exacerbation - continuous salbutamol nebulisers with ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone, and oxygen titrated to SpO2 93 to 95 percent
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma in adults is defined by inability to complete sentences, PEF 40 to 60 percent of best, RR greater than or equal to 25, HR greater than or equal to 110, or SpO2 less than 92 percent; treatment is back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers, ipratropium, systemic corticosteroid, and controlled oxygen to SpO2 93 to 95 percent (Australian Asthma Handbook; eTG Respiratory).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Life-threatening exacerbation - immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation in the resus bay regardless of clinical response to bronchodilator therapy
options.rationale: Life-threatening features are silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, exhaustion, confusion, or PEF <33% - she does not yet meet these. Pre-emptive intubation in severe (not life-threatening) asthma without therapeutic trial increases morbidity; bronchospasm is best treated medically while preparing for escalation. Intubation is correct only after failed maximal medical therapy or imminent respiratory arrest (Australian Asthma Handbook).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Moderate exacerbation - oral prednisolone 50 mg and discharge home with safety-net advice and GP review in 24 hours for reassessment
options.rationale: Discharge without observation in a severe exacerbation is unsafe; she requires intensive bronchodilation, systemic steroid, and reassessment over hours to confirm response (typically 4-hour observation post stabilisation). Oral prednisolone discharge is correct for mild-moderate exacerbation after good response to inhaled bronchodilator in ED (Australian Asthma Handbook).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Severe exacerbation - IV magnesium sulfate 2 g as first-line and observe response before commencing salbutamol nebulisers or systemic corticosteroid
options.rationale: IV magnesium 2 g over 20 minutes is a useful adjunct in severe asthma not responding to maximal first-line bronchodilation; it is not first-line. The anti-pattern is leading with adjunctive therapy. Magnesium is correct after 1 hour of continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium, and systemic steroid with inadequate response (Australian Asthma Handbook; eTG Respiratory).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Australian Asthma Handbook severity in adults uses speech, work of breathing, RR, HR, SpO2, and PEF percent best. Severe exacerbation criteria include any of: cannot complete sentences, accessory muscle use, RR greater than or equal to 25, HR greater than or equal to 110, SpO2 less than 92 percent, or PEF 40 to 60 percent of best. Treatment escalates from salbutamol pMDI plus spacer through nebulised salbutamol plus ipratropium, systemic corticosteroid, IV magnesium, and ICU referral for life-threatening features.
key_points: Severe: short-phrase speech, RR greater than or equal to 25, HR greater than or equal to 110, SpO2 less than 92, PEF 40 to 60 percent best
Life-threatening: silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, PEF less than 33 percent
Steroids: prednisolone 50 mg PO or hydrocortisone 100 mg IV
Target SpO2 93-95 percent (avoid over-oxygenation)
Magnesium 2 g IV over 20 min if severe and not responding
explanation.pearls: A normal pCO2 in a severe asthmatic is ominous - it signals fatigue and impending respiratory failure.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

stem: A 31-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for asthma. Over the past 3 months she has had daytime symptoms 3 to 4 times per week, nocturnal waking once weekly, and uses her salbutamol inhaler roughly 4 times per week. She has had no exacerbations requiring oral steroids in the past year. Her inhaler technique is excellent on observation. She is currently on salbutamol 100 microgram pMDI as needed only. She is planning pregnancy in the next 6 months and asks specifically about whether she can keep using her reliever as her only inhaler in pregnancy. FEV1 is 88 percent predicted with bronchodilator reversibility of 14 percent. She has no rhinitis, reflux, or known occupational trigger.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Chest clear, no wheeze, no signs of allergic rhinitis.
data_table.Investigations: FEV1 88 percent predicted, BDR 14 percent, normal CXR.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue salbutamol PRN alone with a written asthma action plan and review after conception
options.rationale: Salbutamol-only therapy is no longer recommended at any step in adult asthma per the Australian Asthma Handbook (2024); her symptoms also meet criteria for poor control (SABA use greater than 2/week, nocturnal symptoms) and uncontrolled asthma in pregnancy carries higher fetal risk than ICS exposure.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence low-dose budesonide-formoterol 200/6 used as anti-inflammatory reliever only with written action plan
options.rationale: Australian Asthma Handbook Step 1-2 adult therapy is low-dose ICS-formoterol as anti-inflammatory reliever (AIR); budesonide is the ICS with the strongest pregnancy safety data and formoterol is acceptable, so AIR is preferred ahead of conception over SABA-only or daily ICS plus SABA.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence daily montelukast 10 mg nocte with continued salbutamol PRN as reliever
options.rationale: Leukotriene receptor antagonists are an add-on option in selected patients; they are inferior to ICS for first-line control and TGA has a boxed warning about neuropsychiatric effects, making montelukast an inappropriate first-line choice especially pre-conception.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence high-dose fluticasone-salmeterol 500/50 twice daily with salbutamol PRN
options.rationale: High-dose ICS-LABA over-treats mild-persistent asthma; stepwise escalation begins with low-dose AIR therapy (Australian Asthma Handbook).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 25 mg daily for 2 weeks then taper with continued salbutamol PRN
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are reserved for acute exacerbations or severe poorly controlled asthma; chronic oral steroids are inappropriate when inhaled options have not been trialled (Australian Asthma Handbook; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024 abandons SABA-only treatment in adults. The preferred reliever at all steps is low-dose ICS-formoterol (anti-inflammatory reliever, AIR). At higher severity it becomes maintenance and reliever therapy (MART). This reduces severe exacerbations and oral steroid bursts compared with SABA-only or daily ICS plus SABA reliever.
key_points: Adult asthma: no SABA-only at any step
Step 1-2: low-dose budesonide/formoterol PRN (AIR)
Step 3: low-dose budesonide/formoterol maintenance + reliever (MART)
Step 4: medium-dose MART
Step 5: high-dose ICS-LABA + add-on (LAMA, biologic) + specialist review
explanation.pearls: Reach for the brown puffer first, not the blue one - that's the headline shift in modern Australian asthma management.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

stem: A 52-year-old man is diagnosed with acute segmental pulmonary embolism on CTPA, performed for pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea after a long-haul flight from London 5 days ago. He has no prior VTE, no malignancy, no chronic cardiopulmonary disease, and no recent surgery. On arrival he is haemodynamically stable, ambulating, eating, and drinking. His vital signs are stable as below. His troponin I and NT-proBNP are normal, and the CTPA reports no right heart strain. He has stable home circumstances, lives 10 minutes from the hospital, and has a partner at home. He is not pregnant. He has no significant bleeding history and is not on antiplatelets or NSAIDs. His haemoglobin is 142 g/L, platelets 230 x 10^9/L, and creatinine 78 micromol/L. The ED registrar specifically asks whether outpatient DOAC management is safe given sPESI 0 and no right heart strain, or whether inpatient warfarin bridging, systemic thrombolysis, IVC filter or aspirin monotherapy would be more appropriate.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Comfortable at rest, chest clear, calves soft, no peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: CTPA: right segmental PE, no right heart strain. Troponin I normal. NT-proBNP normal. Hb 142 g/L, platelets 230, creatinine 78.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Outpatient management with apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for 3 months
options.rationale: sPESI score of 0 (no malignancy, no chronic cardiopulmonary disease, age less than 80, HR less than 110, SBP greater than or equal to 100, SpO2 greater than or equal to 90 percent) indicates low-risk PE suitable for outpatient management with a DOAC; apixaban regimen is 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD for at least 3 months (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Inpatient management on a therapeutic enoxaparin bridge to warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 for at least 3 months of continued anticoagulation
options.rationale: Warfarin is no longer first-line for unprovoked PE without contraindications to DOACs; DOAC monotherapy avoids parenteral bridging and INR monitoring. Warfarin remains correct for antiphospholipid syndrome (triple positive), severe renal impairment, mechanical valves, or where DOACs are unavailable/contraindicated (THANZ; eTG).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours followed by therapeutic heparin infusion and admission to a coronary care monitored bed
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is reserved for high-risk (massive) PE with haemodynamic instability (sustained hypotension, shock) or selected intermediate-high-risk with deterioration. He is haemodynamically stable with no RV strain. Thrombolysis would only be correct with SBP <90 sustained, cardiac arrest, or progressive RV failure on therapeutic anticoagulation (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Inferior vena cava filter insertion without therapeutic anticoagulation given the recent long-haul flight as a transient provoking factor
options.rationale: IVC filters are reserved for absolute contraindications to anticoagulation or recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation. Recent long-haul flight is a transient provoking factor that justifies anticoagulation for 3 months but is not a contraindication. IVC filter would be correct only with active major bleeding precluding anticoagulation (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily indefinitely without therapeutic anticoagulation given the segmental clot burden and absence of right heart strain
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is inadequate for acute PE treatment; therapeutic anticoagulation for at least 3 months is mandatory. Aspirin has a role in extended secondary prevention AFTER initial 6-12 months of therapeutic anticoagulation in low-recurrence-risk patients (ASPIRE/WARFASA trials), not as initial treatment (eTG; THANZ).
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

explanation.summary: Risk stratification of acute PE uses haemodynamic status, sPESI, RV dysfunction (echo or CT), and cardiac biomarkers. Low-risk PE (sPESI 0, no RV strain, normal biomarkers) can be managed as an outpatient with DOAC monotherapy. Intermediate and high-risk PE require admission, and high-risk (shock) PE may need thrombolysis.
key_points: sPESI: age greater than 80, malignancy, chronic cardiopulmonary disease, HR greater than or equal to 110, SBP less than 100, SpO2 less than 90 percent
sPESI 0 = low risk, suitable for outpatient management
DOAC monotherapy first-line: apixaban or rivaroxaban (no bridging)
Apixaban 10 mg BD x 7 days then 5 mg BD
Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD x 21 days then 20 mg daily
Minimum 3 months anticoagulation; consider extended therapy if unprovoked
explanation.pearls: A long-haul flight makes the PE provoked, which typically caps anticoagulation duration at 3 months once the risk factor has resolved.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand (THANZ) VTE guidance

subtopic_detail: Pulmonary embolism - risk stratification and outpatient management

stem: An 82-year-old nursing home resident with advanced Parkinson disease and known dysphagia is brought to ED with 24 hours of fever, productive cough, and confusion. The nursing home reports she was found coughing during dinner the previous evening. She was hospitalised 3 weeks ago for a urinary tract infection and received IV ceftriaxone and oral ciprofloxacin during that admission. She has no documented multi-drug-resistant organism. On examination she is febrile and tachypnoeic with right lower zone crackles. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. Bloods show neutrophilic leukocytosis and creatinine 96 micromol/L. She has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 2 L NP, T 38.4 degC, GCS 14.
data_table.Examination: Right lower zone crackles and dullness, no neck stiffness, mild dehydration.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. WCC 14.6 x 10^9/L. CRP 180 mg/L. Creatinine 96 micromol/L.
data_table.Medications: Levodopa/carbidopa 100/25 mg QID, no antibiotics in past 6 months.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic therapy?

options.text: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g eight-hourly empirically without antipseudomonal cover
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the standard empirical choice for community-onset aspiration pneumonia without risk factors, but her recent hospitalisation and broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure within 90 days place her in the higher-risk category requiring broader empirical cover (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g six-hourly empirically with daily microbiology review
options.rationale: Recent hospitalisation plus recent broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure are eTG-listed risk factors for Gram-negatives including Pseudomonas in aspiration pneumonia; broader empirical cover with piperacillin-tazobactam is recommended pending cultures, with de-escalation once organism known (eTG Respiratory; Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily as monotherapy without anaerobic cover
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone lacks beta-lactamase coverage for oral anaerobes and Gram-negatives, and oral therapy is inappropriate in this severity with hypoxia and confusion (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg twelve-hourly empirically
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone-metronidazole does not cover Pseudomonas and is less appropriate given her recent ceftriaxone exposure; routine metronidazole is no longer recommended by eTG even when amoxicillin-clavulanate is used (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: IV vancomycin 1.5 g twelve-hourly plus IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg twelve-hourly empirically
options.rationale: Vancomycin plus ciprofloxacin lacks adequate anaerobic cover and empirical vancomycin is not indicated without MRSA risk factors; recent fluoroquinolone exposure also makes empirical ciprofloxacin a poor choice (eTG Respiratory).

explanation.summary: Aspiration pneumonia empirical therapy in Australian guidelines is amoxicillin-clavulanate (IV or oral depending on severity), which covers typical respiratory pathogens plus oral anaerobes. Routine addition of metronidazole is no longer recommended. Severity escalates to broader cover only with structural lung disease, recent hospitalisation, or prior MDRO. Address modifiable risk factors: dysphagia management, oral care, head-of-bed elevation, medication review for sedatives.
key_points: Empirical: amoxicillin-clavulanate (IV 1.2 g 8-hourly, oral 875+125 mg BD)
Routine metronidazole addition no longer recommended
Broaden if recent hospitalisation, structural lung disease, MDRO risk
Address dysphagia, oral hygiene, sedative review
Aspiration pneumonitis (chemical) does not need antibiotics unless secondary infection
explanation.pearls: Distinguish aspiration pneumonitis (acute chemical injury, no antibiotics) from aspiration pneumonia (clinical infection, antibiotics) - the time course matters.

subtopic_detail: Aspiration pneumonia - antibiotic choice and risk factors

stem: A 58-year-old man presents to ED with palpitations and mild dyspnoea that began approximately 32 hours ago, but he reports a similar but milder episode 4 days ago that he ignored. He has hypertension on perindopril and no other medical history. He is haemodynamically stable. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation at 102 bpm. Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram shows preserved LV function (LVEF 60 percent), mild left atrial dilatation (LA 42 mm), and no significant valvular disease. He has no prior stroke, peripheral vascular disease, diabetes, or heart failure, and has never been anticoagulated. CHA2DS2-VA score is 1 (hypertension). The team would like to attempt rhythm control on this presentation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 102 bpm irregular, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, chest clear, no neurological deficit, no peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: AF rate 102. Echo: LVEF 60 percent, LA 42 mm, no valve disease. TSH normal, K 4.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate approach to cardioversion?

options.text: Proceed to electrical cardioversion now without anticoagulation since current episode is under 48 hours
options.rationale: The 48-hour window applies only when AF onset is genuinely certain; his earlier symptomatic episode 4 days ago makes total duration uncertain, mandating either 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation or a TOE before cardioversion (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

options.text: Commence therapeutic anticoagulation and perform TOE-guided cardioversion, then continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks regardless of CHA2DS2-VA
options.rationale: When AF onset is uncertain or greater than 48 hours, CSANZ requires either 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation or TOE-guided cardioversion; anticoagulation must continue for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion due to atrial stunning, independent of CHA2DS2-VA score, which only governs longer-term anticoagulation.

options.text: Commence aspirin 100 mg daily and proceed to elective electrical cardioversion in 24 hours
options.rationale: Aspirin provides no useful stroke protection peri-cardioversion; therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin, LMWH, DOAC, or warfarin is required (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

options.text: Defer cardioversion indefinitely and use rate control with metoprolol without anticoagulation
options.rationale: Indefinite deferral of rhythm control loses the symptomatic benefit of restoring sinus rhythm in a first symptomatic episode (EAST-AFNET 4); rate control alone does not exempt from anticoagulation decisions guided by CHA2DS2-VA.

options.text: Discharge home, commence flecainide pill-in-pocket strategy and review in clinic next week
options.rationale: Pill-in-pocket flecainide is reserved for selected patients with structurally normal hearts, established rhythm-control plan, and proven safety under monitored conditions; it is not appropriate as a first cardioversion attempt in untreated AF with uncertain duration (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

explanation.summary: Cardioversion of AF requires either 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation pre-procedure, or TOE-guided cardioversion to exclude left atrial appendage thrombus if duration is greater than 48 hours or uncertain. Even with less than 48 hour duration, peri-cardioversion anticoagulation is recommended and must continue for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion (atrial stunning); longer-term continuation is governed by CHA2DS2-VA score.
key_points: AF greater than 48 hours or uncertain: 3 weeks anticoagulation OR TOE-guided cardioversion
Minimum 4 weeks anticoagulation post-cardioversion (atrial stunning)
Long-term continuation by CHA2DS2-VA
TOE is alternative when early cardioversion preferred
Aspirin is inadequate peri-cardioversion
explanation.pearls: The 48-hour window is widely misquoted - if duration is uncertain, treat as greater than 48 hours and either anticoagulate for 3 weeks or get a TOE.

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation - cardioversion timing and anticoagulation

stem: A 56-year-old man attends his GP for review of fatigue and nocturia over the last three months. He has hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 31, and his father has T2DM. He has had two haemoglobinopathy screens incidentally documented in the past (beta thalassaemia minor). He is not on glucocorticoids and has had no transfusions or recent illness. A fasting venous plasma glucose taken in a NATA-accredited laboratory two weeks ago was 7.3 mmol/L; today's laboratory HbA1c is 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent); a random venous glucose today is 9.4 mmol/L. He has acanthosis nigricans and is otherwise well, with no polyuria, weight loss, or vomiting.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 31 kg/m^2.
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans over the posterior neck. No ketotic breath. Hydrated.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting venous glucose 7.3 mmol/L (two weeks ago, NATA lab); HbA1c 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent) today, standardised assay; random glucose 9.4 mmol/L today; eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 1.2 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to establish or refute the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes?

options.text: Diagnose type 2 diabetes now on the basis of HbA1c 50 mmol/mol plus fasting glucose 7.3 mmol/L and commence metformin
options.rationale: Beta thalassaemia minor alters red cell turnover and can falsely lower or raise HbA1c depending on the assay; Australian Diabetes Society guidance lists haemoglobinopathy as a setting where HbA1c should not be used, requiring confirmation by repeat glycaemic testing (ADS Position; RACGP).

options.text: Repeat the fasting venous plasma glucose in a NATA-accredited laboratory on a separate day to confirm fasting glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Because his haemoglobinopathy invalidates HbA1c, Australian diagnosis must rely on two abnormal glycaemic tests on separate days (fasting glucose at least 7.0, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1, or random at least 11.1 with classic symptoms); repeating the fasting glucose is the most direct confirmatory test (RACGP; ADS).

options.text: Discharge without diagnosis because the random glucose of 9.4 mmol/L is below 11.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: An isolated random glucose below 11.1 does not exclude diabetes; his fasting glucose of 7.3 mmol/L already meets the diabetes threshold and requires confirmatory testing, not discharge (RACGP).

options.text: Order fructosamine as the primary diagnostic test since HbA1c is unreliable in haemoglobinopathy
options.rationale: Fructosamine is not validated as a primary diagnostic test for diabetes in Australian guidelines; confirmation uses standard glycaemic tests (fasting glucose or OGTT) (ADS).

options.text: Order an oral glucose tolerance test with 2-hour glucose at 75 g load, but commence metformin today regardless of result
options.rationale: Initiating treatment before establishing diagnosis bypasses confirmation; OGTT is an acceptable confirmatory test but treatment must follow the diagnosis (RACGP; ADS).

explanation.summary: In an asymptomatic adult, Australian guidance diagnoses type 2 diabetes by two abnormal tests on separate occasions: fasting venous glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L, HbA1c at least 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on a standardised assay, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1 mmol/L, or a single random glucose at least 11.1 mmol/L plus classic symptoms (RACGP 2024).
key_points: Fasting glucose >= 7.0 mmol/L is diagnostic when confirmed
HbA1c >= 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on standardised assay is diagnostic when confirmed
Random glucose >= 11.1 mmol/L is diagnostic only with classic symptoms
Two abnormal results required in asymptomatic patients
explanation.pearls: Avoid HbA1c if haemoglobinopathy, recent blood loss, transfusion, pregnancy or rapidly changing glycaemia.

stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 9 years attends his GP for diabetes review. He is on metformin XR 2 g daily and has been adherent for 18 months. He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35 percent) on echocardiography after an anterior STEMI two years ago, treated with stented LAD, and is on perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg and aspirin 100 mg. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2 and ACR 6.4 mg/mmol on two occasions. He has no symptoms of urinary tract infection, no recurrent thrush and no history of diabetic foot ulceration. BMI is 29 kg/m^2 and he is normotensive. His most recent HbA1c is 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent), up from 56 mmol/mol six months ago. He denies hypoglycaemic episodes and is keen to optimise his cardiovascular and renal protection in addition to glycaemic control. He has no history of pancreatitis and no personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. He has never had a diabetic foot ulcer, retinal laser or amputation. He is on no NSAID, no potassium-sparing diuretic and has no documented contrast allergy. He fully understands sick-day rules and is independently mobile, performs his own injections if needed and has reliable transport for monitoring bloods.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm.
data_table.Examination: JVP not elevated. Chest clear. No peripheral oedema. Feet warm, pulses present, monofilament intact.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent); eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 6.4 mg/mmol; potassium 4.2 mmol/L; LFTs normal; LVEF 35 percent.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, aspirin 100 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: Add a sulfonylurea such as gliclazide MR 30 mg daily
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas lower HbA1c but carry hypoglycaemia risk, do not improve cardiorenal outcomes, and offer no benefit in HFrEF or CKD progression. The anti-pattern is reaching for legacy HbA1c-only agents in a patient with cardiorenal indications. Gliclazide would be reasonable in T2DM without HFrEF/CKD when cost is constraining or as adjunct to glucose lowering (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Position Statement on T2DM).

options.text: Add an SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin 10 mg daily
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFrEF and slow CKD progression with albuminuria; they are first-choice add-on for T2DM with established HFrEF and CKD with eGFR within approved range (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; Heart Foundation HFrEF guideline 2022; KHA-CARI / KDIGO 2022).

options.text: Add basal insulin glargine starting 10 units nocte
options.rationale: Basal insulin lowers HbA1c but provides no cardiovascular outcome benefit and would be premature when oral and injectable non-insulin options remain. Insulin is correct when patient is glucotoxic (HbA1c >10%, ketonaemia, marked symptoms) or after exhausting oral/GLP-1 RA options (ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Add a DPP4 inhibitor such as sitagliptin 100 mg daily
options.rationale: DPP4 inhibitors are weight-neutral, well tolerated, but cardiorenally neutral. Saxagliptin specifically increases HF hospitalisation (SAVOR-TIMI 53). The anti-pattern is choosing weight neutrality over outcome benefit. DPP4 inhibitors are reasonable as third-line in absence of HFrEF/CKD where SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA are contraindicated (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; AMH).

options.text: Add pioglitazone 15 mg daily for insulin sensitisation
options.rationale: Thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention via PPAR-gamma sodium reabsorption and are contraindicated in HFrEF (NYHA III/IV). Pioglitazone has glucose-lowering and modest cardiovascular benefits in selected patients, but never with established HFrEF (AMH; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

explanation.summary: In T2DM with established HFrEF, CKD with albuminuria, or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor is the preferred add-on regardless of HbA1c, because cardiovascular death, heart failure hospitalisation and CKD progression are reduced independently of glucose lowering (ADS-ADEA; Heart Foundation 2022; KDIGO 2022).
key_points: SGLT2i indicated in HFrEF irrespective of diabetes status
SGLT2i slows CKD progression when ACR is elevated and eGFR is above the threshold (typically >=20 mL/min/1.73m^2 for empagliflozin / dapagliflozin)
Counsel on euglycaemic DKA, sick-day rules, genital mycotic infection
GLP1 receptor agonist (semaglutide, dulaglutide) is the preferred add-on if ASCVD predominates and weight loss is needed
explanation.pearls: Hold SGLT2 inhibitor during intercurrent illness, fasting, or pre-operatively to reduce euglycaemic DKA risk.

stem: A 71-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 14 years attends her GP. She is on metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly. She has tolerated triple therapy for 18 months without hypoglycaemia, weight loss has plateaued and her HbA1c has risen from 58 to 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent) over the last six months. She has no acute illness, drinks no alcohol and lives independently with her husband who can assist with injections. She is willing to start insulin but anxious about night-time hypoglycaemia. Her eGFR is 62 mL/min/1.73m^2, weight 78 kg, and she has retinopathy on annual review. Fasting capillary glucose self-monitoring shows readings of 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner readings are 8 to 10 mmol/L. There is no significant post-prandial glucose excursion documented. She drives a private car only and her vision is corrected to 6/9. She is up to date with her sick-day plan for the SGLT2 inhibitor.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, weight 78 kg.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable. No injection sites yet.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent); eGFR 62; fasting capillary glucose 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner 8 to 10 mmol/L.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg, dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate insulin regimen to commence?

options.text: Premixed biphasic insulin (e.g. NovoMix 30) twice daily before breakfast and dinner
options.rationale: Premixed biphasic insulin covers basal and prandial but has the highest hypoglycaemia risk and least flexibility. This would be correct only when adherence to multi-injection regimens is poor and structured meal patterns are reliable; not first-line for isolated fasting hyperglycaemia (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Basal-bolus regimen with insulin glargine nocte and rapid-acting insulin with each meal
options.rationale: Basal-bolus is reserved for failure of basal alone or significant post-prandial excursions; this patient has isolated fasting hyperglycaemia. The anti-pattern is starting with maximal complexity when minimal intervention suffices. Basal-bolus is correct after basal optimisation if post-prandial glucose remains uncontrolled (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Long-acting basal insulin glargine 10 units nocte titrated by 2 units every 3 days
options.rationale: Once-daily long-acting basal insulin is the preferred initiation strategy in T2DM with elevated fasting glucose; start 10 units (or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg) nocte and titrate to fasting target, continuing metformin, SGLT2i and GLP1 RA (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Twice-daily NPH insulin in equal split doses administered each morning and again each night
options.rationale: NPH has a 4 to 8 hour peak with higher nocturnal hypoglycaemia risk than long-acting analogues; long-acting analogues (glargine, detemir, degludec) are preferred especially in older patients fearful of hypoglycaemia. NPH may be acceptable on cost grounds or in patients without nocturnal hypoglycaemia concern (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Prandial-only rapid-acting insulin aspart given with only the largest carbohydrate meal of the day
options.rationale: Prandial-only insulin addresses post-prandial excursions, not fasting hyperglycaemia, and would miss her dominant abnormality. The anti-pattern is matching insulin profile to the wrong glycaemic pattern. Prandial-only would be correct with isolated post-prandial spikes and normal fasting glucose (RACGP 2024).

explanation.summary: When non-insulin therapy fails in T2DM, the standard initiation is a once-daily long-acting basal insulin (glargine, degludec or detemir) nocte, with self-titration to a fasting target of 6 to 8 mmol/L. Existing metformin, SGLT2i and GLP1 RA should be continued (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).
key_points: Start 10 units nocte or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg/day
Titrate by 2 units every 3 days based on fasting capillary glucose
Continue oral agents and GLP1 RA
Add prandial insulin only if basal target is met but HbA1c is still high
explanation.pearls: Always counsel on hypoglycaemia recognition, glucagon access, sick-day rules and Austroads driving guidance before starting insulin.

stem: A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to ED with 18 hours of vomiting, polyuria and abdominal pain after running out of insulin glargine three days ago. She is alert and oriented but exhausted. Her usual basal-bolus regimen is glargine 18 units nocte and aspart with meals. She has no chest pain and no focal sepsis. On arrival she is tachycardic, hypotensive and breathing deeply. Capillary glucose is high (off the meter), capillary ketones are 5.8 mmol/L and a venous blood gas shows pH 7.10, bicarbonate 7 mmol/L, anion gap 28. Serum potassium on the gas is 5.4 mmol/L. Sodium is 132 mmol/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L, eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m^2. She has voided 200 mL since arrival 30 minutes ago. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without peaked T waves. Urinalysis confirms ketonuria and glycosuria; there is no leucocyte esterase or nitrites and her CXR is clear.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, RR 28 (Kussmaul), T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent on RA, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds. Abdomen soft, generalised mild tenderness. No focal sepsis.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 'HI' (>33 mmol/L); ketones 5.8 mmol/L; pH 7.10, HCO3 7, anion gap 28; K+ 5.4, Na+ 132, Cr 118; ECG sinus tachycardia.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management within the first 15 minutes?

options.text: IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 500 mL bolus over 15 to 30 minutes then reassess
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin in DKA. Adult Australian and JBDS-IP guidance recommend an initial 500 to 1000 mL crystalloid bolus when systolic BP is below 90 mmHg, with reassessment before further boluses (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).

options.text: Commence fixed-rate IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour without any IV fluid
options.rationale: Starting insulin before volume restoration worsens hypotension, accelerates intracellular potassium shift, and risks cardiovascular collapse. The anti-pattern is leading with insulin in shocked DKA. Fluid-then-insulin is the correct sequence; insulin can start within 1 hour of fluid if K>3.5 (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol as an immediate stat bolus dose
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA except in severe acidaemia with pH <6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalaemia; routine use worsens intracellular acidosis and risks cerebral oedema (a key pre-2010 anti-pattern eliminated by current guidelines). Bicarbonate is correct only at pH <6.9 with the strict JBDS regimen (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Give IV potassium chloride 40 mmol prior to any insulin or other therapy
options.rationale: Potassium replacement is withheld while serum K is above 5.5 mmol/L; her K is 5.4 mmol/L (within range to start insulin). Insulin will shift K intracellularly, so K replacement is added once K falls below 5.5. Pre-emptive K bolus risks hyperkalaemic arrhythmia (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Subcutaneous long-acting insulin glargine 18 units administered immediately without IV fluid
options.rationale: Restarting basal insulin within the first 12 to 24 hours of DKA is recommended (JBDS subcut overlap strategy) but does NOT replace IV insulin infusion in established DKA and does not address the priority of volume resuscitation in shock. Subcut basal is correct as adjunct, not as sole therapy (JBDS DKA 2023).

explanation.summary: DKA management begins with IV fluid resuscitation, then a fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (0.1 units/kg/hour), with potassium added once K+ falls below 5.5 mmol/L and dextrose added once glucose falls below 14 mmol/L. Bicarbonate is reserved for pH <6.9 (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).
key_points: Volume resuscitation first when shocked (500 to 1000 mL crystalloid bolus)
Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour after volume started
K+ replacement when serum K+ <5.5 mmol/L; hold insulin if K+ <3.3 mmol/L
Continue long-acting basal insulin throughout to bridge transition off IV insulin
explanation.pearls: Resolution criteria: ketones <0.6 mmol/L AND venous pH >=7.30 AND HCO3 >=18 mmol/L.

stem: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is brought from his nursing home with one week of progressive confusion and reduced oral intake. He has hypertension, chronic kidney disease and a recent UTI treated with trimethoprim. His regular medications include metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. Staff report he has been refusing to drink for the last two days. On arrival he is drowsy, severely dehydrated and has a temperature of 38.4 degrees. Capillary glucose is 48 mmol/L. Venous gas shows pH 7.34, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, anion gap 14, lactate 1.8. Capillary ketones are 0.6 mmol/L. Sodium 152 mmol/L, potassium 4.1, urea 24, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 110), corrected sodium for glucose 158 mmol/L. Calculated effective serum osmolality is 348 mosm/kg. Urinalysis confirms leucocytes and nitrites; he is on no SGLT2 inhibitor. There are no Kussmaul respirations and no ketotic breath.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/56 mmHg, HR 112, RR 18, T 38.4, SpO2 96 percent on RA, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Severely dehydrated, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 4 seconds. No Kussmaul breathing.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 48 mmol/L; ketones 0.6 mmol/L; pH 7.34, HCO3 20, AG 14; Na 152, K 4.1, Cr 198 (baseline 110); effective osmolality 348 mosm/kg.

lead_in: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation as hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) rather than DKA?

options.text: The blood glucose level of 48 mmol/L on bedside capillary testing today
options.rationale: Severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) occurs in both HHS and DKA; glucose level alone does not discriminate. The anti-pattern is using a number that is shared between the two diagnoses to differentiate. HHS typically has higher glucose (often >40) but overlap with severe DKA is substantial (eTG Endocrinology - HHS).

options.text: Effective osmolality above 320, capillary ketones under 3 and pH above 7.30 without acidosis
options.rationale: Diagnostic features of HHS are marked hyperglycaemia (typically >30 mmol/L), effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis (capillary ketones <3 mmol/L) and absence of significant acidosis (pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15) (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The acute kidney injury with creatinine rising from baseline 110 to 198 micromol/L
options.rationale: AKI is common in both DKA and HHS due to volume depletion and does not differentiate. This is the classic shared-feature anti-pattern. AKI severity may favour HHS due to more prolonged osmotic diuresis but is not discriminatory (KDIGO; eTG).

options.text: The presence of a urinary tract infection acting as the precipitating event here
options.rationale: Infection precipitates both HHS and DKA; the precipitant cannot discriminate the metabolic phenotype. The discrimination is biochemical (ketosis and acidosis present in DKA, absent or minimal in HHS), not aetiological (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The reduced Glasgow Coma Scale score on arrival to the emergency department
options.rationale: Drowsiness can occur in both, although depressed consciousness correlates more closely with osmolality (and therefore is more often severe in HHS). However the DEFINING feature remains the biochemistry pattern (ketones, pH, osmolality), not GCS alone. GCS is supportive but not the specific discriminator (JBDS HHS 2022).

explanation.summary: HHS is defined biochemically by severe hyperglycaemia, effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis and absence of significant acidosis. Fluid resuscitation (typically 0.9 percent saline at a slower rate than DKA), VTE prophylaxis and identification of the precipitant are priorities; insulin is added at a lower rate (0.05 units/kg/hour) only after fluids restore perfusion (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG).
key_points: HHS biochemistry: glucose >30, osm >320, ketones <3, pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15
Slower fluid replacement than DKA (older, comorbid, risk of fluid overload)
Mortality up to 20 percent - higher than DKA
Always give thromboprophylaxis (LMWH unless contraindicated)
explanation.pearls: If ketones are >3 mmol/L and there is acidosis, treat as mixed DKA/HHS using DKA pathway.

stem: A 74-year-old woman with T2DM on metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 120 mg daily and insulin glargine 22 units nocte is brought to ED by paramedics after her daughter found her diaphoretic and confused. Capillary glucose was 1.8 mmol/L pre-hospital. She received IM glucagon 1 mg followed by oral juice with rapid clinical improvement; her glucose now is 6.4 mmol/L and she is fully oriented. Over the last week she has reported anorexia, fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 2 kg. She started cefalexin five days ago for a urinary tract infection and the same day her GP added trimethoprim 300 mg daily after sensitivities returned. She has CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2 baseline). She lives alone and reports occasional missed meals. Her last HbA1c three months ago was 49 mmol/mol (6.6 percent). She has no chest pain. ECG shows sinus rhythm rate 78 with no acute ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 78, T 36.6, SpO2 98 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Now alert and oriented. No focal neurology. Mild dry mucous membranes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose now 6.4 mmol/L; eGFR 38; HbA1c 49 mmol/mol; UEC otherwise normal.

lead_in: Which factor is most likely to have precipitated this hypoglycaemic episode?

options.text: The basal insulin glargine dose, which should be reduced as the default first action
options.rationale: Insulin glargine alone is unlikely to cause this severity in a previously stable patient; reflexive dose reduction without identifying precipitants misses the actual cause. The anti-pattern is treating insulin as default culprit when sulfonylurea-antibiotic interaction is present. Glargine dose review is appropriate after identifying and addressing the precipitant (RACGP 2024; AMH).

options.text: Trimethoprim potentiating gliclazide via reduced clearance in CKD plus the reduced oral intake
options.rationale: Trimethoprim potentiates sulfonylureas (CYP2C9 inhibition and competitive renal tubular secretion) and gliclazide accumulates with reduced eGFR; together with poor intake and weight loss, this is the most likely precipitant. Sulfonylureas should be reviewed in CKD and with new interacting antibiotics (AMH; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Direct metformin-induced hypoglycaemia from her usual twice-daily 1 g metformin dose
options.rationale: Metformin does not cause hypoglycaemia in monotherapy and only minimally contributes in combination therapy because it does not stimulate insulin secretion. The anti-pattern is blaming the most familiar drug. Metformin contributes to hypoglycaemia only in extreme overdose or via lactic acidosis indirectly (AMH).

options.text: Cefalexin-induced hypoglycaemia from the recently started urinary tract infection antibiotic
options.rationale: Cefalexin is not a recognised cause of hypoglycaemia. Among antibiotics, fluoroquinolones (gatifloxacin most strongly) and trimethoprim are the main offenders potentiating sulfonylureas. Cefalexin causes hypoglycaemia only through reduced oral intake from nausea, not pharmacologically (AMH).

options.text: Inadequate paramedic glucagon dose given prior to her arrival in the emergency department
options.rationale: 1 mg IM glucagon is the standard adult dose and her glucose responded - this is not the cause of the original event. The anti-pattern is blaming the rescue rather than the precipitant. Glucagon dose review is appropriate only when initial response fails or in glycogen-depleted states (alcohol, prolonged fasting) (ANZCOR; AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe hypoglycaemia in an older patient on a sulfonylurea plus basal insulin should prompt review of recently introduced interacting drugs (trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole) and declining renal function. Sulfonylureas are best avoided in significant CKD and should usually be ceased before reflexively reducing insulin (RACGP 2024; AMH).
key_points: Trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole potentiate sulfonylureas
Sulfonylureas accumulate in CKD - avoid below eGFR ~30 to 45 mL/min/1.73m^2
Always look for missed meals, weight loss, new infections, new drugs
Older adults with HbA1c <53 mmol/mol on sulfonylurea + insulin are over-treated
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia unawareness develops with recurrent lows - target a slightly higher HbA1c in frail older adults (53 to 64 mmol/mol).

stem: A 38-year-old woman is brought to ED with two days of worsening agitation, vomiting and fever after stopping carbimazole 10 days ago because she ran out of her script. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago. On arrival she is agitated, sweaty and tremulous. She is in atrial fibrillation at 152 bpm with a blood pressure of 96/52 mmHg. Temperature is 39.6 degrees and she has had one episode of vomiting in ED. She has bilateral exophthalmos and a smooth diffuse goitre. Her last TSH four weeks ago was <0.01 mIU/L with free T4 48 pmol/L. She is alert but confused (GCS 14) and not following commands consistently. There is no chest pain. Bedside echo at the bedside shows preserved LV function. Capillary glucose is 7.2 mmol/L. She has no recent travel, no respiratory symptoms and clear chest on auscultation. Beta-hCG is negative. CRP is 18 mg/L and white cell count is 11.2 x10^9/L without left shift. She has no recent travel, no known infectious contacts and her urinalysis is negative. There is no rash and no neck stiffness. The endocrinology registrar has been called. She has no documented allergy to iodine, propranolol, propylthiouracil, carbimazole or hydrocortisone.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/52 mmHg, HR 152 (AF), RR 28, T 39.6 degC, SpO2 97 percent, GCS 14.
data_table.Examination: Agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic. Lid lag, exophthalmos, smooth goitre with bruit. Warm peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01, fT4 48 (4 weeks ago); ECG AF rate 152; CXR clear; CRP 18; WCC 11.2; LFTs mildly deranged ALT 78.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial medical management?

options.text: IV propranolol, oral propylthiouracil, Lugol's iodine after PTU, hydrocortisone, paracetamol and active cooling
options.rationale: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky >=45) requires beta blockade, an antithyroid drug (PTU preferred for additional T4-to-T3 conversion blockade), iodine started AFTER the antithyroid drug to prevent substrate loading, hydrocortisone (blocks conversion and treats relative adrenal insufficiency) and supportive care including cooling and treatment of the precipitant (eTG Endocrinology; AMH).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg for prompt rate control of her new atrial fibrillation
options.rationale: Amiodarone is 37% iodine by weight and PROVIDES the iodine substrate that worsens thyroid storm via the Jod-Basedow effect; it is contraindicated in thyrotoxicosis. Beta blockade (propranolol, esmolol) is preferred for rate control in storm. Amiodarone is a classic 'wrong drug at the wrong time' anti-pattern in thyroid emergency (AMH).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and acyclovir for presumed sepsis or encephalitis
options.rationale: The clinical picture fits thyroid storm with a clear precipitant (medication non-adherence). Empirical antimicrobials may be reasonable if infection is also suspected but they do not address the thyroid emergency. Antibiotics first would be correct only if sepsis were the dominant working diagnosis with a separately identified source (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV levothyroxine 200 microgram and IV hydrocortisone 100 mg for presumed myxoedema coma
options.rationale: Myxoedema coma presents with hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, and depressed consciousness - the OPPOSITE picture (hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, tremor here). The anti-pattern is reflexively reaching for thyroid hormone in any thyroid emergency. T4 + steroid is correct only in confirmed myxoedema coma (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Oral carbimazole 60 mg single dose only and immediate discharge home with outpatient endocrinology follow-up
options.rationale: Thyroid storm carries 10 to 30% mortality without aggressive inpatient management; discharge is unsafe regardless of antithyroid drug. The anti-pattern is matching dose to thyrotoxicosis (which would be appropriate for ambulatory severe Graves) rather than to the storm phenotype. Outpatient carbimazole is correct for stable Graves disease, never for storm (eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis (Burch-Wartofsky score) with hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, CNS dysfunction, GI symptoms and a precipitant on a background of hyperthyroidism. Management combines beta blockade, antithyroid drug (PTU or carbimazole), iodine (after antithyroid drug), corticosteroid and treatment of the precipitant. Myxoedema coma is the opposite picture and requires IV thyroid hormone and hydrocortisone (eTG; AMH).
key_points: PTU preferred in storm (blocks peripheral T4->T3 conversion)
Iodine (Lugol's or potassium iodide) given >=1 hour AFTER antithyroid drug
Hydrocortisone covers relative adrenal insufficiency and blocks T4->T3
Avoid amiodarone; avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from binding proteins)
explanation.pearls: Myxoedema coma classically: hypothermia, bradycardia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, hypoventilation, depressed GCS - mirror image of storm.

stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for preconception counselling. She is planning pregnancy in the next 3 months. Blood tests ordered 8 weeks ago showed TSH 5.6 mIU/L with normal free T4. A repeat panel today shows TSH 5.4 mIU/L, free T4 13 pmol/L (normal), and TPO antibodies markedly elevated at 540 IU/mL. She has no goitre, no compressive symptoms, and no other thyroid history. She has hypercholesterolaemia on atorvastatin and is otherwise well. She has no constipation or weight change.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76, HR 68, weight 72 kg.
data_table.Examination: No goitre. No dry skin. Reflexes normal.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (was 7.2); fT4 14 pmol/L; TPO Ab 540 IU/mL (positive); lipids stable.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence levothyroxine to target a pre-conception TSH below 2.5 mIU/L and review TFTs in 6 to 8 weeks
options.rationale: In women planning pregnancy or in early pregnancy, Australian and international endocrine guidance recommends treating subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH above 2.5 in pregnancy planning, above trimester-specific limits in pregnancy) particularly when TPO antibodies are positive, to reduce miscarriage and pregnancy complications; pre-conception TSH target is below 2.5 mIU/L (ESA position; RACGP Red Book; ATA pregnancy guidelines).

options.text: Repeat TFTs and TPO antibodies in 6 to 12 months with no treatment now and advise contraception until reassessed
options.rationale: Deferring treatment delays pre-conception optimisation; positive TPO antibodies plus TSH above 2.5 in a woman planning pregnancy is a treatment indication (ESA; ATA).

options.text: Stop atorvastatin and recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks since statin may interfere with the TSH assay
options.rationale: Statins do not interfere with TSH; atorvastatin should be ceased on confirmation of pregnancy due to fetal risk, but not for thyroid reasons (AMH).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the thyroid for cytology
options.rationale: FNA is indicated for suspicious thyroid nodules; she has no nodule (RACGP).

options.text: Commence T3 (liothyronine) 20 microgram daily as primary thyroid replacement
options.rationale: T3 monotherapy is not recommended for primary hypothyroidism; levothyroxine T4 is the standard (AMH; ESA).

explanation.summary: Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined by an elevated TSH with a normal free T4. Treatment is generally reserved for TSH persistently >=10 mIU/L, or 4 to 10 with attributable symptoms, pregnancy, infertility or large goitre. Otherwise, monitoring is appropriate; positive TPO antibodies confirm Hashimoto and predict progression (ESA; RACGP).
key_points: Confirm with a repeat after 6 to 12 weeks before labelling
Treat if TSH >=10, symptomatic with TSH 4 to 10, pregnant or planning pregnancy
Start levothyroxine 1.6 microgram/kg/day (lower in elderly and IHD)
Recheck TFTs 6 to 8 weeks after dose changes
explanation.pearls: In pregnancy, treat subclinical hypothyroidism if TSH is above the trimester-specific upper limit (typically >4.0 mIU/L).

stem: A 52-year-old woman is reviewed on the surgical ward 14 hours after total thyroidectomy for a multinodular goitre with compressive symptoms. She reports peri-oral and finger tingling for the last hour and one episode of carpal spasm when the cuff was inflated. She is otherwise well, with no airway compromise and no haematoma at the wound. She is on regular paracetamol and prophylactic enoxaparin. Bloods drawn at the onset of symptoms show: corrected calcium 1.78 mmol/L (corrected for albumin 38 g/L), ionised calcium 0.92 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, parathyroid hormone 0.4 pmol/L (low), 25-OH vitamin D 64 nmol/L, potassium 4.1, creatinine 76. Her ECG shows a prolonged QTc of 510 ms. She has no history of renal stones, no malignancy and her pre-operative calcium was 2.34 mmol/L. She is alert with GCS 15. There is no respiratory distress.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 98 percent on RA, T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Peri-oral and finger paraesthesiae. Positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs. No stridor. Wound dry.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 1.78 mmol/L, ionised Ca 0.92; Mg 0.74; PTH 0.4 pmol/L; ECG QTc 510 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral calcium carbonate 1.5 g elemental calcium with calcitriol 0.5 microgram and clinical review in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is correct for asymptomatic mild hypocalcaemia or as maintenance after IV correction. Symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV calcium for prompt myocardial protection. Oral calcium + calcitriol is the maintenance pathway after IV stabilisation (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL bolus followed by infusion, replace magnesium, then oral calcium plus calcitriol
options.rationale: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus followed by infusion to prevent rebound. Magnesium replacement is required if low because it perpetuates hypocalcaemia. Oral calcium and active vitamin D (calcitriol) are added for maintenance, with active D needed because of low PTH (eTG Endocrinology; AMH). Practically: 10 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate (2.2 mmol Ca) in 100 mL of 5 percent dextrose over 10 minutes, then infusion such as 100 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate in 1 L over 6 to 8 hours, with active vitamin D needed because PTH is low.

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium chloride 10 mL given via a peripheral cannula stat dose
options.rationale: Calcium chloride contains 3x more elemental calcium per mL but is highly irritant and risks severe extravasation injury via peripheral line; calcium gluconate is preferred for peripheral administration. Calcium chloride is correct only via central venous access or in cardiac arrest (AMH; eTG).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 10 mmol over 20 minutes given as the only acute therapy
options.rationale: Magnesium 0.74 mmol/L is borderline and replacement is appropriate (low Mg perpetuates hypocalcaemia by impairing PTH release and action), but Mg alone will not correct her acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia in the required timeframe. Mg-alone is correct in pure hypomagnesaemic hypocalcaemia with mild symptoms (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV cinacalcet 30 mg given as a single calcimimetic dose for acute symptom control
options.rationale: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic that LOWERS calcium in hyperparathyroidism; it is contraindicated in hypocalcaemia and would worsen her presentation - a classic wrong-direction-drug anti-pattern. Cinacalcet is correct in primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism, never in hypocalcaemia (AMH).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, laryngospasm, QT prolongation, prolonged ECG changes) is treated with IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus and infusion, replacement of magnesium if low, and transition to oral calcium plus active vitamin D (calcitriol or alfacalcidol) once stable. Calcitriol is required in hypoparathyroidism because PTH-dependent 1-alpha-hydroxylation is impaired (eTG; AMH).
key_points: 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL = 2.2 mmol elemental Ca (safer peripherally)
Always replete magnesium - low Mg causes refractory hypocalcaemia
Active vitamin D (calcitriol) needed when PTH is low
Continuous ECG monitoring during IV infusion
explanation.pearls: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia: check Ca at 6, 12 and 24 hours; transient post-op hypoparathyroidism is common, permanent in 1 to 3 percent.

stem: A 68-year-old man with diabetic nephropathy (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) attends ED after his GP found hyperkalaemia on routine bloods. He takes perindopril 8 mg, spironolactone 25 mg (started two weeks ago for resistant hypertension) and metformin which was stopped on his GP's advice. He feels generally fatigued and has had mild palpitations but no syncope. He is haemodynamically stable. ED bloods confirm potassium 7.2 mmol/L (venous, repeat 7.0), creatinine 268 micromol/L (baseline 220), bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L, calcium 2.31 mmol/L. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2 to V5, PR interval 220 ms and QRS 110 ms with normal axis; there are no ischaemic changes. He is on no other K-sparing medication and has not used potassium-containing salt substitutes. Urine output is preserved. There is no evidence of haemolysis on the sample. He has no salt substitute use at home, no recent NSAID use and no missed dialysis sessions. He has no pre-existing pacemaker.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 146/86, HR 72, RR 14, T 36.5, SpO2 97 percent.
data_table.Examination: Comfortable. No focal weakness. No pericardial rub.
data_table.Investigations: K+ 7.2 mmol/L; Cr 268 (baseline 220); HCO3 18; glucose 8.4; Ca 2.31; ECG peaked T waves V2-V5, PR 220, QRS 110.

lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes to stabilise the myocardium
options.rationale: When hyperkalaemia is accompanied by ECG changes, the first action is calcium (gluconate 10 mL of 10 percent IV, or chloride if central access) to stabilise cardiac membranes; effect within minutes, duration 30 to 60 minutes. Repeat after 5 minutes if ECG not improved (eTG Endocrinology; ANZCOR ALS).

options.text: IV insulin actrapid 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts K intracellularly and is part of the standard regimen, but it is given AFTER calcium when ECG changes are present; insulin does NOT stabilise the myocardium and takes 15 to 30 minutes for peak effect. The anti-pattern is starting potassium shift therapy before membrane stabilisation when ECG is abnormal (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg via continuous oxygen-driven nebuliser at the bedside
options.rationale: Beta-2 agonists shift K intracellularly and complement insulin-dextrose, but again do not stabilise the myocardium and act in 15 to 30 minutes. The anti-pattern is using a slower-onset shift agent ahead of immediate myocardial protection. Nebulised salbutamol is appropriate as part of combined K-shift therapy after calcium (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Oral resonium A (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) 30 g as the immediate priority intervention
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins remove K from the body but act over hours; they are useful for ongoing removal alongside dialysis planning. The anti-pattern is using a slow-acting agent in an ECG emergency. Resonium is correct as adjunct for ongoing removal after immediate threats addressed (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis arranged after a CT scan to exclude obstructive uropathy first
options.rationale: Dialysis is definitive for refractory hyperkalaemia or fluid overload but takes time to arrange; emergency medical therapy (calcium, insulin-dextrose, salbutamol) must not be delayed for imaging or vascular access. The anti-pattern is letting definitive intervention delay emergency stabilisation (eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia treatment ladder when ECG changes are present: (1) calcium gluconate (or chloride) IV to stabilise the myocardium, (2) insulin-dextrose AND nebulised salbutamol to shift K+ intracellularly, (3) cation exchange resin or potassium binder (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) for ongoing removal, (4) dialysis for refractory cases or end-organ failure (eTG Endocrinology; ANZCOR ALS).
key_points: Calcium first when ECG changes - stabilises within minutes
Insulin-dextrose: peaks at 30 to 60 minutes, monitor glucose for 6 hours
Stop precipitant drugs (ACEi/ARB, K-sparing diuretic, NSAID, trimethoprim)
Hold for 6 to 8 hours and recheck; arrange definitive removal
explanation.pearls: Peaked T waves first, then PR prolongation, then widened QRS, then sine-wave - call for help at any of the later stages.

stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP three hours after an episode of left arm and face weakness lasting 35 minutes, now completely resolved. She has hypertension on amlodipine, T2DM on metformin, current smoker (20 pack-years), and no prior atrial fibrillation. Examination is now normal. BP today 168/96, blood glucose 9.2, ECG sinus rhythm, no carotid bruit. She has no anticoagulation, no bleeding tendency, and no recent head injury.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/96, HR 78, weight 76 kg.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination now. No carotid bruit. ECG sinus rhythm.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 9.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Same-day ED transfer for non-contrast CT brain and CT angiography of head and neck, load with aspirin 300 mg now, and arrange admission to a stroke unit with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines mandate urgent same-day assessment for all suspected TIAs regardless of ABCD2 score, with same-day brain and vessel imaging to identify large-vessel stenosis that may warrant urgent intervention, initial antiplatelet load, and cardiac monitoring to detect occult AF. DAPT for 21 days (aspirin plus clopidogrel) is then recommended after high-risk TIA per CHANCE/POINT data, but the initial step is urgent referral with aspirin loading.

options.text: Calculate ABCD2 and refer urgently only if 4 or more; otherwise manage in primary care with aspirin and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Australian guidance has moved away from ABCD2-based triage because lower scores still carry meaningful early stroke risk; all suspected TIAs require same-day specialist assessment (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient specialist clinic review in 2 weeks with aspirin 100 mg daily today
options.rationale: Two-week deferral risks recurrent stroke; same-day specialist assessment with same-day imaging is the standard (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Commence rivaroxaban 20 mg daily empirically for presumed paroxysmal AF and arrange outpatient Holter monitor
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation without documented AF exposes her to bleeding risk in the immediate post-TIA window before imaging excludes haemorrhage; rhythm monitoring is appropriate but not in place of urgent imaging (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Arrange a carotid Doppler within the next 4 weeks and review her again after the result is back
options.rationale: Carotid imaging is critical but must occur within 24 to 48 hours, not weeks; same-day referral with CTA is the standard pathway (Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Modern Australian guidance for TIA emphasises urgent same-day specialist assessment regardless of ABCD2 score. Brain and vascular imaging, antiplatelet initiation (DAPT for 21 days after high-risk TIA or minor non-cardioembolic stroke per CHANCE/POINT), risk factor control and rhythm monitoring for occult AF underpin secondary prevention (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).
key_points: All suspected TIAs: same-day specialist assessment
Aspirin 300 mg loading, then DAPT 21 days for high-risk TIA / minor stroke
BP, lipid, glucose, smoking cessation, anticoagulation if AF detected
Carotid imaging within 24 to 48 hours for anterior circulation events
explanation.pearls: ABCD2 is still useful as a teaching tool but does not safely triage TIA to deferred care.

stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to ED by his partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds while watching television. He has no prior seizures, no head injury, no drug or alcohol use and takes no medications. He was confused for about 20 minutes after the event and bit the side of his tongue. On arrival he is back to his baseline. Capillary glucose, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium and a urine drug screen are normal. ECG is normal sinus rhythm. He works as a self-employed delivery driver using a standard private licence (no commercial endorsement). His non-contrast CT brain is normal. He wants to know when he can drive and what investigations are needed. He has no fever, no headache, no neck stiffness and no focal neurology. He has no family history of epilepsy and no recent travel. He is otherwise well and has not started any new medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 76, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurology. Lateral tongue bite. No focal weakness.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose normal; electrolytes, Ca, Mg, drug screen all normal; CT brain normal; ECG normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of next investigation and driving advice?

options.text: MRI brain and EEG as outpatient; no driving for at least 6 months (Austroads private standard)
options.rationale: First unprovoked seizure workup includes outpatient MRI brain (preferred over CT for structural lesions) and EEG to assess recurrence risk. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 requires a minimum 6-month non-driving period after a first seizure for private licence, longer for commercial. The treating doctor must counsel and document this (Austroads AFTD 2022; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Commence sodium valproate immediately and clear him to return to driving in 1 month
