options.rationale: Antiepileptic monotherapy after a single unprovoked seizure with no abnormalities is not routinely recommended; the modest reduction in recurrence is offset by drug burden. 1-month suspension is well short of the Austroads minimum (6 months private). Valproate is teratogenic and inappropriate in a woman of reproductive age without explicit pre-conception counselling. Multiple errors (eTG Neurology; Austroads).

options.text: Lumbar puncture today and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial meningitis empirically
options.rationale: There are no features of meningitis (no fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered consciousness); LP is not indicated for an isolated first seizure. The anti-pattern is overinvestigating in low-pretest-probability scenarios. LP is correct with fever, headache, or focal neurology suggestive of CNS infection (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Continuous video-EEG admission for 72 hours starting today as the immediate next investigation
options.rationale: Continuous video-EEG is for diagnostic uncertainty (PNES vs epilepsy) or pre-surgical evaluation, not standard first-seizure workup. The anti-pattern is escalating to specialised testing before standard workup. Routine outpatient EEG (with sleep deprivation if first study negative) is the standard first investigation (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Cleared to return to commercial driving as soon as he feels well, neurology in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Commercial driving requires longer abstinence under Austroads AFTD: typically 10 years seizure-free off medication, or 5 years on medication with strict conditions. Immediate return is unsafe and legally non-compliant. The treating doctor must specifically document commercial-vs-private status (Austroads AFTD 2022).

explanation.summary: After a first unprovoked seizure in an adult, MRI brain and EEG outpatient form the standard recurrence-risk workup. Antiepileptic drug initiation is not routine but may be considered if recurrence risk is high (epileptiform EEG, structural lesion). Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 mandates a minimum 6-month non-driving period for private vehicles after a first seizure (longer with risk factors), and longer abstinence for commercial vehicles (eTG Neurology).
key_points: Workup: MRI brain, EEG, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, magnesium, drug screen
AED start considered if recurrence risk high (e.g. structural lesion, epileptiform EEG, nocturnal seizure)
Private vehicle: minimum 6 months off driving after first seizure
Commercial vehicle: minimum 5 years seizure-free off medication
explanation.pearls: Counselling and documentation of driving restriction is the doctor's legal and professional duty - patients self-report to road authority.

stem: A 56-year-old man with known epilepsy on sodium valproate is brought to ED in convulsive status epilepticus. The seizure started 25 minutes ago in the ambulance. Paramedics gave 10 mg IM midazolam at 12 minutes and another 10 mg IV at 20 minutes without resolution; he continues to have generalised tonic-clonic activity. On arrival his airway is maintained with nasopharyngeal airway and SpO2 96 percent on 15 L non-rebreather. BP is 156/92, HR 124, temperature 37.1. Capillary glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. He has IV access and is now 27 minutes into the seizure. He has no recent head injury and his valproate level is pending. There is no fever, no rash, and no obvious focal sepsis. His airway position is maintained and there is no audible stridor. His pupils are equal and reactive. Bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 156/92, HR 124, RR 18 (assisted), T 37.1, SpO2 96 percent on 15 L NRB, GCS 3 during seizure.
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure continuing. Airway maintained with NPA.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 7.4 mmol/L; valproate level pending; ECG sinus tachycardia.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat a further IV midazolam 10 mg bolus dose as third benzodiazepine attempt
options.rationale: Two adequate benzodiazepine doses have already been given with no response; further benzodiazepine risks respiratory depression without added benefit. The anti-pattern is repeating first-line when escalation is mandated. Escalate to second-line antiepileptic (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or valproate) (eTG Neurology; ANZCOR).

options.text: IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (maximum 4.5 g) given over 10 minutes as second-line agent
options.rationale: After two adequate doses of a benzodiazepine, second-line therapy in convulsive status epilepticus is IV levetiracetam, phenytoin (or fos-phenytoin), or sodium valproate. Levetiracetam at 60 mg/kg over 10 minutes is recommended by ESETT and current Australian eTG and NSW Health guidance (eTG Neurology; ESETT 2019).

options.text: IV propofol 2 mg/kg induction and intubate without any further antiepileptic drug given
options.rationale: Anaesthetic induction is third-line, after a second-line antiepileptic has failed; jumping to anaesthesia skips a step and commits to ICU intubation unnecessarily. Propofol induction is correct in confirmed refractory status epilepticus (failure of second-line agent) (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Oral phenytoin 300 mg administered via nasogastric tube as the next antiepileptic agent
options.rationale: Oral phenytoin via NG tube has unpredictable absorption and is too slow for acute status; IV phenytoin (20 mg/kg, max rate 50 mg/min) is the parenteral option if levetiracetam is not chosen. The anti-pattern is using oral route in an emergency. NG phenytoin is correct only for outpatient initiation or maintenance, not acute status (eTG Neurology; AMH).

options.text: IV thiamine 200 mg and 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose given as the next intervention
options.rationale: Empirical glucose is reserved for hypoglycaemia; his glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. Thiamine is appropriate if alcohol use disorder is suspected (always BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke precipitation) but does not terminate the seizure. Thiamine + glucose is correct in suspected alcohol-related seizure with hypoglycaemia (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Status epilepticus management: first-line is benzodiazepine (IM midazolam 10 mg or IV lorazepam 4 mg, repeatable once); second-line after 20 minutes is IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, sodium valproate 40 mg/kg, or phenytoin 20 mg/kg (ESETT showed equivalence); third-line is induction with propofol, midazolam infusion or thiopental in ICU with intubation. Continuous EEG once anaesthetised (eTG Neurology; ESETT 2019).
key_points: Two benzodiazepine doses then escalate, do not repeat indefinitely
Second-line: levetiracetam, phenytoin or valproate (ESETT showed equivalent efficacy)
Refractory (>30 min despite second-line): intubation and continuous anaesthetic infusion
Always check glucose; consider thiamine, eclampsia, hyponatraemia, toxic causes
explanation.pearls: Do not delay treatment for imaging in convulsive status - terminate the seizure first, image when safe.

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to her GP with a three-week history of new bilateral temporal headache progressively worsening, with scalp tenderness on hair brushing and jaw pain with chewing tough food. Yesterday she had a 10-minute episode of curtain-like vision loss in her right eye that has now resolved. Today on examination her right eye visual acuity is 6/9 (was 6/6 four months ago at optometry) with a relative afferent pupillary defect; left eye 6/6. Temporal arteries tender and pulsatile bilaterally. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 84 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 484. She has hypertension on perindopril and T2DM on metformin (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol). Bone mineral density was T-score -1.4 at the lumbar spine 6 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/82, HR 72, T 36.9, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Tender pulsatile bilateral temporal arteries. Normal fundoscopy. VA 6/9 R, 6/6 L.
data_table.Investigations: ESR 92, CRP 84, Hb 108 (NCNC), platelets 484.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit for IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily, with same-day ophthalmology and rheumatology review and arrangement of temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Amaurosis fugax and a new relative afferent pupillary defect with reduced visual acuity indicate ophthalmic involvement of giant cell arteritis, which is a sight-threatening emergency requiring IV pulse methylprednisolone rather than oral prednisolone alone. Biopsy can still be performed within 1 to 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Calcium, vitamin D and PJP and bone protection planning are needed but not immediate (eTG Rheumatology; ARA position).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 60 mg is standard for GCA without visual involvement; the presence of amaurosis fugax and RAPD escalates to IV pulse methylprednisolone (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Order CT brain today and start prednisolone only if temporal artery biopsy is positive within 2 weeks
options.rationale: CT brain does not diagnose GCA and waiting for biopsy risks permanent blindness within hours to days; steroid must be started on clinical suspicion (eTG).

options.text: Commence tocilizumab subcutaneous 162 mg weekly as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing add-on therapy for established disease; it does not replace acute high-dose corticosteroid in the emergency setting (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Treat empirically as migraine with sumatriptan 50 mg and arrange neurology follow-up in 2 weeks
options.rationale: New severe headache in someone over 50 with raised inflammatory markers, jaw claudication and visual loss is not migraine, and triptans are contraindicated in suspected ischaemic disease (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency due to risk of permanent visual loss. Diagnosis is clinical (age >=50, new headache, jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, raised ESR/CRP) supported by temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions; sensitivity ~75 percent) or vascular ultrasound (halo sign). High-dose corticosteroid is started immediately, with biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing in established disease (eTG Rheumatology).
key_points: Visual symptoms or jaw claudication: IV methylprednisolone 0.5 to 1 g daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone
Otherwise: oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily
Aspirin 100 mg daily reduces ischaemic complications (controversial; review individual bleeding risk)
Tocilizumab and methotrexate are steroid-sparing options
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate), PCP prophylaxis if high-dose long-duration steroid
explanation.pearls: ESR and CRP are usually both elevated; isolated normal CRP makes GCA very unlikely.

stem: A 48-year-old previously well woman presents to ED 14 hours after the abrupt onset of the worst headache of her life while gardening. The headache reached maximum intensity within seconds and was associated with one episode of vomiting. She now has no neurological deficits, GCS 15, no neck stiffness, no rash, no photophobia. BP 162/94. She is not anticoagulated and has no recent trauma. A non-contrast CT brain is performed and reported by an experienced radiologist as normal. She has had similar but less severe headaches with exertion previously, never investigated.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/94, HR 88, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurology including fundoscopy. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose normal; ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF xanthochromia and red cell count
options.rationale: Non-contrast CT brain has near-100 percent sensitivity for SAH within 6 hours of onset but sensitivity falls beyond that window; her presentation 14 hours after onset means a normal CT does not exclude SAH, and lumbar puncture for xanthochromia (visible from 12 hours after bleed) is the next step. CT angiography is added if LP confirms SAH (eTG Neurology; ACEM).

options.text: Discharge with simple analgesia since the CT brain is normal and arrange neurology follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Discharge after a normal CT brain done beyond 6 hours from onset for thunderclap headache is unsafe; CT sensitivity drops below 100 percent and SAH must still be excluded by LP (eTG).

options.text: Proceed directly to CT angiography of intracranial vessels for unruptured aneurysm screening
options.rationale: CTA can miss a recently bled small aneurysm that has thrombosed, and identifies incidental aneurysms in around 3 percent of adults whose findings cannot be linked to the headache without confirming a bleed; LP is the correct next step (eTG; ACEM).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and IV dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: No fever, neck stiffness, photophobia or rash; meningitis cover is not the priority and the headache pattern fits thunderclap rather than meningitic onset (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Order MRI brain with gadolinium and FLAIR sequences as the definitive next investigation
options.rationale: MRI with FLAIR or GRE can demonstrate subacute SAH but is less universally available and slower than LP; in Australian practice, LP is the recommended next step after a non-diagnostic CT beyond 6 hours (eTG).

explanation.summary: Thunderclap headache reaching maximum severity within seconds to a minute is investigated with non-contrast CT brain (within 6 hours, sensitivity ~100 percent). Beyond 6 hours, LP for xanthochromia (after 12 hours from onset) confirms or excludes SAH. Positive findings require CT angiography and neurosurgical consultation for aneurysm management (eTG Neurology).
key_points: Thunderclap headache definition: maximal severity within 60 seconds
CT sensitivity ~100 percent within 6 hours, drops to ~85 percent at 24 hours
LP for xanthochromia from 12 hours after onset
CT angiography after positive findings to plan aneurysm intervention
Other thunderclap causes: cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, RCVS, dissection, pituitary apoplexy
explanation.pearls: Nimodipine 60 mg orally every 4 hours reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia after confirmed aneurysmal SAH.

stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to ED 25 minutes after eating a peanut-containing dessert. She has a known peanut allergy. On arrival: stridor and dyspnoea, widespread urticaria and lip swelling, BP 78/42, HR 132, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L non-rebreather, RR 28, alert but distressed. You give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg into the anterolateral mid-thigh and lie her supine with legs elevated. Five minutes later, BP is 82/46, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 92 percent, with persistent stridor. A second IM adrenaline 0.5 mg is given. Five minutes after that, BP is 84/48, HR 128, SpO2 92 percent, stridor persists. Two large-bore IVs are in. She has no past cardiac history and takes no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/42, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L NRB, T 36.6, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Stridor, widespread urticaria, lip swelling, accessory muscle use.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside investigations pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 5 microgram per minute titrated to clinical response, give 20 mL/kg IV crystalloid bolus, and prepare for advanced airway management
options.rationale: After two doses of IM adrenaline without adequate response, ASCIA recommends IV adrenaline infusion (5 to 15 microgram per minute titrated) alongside aggressive fluid resuscitation; persistent stridor and shock require airway preparation including senior anaesthetic and ENT support, with surgical airway equipment ready (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline 0.5 mg every 5 minutes indefinitely and avoid intravenous adrenaline
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis (no response after two IM doses) is the indication for IV adrenaline infusion under monitored conditions; persisting with IM alone delays effective therapy (ASCIA 2024).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and IV promethazine 25 mg and observe for further response
options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only and do not reverse acute anaphylaxis; promethazine can worsen hypotension; neither addresses refractory disease (ASCIA 2024; AMH).

options.text: Give IV glucagon 1 to 5 mg bolus as the next escalation step
options.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis in patients on beta-blocker; she is on no medications, so IV adrenaline infusion is the next step (ASCIA 2024).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, then reassess
options.rationale: Nebulised therapies are adjuncts for persistent bronchospasm or laryngeal oedema after systemic adrenaline; they do not replace IV adrenaline infusion in refractory shock (ASCIA 2024).

explanation.summary: IM adrenaline is the single most important treatment in anaphylaxis. Adult dose is 0.5 mg of 1:1000 (10 microgram/kg, maximum 0.5 mg) into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Place supine with legs elevated unless airway compromise. Give high-flow oxygen and IV fluid (20 mL/kg crystalloid). Corticosteroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).
key_points: IM adrenaline first, IV adrenaline infusion if refractory after 2 to 3 doses
Supine with legs elevated, high-flow oxygen, IV crystalloid bolus
Observe for biphasic reaction for at least 4 hours (longer if severe)
Discharge with EpiPen (or two), ASCIA action plan, allergy referral
explanation.pearls: If the patient is on a beta blocker and refractory, glucagon 1 to 5 mg IV may help by bypassing the beta receptor.

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal IDA mandates upper and lower GI endoscopy
stem: A 64-year-old postmenopausal woman attends her GP with three months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnoea climbing two flights of stairs. She has no overt PR bleeding, no melaena and no haematemesis but notes mild epigastric discomfort relieved by food. She takes ibuprofen 400 mg PRN for osteoarthritis (about three times per week) and is otherwise well. She has never had a colonoscopy. Her father died of colorectal cancer at 67. She does not drink alcohol and has never smoked. Her diet includes red meat twice weekly. There is no overt haemorrhage source on history. Examination shows conjunctival pallor without jaundice or lymphadenopathy. Abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness, no masses, PR exam reveals no haemorrhoids and the stool sample sent for FOBT is positive. Coeliac serology is pending. She has not had bariatric surgery and is not vegetarian. She wants to know what investigation is most appropriate now.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, T 36.7 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, no lymphadenopathy, abdomen soft with mild epigastric tenderness, PR no masses, FOBT positive.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L, MCV 71 fL, MCH 23 pg, ferritin 6 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 8%, CRP 4 mg/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2, coeliac serology pending.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Capsule endoscopy of the small bowel
options.rationale: Capsule endoscopy is reserved for obscure overt or occult GI bleeding after negative bidirectional endoscopy; it is not first-line. The anti-pattern is reaching for small-bowel imaging before excluding the much higher-yield colon and stomach. Capsule is correct only after negative gastroscopy and colonoscopy with persistent IDA (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).

options.text: Oral ferrous sulfate trial and reassess Hb in three months
options.rationale: Empirical iron without source identification is inappropriate in this high-risk group (postmenopausal woman, positive FOBT, family history); delay risks missing colorectal cancer. Empirical iron is correct only in pre-menopausal women with adequate clinical explanation (heavy menses, dietary), where endoscopy can be deferred (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).

options.text: CT colonography as first-line in place of colonoscopy
options.rationale: CTC is an alternative when colonoscopy is contraindicated or incomplete; it cannot biopsy or treat lesions and misses upper GI pathology, which still requires separate gastroscopy. CTC is correct only when colonoscopy fails or is contraindicated, and always alongside gastroscopy in IDA workup (NHMRC Colorectal Cancer Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Repeat FBC and ferritin in 6 weeks before any endoscopic referral
options.rationale: Ferritin 6 micrograms/L is definitively diagnostic of iron deficiency (no need to repeat); repeating bloods delays cancer workup. The anti-pattern is treating definitively abnormal results as 'await trend'. Repeat is correct only in borderline ferritin (15-45) where inflammation may confound (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).

options.text: Bidirectional upper and lower GI endoscopy at the same session
options.rationale: Postmenopausal women and all men with confirmed IDA require bidirectional endoscopy to exclude an occult GI malignancy or lesion; ferritin 6 with microcytic anaemia and a positive FOBT in a 64-year-old with a family history of colorectal cancer is a category 1 indication (BSG IDA Guideline 2021; NHMRC Colorectal Cancer Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal women and adult men with confirmed iron deficiency anaemia require bidirectional endoscopy at the same session to exclude GI malignancy, regardless of NSAID exposure or overt symptoms (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).
key_points: Ferritin <30 micrograms/L confirms iron deficiency; <15 highly specific
Bidirectional endoscopy is category 1 in postmenopausal women and all men
NSAID use does not negate the need for colonoscopy
Coeliac serology should be checked in parallel
explanation.pearls: FOBT positivity in confirmed IDA does NOT make upper endoscopy unnecessary - dual workup remains standard.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Subacute combined degeneration - parenteral B12 indicated
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with six months of progressive fatigue and three months of paraesthesiae in both feet that have ascended to mid-calf. She reports unsteadiness in the dark and a recent fall when reaching for an upper shelf. She has type 1 diabetes for 20 years on basal-bolus insulin, autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 micrograms and takes metformin 1 g BD for adjunctive insulin resistance. She has been on long-term proton pump inhibitor for reflux. She does not drink alcohol and is not vegan. She has had no bariatric surgery. On examination she has a beefy red tongue, brisk knee jerks but absent ankle jerks, impaired joint position and vibration sense to the mid-shins and a positive Romberg. There is no anaemic murmur. The GP requests urgent bloods. The MMSE is 28/30 with reduced recall. She is independently mobile with a four-wheel walker she has been using for the last month.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Beefy red tongue, glossitis, conjunctival pallor; absent ankle jerks, brisk knee jerks, reduced JPS and vibration to mid-shins, positive Romberg; no peripheral neuropathy of stocking distribution beyond mid-calf.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, MCV 114 fL, WCC 4.2, platelets 142, hypersegmented neutrophils on blood film, serum B12 78 pmol/L (RR >150), red cell folate 480 nmol/L, TSH 2.4, ferritin 145, LDH elevated, anti-intrinsic factor antibody positive.
data_table.Medications: Insulin glargine + aspart, metformin 1 g BD, levothyroxine 75 mcg, esomeprazole 20 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral cyanocobalamin 1000 micrograms daily indefinitely and review symptoms in clinic in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Oral cyanocobalamin is acceptable for asymptomatic dietary B12 deficiency where intrinsic factor is preserved, but NOT for pernicious anaemia with neurological involvement. Parenteral loading is required to prevent permanent SACD. Oral B12 is correct only after IM loading completion and stable maintenance in low-risk patients (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014; AMH).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM on alternate days until improvement plateaus, then 1 mg every 2 months
options.rationale: For B12 deficiency with neurological features (SACD), hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM is given on alternate days until no further improvement, then 2-monthly maintenance for life in pernicious anaemia. Anti-intrinsic factor antibody confirms pernicious anaemia (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014; AMH).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Start oral folic acid 5 mg daily while awaiting the Schilling test or holotranscobalamin level
options.rationale: Folate replacement in unrecognised B12 deficiency can precipitate or worsen SACD by depleting remaining B12 in DNA synthesis; this is the classic 'never give folate before B12 in macrocytic anaemia' anti-pattern. The Schilling test is obsolete in Australia. Folate is correct only after B12 status is confirmed adequate (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Cease the metformin and recheck the B12 level at routine follow-up in 3 months
options.rationale: Metformin can reduce B12 absorption (~7% prevalence after 5+ years) but is not the primary driver given positive anti-intrinsic factor antibody (which confirms pernicious anaemia). Stopping metformin alone does not treat established SACD. Metformin review is correct as adjunct after parenteral B12 loading (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today given the new neurological symptoms and anaemia
options.rationale: Hb 96 g/L in a haemodynamically stable patient does not meet transfusion thresholds; replacement of B12 corrects the underlying deficit. Transfusion in megaloblastic anaemia is dangerous because of low cardiac reserve from chronic anaemia and may precipitate pulmonary oedema. Transfusion is correct only with haemodynamic instability or symptomatic ischaemia (ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

explanation.summary: Pernicious anaemia with subacute combined degeneration requires prompt parenteral hydroxocobalamin loading (alternate days until response plateaus) then 2-monthly maintenance for life. Folate alone risks precipitating SACD (BCSH 2014; AMH).
key_points: Neurological B12 deficiency = parenteral loading, NOT oral
Anti-intrinsic factor antibody is highly specific for pernicious anaemia
Folate before B12 can worsen neurology
PPI and metformin both impair B12 absorption
explanation.pearls: Brisk knee jerks + absent ankle jerks + sensory ataxia = SACD until proven otherwise.

sources.label: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

stem: A 58-year-old woman with well-controlled SLE on hydroxychloroquine presents with 10 days of fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and dark urine, with mild scleral icterus for 3 days. She has no fever, weight loss or recent infections, has not started any new medications, and has never been transfused. Examination: pallor, scleral icterus, 2 cm spleen tip, no lymphadenopathy, no acrocyanosis. Bloods: Hb 62 g/L, MCV 102, reticulocytes 11 percent, LDH 920 U/L, haptoglobin under 0.1, unconjugated bilirubin 64 micromol/L, DAT IgG strongly positive and C3d weakly positive, blood film spherocytes and polychromasia, cold agglutinin titre negative. She is haemodynamically stable but has new exertional angina on transferring to the bed. ECG shows no ischaemic changes; troponin is mildly elevated (28 ng/L, normal under 14).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 97%, T 36.8 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Pale, scleral icterus, 2 cm palpable spleen tip, no lymphadenopathy, no acrocyanosis.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 72 g/L, MCV 102 fL, reticulocytes 11%, LDH 820 U/L, haptoglobin <0.1 g/L, unconjugated bilirubin 58 micromol/L, DAT IgG strongly positive C3d weakly positive, blood film spherocytes and polychromasia, cold agglutinin titre negative.

lead_in: Which combination of treatments is most appropriate as first-line management?

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily, transfuse the least incompatible packed red cells with close clinical and laboratory monitoring, and add folate replacement
options.rationale: Warm AIHA first-line therapy is prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. Her Hb of 62 with exertional angina and troponin rise reflects symptomatic anaemia threatening end-organ perfusion, which is an indication to transfuse despite cross-match difficulty; least incompatible units are given with close monitoring. Folate supports the marrow response (BSH AIHA 2017; eTG Haematology; ANZSBT).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and strictly avoid blood transfusion regardless of severity
options.rationale: Transfusion is not absolutely contraindicated in warm AIHA; least incompatible units may be life-saving when there is end-organ ischaemia (here, exertional angina with troponin rise) (BSH AIHA; ANZSBT).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Urgent splenectomy and IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Splenectomy and IVIg are not first-line in warm AIHA; prednisolone is. Splenectomy is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing disease, after vaccination against encapsulated organisms (BSH AIHA).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

options.text: Plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement and rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is used in TTP and severe cold agglutinin disease with hyperviscosity, not warm AIHA. Rituximab is second-line therapy for steroid-refractory cases (BSH AIHA).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

options.text: Continue hydroxychloroquine only and recheck FBC in 1 week before considering steroid initiation
options.rationale: Symptomatic warm AIHA with Hb 62 and cardiac compromise is not safely managed expectantly; immediate prednisolone is required (BSH AIHA; eTG Haematology).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

explanation.summary: Warm AIHA (DAT IgG positive, often C3d weak) is treated first-line with prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. Splenectomy and rituximab are second-line. Cold agglutinin disease (DAT C3d only, cold titre high) is managed differently (BSH AIHA 2017).
key_points: DAT IgG positive = warm AIHA
First-line: prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day
Add folate; consider PJP prophylaxis if prolonged steroids
Transfusion with least-incompatible units if life-threatening
explanation.pearls: Warm AIHA in SLE is the prototype secondary warm AIHA - control the underlying disease too.

sources.label: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

subtopic_detail: Indications for red cell exchange transfusion
stem: A 28-year-old man with homozygous HbSS sickle cell disease presents with three days of bilateral pleuritic chest pain, fever and dyspnoea. He was admitted six days ago for a typical vaso-occlusive crisis of the lower limbs and has been on regular morphine PCA, IV fluids and incentive spirometry. He has had four prior admissions for VOC this year and one previous episode of acute chest syndrome two years ago. He is fully vaccinated including pneumococcal and is on penicillin V prophylaxis. He has no known allergies. He is normally on hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg daily but adherence has been poor. He works as a delivery driver. On examination he is in respiratory distress, with new bilateral basal crepitations and oxygen saturation of 88% on room air, improving to 93% on 6 L via Hudson mask. There is no calf swelling. CXR shows new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates. Hb has dropped from baseline 88 to 64 g/L. He has a leucocytosis with WCC 22 and CRP 180. He is started on IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 28, BP 102/64 mmHg, SpO2 88% RA, 93% on 6 L O2.
data_table.Examination: Respiratory distress, bilateral basal crepitations, no focal lower limb signs.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 64 g/L (baseline 88), HbS 78%, WCC 22 x10^9/L, platelets 280, reticulocytes 14%, CRP 180, troponin negative, ABG pH 7.36 pCO2 38 pO2 58 on 6 L, CXR new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, D-dimer 4.2.
data_table.Medications: Morphine PCA, IV fluids, ceftriaxone, azithromycin, hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg (poor adherence).

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Simple top-up transfusion of 1 unit packed red cells
options.rationale: Simple top-up transfusion may be used in mild ACS but is inadequate for severe ACS with hypoxia and high HbS load; it does NOT reduce HbS fraction proportionally and risks hyperviscosity (raising HCT above 30% increases sickling). Simple transfusion is correct for mild ACS without hypoxia or for chronic anaemia management (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Empirical therapeutic dose enoxaparin for presumed pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: PE must be considered, but bilateral infiltrates + fever + pleuritic pain + HbS load + falling Hb make ACS the primary process; full anticoagulation without CTPA risks haemorrhage and does not treat sickling. Anticoagulation is correct only if PE is confirmed on imaging or strongly suspected on POCUS with RV strain (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Urgent red cell exchange transfusion targeting HbS less than 30%
options.rationale: Severe acute chest syndrome (hypoxia despite supplemental O2, bilateral infiltrates, falling Hb from baseline, multilobar involvement) is an indication for urgent red cell exchange aiming HbS <30%. This rapidly reduces sickling, improves oxygenation and reduces mortality (BSH ACS Guideline 2015; HSANZ).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015
HSANZ position statement on sickle cell disease

options.text: Continue current oxygen, fluids and antibiotics with hourly observations only
options.rationale: Conservative management alone is inadequate for severe ACS with hypoxaemia and falling Hb; mortality without transfusion intervention is substantial. Conservative management is correct only in mild ACS (no hypoxia, single-lobe involvement, stable Hb) (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Increase hydroxyurea dose to drive HbF production
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is chronic disease-modifying therapy taking weeks to months to raise HbF and reduce crisis frequency; it has no role in acute severe ACS management. Hydroxyurea is correct as outpatient prophylaxis after >=3 crises/year or any prior ACS, started after the acute event resolves (BSH 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

explanation.summary: Severe acute chest syndrome requires urgent red cell exchange to reduce HbS to under 30%, alongside broad-spectrum antibiotics covering atypicals, supplemental oxygen, analgesia and adequate (but not excessive) IV fluids. Mortality is high without prompt exchange (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
key_points: Severe ACS = hypoxia despite supplemental O2 or multilobar infiltrates
Exchange transfusion targets HbS <30%
Antibiotic cover for typical AND atypical pathogens
Continue analgesia and incentive spirometry
explanation.pearls: VOC + new respiratory signs + new infiltrate = ACS; treat early, transfuse fast.

sources.label: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015
HSANZ position statement on sickle cell disease

stem: A 74-year-old woman attends ED with one day of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild exertional dyspnoea. She had a knee arthroplasty 8 weeks ago. She has hypertension on perindopril, osteoarthritis, no haemoptysis, no calf swelling, no malignancy and is otherwise active. Examination: comfortable at rest, clear chest, no calf signs, SpO2 96 percent on room air. ECG sinus tachycardia 102. CXR normal. Bloods: troponin negative, FBC normal, eGFR 68. Wells score is 1.5 (recent surgery 1.5; PE most likely diagnosis criterion not counted). Age-adjusted D-dimer returns at 680 microgram/L FEU.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.7 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Clear chest, no calf signs, comfortable at rest.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin negative, ECG sinus tachycardia, CXR normal, eGFR 68, FBC normal, Wells score 1.5 (PE unlikely).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Exclude PE based on Wells unlikely plus D-dimer 680 microgram/L below the age-adjusted cut-off of 740, and discharge with safety netting
options.rationale: In Wells PE-unlikely patients over 50, the age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off is age x 10 microgram/L FEU (740 here). A value of 680 lies below this cut-off and safely excludes PE per ADJUST-PE and eTG, sparing her CTPA contrast and radiation; safety netting and follow-up are appropriate.

options.text: Proceed to CTPA because the D-dimer is above the standard fixed cut-off of 500 microgram/L
options.rationale: Applying the fixed cut-off in patients over 50 over-investigates them; age-adjusted cut-off is the correct rule and avoids unnecessary CTPA (ADJUST-PE; eTG).

options.text: Start therapeutic anticoagulation empirically and image with CTPA within 24 hours
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation pending imaging is reserved for Wells-likely patients with delayed imaging, not Wells-unlikely with a negative age-adjusted D-dimer (eTG; BTS).

options.text: Perform V/Q scan as the first-line confirmatory test because CXR is normal
options.rationale: V/Q is preferred when contrast is contraindicated; in her case PE is already excluded by Wells-unlikely plus negative age-adjusted D-dimer (eTG).

options.text: Repeat D-dimer in 24 hours before deciding on imaging
options.rationale: Repeating D-dimer adds no diagnostic information when the original is already below the age-adjusted cut-off; PE is excluded (eTG).

explanation.summary: In Wells PE-unlikely patients (score <= 4), age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 micrograms/L FEU for those over 50) safely excludes PE without CTPA. A negative result has high NPV; if positive, proceed to imaging (eTG; ADJUST-PE).
key_points: Wells <= 4 = PE unlikely; use D-dimer
Age-adjusted cut-off (age x 10) above age 50
Wells > 4 = PE likely; go to CTPA directly
V/Q reserved for contrast contraindications
explanation.pearls: The Wells 'PE most likely diagnosis' criterion is 3 points and the most subjective - apply judiciously.

stem: A 67-year-old man with newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal cancer (liver metastases) presents with a confirmed unprovoked left iliofemoral DVT and segmental subsegmental PE on CTPA. He started FOLFOX chemotherapy 10 days ago. He is haemodynamically stable, normotensive and not hypoxic. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, no liver dysfunction (bilirubin 16, ALT 28), and no prior GI bleeding. He has a colostomy after primary resection. There is no upper GI lesion documented on endoscopy 3 months ago. He has no thrombocytopenia (platelets 188) and no anaemia. He is not on antiplatelets. He has no history of intracranial haemorrhage. He weighs 78 kg. He is on ondansetron for nausea, dexamethasone PRN with chemotherapy, paracetamol for analgesia and atorvastatin 40 mg. He is keen to take an oral agent and avoid daily injections if possible. He understands that anticoagulation must continue for at least the duration of active malignancy. He has no platelet transfusion history and no autoimmune disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: Left leg swelling to mid-thigh, palpable colostomy site, no jaundice.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, platelets 188, eGFR 38, bilirubin 16, ALT 28, INR 1.0, no upper GI lesion on endoscopy 3 months ago.
data_table.Medications: FOLFOX chemotherapy, ondansetron, dexamethasone PRN, paracetamol, atorvastatin 40 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation choice?

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is acceptable for non-luminal cancer VTE but has higher GI bleed risk than apixaban in CAT trials (SELECT-D, CARAVAGGIO); the standard 20 mg dose also requires CrCl >50 mL/min. The anti-pattern is reflexive rivaroxaban for any DOAC indication. Rivaroxaban would be correct only if apixaban were unavailable and bleeding risk were low (eviQ; AMH).

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg BD after 5 days of LMWH lead-in
options.rationale: Dabigatran requires a 5-day parenteral LMWH lead-in (no immediate oral start) and is contraindicated when CrCl <30 with limited data at CrCl 30-50. Dabigatran was not studied as first-line in CAT trials and is not preferred. Dabigatran would be reasonable in non-cancer VTE with normal renal function (AMH; eviQ).

options.text: Warfarin bridged with LMWH until INR 2-3 stable
options.rationale: Warfarin is inferior to LMWH and DOACs in CAT due to drug-drug interactions with chemo, erratic INR control during nausea/vomiting, and lack of evidence for non-inferiority. Warfarin is correct only when DOACs and LMWH cannot be used (mechanical valve, severe renal impairment, prior DOAC failure) (eviQ).

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD
options.rationale: In cancer-associated thrombosis without luminal GI malignancy and with eGFR over 30, apixaban (preferred for renal impairment among DOACs) or LMWH are first-line; apixaban has the lowest GI bleed signal of DOACs and meets the patient's oral-preference goal (eviQ Cancer-associated thrombosis; eTG).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as secondary prevention
options.rationale: Aspirin is NOT acceptable monotherapy for acute VTE. It is considered only for extended secondary prevention AFTER 3-6 months of full anticoagulation in selected non-cancer low-recurrence patients (ASPIRE/WARFASA). Aspirin alone is essentially never correct for acute CAT (eTG).

explanation.summary: For cancer-associated thrombosis without luminal GI or GU malignancy, apixaban or LMWH are first-line. Apixaban has the lowest bleeding signal of DOACs and is preferred when eGFR is 30-49 (eviQ; eTG).
key_points: Luminal GI/GU cancer = caution with DOACs; consider LMWH
Apixaban dose: 10 mg BD x 7 days then 5 mg BD
Continue anticoagulation while cancer active
Avoid dabigatran when CrCl is borderline
explanation.pearls: CAT recurrence is highest in the first 3 months - don't switch agents prematurely.

stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been on prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since day 1 plus an intraoperative and 48-hour post-op unfractionated heparin infusion. He develops a new right calf DVT confirmed on ultrasound. Platelets have fallen from 280 to 78 (a 72 percent drop). No bleeding, no skin necrosis, no schistocytes on film. PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is pending. 4Ts score is 7. He has CKD with eGFR 38 (creatinine 168 micromol/L) and severe hepatic dysfunction from chronic right heart failure (bilirubin 62, INR 1.6).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.0 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Right calf swollen and tender, no skin necrosis, no bleeding, sternotomy wound clean.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 78 x10^9/L (pre-op 280), Hb 102 g/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, no schistocytes, PF4 antibody strongly positive ELISA, 4Ts 7 (high probability), eGFR 42.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) and commence IV bivalirudin at adjusted dose given combined renal and hepatic impairment, with close aPTT monitoring
options.rationale: HIT requires stopping all heparins and starting a non-heparin therapeutic anticoagulant. Argatroban is preferred in renal impairment but contraindicated in significant hepatic dysfunction (predominantly hepatic clearance), and fondaparinux is renally cleared and contraindicated at eGFR under 30. Bivalirudin has both renal and hepatic dose-adjustable clearance, making it the appropriate choice when both organs are impaired (ASH 2018; eTG).

options.text: Stop heparin and commence fondaparinux 7.5 mg SC daily off-label at standard dose
options.rationale: Fondaparinux is renally cleared and contraindicated at eGFR under 30; her eGFR of 38 sits in the dose-reduction zone but combined with hepatic dysfunction makes bivalirudin a safer choice (ASH 2018; AMH).

options.text: Stop heparin and commence IV argatroban at standard dose
options.rationale: Argatroban is hepatically cleared and contraindicated in significant hepatic dysfunction (bilirubin 62, INR 1.6); it would accumulate and over-anticoagulate (ASH 2018; AMH).

options.text: Stop heparin and immediately commence warfarin bridging to therapeutic INR 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin must not be started in acute HIT until platelets recover above 150, due to risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis from protein C depletion (ASH 2018).

options.text: Stop all heparin and observe expectantly without alternative anticoagulation until platelets recover
options.rationale: HIT is prothrombotic; stopping heparin without substitute therapeutic anticoagulation leaves him at high risk of further venous and arterial events (ASH 2018).

explanation.summary: HIT is a clinical-laboratory diagnosis with a high 4Ts score plus a positive PF4 antibody confirmed by functional assay. Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) AND substitute a non-heparin therapeutic anticoagulant; do not give warfarin or platelets in acute phase (ASH 2018).
key_points: 4Ts 0-3 low, 4-5 intermediate, 6-8 high probability
Stop all heparin AND substitute therapeutic non-heparin agent
Avoid warfarin in acute HIT until platelets > 150
Avoid prophylactic platelet transfusion
explanation.pearls: Timing nadir 5-10 days post heparin exposure is classic; rapid-onset HIT (<24 hours) occurs with recent prior exposure within 100 days.

sources.label: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with one week of mucocutaneous bruising, gum bleeding, two short epistaxis episodes, and today new heavy menstrual bleeding requiring an overnight pad change every 90 minutes. She takes no medications, has not been recently vaccinated, has no recent viral illness, no pregnancy. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Bloods: isolated platelets 4 x10^9/L with normal Hb, WCC, differential, coagulation profile, renal and liver function. Blood film: large platelets, no schistocytes, no blasts. HIV, hepatitis B and C, H. pylori, ANA all negative. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids or IVIG. The team plans urgent treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Petechiae lower limbs and buccal mucosa, bruising, gingival oozing, no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 8 x10^9/L, Hb 132, WCC 6.4 normal differential, INR 1.0, APTT 30, blood film large platelets no blasts, HIV/HBV/HCV negative, ANA negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days plus IVIG 1 g/kg as combination first-line therapy
options.rationale: Severe ITP (platelets under 20 with active bleeding requiring intervention) is treated with combination corticosteroid plus IVIG for rapid platelet rise, per ASH 2019 and eTG; IVIG raises platelets within 24 to 48 hours, while corticosteroid (oral or pulsed IV) provides sustained immunomodulation. Heavy menstrual bleeding with platelets of 4 is bleeding sufficient to warrant combination, not monotherapy (ASH 2019; eTG Haematology).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily as monotherapy and review platelet count in 1 week
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy takes days to weeks to raise platelets; with active heavy bleeding and platelets of 4, the more rapid combined IVIG plus steroid response is preferred (ASH 2019).

options.text: Platelet transfusion of 2 adult therapeutic doses to correct count above 50 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Transfused platelets are rapidly consumed by the same autoantibody and rarely raise count meaningfully in stable ITP; transfusion is reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage (intracranial, major GI) alongside other therapies (ASH 2019; ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly for 4 weeks as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Rituximab is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing ITP, not first-line (ASH 2019).

options.text: Bone marrow biopsy first to confirm the diagnosis before commencing any treatment
options.rationale: Bone marrow biopsy is not required in typical adult ITP with isolated thrombocytopenia and normal film; treatment must not be delayed when bleeding is active (ASH 2019).

explanation.summary: Adult ITP first-line is corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg or short dexamethasone pulse). IVIG is added when more rapid platelet rise is needed (active bleeding, procedure imminent). Platelet transfusion only for life-threatening bleeding (ASH 2019).
key_points: First-line: corticosteroids
Add IVIG 1 g/kg if rapid response needed
Anti-D in Rh-positive non-splenectomised patients - alternative
Treatment threshold: platelets <30 or bleeding
explanation.pearls: Avoid platelet transfusion in stable ITP - the antibodies eat them in hours.

sources.label: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

subtopic_detail: Urgent FBC + blood film + bone marrow
stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to her GP with three weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising of the limbs, gum bleeding and a low-grade fever. She had two episodes of unexplained epistaxis lasting 15 minutes each. She has no past medical history of note and takes no medications. She has lost 3 kg unintentionally in the last month and has drenching night sweats. She has no foreign travel and no contact with TB. On examination she is pale, with widespread bruising and petechiae, gum hypertrophy and bleeding, a 4 cm palpable spleen and shotty cervical lymphadenopathy. Bloods at her GP show pancytopenia: Hb 76 g/L, WCC 38 x10^9/L (with 70% blasts on differential), platelets 14 x10^9/L. Blood film reports myeloid blasts with Auer rods. Coagulation profile shows fibrinogen 1.0 g/L (low) and a raised D-dimer. She has not received transfusion. She is haemodynamically stable but the GP is concerned about possible acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML). She has no current bleeding source other than gums.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 37.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Pale, widespread bruising and petechiae, gum hypertrophy and bleeding, 4 cm splenomegaly, shotty cervical nodes.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 76 g/L, WCC 38 x10^9/L (70% blasts with Auer rods), platelets 14 x10^9/L, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.0 g/L, D-dimer 6.5, LDH 980, urate 0.52 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with routine outpatient haematology referral in 2 weeks for further workup
options.rationale: Suspected APML with coagulopathy is a haematological emergency with very high early mortality (DIC-driven intracranial haemorrhage). Outpatient referral is unsafe and misses the time-critical window for ATRA. Outpatient haematology is correct only for stable chronic conditions, never with circulating blasts plus DIC (eviQ).

options.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe in ED for 24 hours before specialist referral
options.rationale: Antibiotics may be needed if febrile neutropenia develops, but they must not delay urgent haematology transfer and empirical ATRA. The anti-pattern is treating one issue (potential sepsis) and missing the dominant threat (coagulopathy and early haemorrhagic death). Antibiotics are concurrent, not first (eviQ).

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells and 1 pool of platelets then reassess clinically
options.rationale: Transfusion support (FFP, cryoprecipitate to keep fibrinogen >1.5, platelets >50) may be appropriate but is not the SINGLE next step - definitive treatment (ATRA + bone marrow + transfer) is the priority. Aggressive transfusion is correct as adjunct to ATRA during the high-risk first 14 days (ANZSBT; eviQ).

options.text: Same-day transfer to haematology for urgent bone marrow biopsy with empirical ATRA if APML suspected
options.rationale: Acute leukaemia with coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, raised D-dimer) and Auer rods on film highly suggests APML; empirical all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) is started on clinical suspicion (do not wait for confirmation) because of bleeding-death risk. Urgent transfer for bone marrow, cytogenetics and PML-RARA testing (eviQ; BSH AML Guideline).

options.text: Repeat the full blood count in 48 hours to confirm the pancytopenia before any specialist referral
options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts is diagnostic of leukaemia until proven otherwise; delay is unsafe especially with coagulopathy. The anti-pattern is treating any abnormal result as needing 'await trend' before specialist involvement. Repeat FBC is appropriate only for borderline cytopenia without blasts (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Acute leukaemia with Auer rods plus coagulopathy is APML until proven otherwise. Same-day transfer to a haematology centre and empirical ATRA initiation reduce early haemorrhagic death (eviQ; BSH AML Guideline).
key_points: Blasts + Auer rods + coagulopathy = APML
ATRA empirically on suspicion - do not wait for confirmation
Same-day haematology transfer
Aggressive blood product support during induction
explanation.pearls: APML is the most curable acute leukaemia but early death from coagulopathy is the killer - ATRA is started before cytogenetics confirm PML-RARA.

stem: A 48-year-old man is referred from his GP after a routine FBC for life insurance showed WCC 86 x10^9/L with a full myeloid maturation spectrum (myelocytes, metamyelocytes, basophilia 5 percent, eosinophilia 4 percent, blasts under 2 percent), Hb 124, platelets 480. He has mild left upper quadrant discomfort, no fevers, weight loss, or B symptoms. He has 6 cm splenomegaly. He has a 12-year history of bipolar disorder on lithium 800 mg daily and has a recurrent left-sided pleural effusion from chronic congestive heart failure (LVEF 40 percent). Cytogenetics confirm t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 transcript by PCR. Bone marrow shows 1 percent blasts (chronic phase). He has no other comorbidities.

data_table.Examination: 6 cm splenomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, no other organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 86 x10^9/L with myeloid left shift, basophilia 5%, eosinophilia 4%, blasts <2%, Hb 124 g/L, platelets 480 x10^9/L; bone marrow hypercellular myeloid 1% blasts; cytogenetics t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 positive.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor?

options.text: Imatinib 400 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Chronic phase CML is treated with a BCR-ABL1 TKI. Among second-generation TKIs, dasatinib is associated with pleural effusion and is contraindicated in his recurrent CHF-related effusion; nilotinib has cardiac and QT risks; ponatinib has significant vascular toxicity. Imatinib remains a reasonable first-line in older patients or those with comorbidities (pleural effusion, cardiac disease) where the side-effect profile favours it over second-generation TKIs (eviQ CML; HSANZ).

options.text: Dasatinib 100 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Dasatinib is associated with pleural effusion in 20 to 30 percent of patients; contraindicated in someone with a recurrent CHF-related pleural effusion (eviQ; AMH).

options.text: Nilotinib 300 mg orally twice daily
options.rationale: Nilotinib has cardiac and QT-prolongation risks and significant interactions with QT-prolonging drugs; lithium also prolongs QT, making this combination cardiotoxic (eviQ; AMH).

options.text: Ponatinib 45 mg orally daily as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Ponatinib is reserved for T315I mutation or TKI-resistant disease due to significant vascular and arterial thrombotic toxicity; not first-line in chronic phase (eviQ).

options.text: Hydroxyurea 1 to 2 g daily as definitive cytoreductive therapy
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea controls counts but does not target BCR-ABL1 biology; used as a bridge before TKI in severe hyperleucocytosis, not as definitive therapy (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Chronic phase CML is treated with a BCR-ABL1 TKI - imatinib is standard first-line; transplant is reserved for advanced or refractory disease. Monitoring uses serial BCR-ABL1 transcript PCR by International Scale (eviQ).
key_points: Diagnosis: t(9;22) Philadelphia / BCR-ABL1
First-line: TKI (imatinib 400 mg, or 2nd-gen)
Monitor with BCR-ABL1 PCR transcript
Transplant for blast crisis or TKI failure
explanation.pearls: Basophilia on the differential is a clue to CML - rare in other myeloproliferative neoplasms.

stem: A 68-year-old man with stage A (Binet) CLL diagnosed 18 months ago has been on watch-and-wait. His lymphocyte count has been stable around 18-22 x10^9/L. He attends with three weeks of unilateral right cervical lymph node enlargement that is now 5 cm, firm and rapidly growing. He has lost 6 kg in the last month, has drenching night sweats and a temperature of 38.4 deg C this morning. His Hb is 102 g/L (was 132), LDH is 920 U/L (was 220) and uric acid is 0.48 mmol/L. He has no infection elsewhere. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows bulky cervical, mediastinal and abdominal lymphadenopathy with a dominant 5 x 4 cm right cervical node. PET-CT shows the dominant node has SUVmax 18 with significantly higher avidity than other nodes. He has not had previous chemotherapy. He has not received rituximab. His original CLL FISH showed del(13q) only. He has no concurrent infection on cultures.

data_table.Examination: 5 cm firm right cervical lymph node, generalised lymphadenopathy, 3 cm splenomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (was 132), WCC 24 (lymphocytes 18), platelets 142, LDH 920 (was 220), uric acid 0.48, beta-2 microglobulin elevated, blood film smudge cells with some larger atypical cells, PET-CT dominant node SUVmax 18.

lead_in: Which finding most strongly suggests Richter transformation requiring biopsy?

options.text: Lymphocyte count rising from 18 to 22 x10^9/L over 18 months
options.rationale: Slow lymphocyte rise is expected in untreated CLL and does NOT indicate transformation; doubling time matters more than absolute count (lymphocyte doubling <6 months is a treatment-indicating feature). The anti-pattern is interpreting any rise as transformation. Rapid rise (doubling <6 months) indicates active progression, but transformation requires biopsy evidence (BSH CLL Guideline).

options.text: Smudge cells on blood film
options.rationale: Smudge cells (Gumprecht shadows) are characteristic of CLL itself - fragile lymphocytes ruptured during smear preparation. They are a diagnostic feature, not a transformation marker. Their absence/presence does not track disease evolution (BSH CLL).

options.text: PET avidity SUVmax >10 in a dominant node alongside rising LDH and B symptoms
options.rationale: Richter transformation (CLL evolving to aggressive lymphoma, usually diffuse large B-cell) is suggested by rapidly enlarging asymmetric nodes, B symptoms, marked LDH rise and PET SUVmax >10 in a dominant node. Biopsy of the most FDG-avid node is required for diagnosis (BSH CLL Guideline; eviQ).

options.text: Hb 102 g/L alone
options.rationale: Mild anaemia can reflect marrow infiltration in progressive CLL without indicating transformation; Hb 102 in isolation could also be unrelated (concurrent IDA, ACD). Combined with other features it may support transformation, but in isolation it is non-specific. Anaemia is a Rai/Binet progression marker, not specifically Richter (BSH CLL).

options.text: Original FISH showing del(13q)
options.rationale: Isolated del(13q) is a FAVOURABLE prognostic marker in CLL with the best survival of all FISH subtypes; it does not predict Richter transformation. The anti-pattern is treating any cytogenetic finding as ominous. del(17p) and TP53 mutation carry higher transformation risk (BSH CLL).

explanation.summary: Richter transformation should be suspected in CLL patients with rapidly enlarging asymmetric nodes, B symptoms, sharply rising LDH and PET SUVmax >10 in a dominant node; excisional biopsy of the most FDG-avid node confirms diagnosis (BSH CLL Guideline).
key_points: Watch-and-wait: stable CLL without symptoms, cytopenias or bulk
Treatment triggers: B symptoms, progressive cytopenias, bulky/symptomatic nodes, doubling time <6 months
Richter transformation: ~5-10% lifetime risk
Biopsy most FDG-avid node
explanation.pearls: del(17p) and TP53 mutations predict Richter risk and require targeted agents (BTK/BCL2 inhibitors) rather than chemoimmunotherapy.

stem: A 24-year-old previously well woman has been diagnosed with classical Hodgkin lymphoma after excisional left supraclavicular lymph node biopsy. Immunohistochemistry: CD30 positive, CD15 positive, CD20 negative Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. She reports drenching night sweats, fevers above 38, and 8 kg weight loss over 6 weeks. PET-CT shows supradiaphragmatic-only nodal involvement (left supraclavicular, mediastinal, bilateral cervical). Bloods: Hb 116, WCC normal, ESR 68, LDH normal, HIV negative. Echocardiogram shows LVEF 60 percent. Pulmonary function tests are normal. She has not commenced any treatment yet. She has no children but wishes to preserve fertility.

data_table.Examination: 4 cm firm left supraclavicular lymph node, bilateral cervical nodes, mild splenomegaly, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: Excisional node biopsy: CD30+ CD15+ CD20- bi-nucleate Reed-Sternberg cells in mixed inflammatory infiltrate. PET-CT supradiaphragmatic only.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before commencing chemotherapy?

options.text: Refer urgently for fertility preservation counselling and oocyte or embryo cryopreservation, then commence ABVD chemotherapy
options.rationale: ABVD chemotherapy (standard first-line for early-stage classical Hodgkin lymphoma with B symptoms is generally early-unfavourable disease per Australian and European stratification) carries low to moderate gonadotoxicity but the cumulative effect with potential radiotherapy and BEACOPP for relapse means fertility preservation should be offered before treatment begins. eviQ and HSANZ explicitly recommend urgent fertility referral before chemotherapy in young patients (eviQ Hodgkin; HSANZ).

options.text: Commence ABVD chemotherapy immediately and defer fertility discussion until after first cycle
options.rationale: Delaying fertility discussion until after chemotherapy starts misses the window for oocyte or embryo cryopreservation (which requires ovarian stimulation pre-treatment); fertility referral must occur before first cycle (eviQ).

options.text: Commence BEACOPP chemotherapy as first-line for B symptoms instead of ABVD
options.rationale: ABVD is standard first-line in Australia for classical Hodgkin lymphoma; BEACOPP is reserved for advanced-stage disease or relapse, with greater gonadotoxicity (eviQ; HSANZ).

options.text: Proceed directly to involved-site radiotherapy as monotherapy without chemotherapy
options.rationale: Combined modality (chemotherapy plus radiotherapy in selected cases) is standard; radiotherapy monotherapy is not used for HL with B symptoms (eviQ).

options.text: Repeat excisional lymph node biopsy to confirm the immunohistochemistry findings before treatment
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established with CD30 positive CD15 positive CD20 negative Reed-Sternberg cells; repeat biopsy is not required and delays treatment (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma rests on Reed-Sternberg cells with the CD30+ CD15+ CD20- immunophenotype in a mixed inflammatory background; clinical features (alcohol-induced pain, contiguous spread) are supportive but not diagnostic (BSH Hodgkin; eviQ).
key_points: Reed-Sternberg = Hodgkin (classical)
CD30+ CD15+ CD20- in classical Hodgkin
B symptoms not discriminating between HL and NHL
Stage by Ann Arbor; A/B for absence/presence of B symptoms
explanation.pearls: Bimodal age distribution (15-35 and >50) is characteristic of Hodgkin.

stem: A 70-year-old man is being evaluated for symptomatic multiple myeloma. He has back pain (mid-thoracic, worse at night), 4 kg unintentional weight loss, two recent pneumonias, T6 spinal tenderness without neurological deficit. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28; baseline 78), corrected calcium 2.78 mmol/L (RR 2.10 to 2.60), albumin 28, IgG kappa paraprotein 42 g/L, kappa free light chain 320 mg/L (ratio 28), urinary Bence Jones protein positive. CT shows multiple lytic lesions and a T6 compression fracture. Bone marrow biopsy: 45 percent clonal plasma cells.

data_table.Examination: T6 spinal tenderness, no neurological deficit, no peripheral neuropathy.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28), corrected Ca 2.78, albumin 28, IgG kappa paraprotein 42 g/L, FLC kappa 320 mg/L (ratio 28), urinary BJP positive, lytic lesions on CT, compression fx at T6.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of next steps in management?

options.text: Urgent MRI whole spine for cord compression risk, IV hydration with isotonic crystalloid, IV zoledronic acid (dose-adjusted for renal function), and refer to haematology to commence bortezomib-based induction
options.rationale: He meets CRAB criteria with active myeloma. The T6 compression fracture in painful spine mandates urgent MRI to exclude cord compression. Hypercalcaemia requires IV hydration and bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid is the standard, with dose adjustment at eGFR 28 per AMH); bortezomib-based induction (e.g. VTd or VRd) is preferred over lenalidomide-based regimens in significant renal impairment (eviQ Myeloma; HSANZ; eTG).
source_refs: HSANZ position statement on multiple myeloma

options.text: Refer for bone marrow biopsy and skeletal survey before commencing any treatment
options.rationale: Bone marrow (45 percent plasma cells) and CT skeletal imaging are already done; further delay risks cord compression and worsening renal failure (eviQ).

options.text: Commence high-dose dexamethasone 40 mg daily and discharge on outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is inadequate induction and discharge with painful compression fracture and severe AKI is unsafe; multidisciplinary inpatient management is required (eviQ).

options.text: Commence high-dose lenalidomide-based induction immediately without renal dose adjustment
options.rationale: Lenalidomide requires substantial dose reduction at eGFR under 30 and bortezomib-based regimens are preferred in significant renal impairment due to faster reduction of paraprotein and better renal recovery (eviQ; AMH).

options.text: Commence emergency haemodialysis to clear paraprotein and defer chemotherapy until renal function improves
options.rationale: Dialysis does not effectively clear intact paraprotein; renal recovery in myeloma is driven by reducing light chain production via systemic therapy (bortezomib-based), with dialysis reserved for life-threatening uraemia or fluid overload (eviQ).

explanation.summary: CRAB criteria define active myeloma: hyperCalcaemia >2.75, Renal impairment (Cr >177 or CrCl <40), Anaemia (Hb <100), Bone lesions. The 2014 update added SLiM criteria: clonal plasma cells >=60%, FLC ratio >=100, >1 focal lesion on MRI. Paraprotein size separates MGUS from smouldering myeloma but is not CRAB (IMWG).
key_points: CRAB = Calcium, Renal, Anaemia, Bone
SLiM = >=60% plasma cells, FLC ratio >=100, >1 MRI focal lesion
MGUS = paraprotein <30, no CRAB, plasma cells <10%
Smouldering = paraprotein >=30 OR plasma cells 10-60%, no CRAB
explanation.pearls: Back pain + anaemia + raised Ca + raised creatinine in an older adult = myeloma until proven otherwise.

sources.label: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma
HSANZ position statement on multiple myeloma

stem: A 38-year-old man is admitted with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma with extensive abdominal disease, bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bone marrow involvement. He is haemodynamically stable. Pre-chemotherapy bloods show urate 0.62 mmol/L (RR 0.20-0.42), potassium 4.6, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, calcium 2.30, creatinine 92 (eGFR 86), LDH 4800 U/L (>10x upper limit), WCC 14 with 8% blasts on film. He has no known G6PD deficiency but has not been formally tested. He is not pregnant. He is to start intensive R-CODOX-M/IVAC chemotherapy within 24 hours. He has no significant cardiac history. He is in a tertiary haematology unit with continuous monitoring. He has good IV access. He has no allopurinol allergy. The haematology team classifies him as high risk for tumour lysis syndrome by Cairo-Bishop criteria. He has no contraindication to IV hydration. The unit has rasburicase available. Renal output is currently 80 mL/hr. He weighs 75 kg.

data_table.Examination: Bulky abdominal mass, otherwise well.
data_table.Investigations: Urate 0.62 mmol/L, K 4.6, phosphate 1.6, corrected Ca 2.30, creatinine 92, LDH 4800, WCC 14 with 8% blasts.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylactic strategy for tumour lysis syndrome in this high-risk patient?

options.text: Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV daily plus IV hydration 3 L/m^2/day with close electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: High-risk TLS (Burkitt, raised baseline urate, LDH >10x ULN) is managed with rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) which actively breaks down existing urate, generous IV hydration to maintain urine output >100 mL/m^2/hr and 6-8 hourly electrolyte monitoring during the first 72 hours. Screen for G6PD deficiency first - rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (BSH TLS; eviQ).

options.text: Allopurinol 300 mg daily and IV fluid hydration 2 L/m^2/day, without any rasburicase use
options.rationale: Allopurinol blocks xanthine oxidase to prevent NEW urate formation but does NOT clear existing urate. Adequate for intermediate-risk TLS (lower LDH, less bulky disease). The anti-pattern is using allopurinol in true high-risk TLS where pre-existing urate is already very high. Allopurinol is correct for intermediate-risk only (Cairo-Bishop; BSH TLS Guideline; eviQ).

options.text: Urinary alkalinisation with intravenous sodium bicarbonate infusion targeting urine pH above 7.5 consistently
options.rationale: Routine urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended; it increases calcium phosphate precipitation in tubules (which is less soluble at higher pH) and offers no benefit when rasburicase is used. The anti-pattern is using an obsolete strategy from the pre-rasburicase era. Alkalinisation is correct only if rasburicase is unavailable AND allopurinol is being used (BSH TLS).

options.text: Delay chemotherapy by 7 days to allow oral allopurinol to reduce baseline serum urate substantially
options.rationale: Burkitt lymphoma has a doubling time of 24-48 hours; delaying chemotherapy by 7 days for prophylactic urate lowering is inappropriate and risks rapid clinical deterioration. Rasburicase normalises urate within hours, eliminating the need to delay. Delay would be correct only in very low-grade malignancy with TLS risk (essentially never) (eviQ).

options.text: Prophylactic upfront haemodialysis arranged via nephrology prior to commencing the first chemotherapy cycle
options.rationale: Prophylactic dialysis is reserved for established TLS with refractory hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, or oliguria; pre-emptive dialysis is not standard prophylaxis. Dialysis is correct for severe established TLS with AKI requiring renal replacement, not as primary prevention (BSH TLS).

explanation.summary: High-risk TLS (Burkitt, ALL with WCC >100, raised baseline urate, LDH >10x ULN) requires rasburicase plus generous IV hydration plus 6-8 hourly electrolyte monitoring. Screen for G6PD before rasburicase. Allopurinol is for intermediate risk (BSH TLS; eviQ).
key_points: Cairo-Bishop laboratory TLS: 2+ of urate, K, phosphate, Ca derangements
High risk: Burkitt, ALL WCC >100, bulky LDH >10x ULN
Rasburicase: clears existing urate (not allopurinol)
Avoid rasburicase in G6PD deficiency
explanation.pearls: Routine alkalinisation is OUT - increases calcium phosphate crystallisation.

stem: A 54-year-old woman with AML on day 12 of induction chemotherapy presents to the haematology ward with a temperature of 38.5 deg C on two readings 30 minutes apart. She has had mild rigors. She has no localising symptoms. Her central venous catheter exit site is clean. Her oral mucosa shows grade 2 mucositis without obvious infection. Her last chemotherapy was cytarabine plus daunorubicin completed 8 days ago. She has no penicillin allergy. She has been on aciclovir and fluconazole prophylaxis. She has had no recent travel. She is haemodynamically stable. Her current bloods show neutrophil count 0.2 x10^9/L (severe neutropenia), Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 72, lactate 1.4, CRP 78. Blood cultures from the line and a peripheral site are drawn before any antibiotic is given. CXR is clear. Urine dipstick is negative. There is no diarrhoea or cough. She has good IV access. Her MASCC score is calculated as 18 (lower risk, but with mucositis). She has had no antibiotics in the last 90 days. She has no recent C. difficile.

data_table.Examination: Grade 2 oral mucositis, clean CVC exit site, no rash, no focal signs.
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 0.2 x10^9/L, Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 72, lactate 1.4, CRP 78, CXR clear, blood cultures sent.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate plus ciprofloxacin home outpatient regimen
options.rationale: Outpatient oral regimens are restricted to selected low-risk MASCC >=21 patients without mucositis or profound/prolonged neutropenia and with reliable follow-up. This patient with grade 2 mucositis and neutrophils 0.2 is not eligible. Outpatient oral therapy is correct for MASCC >=21 with no mucositis and stable observation arrangements (eTG; eviQ).

options.text: Meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours as first-line
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line in persistent fever after 72-96 hours on first-line, septic shock, or known ESBL/AmpC carriage - not first-line empirical therapy. The anti-pattern is broad-spectrum-first stewardship failure. Meropenem is correct after first-line failure, septic shock, or documented resistant organism (eTG).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus caspofungin from the outset
options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin is not added routinely from the outset; reserved for suspected line infection, skin/soft tissue infection, haemodynamic instability, or MRSA colonisation. Empirical antifungal is added if fever persists beyond 72-96 hours despite broad antibacterials. Triple therapy from outset is over-treatment with toxicity. This combination is correct only in septic shock with multiple risk features (eTG; eviQ).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone lacks anti-pseudomonal cover and is inadequate empirical therapy in high-risk neutropenic sepsis. The anti-pattern is using community-acquired-pneumonia drugs in neutropenic sepsis. Ceftriaxone is correct in non-neutropenic CAP, not febrile neutropenia (eTG).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours after cultures
options.rationale: First-line empirical therapy for high-risk febrile neutropenia in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours (or cefepime); broad gram-negative cover including Pseudomonas. Cultures drawn first. Add vancomycin only if line infection, skin/soft tissue infection or haemodynamic instability suspected (eTG Antibiotic; eviQ).

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for high-risk febrile neutropenia in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours (or cefepime if pip-tazo unavailable) after cultures. Antifungal added if fever persists beyond 72-96 hours. Vancomycin only when line/SSTI/instability suspected (eTG; eviQ).
key_points: Define: T >=38.5 once or >=38.0 over 1 hour with neutrophils <0.5
First-line: pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h (or cefepime)
Escalate to meropenem if persistent fever + shock
Add antifungal if fever persists 72-96 h
explanation.pearls: Always take cultures FIRST but never delay antibiotics beyond 60 minutes from recognition.

stem: A 64-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (PSA 480 ng/mL, known bony metastases) on androgen deprivation therapy presents to ED with 3 days of progressive mid-thoracic back pain that is worse at night and on coughing, and 12 hours of bilateral lower limb weakness. He noticed difficulty initiating micturition this morning. He has had 4 kg weight loss recently. He has no fevers and no constitutional features of infection. On examination he has marked tenderness over the T7-T8 spinous processes. Power is 3/5 bilaterally in hip flexion and knee extension, with brisk knee jerks, upgoing plantars and a sensory level at approximately T8. There is no saddle anaesthesia tested. The post-void bladder scan shows 580 mL retained urine. He is haemodynamically stable. He has not received recent radiotherapy. He has had no falls. He has no anticoagulation. He has no penicillin allergy. He is otherwise mentating normally. He arrived in ED 90 minutes ago. The on-call oncology team is contactable. He has good IV access. He has no contraindication to dexamethasone.

data_table.Examination: T7-T8 spinal tenderness, bilateral lower limb weakness 3/5, brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars, T8 sensory level, urinary retention 580 mL.
data_table.Investigations: PSA 480, FBC normal, U&Es normal, alk phos 320 U/L, calcium 2.45, INR 1.0.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Plain spine X-rays in the emergency department and outpatient MRI booked within 1 week
options.rationale: Plain X-rays miss up to 20% of MSCC (especially intramedullary or epidural disease without bony erosion) and outpatient MRI delays definitive treatment; any delay risks permanent paraplegia.
