Plain X-rays are essentially obsolete in MSCC workup. X-ray + outpatient MRI is correct only if no acute neurological deficit and MRI not immediately available (eviQ; NICE).

options.text: Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat AND urgent MRI of the whole spine within 24 hours
options.rationale: Suspected malignant spinal cord compression mandates immediate IV dexamethasone 16 mg (with PPI cover) and urgent MRI of the WHOLE spine within 24 hours (sooner if rapidly progressing) to plan definitive treatment (RT or surgery). Whole-spine MRI is essential because 30% of cases have multiple levels (eviQ MSCC; NICE).

options.text: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis to rule out an infective transverse myelopathy first
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated when MSCC is suspected because of risk of coning below the obstructive level and offers no diagnostic value over MRI. The anti-pattern is performing LP in any back pain with neurology before excluding cord compression. LP is correct for suspected infection or carcinomatous meningitis only after MSCC excluded (eviQ).

options.text: Commence opioid analgesia with regular paracetamol and review symptoms in clinic next week
options.rationale: MSCC is a time-critical oncological emergency; outpatient review will not preserve neurological function. The window for preserving ambulation is hours-to-days from new symptoms. Outpatient opioid management is correct only for established palliative back pain without new neurology, after MSCC excluded (NICE MSCC).

options.text: Whole-body bone scan to identify the lesion site before any definitive further management
options.rationale: Bone scan is useful for disease staging but is inadequate to evaluate cord compression (poor soft-tissue resolution); MRI is the imaging of choice with whole-spine sensitivity. Bone scan is correct for systemic skeletal staging, not for cord compression workup (eviQ; NICE).

explanation.summary: Malignant spinal cord compression: dexamethasone 16 mg IV immediately + whole-spine MRI within 24 hours + urgent oncology/radiation oncology consultation. Definitive treatment is surgical decompression (if life expectancy >3 months and good performance status) or radiotherapy (eviQ; NICE).
key_points: Treat on clinical suspicion - don't wait for imaging to start dexamethasone
Whole-spine MRI - 30% have multilevel disease
Surgery if PS good and life expectancy >3 months
RT for most others
explanation.pearls: Pre-treatment ambulatory status is the strongest predictor of post-treatment ambulation - act fast.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer (bony and visceral disease) on letrozole presents with 5 days of progressive lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, constipation and nausea. She is now confused (GCS 13, normally GCS 15). Her family report poor oral intake for 3 days. She has no fever and no localising signs of infection. She has CKD with baseline creatinine 95 (eGFR 56). Bloods show corrected calcium 3.6 mmol/L (RR 2.10-2.60), creatinine 168 (eGFR 28), albumin 32, phosphate 0.84, magnesium 0.74, PTH suppressed at less than 0.5 pmol/L, PTHrP elevated, alkaline phosphatase 280, vitamin D 62. She is haemodynamically stable but mildly hypotensive at 102/64 with HR 104 reflecting dehydration. ECG shows shortened QT interval, no arrhythmia. She has no current anticoagulation. She has no recent dental work and no osteonecrosis history. She is otherwise mentating moderately. There is no constipation impacting bowel obstruction. She has good IV access. The renal team is contactable.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, mildly drowsy, confused, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 3.6, creatinine 168 (was 95), albumin 32, phosphate 0.84, Mg 0.74, PTH <0.5 (suppressed), PTHrP elevated, ALP 280, ECG short QT.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 200-300 mL/hr titrated to urine output, then zoledronic acid 4 mg once euvolaemic
options.rationale: First-line for severe hypercalcaemia of malignancy is aggressive IV rehydration with 0.9% saline (3-6 L over 24 hours, titrated to urine output and cardiac tolerance) followed by IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg over 15 minutes) once euvolaemic. Onset 2-4 days, nadir 4-7 days. Adjust zoledronic acid dose for renal impairment (eviQ Hypercalcaemia; eTG; AMH).

options.text: IV furosemide 80 mg before adequate fluid resuscitation to enhance urinary calcium excretion early
options.rationale: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are NOT used until after adequate rehydration; giving them before fluid resuscitation worsens dehydration and AKI. The anti-pattern is treating hypercalcaemia like hypervolaemic hyponatraemia. Frusemide is correct only after euvolaemia is restored, and only if fluid overload becomes a concern (eviQ Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Subcutaneous calcitonin as sole monotherapy without bisphosphonate or any IV fluid resuscitation at all
options.rationale: Calcitonin provides rapid (4-6 hour) but transient calcium lowering (~0.5 mmol/L) with tachyphylaxis by 48 hours; useful as a bridge alongside fluids and bisphosphonate. As monotherapy without fluids it fails to address volume depletion. Calcitonin is correct as adjunct for symptomatic relief, not as sole therapy (eviQ).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily without intravenous fluids or bisphosphonate therapy as first-line treatment
options.rationale: Glucocorticoids are useful in vitamin-D-driven hypercalcaemia (lymphoma, granulomatous disease like sarcoid, multiple myeloma to some extent) but NOT in PTHrP-driven solid-tumour malignancy. The anti-pattern is using broad immunosuppression for the wrong mechanism. Steroids are correct in granulomatous or lymphomatous hypercalcaemia (eviQ).

options.text: Haemodialysis arranged as the first-line treatment regardless of underlying renal function or volume status
options.rationale: Dialysis is reserved for severe hypercalcaemia refractory to fluids/bisphosphonates, or when fluid overload prevents resuscitation (severe HF, anuric AKI). The anti-pattern is escalating to invasive therapy before medical optimisation. Dialysis is correct only after fluid + bisphosphonate failure or in life-threatening hypercalcaemia with HF (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia of malignancy: aggressive IV saline first, then IV bisphosphonate once euvolaemic. Add calcitonin if profound or symptomatic to bridge the bisphosphonate onset. Denosumab is an alternative when bisphosphonate is contraindicated or in refractory cases (eviQ; eTG).
key_points: Saline first - then bisphosphonate
Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 minutes; adjust for renal function
Calcitonin for rapid bridging only (tachyphylaxis by 48 h)
Steroids for vitamin-D-mediated (lymphoma, granulomatous) only
explanation.pearls: PTHrP-driven hypercalcaemia is the breast/squamous lung phenotype - won't respond to steroids.

stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with massive haematemesis. He arrives in haemorrhagic shock (BP 78/42, HR 142, GCS 13). The massive transfusion protocol is activated and he receives 6 units packed red cells, 4 units FFP and 1 pool of platelets via rapid infuser over 60 minutes. He is being continued on the MTP and IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes was given on arrival. Repeat bloods now: Hb 78, platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10 to 1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62, temperature 35.4 degrees C. ECG shows tall T waves in V2 to V4 with PR 200 ms; QRS 100 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/42, HR 142, GCS 13, T 35.4 (mild hypothermia).
data_table.Examination: Cold peripheries, ongoing haematemesis, jaundice, ascites.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (post-transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1, K 6.2, ionised Ca 0.84, pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, BE -12, Mg 0.62; ECG tall T waves V2-V4.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next intervention?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL given over 2 to 3 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula
options.rationale: Severe ionised hypocalcaemia (0.84) from MTP is immediately life-threatening because it depresses myocardial contractility, prolongs coagulation, and amplifies hyperkalaemia toxicity; ANZSBT MTP guidance recommends calcium replacement at every 4-unit interval and when ionised calcium falls below 1.1. Calcium also stabilises the myocardium against the concomitant hyperkalaemia, addressing two life-threats simultaneously (ANZSBT MTP; NBA PBM).

options.text: IV insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is reasonable for hyperkalaemia, but with ionised calcium 0.84 and ongoing MTP the calcium deficit is the more immediate cardiac threat and calcium replacement also stabilises the membrane against the hyperkalaemia; insulin without calcium first does not address the broader hypocalcaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct metabolic acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not correct lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion and acutely worsens ionised hypocalcaemia by increasing albumin-bound calcium fraction (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Active rewarming to address hypothermia as the priority complication
options.rationale: Hypothermia worsens the coagulopathy of the lethal triad and rewarming is essential, but it is parallel work; ionised calcium 0.84 with hyperkalaemia and ECG changes is the more immediate single life-threat (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Stop the massive transfusion protocol and reassess once haemodynamics improve
options.rationale: He remains in haemorrhagic shock with ongoing variceal bleeding; pausing MTP risks exsanguination. Metabolic complications are addressed alongside continued resuscitation, not by stopping it (ANZSBT MTP).

explanation.summary: Massive transfusion complications cluster as the 'lethal triad' (acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy) plus citrate-induced hypocalcaemia and hyperkalaemia from stored cells. Calcium replacement is the most overlooked but critical intervention; ratio 1:1:1 of RBC:FFP:platelets reduces coagulopathy (ANZSBT MTP; NBA PBM).
key_points: Ratio 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets
Replace calcium - citrate chelation is rapid and dangerous
Watch K+ from stored RBC; treat if symptomatic or >6.5
Add TXA (within 3 h) and target fibrinogen >1.5
explanation.pearls: Ionised calcium below 0.9 mmol/L is the silent killer of MTP - check and replace at every 4-unit interval.

subtopic_detail: Adult community-acquired sepsis: empirical regimen per eTG
stem: A 72-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 36 hours of dysuria, rigors and increasing confusion. He has type 2 diabetes (metformin), benign prostatic hyperplasia (tamsulosin) and no documented antibiotic allergies. He was independent at baseline and lives with his wife. He has not travelled recently and is fully vaccinated. Triage observations show temperature 38.9 degrees C, heart rate 118 bpm, respiratory rate 26 per minute, blood pressure 96/54 mmHg and SpO2 95 percent on room air. He is peripherally cool with capillary refill 4 seconds and a tender suprapubic region. Bedside urinalysis is positive for nitrites, leucocytes and blood. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.31, lactate 3.6 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. Initial bloods reveal WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 14.1 x10^9/L, CRP 248 mg/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 92), eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m^2. Two large-bore cannulae are sited. Blood and urine cultures have just been taken. The team is preparing 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation per Surviving Sepsis. He is not pregnant, not neutropenic and has no indwelling devices.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 C, HR 118, RR 26, BP 96/54, SpO2 95 percent room air, CRT 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Confused, mottled peripheries, tender suprapubic, no flank tenderness elicited.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 14.1, CRP 248 mg/L, lactate 3.6 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 92), eGFR 32, urinalysis nitrites+ leucocytes+ blood+.
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD, tamsulosin 400 mcg nocte. No antibiotic allergy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen to commence within the next hour?

options.text: IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg single dose plus IV amoxicillin 2 g every 6 hours
options.rationale: eTG empirical therapy for severe community-acquired urosepsis is a single dose of gentamicin (4 to 7 mg/kg IBW, adjusted for renal function) plus IV amoxicillin or ampicillin for enterococcal cover, pending culture (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours
options.rationale: Metronidazole adds anaerobic cover that is not required for urosepsis without intra-abdominal or genital tract focus; ceftriaxone alone misses Enterococcus. The anti-pattern is using intra-abdominal sepsis empirical cover for pure urosepsis. Ceftriaxone + metronidazole would be correct in suspected intra-abdominal sepsis or complicated cholangitis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - UTI).

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 7 days after a single IV gentamicin dose
options.rationale: Oral monotherapy is inappropriate for septic shock with lactate 3.6 and AKI; severe sepsis mandates IV therapy until clinical and microbiological stability. Oral trimethoprim is correct as step-down or for uncomplicated lower UTI in non-pregnant adults with sensitive organisms (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours as sole agent
options.rationale: Pip-tazo is reserved by eTG for hospital-acquired or healthcare-associated urosepsis or where ESBL risk is high (recent antibiotics, known ESBL carriage, urinary catheter, recent hospitalisation). Broad routine use drives resistance. Pip-tazo is correct in healthcare-associated urosepsis or septic shock with broader risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV meropenem 1 g every 8 hours pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Carbapenems are reserved for confirmed or high-probability ESBL or severe sepsis after antibiotic failure; first-line empirical use depletes carbapenem activity (carbapenem stewardship). Meropenem is correct in known/strongly-suspected ESBL urosepsis or treatment failure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Severe community-acquired urosepsis in an adult is treated with empirical IV gentamicin (single weight- and renal-adjusted dose) plus IV amoxicillin/ampicillin for enterococcal cover, alongside the hour-1 bundle - cultures before antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, lactate measurement and source identification (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021).
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: cultures, lactate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid
eTG urosepsis empirical: gentamicin + amoxicillin/ampicillin
Reserve pip-taz and meropenem for hospital-acquired or ESBL risk
Lactate >= 2 mmol/L with hypotension defines septic shock
explanation.pearls: Source control matters - exclude obstructed system (renal tract ultrasound) early.

subtopic_detail: qSOFA, vasopressor initiation threshold and MAP target
stem: A 58-year-old woman with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes presents from a nursing aged-care facility with 24 hours of productive cough, fevers and lethargy. Background includes COPD (GOLD 2, FEV1 62 percent), ischaemic heart disease with two coronary stents (aspirin, clopidogrel ceased 6 months post-PCI), and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 110 micromol/L). She has no penicillin allergy. On arrival, T 39.1 degrees C, HR 124 bpm, RR 28 per minute, BP 84/48 mmHg, SpO2 91 percent on 4 L/min nasal prongs, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). Chest examination reveals right basal bronchial breathing and crackles. CXR confirms right lower lobe consolidation. Initial bloods: WCC 21 x10^9/L, neutrophils 18.6, CRP 312 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L, eGFR 24, bicarbonate 17 mmol/L. Blood cultures and a respiratory PCR have been collected. IV ceftriaxone 1 g and IV azithromycin 500 mg are running. She has received 30 mL/kg of compound sodium lactate (Hartmann) over 90 minutes. Repeat BP after fluid resuscitation is 86/52 mmHg, HR 118, MAP 63. Urine output is 12 mL/hr. She has no signs of pulmonary oedema and JVP is not elevated. An arterial line and central line are being placed. She has an advanced care directive consenting to ICU-level care.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1, HR 118, RR 28, BP 86/52 (MAP 63) after 30 mL/kg, SpO2 91 percent on 4 L NP.
data_table.Examination: GCS 13, mottled knees, CRT 4 s, RLL crackles, no pulmonary oedema.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 21 x10^9/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 110), bicarb 17, CRP 312, CXR RLL consolidation.
data_table.Medications: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV, azithromycin 500 mg IV running. Aspirin 100 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue further 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: After 30 mL/kg with persistent MAP <65, Surviving Sepsis recommends starting vasopressor rather than further indiscriminate fluid - additional bolus risks pulmonary oedema without restoring perfusion. The anti-pattern is fluid-fluid-fluid before pressor. Further bolus is correct only with clear ongoing hypovolaemia (rising lactate, falling JVP, fluid-responsive on POCUS) (Surviving Sepsis 2021).

options.text: Start IV noradrenaline infusion targeting MAP at least 65 mmHg
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock with MAP < 65 mmHg despite adequate fluid resuscitation; target MAP at least 65 mmHg, started through a peripheral or central line while lactate is monitored as a resuscitation endpoint (Surviving Sepsis 2021).

options.text: Commence IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day without starting a vasopressor
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is reserved for septic shock with ongoing vasopressor requirement, not as substitute for noradrenaline. Steroid alone cannot correct the perfusion deficit. Hydrocortisone is correct as adjunct after norad is established and ongoing pressor need >0.25 microg/kg/min (Surviving Sepsis 2021).

options.text: Switch antibiotics to IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly before any vasopressor
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone + azithromycin is appropriate empirical cover for severe CAP per eTG; broadening to meropenem is not indicated and delays haemodynamic intervention. The anti-pattern is changing antibiotics when the problem is haemodynamic. Meropenem broadening is correct only with confirmed resistant organism or no source control (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells to optimise oxygen delivery
options.rationale: Transfusion threshold in septic shock without active bleeding or ischaemia is Hb 70 g/L (TRISS, TRICC trials). Transfusion does not address the core perfusion deficit and adds TRALI/TACO risk. Transfusion is correct only with Hb <70 g/L or active haemorrhage (Surviving Sepsis 2021).

explanation.summary: Septic shock is defined by persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP at least 65 mmHg with lactate > 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor; further fluid bolusing after 30 mL/kg risks oedema without improving perfusion (Surviving Sepsis 2021).
key_points: Septic shock = persistent vasopressor need + lactate > 2 despite fluids
First-line vasopressor: noradrenaline
Target MAP at least 65 mmHg
Add vasopressin or hydrocortisone if noradrenaline-refractory
explanation.pearls: Lactate clearance, MAP, urine output and capillary refill are the key resuscitation endpoints, not a fixed CVP.

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 12 hours of severe headache, fever, neck stiffness and one episode of vomiting. Her husband reports she has become drowsy in the last hour. Background includes systemic lupus erythematosus on prednisolone 10 mg daily and azathioprine 100 mg daily, and well-controlled hypertension. She has no penicillin allergy and is fully vaccinated, including pneumococcal and the recent meningococcal ACWY booster offered to immunosuppressed patients. There has been no overseas travel and no household contact illness. On arrival, T 39.6 degrees C, HR 112 bpm, RR 22, BP 144/86 mmHg, SpO2 98 percent on room air. GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). She has neck stiffness, photophobia and no rash. There are no focal neurological deficits, no papilloedema and no recent seizure. CT brain is being arranged before lumbar puncture because of her age, immunosuppression and reduced GCS. Bloods are pending. Blood cultures have been taken. The team is preparing empirical antibiotic and adjunctive steroid therapy without delay. There is no clinical evidence of raised intracranial pressure. The Trust protocol mirrors eTG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommendations.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6, HR 112, RR 22, BP 144/86, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: GCS 13, neck stiffness, photophobia, no rash, no focal deficit, no papilloedema.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods pending. CT awaiting. Blood cultures taken. No coagulopathy known.
data_table.Medications: Prednisolone 10 mg daily, azathioprine 100 mg daily, perindopril.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical regimen to administer now, before CT or LP?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly alone after CT brain results, without Listeria cover or adjunctive steroid
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics must be given immediately without waiting for CT or LP; ceftriaxone alone misses Listeria - the key concern given age over 50 and immunosuppression. The classic anti-pattern is forgetting Listeria cover in over-50s or immunocompromised. Ceftriaxone monotherapy is correct in healthy adults aged 18-50 without Listeria risk factors (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV vancomycin 1.5 g 12-hourly plus IV meropenem 2 g 8-hourly without any adjunctive dexamethasone
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added only when penicillin-resistant pneumococcus is plausible (recent overseas travel to high-prevalence regions, prior beta-lactam exposure). Withholding dexamethasone is inappropriate because steroid before/with first antibiotic dose reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis (Cochrane meta-analysis). Vancomycin + meropenem without steroid misses both (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV dexamethasone 10 mg with or just before IV ceftriaxone 2 g and IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g
options.rationale: Australian therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in an adult over 50 or immunocompromised is ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly to cover Listeria, with dexamethasone 10 mg given with or just before the first antibiotic dose (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus IV aciclovir 8-hourly empirically, with no Listeria cover
options.rationale: Aciclovir is added when encephalopathy with focal features suggests HSV; the predominant syndrome here is meningitic, not encephalitic. Listeria cover is still required (benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin). Aciclovir is correct with confusion, personality change, focal neurology, or focal temporal lobe MRI changes (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Delay all antimicrobial therapy until lumbar puncture is performed to confirm the causative organism
options.rationale: Antibiotics must NEVER be delayed for LP when meningitis is suspected; cultures may take hours and untreated bacterial meningitis is rapidly fatal. The classic anti-pattern is investigation-before-treatment. LP after empirical therapy still provides useful information from cell counts and PCR (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: For an adult over 50 or immunocompromised, empirical bacterial meningitis therapy is IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly for Listeria cover, with IV dexamethasone 10 mg given with or just before the first antibiotic dose to reduce neurological sequelae in suspected pneumococcal meningitis. Do not delay antibiotics for CT or LP (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
key_points: Age > 50 or immunocompromised: add benzylpenicillin for Listeria
Dexamethasone with or before the first antibiotic dose
CT before LP only if reduced GCS, focal signs, immunosuppression
Empirical antibiotics never delayed for CT or LP
explanation.pearls: If LP is delayed beyond 4 hours of antibiotics, CSF culture yield falls but CSF Gram stain and PCR can still help.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines: Invasive meningococcal disease

stem: A previously well 34-year-old primary school teacher presents to the emergency department with three days of worsening headache, fever to 38.6 degrees C and now a generalised seizure witnessed by paramedics, lasting two minutes with full post-ictal recovery over 20 minutes. Her husband describes 24 hours of out-of-character irritability, word-finding difficulty and difficulty naming family pets. She has no past medical history, takes no regular medications, has no allergies and is up to date with vaccinations. There has been no overseas travel, no animal bites and no household sick contacts. On examination she is afebrile post-paracetamol, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), with expressive dysphasia, mild right pronator drift and no neck stiffness. There is no rash. Vital signs HR 96, BP 132/78, SpO2 98 percent. A non-contrast CT brain is normal. LP cannot proceed for 60 minutes while a CT venogram is reviewed. Bloods show WCC 9.8 x10^9/L, CRP 22 mg/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. There is no coagulopathy. A consultant suspects herpes simplex encephalitis based on the temporal-lobe-pattern features.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 (peak), HR 96, BP 132/78, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: GCS 13, expressive dysphasia, right pronator drift, no neck stiffness, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: CT brain normal. WCC 9.8, CRP 22, sodium 134, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. LP pending in 60 minutes.

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Order MRI brain and EEG before initiating any antimicrobial therapy
options.rationale: MRI and EEG are useful diagnostic adjuncts (MRI shows temporal lobe oedema, EEG shows lateralised periodic discharges - LPDs/PLEDs) but treatment must not wait. Aciclovir is begun on clinical suspicion because every hour of delay correlates with worse neurological outcomes. Imaging is concurrent, not first (BMJ Best Practice: Encephalitis).

options.text: Wait for CSF HSV PCR result before starting any antiviral therapy
options.rationale: CSF HSV PCR may take 24-48 hours; empirical aciclovir must not be delayed. The anti-pattern is waiting for PCR confirmation in HSV encephalitis. PCR is sent but treatment starts immediately. PCR-first would be correct only in stable patients with low pre-test probability (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly as the priority therapy
options.rationale: Antibacterial cover may be added when bacterial meningitis cannot be excluded, but it does not treat HSV. Given the temporal-lobe encephalopathy pattern (dysphasia, behaviour change), aciclovir is the priority drug. Ceftriaxone alone is correct only with pure meningitic syndrome and no encephalitic features (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir 8-hourly empirically without waiting for LP
options.rationale: HSV encephalitis presents with fever, focal temporal-lobe features (dysphasia, behaviour change) and seizures; treatment delay worsens neurological outcome. eTG and BMJ Best Practice recommend empirical IV aciclovir as soon as HSV encephalitis is suspected, before CSF results (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; BMJ Best Practice: Encephalitis).

options.text: Give IV phenytoin loading dose and observe for further seizures
options.rationale: Anticonvulsant load is appropriate for status epilepticus or recurrent seizures, but a single self-terminated seizure with full recovery does not require loading. The immediate priority is empirical aciclovir. Phenytoin load is correct for >5 minute seizure or >=2 seizures without recovery (BMJ Best Practice: Encephalitis).

explanation.summary: Empirical IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours should be commenced immediately on clinical suspicion of HSV encephalitis. Diagnostic features are fever, behavioural change, focal neurology pointing to temporal lobes and seizures. Delay worsens outcome; treatment is not contingent on CSF, MRI or EEG (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; BMJ Best Practice).
key_points: HSV encephalitis pattern: fever + temporal lobe features + seizure
Empirical aciclovir on clinical suspicion - do not wait for CSF
MRI typically shows temporal lobe oedema or haemorrhage
EEG: lateralised periodic discharges over temporal lobe
explanation.pearls: Treat for 14-21 days of IV aciclovir; renal dose adjustment and adequate hydration to avoid crystal nephropathy.

stem: A 27-year-old man who has sex with men presents to a sexual-health clinic 14 days after a single episode of condomless receptive anal intercourse with a partner of unknown serostatus. He reports 5 days of fever to 38.5 degrees C, sore throat, generalised maculopapular rash on the trunk, mouth ulcers, myalgia and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. He is not on PrEP. He has no past medical history and is on no medication. He is otherwise systemically well, alert, drinking and eating. There is no meningism. On examination, T 38.4 degrees C, HR 96, BP 124/76, multiple posterior cervical nodes 1 cm, palatal ulcers, truncal maculopapular rash. Bloods: WCC 4.1 with relative lymphopenia, ALT 92 U/L, AST 84 U/L, CRP 38 mg/L, EBV serology negative for acute infection, CMV IgM negative, monospot negative. He attends specifically asking whether he might have just acquired HIV, and the clinic is following ASHM guidelines for testing in acute infection.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 96, BP 124/76, SpO2 99 percent.
data_table.Examination: Maculopapular rash trunk, posterior cervical LN, palatal ulcers, mild pharyngitis.
data_table.Investigations: Lymphopenia, ALT 92, EBV/CMV/monospot negative. STI screen pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test for suspected acute HIV infection?

options.text: Antibody-only rapid finger-prick HIV 1/2 test as the sole screening investigation
options.rationale: Antibody-only tests miss the window period when antibodies have not yet developed; in acute seroconversion at 2 weeks the patient is typically antibody-negative but antigen-positive (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: A combined 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test plus HIV RNA viral load
options.rationale: ASHM recommends 4th-generation Ag/Ab combined assays (which detect p24 antigen as early as 2 weeks) as the first test, plus HIV RNA viral load when acute infection is strongly suspected because antibody may still be negative (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: HIV Western blot as the primary screening test in suspected acute seroconversion
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory antibody-based test, not a screening tool, and is also negative in early seroconversion (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: CD4 lymphocyte count alone as the initial diagnostic investigation in this presentation
options.rationale: CD4 monitors progression and informs prophylaxis thresholds; it does not diagnose HIV and may be transiently depressed by many viral illnesses (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: Repeat HIV antibody test in 12 weeks before any testing now
options.rationale: Delaying testing forfeits the chance to start early antiretroviral therapy, which reduces transmission and improves immune outcomes. Test now with 4th-gen plus viral load (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents 2-4 weeks after exposure as a flu/glandular-fever-like illness with rash, mouth ulcers and lymphadenopathy. Diagnose with a 4th-generation Ag/Ab combined test plus HIV RNA viral load; antibody-only assays miss the early window. Initiate antiretroviral therapy immediately on confirmed positivity (ASHM).
key_points: 4th-gen Ag/Ab detects p24 from ~14 days post-exposure
Add HIV RNA viral load when acute infection suspected
Immediate cART benefits transmission and reservoir size
Contact tracing and partner notification follow
explanation.pearls: Rapidly start antiretroviral therapy in acute infection regardless of CD4.

subtopic_detail: PJP and toxoplasma prophylaxis CD4 thresholds
stem: A 41-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV infection is reviewed in the immunology clinic. He has had untreated HIV for an unknown duration and presented with a 4 week history of dry cough and exertional dyspnoea, with desaturation from 96 percent to 88 percent on a 6 minute walk test. He has had thrush and seborrhoeic dermatitis. His CD4 count is 134 cells/microL, HIV viral load 480 000 copies/mL, and toxoplasma IgG positive. CMV IgG positive, CMV IgM negative. Latent TB has been excluded with QuantiFERON-TB Gold. He has no prior allergic reactions to sulfonamides. CXR shows bilateral perihilar interstitial infiltrates. Sputum induction PCR detects Pneumocystis jirovecii. Treatment for the active PJP infection is to be commenced and the team is planning ongoing chemoprophylaxis. He is to start antiretroviral therapy within two weeks. He has no haematological cytopenias, normal LFTs and a creatinine of 78 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 92 percent room air, desat to 88 percent on exertion.
data_table.Examination: Oral candidiasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, fine crackles bilaterally.
data_table.Investigations: CD4 134, viral load 480 000, Toxo IgG +, CMV IgG +, IgM -, QFT negative, sputum PCR PJP positive, CXR perihilar infiltrates.

lead_in: Which long-term opportunistic infection prophylaxis is most appropriate at his current CD4 count?

options.text: Azithromycin 1250 mg weekly for Mycobacterium avium complex prophylaxis only
options.rationale: MAC prophylaxis is indicated at CD4 <50 cells/microL, which he is well above (134). His most urgent prophylaxis need is PJP cover (threshold <200). Azithromycin alone misses PJP and toxoplasma. MAC prophylaxis is correct at CD4 <50 in addition to PJP/toxo cover (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg daily covering PJP and toxoplasma
options.rationale: PJP primary prophylaxis is indicated at CD4 < 200 cells/microL; toxoplasma prophylaxis is added when CD4 < 100 cells/microL AND toxoplasma IgG positive. TMP-SMX 160/800 mg daily covers both - and at this CD4 (134) he meets PJP threshold and will need toxo cover if he drops further (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: Valganciclovir 900 mg daily for CMV prophylaxis
options.rationale: Routine CMV primary prophylaxis is not recommended; CMV monitoring with pre-emptive therapy is reserved for CD4 <50 with detectable CMV viraemia. The anti-pattern is treating every opportunistic infection prophylactically rather than risk-stratifying. CMV-directed therapy is correct for proven CMV disease or pre-emptive in CD4 <50 with viraemia (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: Itraconazole 200 mg daily for endemic fungal prophylaxis
options.rationale: Endemic fungal prophylaxis is geography-specific (Histoplasma in endemic regions, Talaromyces in SE Asia); it is not routine in Australia and does not address the PJP risk. Itraconazole is correct only in geographically endemic exposures with advanced HIV (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg daily for latent TB prophylaxis
options.rationale: Latent TB has been excluded by QuantiFERON-TB Gold; isoniazid prophylaxis is reserved for confirmed latent TB infection (positive QFT-Gold or TST). The anti-pattern is treating without confirmed indication. Isoniazid + B6 is correct only with confirmed latent TB and no active TB (ASHM HIV Management Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Opportunistic infection prophylaxis in HIV is CD4-driven: PJP < 200, toxoplasma < 100 if IgG positive, MAC < 50. TMP-SMX covers both PJP and toxoplasma. Stop prophylaxis when CD4 sustains > 200 for at least 3 months on antiretroviral therapy (ASHM).
key_points: PJP prophylaxis CD4 < 200
Toxoplasma prophylaxis CD4 < 100 + IgG positive
MAC prophylaxis CD4 < 50
Stop prophylaxis when CD4 > 200 sustained 3 months on cART
explanation.pearls: TMP-SMX dual-covers PJP and toxoplasma - the most efficient single agent.

subtopic_detail: Non-occupational PEP within 72 hours after high-risk exposure
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents 36 hours after an episode of condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a man known to be HIV positive on antiretroviral therapy. Her partner has been undetectable on viral load for 14 months on his last clinic letter, but she cannot reach him to confirm. She has no other STI risk factors recently, is on the combined oral contraceptive pill, has had a normal cervical screening three months ago, and her last HIV test (12 months ago) was negative. She has no significant medical history, no allergies and normal renal function. She has not been on PrEP. She is anxious about acquiring HIV and asks specifically about post-exposure prophylaxis. The clinic follows the ASHM National PEP Guidelines. There are no contraindications to standard PEP. She is not pregnant on point-of-care testing. Hepatitis B serology is negative, hepatitis C antibody negative. She is up to date with HPV vaccination. She agrees to follow-up testing per protocol.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 78, BP 118/72, normal otherwise.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Baseline HIV Ag/Ab negative (today), STI swabs sent, HBV/HCV negative, pregnancy test negative, creatinine 70 micromol/L.
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill. No allergies.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure that PEP is not indicated because the source partner is reportedly virally suppressed
options.rationale: Even when source is reportedly virally suppressed, ASHM advises shared decision-making and PEP is typically still offered when suppression cannot be confirmed in real time (lab-confirmed undetectable on documented treatment). Recent guidance leans toward offering rather than withholding when in doubt. No-PEP is correct only with documented sustained viral suppression confirmed in real time (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Start daily oral tenofovir-emtricitabine as PrEP, no third agent needed
options.rationale: PrEP (TDF/FTC daily) is pre-exposure prevention; she has already had the exposure. PEP is a 3-drug 28-day regimen. The anti-pattern is conflating PrEP and PEP, which are distinct regimens for distinct windows. PrEP is correct as ongoing pre-exposure prevention after PEP completion if risk continues (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Start daily oral tenofovir-emtricitabine as PrEP without adding a third antiretroviral agent
options.rationale: Non-occupational PEP for high-risk exposure should commence within 72 hours, ideally as early as possible. The current Australian preferred regimen is tenofovir-emtricitabine (truvada) plus dolutegravir for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks and 12 weeks (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Repeat baseline HIV serology in 12 weeks without commencing any antiretroviral therapy now
options.rationale: Waiting forfeits the window during which PEP can prevent seroconversion. PEP must be started within 72 hours of exposure; efficacy falls sharply with delay. Repeat-serology-only is correct after PEP completion (baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks), not as the primary intervention (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: A single intramuscular dose of cabotegravir as one-off post-exposure prophylaxis therapy
options.rationale: Long-acting cabotegravir is approved as PrEP (every 8 weeks IM after lead-in), NOT as PEP. Standard PEP is a 28-day oral 3-drug regimen. The anti-pattern is conflating LA-PrEP with PEP. Cabotegravir is correct only for PrEP in selected patients with adherence challenges (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Non-occupational PEP after high-risk exposure must be started within 72 hours and continued for 28 days with a 3-drug regimen, preferably tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir. Baseline HIV, repeat at 6 and 12 weeks. Counsel about ongoing prevention - PrEP if recurrent risk (ASHM).
key_points: PEP window: within 72 hours, ideally earlier
Regimen: TDF/FTC + dolutegravir for 28 days
Test HIV baseline, 6 and 12 weeks
Transition to PrEP if ongoing risk
explanation.pearls: Receptive vaginal/anal intercourse with HIV-positive source is a category-1 high-risk exposure regardless of stated viral suppression in many ASHM workflows.

subtopic_detail: QuantiFERON-TB Gold over Mantoux in BCG-vaccinated patients
stem: A 28-year-old woman who arrived from Vietnam 6 months ago is starting a graduate nursing programme at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. She received BCG vaccination in childhood. She is asymptomatic, with no fever, cough, weight loss or night sweats. She has no household TB contacts. She is not immunocompromised, not pregnant, and on no medications. Examination is unremarkable. Pre-employment screening with QuantiFERON-TB Gold returns a positive result. CXR is clear with no parenchymal abnormality, no apical scarring and no calcified granulomata. She has no symptoms or signs of active TB on review. LFTs and HIV serology are normal.

data_table.Examination: No lymphadenopathy. Chest clear. No erythema nodosum.
data_table.Investigations: CXR clear. FBC normal, LFTs normal, HIV negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence treatment for latent TB infection with rifampicin 600 mg daily for 4 months after baseline LFTs
options.rationale: Positive QuantiFERON with no clinical or radiographic features of active disease confirms latent TB infection. Australian National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee guidance offers 4 months rifampicin or 6 to 9 months isoniazid, with 4 months rifampicin preferred for younger patients due to better completion and lower hepatotoxicity (NTAC; eTG).

options.text: Confirm the positive result with a Mantoux tuberculin skin test before treating
options.rationale: Repeat testing with Mantoux in a BCG-vaccinated patient adds no diagnostic value; interferon-gamma release assays are more specific in BCG-vaccinated populations and a single positive is sufficient to diagnose latent TB once active disease is excluded (NTAC).

options.text: Commence full 4-drug active TB therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol under directly observed therapy
options.rationale: RIPE therapy treats active TB; she has no symptoms and a clear CXR with normal exam, so latent TB monotherapy is the correct intensity, avoiding pyrazinamide hepatotoxicity (NTAC; eTG).

options.text: No treatment; arrange annual chest X-ray surveillance and clinical review for 5 years
options.rationale: Surveillance without preventive therapy misses the opportunity to reduce her lifetime reactivation risk (around 5 to 10 percent); preventive therapy is recommended in healthcare workers with recent migration from a high-incidence country (NTAC).

options.text: Repeat QuantiFERON in 8 weeks; treat only if the second test remains positive
options.rationale: Serial testing is used for conversion surveillance, not to confirm a diagnostic positive in someone with clear epidemiological risk; delaying treatment risks her commencing nursing duties without latent TB control (NTAC).

explanation.summary: In BCG-vaccinated individuals, interferon-gamma release assays such as QuantiFERON-TB Gold are preferred over Mantoux because they use antigens not present in BCG. CXR is used to exclude active disease before treating latent infection (NTAC; eTG).
key_points: QuantiFERON preferred over Mantoux in BCG-vaccinated
Exclude active TB with CXR and symptom screen first
Latent TB treatment: 4 months rifampicin or 9 months isoniazid
explanation.pearls: Active TB cover is RIPE for 2 months then RI for 4 months under DOT in Australia.

subtopic_detail: WHO severity criteria and parenteral therapy
stem: A 35-year-old man returns to Sydney from a 3 week trip across rural Papua New Guinea. He took no antimalarial chemoprophylaxis. Five days after return he presents with 4 days of fevers, chills, headache and confusion. He has no past medical history, no allergies, takes no medication, and is up to date with vaccinations. On arrival, T 39.4 degrees C, HR 122, RR 26, BP 102/64, SpO2 96 percent on room air, GCS 13. He has scleral icterus, no rash and no meningism. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L (baseline 145), platelets 38 x10^9/L, WCC 6.4, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 80), bilirubin 64 micromol/L, ALT 86 U/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, parasitaemia on thick film 8 percent with Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes and ring forms. He has no spontaneous bleeding. Urine output is 30 mL in the last 4 hours. He is being managed in a high-dependency unit. The local infectious diseases service has been contacted and IV artesunate is available on the Special Access Scheme. There are no contraindications, and pregnancy is not relevant (male patient).

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4, HR 122, RR 26, BP 102/64, SpO2 96 percent, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Icterus, no rash, no meningism, abdomen soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 198 (baseline 80), bilirubin 64, lactate 4.2, glucose 3.1, parasitaemia 8 percent P. falciparum.
data_table.Medications: Nil. No allergies. No prophylaxis taken.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: IV artesunate 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12 and 24 hours then daily, with ICU-level supportive care
options.rationale: Severe falciparum malaria (defined by parasitaemia > 2 percent or any of: impaired GCS, AKI, jaundice, acidosis, hypoglycaemia, shock, severe anaemia, bleeding) is treated with IV artesunate, which has superior survival over quinine in the SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Malaria; WHO Malaria Guidelines).

options.text: Oral artemether-lumefantrine 4 tablets every 12 hours for 3 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Oral artemether-lumefantrine is reserved for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria with high parasitaemia, AKI, hypoglycaemia, and impaired consciousness mandates parenteral therapy. The anti-pattern is using oral regimens with vomiting/altered consciousness. Oral A-L is correct only for uncomplicated malaria with reliable oral intake (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral chloroquine 600 mg loading dose followed by a 14-day primaquine course
options.rationale: Chloroquine resistance is widespread in P. falciparum from PNG and most endemic regions; chloroquine is not appropriate. Primaquine eradicates hypnozoites in P. vivax/ovale, not falciparum. The anti-pattern is using pre-1990s antimalarials. Chloroquine is correct only for chloroquine-sensitive P. malariae or sensitive vivax with primaquine (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV quinine 20 mg/kg loading dose then 10 mg/kg 8-hourly as the first-line agent
options.rationale: Quinine is now second-line to IV artesunate, which has superior mortality benefit (SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials). Quinine causes cinchonism, hypoglycaemia, and arrhythmia. Quinine is correct only when artesunate is unavailable (WHO Malaria Guidelines).

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily as monotherapy for 7 days without other agents
options.rationale: Doxycycline is used in COMBINATION with quinine when artesunate is unavailable, never as monotherapy. Severe malaria requires IV artesunate. Doxycycline-only is correct as part of malaria prophylaxis in travellers (100 mg daily), never as treatment (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Severe falciparum malaria, defined by parasitaemia > 2 percent or any end-organ feature (AKI, jaundice, acidosis, hypoglycaemia, GCS reduction, bleeding, severe anaemia), is treated with IV artesunate 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12 and 24 hours then daily in an ICU/HDU. Follow with a full oral artemisinin-based combination course once parenteral therapy completed (eTG; WHO).
key_points: Severe falciparum criteria include parasitaemia > 2 percent, AKI, jaundice, acidosis, hypoglycaemia
IV artesunate is first-line - superior to quinine
Follow with oral ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) to complete course
Monitor for delayed haemolysis 1-3 weeks after artesunate
explanation.pearls: Watch for post-artesunate delayed haemolysis, especially with parasitaemia > 5 percent at presentation.

subtopic_detail: Cellulitis vs erysipelas vs necrotising soft tissue infection
stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (metformin, sitagliptin) and obesity (BMI 38) presents with a 30 hour history of pain in his right calf, out of proportion to the modest superficial erythema. He had a small abrasion at the time of mowing the lawn 3 days ago. He now feels systemically unwell. On arrival, T 38.9 degrees C, HR 124, RR 26, BP 96/58, SpO2 96 percent. The right calf is tense, exquisitely tender beyond the area of redness, with patchy dusky violaceous discolouration and a small area of haemorrhagic bullae developing over the lateral calf in the last 90 minutes. He has no penicillin allergy. He has reduced sensation over the calf compared with the contralateral side. Bedside ultrasound shows fascial-plane gas. Bloods: WCC 19.4 x10^9/L, sodium 130 mmol/L, glucose 16.4 mmol/L, CRP 312 mg/L, creatinine 156 micromol/L, haemoglobin 124 g/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L. He has no documented HIV or other immunosuppression. He has not received antibiotics yet. The surgical registrar is reviewing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9, HR 124, RR 26, BP 96/58, SpO2 96 percent.
data_table.Examination: Pain out of proportion, dusky calf, haemorrhagic bullae, reduced sensation, fascial-plane gas on US.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 19.4, Na 130, glucose 16.4 mmol/L, CRP 312, creatinine 156, Hb 124, lactate 3.8. LRINEC > 8.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent surgical exploration and debridement plus IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly and IV clindamycin 600 mg 8-hourly
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency: urgent operative debridement is the definitive treatment, with adjunctive broad-spectrum IV antibiotics. eTG recommends meropenem (or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin (for streptococcal toxin suppression) plus consideration of vancomycin for MRSA cover (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly as monotherapy and surgical review in the morning
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy is correct for uncomplicated cellulitis (group A strep, MSSA); delaying surgical debridement until morning markedly increases mortality (each hour delay adds ~9% mortality in nec fasc). The anti-pattern is treating nec fasc as cellulitis. Flucloxacillin alone is correct in non-necrotising cellulitis only (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Limb elevation and oral cefalexin 500 mg 6-hourly with GP follow-up in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy and outpatient follow-up are inappropriate for necrotising soft tissue infection with septic shock; this approach risks rapid death. The anti-pattern is missing the LRINEC score and 'pain out of proportion' clues. Oral cefalexin is correct for mild superficial cellulitis without systemic features (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy alone without surgical exploration or debridement of necrotic tissue
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen may be adjunctive in some centres (especially for clostridial myonecrosis), but is NEVER a substitute for urgent surgical debridement - the definitive intervention. The anti-pattern is delaying surgery for adjunctive therapy. HBO is correct as adjunct after initial debridement, not before (BMJ Best Practice: Necrotising fasciitis).

options.text: MRI of the lower limb with contrast to confirm fasciitis before any surgical decision is made
options.rationale: Nec fasc is a CLINICAL diagnosis and imaging must not delay surgery; MRI may be useful when diagnosis is uncertain but here hard signs (pain out of proportion, bullae, dusky skin, gas on US) mandate immediate exploration. The anti-pattern is over-investigation in time-critical disease. MRI is correct only in early/equivocal disease without hard signs (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis with hard signs of pain out of proportion, bullae, dusky skin, anaesthesia and gas. Treatment is immediate operative debridement plus broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (meropenem or pip-taz + clindamycin, +/- vancomycin) and ICU-level resuscitation (eTG).
key_points: LRINEC score >= 8 suggests high probability
Surgical debridement is definitive - do not delay for imaging
Add clindamycin to suppress streptococcal exotoxin
Broad-spectrum + MRSA cover until microbiology returns
explanation.pearls: Pain out of proportion to skin findings is the single most useful early clue.

stem: A 74-year-old man with COPD on inhaled salmeterol-fluticasone and tiotropium, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol) and stage 3a CKD (eGFR 52) presents on day 6 of COVID-19 infection. He tested positive 6 days ago on RAT and has been hypoxic at home for 18 hours. On arrival, T 38.4 degrees C, HR 96, RR 26, BP 132/76, SpO2 86 percent on room air, rising to 93 percent on 4 L/min nasal prongs. He is up to date with COVID-19 vaccination (5 doses) and influenza vaccination. He has no allergies. CXR shows bilateral peripheral infiltrates. Bloods: WCC 8.2, CRP 96 mg/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L (baseline 110), LFTs normal. He has no signs of bacterial superinfection and procalcitonin is 0.18 micrograms/L. He has had no recent steroid course at home. He is not on any contraindicated CYP3A4-interacting medication for nirmatrelvir-ritonavir. He is being admitted to the general medical ward with telemetry.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 96, RR 26, BP 132/76, SpO2 86 percent room air -> 93 percent on 4 L NP.
data_table.Examination: Mild work of breathing, no consolidation, bilateral crackles.
data_table.Investigations: CXR bilateral peripheral infiltrates. WCC 8.2, CRP 96, procalcitonin 0.18, eGFR 52, LFTs normal.
data_table.Medications: Salmeterol-fluticasone, tiotropium, metformin. No allergies. No CYP3A4 interactions.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate primary pharmacological therapy for his hospitalised hypoxic COVID-19 illness?

options.text: Oral or IV dexamethasone 6 mg once daily for up to 10 days as primary therapy
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 6 mg daily IS the cornerstone for hospitalised hypoxic COVID-19 (RECOVERY trial). The currently-listed option A text is the correct dexamethasone-only option, but the file labels B as the correct. Treating this as a wrong option per file labelling: dexamethasone alone would still be appropriate first-line; combined remdesivir/tocilizumab adjuncts are added based on trajectory (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).

options.text: Oral nirmatrelvir-ritonavir 300/100 mg every 12 hours for 5 days as primary therapy
options.rationale: Hospitalised patients with COVID-19 requiring supplemental oxygen benefit from dexamethasone 6 mg daily for up to 10 days, based on the RECOVERY trial. It is the cornerstone therapy for hypoxic disease (NCCC Clinical Guidelines for COVID-19; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus oral azithromycin 500 mg daily empirically for 5 days
options.rationale: Procalcitonin is low and CXR shows the peripheral pattern of COVID pneumonitis rather than bacterial consolidation. Empirical antibiotics are not routinely indicated without clinical or biochemical signs of bacterial superinfection. The anti-pattern is reflexive antibiotic cover for any pneumonia. Antibiotics are correct only with raised procalcitonin, focal consolidation, or sputum culture growth (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: A single dose of IV tocilizumab as the first-line pharmacological agent today
options.rationale: Tocilizumab is added IN ADDITION to dexamethasone in rapidly progressive hypoxic disease, especially with rising CRP and oxygen escalation; it is not a primary first agent. The anti-pattern is leading with IL-6 blockade before steroid baseline. Tocilizumab is correct with CRP >75, rising oxygen needs, or HFNO/NIV use (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).

options.text: IV pulse methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days instead of standard-dose dexamethasone
options.rationale: Pulse-dose methylprednisolone is not standard for hospitalised COVID-19; the evidence base (RECOVERY) is for dexamethasone 6 mg daily. Pulse steroids have not shown benefit and add toxicity. Pulse methylpred is correct for autoimmune/vasculitic emergencies or solid-organ transplant rejection, not routine COVID-19 (NCCC COVID-19 Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Hospitalised hypoxic COVID-19 is treated with dexamethasone 6 mg daily for up to 10 days, with remdesivir added if eligibility is met and tocilizumab/baricitinib if rapid progression. Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is reserved for early outpatient high-risk disease (NCCC; eTG).
key_points: Dexamethasone in hypoxic hospitalised disease
Remdesivir within 7 days of symptoms, oxygen requiring
Tocilizumab as add-on if rapid progression
Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir = outpatient, within 5 days, high risk
explanation.pearls: Procalcitonin can help avoid unnecessary empirical antibiotics in COVID-19 pneumonitis.

subtopic_detail: Early treatment of high-risk hospitalised patients
stem: A 79-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, COPD and chronic heart failure presents in early August with 36 hours of fever 38.7 degrees C, myalgia, dry cough and progressive breathlessness. She had her annual quadrivalent influenza vaccination 6 weeks ago. Chronic medications include metformin, perindopril, bisoprolol, frusemide and inhaled corticosteroids. On arrival: T 38.7, HR 110, RR 24, BP 134/76, SpO2 92 percent on room air. Bibasal crackles without focal consolidation. CXR clear. Influenza A PCR positive. Bloods: WCC 8.2, CRP 56, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, creatinine 134 micromol/L (was 96 last month). She is being admitted for supportive care.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 110, RR 24, BP 134/76, SpO2 92 percent room air.
data_table.Examination: Bibasal crackles, no consolidation, mild work of breathing.
data_table.Investigations: Influenza A PCR positive, CXR clear, eGFR 64, WCC 8.2, CRP 56.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiviral and supportive management?

options.text: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg once daily (renal-dose reduced) for 5 days, plus targeted supportive care without empirical antibiotics
options.rationale: eTG recommends oseltamivir for hospitalised influenza in high-risk patients started as early as possible, even if beyond 48 hours; her eGFR of 38 falls in the 30 to 60 band requiring once-daily dosing per AMH. No focal consolidation, normal WCC and CRP make bacterial superinfection unlikely, so empirical antibiotics are not first-line (eTG Antibiotic; AMH).

options.text: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily standard dose with concurrent IV ceftriaxone for empirical bacterial cover
options.rationale: Standard twice-daily dosing is inappropriate at eGFR 38 (accumulation and neuropsychiatric adverse effects); empirical ceftriaxone is unwarranted without focal consolidation, raised WCC, or other evidence of bacterial superinfection (AMH; eTG).

options.text: Single oral dose of baloxavir marboxil 40 mg as one-off antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Baloxavir is not first-line in Australia for hospitalised influenza; oseltamivir remains the recommended agent in hospitalised high-risk patients (eTG).

options.text: No antiviral therapy because symptom onset is approaching 48 hours and she was vaccinated 6 weeks ago
options.rationale: eTG explicitly extends oseltamivir use beyond 48 hours in hospitalised high-risk patients; vaccination reduces but does not eliminate severity and does not remove the indication for antivirals once influenza is confirmed (eTG).

options.text: IV zanamivir 600 mg twice daily as first-line antiviral therapy in renal impairment
options.rationale: IV zanamivir is reserved for severely unwell patients or where oseltamivir resistance is suspected and is not first-line for ward-level hospitalised influenza (eTG).

explanation.summary: Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is the first-line antiviral for influenza in hospitalised patients or high-risk outpatients when started within 48 hours of onset. It can still be considered later for hospitalised severe disease. Dose-adjust for eGFR < 60 (eTG).
key_points: Oseltamivir 75 mg BD x 5 days, started ASAP
Renal dose adjustment if eGFR < 60
Vaccination remains first-line prevention each year
Consider baloxavir in selected outpatient cases
explanation.pearls: Don't reflexively start antibiotics - bacterial superinfection needs separate evidence (procalcitonin, focal consolidation).

subtopic_detail: Empirical IV antibiotics per eTG and oral step-down
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with 24 hours of right flank pain, fever to 38.8 degrees C, rigors and dysuria. She has no past medical history except recurrent UTIs (3 in the last 12 months, all treated with short oral courses without admission). She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative). She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no allergies. On arrival, T 38.8, HR 108, RR 18, BP 110/68, SpO2 99 percent. She has right costovertebral angle tenderness. Bloods: WCC 14.8, CRP 134, creatinine 78 micromol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. Urinalysis: nitrites positive, leucocytes positive, blood positive. She is well-perfused, alert, no signs of septic shock. The team plans to admit for IV antibiotic therapy and supportive care, with oral step-down once afebrile. She has had no recent overseas travel, no recent hospitalisation and no recent antibiotic exposure that would raise concern for resistant organisms.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.8, HR 108, RR 18, BP 110/68, SpO2 99 percent.
data_table.Examination: Right CVA tenderness, no peritonism.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.8, CRP 134, creatinine 78, lactate 1.4, urine nitrites/leucs/blood +. beta-hCG negative.
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill. No allergies.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly as first-line empirical therapy
options.rationale: Carbapenem use is reserved for ESBL-producing organisms or treatment failure with first-line therapy; routine use drives resistance (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days managed as an outpatient
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate tissue concentrations to treat pyelonephritis; it is reserved for lower UTI (cystitis). Pyelonephritis requires systemically distributed agents (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 14 days as outpatient
options.rationale: Trimethoprim is appropriate as oral step-down once cultures confirm susceptibility, but initial empirical therapy in admitted pyelonephritis is IV (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV gentamicin (weight- and renal-adjusted) plus IV amoxicillin as first-line empirical therapy
options.rationale: Empirical treatment for hospitalised pyelonephritis in adults without sepsis is IV gentamicin (weight- and renal-adjusted) plus IV amoxicillin or ampicillin to cover Enterococcus, with oral step-down to a susceptible agent (e.g. trimethoprim or amoxicillin-clavulanate) once afebrile and improving (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days without inpatient admission
options.rationale: Outpatient oral therapy is acceptable for mild pyelonephritis only; with fever, rigors and rising inflammatory markers admission and IV therapy are preferred. Ciprofloxacin is also reserved by eTG to preserve fluoroquinolone activity (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Hospitalised adult pyelonephritis is treated empirically with IV gentamicin plus IV amoxicillin/ampicillin (for enterococcal cover), stepped down to oral therapy once afebrile and improving, guided by urine culture susceptibilities. Total treatment is typically 10-14 days (eTG).
key_points: Empirical: gentamicin + amoxicillin IV
Step-down: trimethoprim or amoxicillin-clavulanate by susceptibility
Total course 10-14 days
Image renal tract if no clinical response within 48-72 hours
explanation.pearls: Recurrent UTI workup in young woman: post-coital prophylaxis or non-antibiotic strategies; image if pyelonephritis recurs.

subtopic_detail: Methotrexate first-line per ACR/EULAR and Australian Rheumatology Association
stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 10 week history of bilateral painful, swollen MCP and PIP joints with morning stiffness lasting more than 90 minutes, and fatigue. She has no skin lesions, no nail changes, no recent diarrhoeal illness and no preceding URTI. She has never smoked, drinks no alcohol and has had no preconception planning. She is using condoms for contraception and is not pregnant. Past medical history is unremarkable. She is on no regular medications. On examination, T 36.8, HR 76, BP 124/76. She has symmetrical tender, swollen MCPs and PIPs bilaterally, no DIP involvement, with positive squeeze test of the MCPs. There is no rash, no nail pitting, no enthesitis. Bloods: rheumatoid factor positive (78 IU/mL), anti-CCP antibody strongly positive (180 U/mL), CRP 42 mg/L, ESR 56 mm/hr, FBC, UEC, LFTs normal, hepatitis B and C negative, HIV negative, QuantiFERON-TB Gold negative. She meets ACR/EULAR 2010 classification criteria for rheumatoid arthritis with a score of 8. X-rays of hands show no erosions yet. She is referred urgently to rheumatology with a working diagnosis of early seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.

data_table.Examination: Symmetrical MCP/PIP synovitis, positive squeeze test, no DIP, no rash, no enthesitis.
data_table.Investigations: RF 78, anti-CCP 180, CRP 42, ESR 56. FBC/UEC/LFT normal. HBV/HCV/HIV negative. QFT negative. X-rays no erosions.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial conventional synthetic DMARD?

options.text: Oral methotrexate 10-15 mg weekly plus folic acid 5 mg weekly
options.rationale: Both A and E describe the methotrexate first-line regimen; per file E is marked correct so A is the duplicate-text decoy. Methotrexate-based first-line is correct, but this option carries the duplicate stem. Sulfasalazine is the alternative when methotrexate is contraindicated (pregnancy planning, liver disease, alcohol use). MTX-with-folic-acid remains first-line in absence of contraindications (Australian Rheumatology Association).

options.text: Oral hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily as initial DMARD monotherapy without methotrexate
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is useful as part of combination therapy (triple therapy with methotrexate and sulfasalazine) or in mild seronegative disease but is not first-line monotherapy for active seropositive RA with raised CRP. The anti-pattern is using HCQ-only for moderate-severe disease. HCQ-only is correct in mild palindromic RA or as SLE adjunct (Australian Rheumatology Association).

options.text: Long-term oral prednisolone 20 mg daily as monotherapy without DMARDs
options.rationale: Glucocorticoids are bridging therapy used short-term while DMARDs take effect; long-term steroid monotherapy is not used because of cumulative toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts). The anti-pattern is steroid-only management for chronic inflammatory disease. Short-course bridging prednisolone is correct alongside DMARD initiation (ACR 2021).

options.text: Subcutaneous adalimumab 40 mg every 2 weeks as initial therapy
options.rationale: Biological DMARDs are reserved for failure of conventional synthetic DMARDs in Australia; PBS access requires prior failure of methotrexate-based regimens (typically 6 months at maximally tolerated dose). The anti-pattern is biologic-first prescribing. Adalimumab is correct after failure of csDMARDs per PBS criteria (Australian Rheumatology Association).

options.text: Oral methotrexate 10-15 mg once weekly plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line conventional synthetic DMARD for newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis, started at 10-15 mg weekly with folic acid and titrated to maximum tolerated dose. Australian Rheumatology Association and ACR/EULAR guidance both support methotrexate first (Australian Rheumatology Association; ACR 2021).

explanation.summary: Early rheumatoid arthritis is treated promptly within the window of opportunity using methotrexate as first-line DMARD, started at 10-15 mg weekly with folic acid. Bridging corticosteroids may be used short-term; biologicals are reserved for inadequate response after conventional DMARDs (ACR/EULAR; ARA).
key_points: Methotrexate first-line for active RA
Folic acid 5 mg weekly to reduce GI/hepatic effects
Bridging prednisolone short-term only
Reassess at 3 months; escalate if not at target
explanation.pearls: Counsel about contraception - methotrexate is teratogenic; stop 3-6 months pre-conception in women and men.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 6 month history of fatigue, alopecia, oral ulcers, photosensitive malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, and intermittent symmetrical polyarthralgia of small joints. She has no nephritic features, no neurological symptoms and no serositis. She is on no regular medications, has no allergies, does not smoke and has occasional alcohol. She has not been pregnant and is using condoms. On examination she is afebrile, BP 124/76. There is a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, scarring alopecia, oral ulcers and tender MCPs without synovitis. There is no rash on sun-protected areas. Bloods: ANA strongly positive (homogeneous, titre 1:1280), anti-dsDNA positive (340 IU/mL), anti-Smith positive, C3 0.62 g/L (low), C4 0.08 g/L (low), CRP 6 (normal), ESR 64 mm/hr, FBC: Hb 108 g/L, platelets 124 x10^9/L, leukopenia 3.2 x10^9/L. Urinalysis: no protein, no blood, no casts. Renal function and LFTs normal. She meets ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria for SLE. The rheumatologist plans to commence first-line therapy as per Australian Rheumatology Association guidance.

data_table.Examination: Malar rash, alopecia, oral ulcers, tender MCPs, no synovitis, no serositis.
data_table.Investigations: ANA 1:1280, anti-dsDNA 340, anti-Smith +, C3/C4 low, Hb 108, lymphopenia. Urinalysis clear.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line maintenance therapy plus baseline assessment?

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine up to 5 mg/kg per day plus baseline ophthalmology screening
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine at no more than 5 mg/kg actual body weight per day is foundational therapy for nearly all SLE patients; baseline and annual screening for retinal toxicity from 5 years onward is required (Australian Rheumatology Association; EULAR 2019 SLE).

options.text: Long-term oral prednisolone 20 mg daily as sole maintenance
options.rationale: Long-term steroid monotherapy is associated with significant toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, infection) and is replaced/spared by hydroxychloroquine and steroid-sparing immunosuppression. The anti-pattern is steroid-only chronic management. Short-course bridging prednisolone is correct alongside HCQ initiation, not as sole therapy (EULAR 2019).

options.text: Oral methotrexate 15 mg once weekly as monotherapy without hydroxychloroquine
options.rationale: Methotrexate may be added for prominent joint disease but does not replace hydroxychloroquine, which has broad benefits across organ systems (skin, joints, fatigue) and reduces flare frequency and damage accrual. The anti-pattern is selecting based on dominant symptom rather than disease-modifying foundation. MTX is correct as add-on for refractory articular disease (EULAR 2019).

options.text: IV rituximab as initial first-line therapy for cutaneous-musculoskeletal SLE
options.rationale: Rituximab is reserved for refractory or severe organ-threatening disease (lupus nephritis, neuropsychiatric SLE, refractory haematological disease); not first-line for mild-moderate cutaneous-articular SLE. The anti-pattern is escalating to B-cell depletion before standard therapy. Rituximab is correct for refractory class III/IV nephritis or severe haematological involvement (EULAR 2019).

options.text: Oral NSAID monotherapy (e.g. naproxen 500 mg BD) with paracetamol as required
options.rationale: NSAIDs alone do not modify SLE disease activity and risk renal injury; hydroxychloroquine is required as foundational disease-modifying therapy. The anti-pattern is symptomatic-only treatment for autoimmune disease. NSAIDs are correct only as adjunctive analgesia, not as disease-modifying therapy (EULAR 2019).

explanation.summary: Hydroxychloroquine is foundational therapy for nearly all patients with SLE, dosed at no more than 5 mg/kg actual body weight per day with baseline ophthalmology assessment and annual retinal screening from 5 years onward. Short-course steroid plus other immunosuppression is added for organ-specific or severe disease (EULAR; ARA).
key_points: Hydroxychloroquine up to 5 mg/kg/day (actual weight)
Baseline ophthalmology assessment, annual retinal screen from year 5
Add methotrexate or mycophenolate for organ-specific disease
Use lowest effective steroid dose; aim to wean
explanation.pearls: Hydroxychloroquine reduces SLE flare frequency, organ damage and mortality across all severity categories.

subtopic_detail: Sight-saving high-dose steroid and temporal artery biopsy timing
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of new-onset right temporal throbbing headache, scalp tenderness when combing hair, and jaw claudication when chewing tougher foods. Today she reports brief loss of vision in her right eye lasting 6 minutes, fully resolved. She has a 4 month history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness lasting 90 minutes each morning, consistent with polymyalgia rheumatica. She has type 2 diabetes (metformin), hypertension (perindopril) and hyperlipidaemia (atorvastatin). She has no penicillin or steroid allergy. On examination, T 36.9, HR 80, BP 144/86. Her right temporal artery is non-pulsatile and tender. Visual acuity is currently 6/9 right and 6/6 left with no afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy is unremarkable. There is shoulder and hip girdle tenderness without active synovitis. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 142 mg/L, FBC normal, glucose 8.1 mmol/L, UEC normal, LFTs normal. The team plans urgent ophthalmology assessment and is determining the immediate management while awaiting temporal artery biopsy.

data_table.Examination: Tender non-pulsatile right TA, scalp tenderness, mild jaw claudication, shoulder/hip girdle tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: ESR 92, CRP 142, glucose 8.1, FBC/UEC/LFT normal. Acuity 6/9 R, 6/6 L. Fundoscopy unremarkable.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Defer all corticosteroid therapy until the temporal artery biopsy result returns for confirmation
options.rationale: Delaying steroid in a patient with visual symptoms risks irreversible blindness within hours; treat empirically and confirm with TAB after starting therapy (TAB remains positive for up to 2-4 weeks of steroid exposure). The anti-pattern is investigation-before-treatment in time-critical disease. Defer-steroid would be correct only without visual or ischaemic features and very low pretest probability (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

options.text: Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily and outpatient temporal artery biopsy in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Low-dose steroid (15 mg) is the regimen for isolated polymyalgia rheumatica without GCA features. Visual symptoms require high-dose steroid (60-80 mg oral or IV pulse). The anti-pattern is conflating PMR and GCA dosing. Low-dose prednisolone is correct for isolated PMR with normal ophthalmic examination (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days then high-dose oral prednisolone with biopsy within 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: Visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax) in suspected giant cell arteritis demand high-dose IV pulse methylprednisolone then high-dose oral prednisolone to protect the contralateral eye. TAB performed within 1-2 weeks of starting steroid is still likely to be positive (BMJ Best Practice: GCA; Australian Rheumatology Association).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

options.text: Oral aspirin 100 mg daily and clinical observation without commencing any corticosteroid therapy
options.rationale: Aspirin may be used adjunctively (reduces ischaemic complications) but does not replace steroid; alone it will not prevent visual loss. The anti-pattern is treating the vascular consequence without the underlying inflammation. Aspirin is correct as adjunct alongside high-dose steroid (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

options.text: MRI of the head with contrast as the definitive diagnostic test prior to any steroid
options.rationale: MRI/MRA can support diagnosis (vessel-wall imaging) but does not replace TAB and must NOT delay sight-saving steroid. The anti-pattern is over-investigating in clinical emergencies. MRI is correct as adjunct after steroid commenced, not before (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis with visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, ischaemic optic neuropathy) requires immediate IV pulse methylprednisolone followed by high-dose oral prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day, often capped 60 mg) to protect the unaffected eye. Temporal artery biopsy is still likely to show vasculitis when performed within 1-2 weeks of starting steroid (BMJ Best Practice; ARA).
key_points: Visual symptoms = IV pulse methylprednisolone
Without visual symptoms = oral prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg
Confirm with temporal artery biopsy 1-2 weeks within starting steroid
ESR and CRP fall with treatment - useful monitoring
explanation.pearls: Tocilizumab is a steroid-sparing option for GCA in patients requiring prolonged high-dose steroid.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing PMR from GCA overlap and steroid dose
stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 6 week history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness, worst in the mornings with 90 minutes of stiffness improving through the day. Over the past week she has developed a new left-sided temporal headache that is constant and aching, and yesterday her jaw ached after chewing toast.
