explanation.summary: SOMANZ/RANZCOG magnesium sulphate regimen for severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia: 4 g IV loading over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance. Monitor reflexes, RR and UO hourly. Treat recurrent seizures with a further 2-4 g IV bolus. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV is the antidote for toxicity.
key_points: Loading dose: 4 g IV over 15-20 min in 100 mL saline
Maintenance: 1 g/h IV (reduce if renal impairment)
Recurrent seizure: further 2-4 g IV bolus over 5 minutes
Toxicity signs: loss of reflexes, RR <12, UO <100 mL/4 h
Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV slow
explanation.pearls: Magnesium reduces eclampsia by half compared with placebo and is more effective than phenytoin or diazepam (Magpie trial; Cochrane).

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 37 weeks and 4 days gestation, previously well with an uncomplicated pregnancy until last week when her blood pressure was first noted to be 142/88 mmHg, is brought to the birth suite by her partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting two minutes followed by a period of unresponsiveness. She has had a worsening frontal headache, blurred vision and epigastric pain for 24 hours. On arrival she is post-ictal but rousable to voice. Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg and 172/110 mmHg on repeat 5 minutes apart with an appropriately sized cuff. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, mild peripheral oedema and hyperreflexia with three beats of clonus. There is no focal neurology, no neck stiffness and no fever, making other intracranial causes less likely. Foetal heart rate on CTG is 145 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. The senior registrar is in the room, the anaesthetist and obstetric consultant are being paged, an IV line is in situ and bloods are being drawn for urgent FBC, coagulation, U&E and LFTs.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 175/115 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min, SpO2 96% on 4 L O2, GCS 13 (E3V4M6).
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, drowsy but rousable. Reflexes 4+ with 3 beats clonus. Tongue laterally bitten.
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: protein 3+, blood trace. Bedside Hb 116 g/L, platelets pending. CTG: baseline 145, reduced variability, no decelerations.

lead_in: Which sequence of immediate management steps is most appropriate?

options.text: Immediate caesarean section under general anaesthesia before commencing any magnesium sulphate or antihypertensive therapy at all
options.rationale: Maternal stabilisation with magnesium and BP control must precede delivery; rushing to theatre without stabilisation worsens both maternal and foetal outcomes. The anti-pattern is treating eclampsia as 'just deliver'. Immediate caesarean is correct only with confirmed foetal distress AFTER maternal stabilisation (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: IV diazepam 10 mg as the first-line anticonvulsant, then a phenytoin loading dose, then expedite delivery in theatre
options.rationale: Magnesium sulphate is SUPERIOR to both diazepam and phenytoin for eclampsia (Magpie trial showed 58% reduction in recurrent seizures vs phenytoin) and is first-line. Benzodiazepines are reserved for refractory seizures after adequate magnesium loading. Diazepam-first is correct only when magnesium is unavailable or contraindicated (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine to rapidly reduce blood pressure, then immediate transfer directly to theatre without anticonvulsant cover at all
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrolled BP drops with placental hypoperfusion risk - a classic obstetric anti-pattern. Oral nifedipine SR or IV labetalol/hydralazine is preferred for controlled reduction. SL nifedipine is essentially never correct in pregnancy hypertension (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Left lateral with oxygen, magnesium sulphate 4 g IV over 15 minutes then 1 g/h, labetalol or hydralazine, then deliver
options.rationale: This is the standard SOMANZ/RANZCOG sequence: left lateral position to reduce aortocaval compression and aspiration risk, airway/oxygen, magnesium loading and maintenance, antihypertensive control aiming for systolic <160 and diastolic <110, then expedited delivery (vaginal or caesarean) once mother is stable (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023). Full SOMANZ sequence: airway-resuscitation, magnesium 4 g load then 1 g/h maintenance, IV labetalol or hydralazine for severe hypertension, delivery once stabilised.

options.text: Continuous EEG monitoring at the bedside before commencing any anticonvulsant therapy or antihypertensive medication in this setting
options.rationale: EEG is not required to diagnose or treat eclampsia; treatment is initiated on clinical grounds. The anti-pattern is over-investigating in clinical emergencies. EEG is correct only in post-ictal patients with persistent decreased GCS to exclude non-convulsive status, never before stabilising therapy (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: Eclampsia management priorities (mother first, then foetus): left lateral, ABC, magnesium sulphate load and maintenance, control severe hypertension, then expedite delivery. Magnesium is first-line, not benzodiazepines.
key_points: Mother before foetus: stabilise then deliver
Magnesium 4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance
BP targets: SBP <160, DBP <110 with IV labetalol/hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
Continue magnesium for 24 h after delivery or last seizure
Avoid sublingual nifedipine and diazepam first-line
explanation.pearls: Up to one third of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum, most within 48 hours; magnesium continues for 24 h post-delivery.

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 with booking BMI 27, no diabetes history, no PCOS, no previous GDM has a 75 g OGTT at 27 weeks: fasting 5.3, 1 h 8.4, 2 h 7.1 mmol/L. The midwife asks whether this meets Australian (ADIPS) criteria for gestational diabetes, and if so, what the immediate next step is. The woman has no symptoms and her foetus is growing on the 50th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.
data_table.Examination: BMI 28. Uterus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Awaiting OGTT result.

lead_in: Which interpretation and next step are correct?

options.text: GDM diagnosed on the fasting >=5.1 mmol/L cut-off; commence dietitian and diabetes educator referral, capillary glucose monitoring 4 times daily, and review at the joint diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic within 1 week
options.rationale: ADIPS 2014 criteria: ANY of fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L. Her fasting 5.3 meets the threshold; even though the 1 h and 2 h values are below cut-off, GDM is diagnosed. Initial management is multidisciplinary team referral with SMBG (RANZCOG; ADIPS).

options.text: Not GDM; only the fasting result is borderline; repeat OGTT in 4 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS criteria require only ONE elevated value to diagnose GDM (IADPSG single-value rule); her fasting 5.3 already meets the threshold. The anti-pattern is requiring multiple abnormal values (old WHO/two-step criteria). One-value rule is the Australian standard (ADIPS).

options.text: GDM only if fasting >=5.5 mmol/L; her result is below threshold
options.rationale: The Australian fasting cut-off is 5.1 mmol/L, not 5.5. The anti-pattern is using older/American thresholds in an Australian context. 5.5 mmol/L threshold was used historically but Australia adopted the IADPSG/ADIPS 5.1 in 2014 (ADIPS).

options.text: Type 2 diabetes diagnosed (fasting >=7.0); commence insulin today
options.rationale: Diabetes outside pregnancy uses fasting >=7.0; in pregnancy the GDM cut-off is 5.1. She has GDM, not overt T2DM. Overt diabetes in pregnancy is diagnosed only with fasting >=7.0, HbA1c >=6.5%, or random glucose >=11.1 with symptoms - thresholds for T2DM diagnosis (ADIPS).

options.text: Borderline only; reassure with healthy-eating advice and re-test at 32 weeks
options.rationale: She meets diagnostic criteria; deferral risks foetal complications (macrosomia, stillbirth, neonatal hypoglycaemia). The anti-pattern is reassurance without acting on confirmed diagnostic results. Re-testing is correct only when initial OGTT is in borderline ambiguous range (very rare with the single-value rule) (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: Australia uses ADIPS 2014 (IADPSG-aligned) criteria: 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks for universal screening. Any one value at or above fasting 5.1, 1 h 10.0 or 2 h 8.5 mmol/L makes the diagnosis.
key_points: Universal OGTT at 24-28 weeks
Early OGTT in first or early second trimester if high risk (previous GDM, BMI >=30, age >=40, ethnicity, family hx, PCOS, prior macrosomia)
One abnormal value is enough
Repeat 75 g OGTT 6-12 weeks postpartum
explanation.pearls: Memorise 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 - they are the only three numbers you need for diagnosis in Australia.

subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes - ADIPS 75 g OGTT thresholds

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 30 weeks and 2 days gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 26 weeks (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 2 h 9.1 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT). She has been on structured dietary modification with a credentialled dietitian and 30 minutes of daily walking for four weeks. Her four-times-daily capillary glucose diary now shows fasting values consistently 5.6-6.2 mmol/L (ADIPS target <5.1 mmol/L) and 2-hour postprandial values 5.8-6.8 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L) after all three meals. There is no overnight hypoglycaemia, no symptomatic hypoglycaemia, no glycosuria and no ketonuria on bedside testing. Foetal ultrasound at 30 weeks shows estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with appropriate liquor and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She has no contraindication to insulin or metformin, her renal function is normal (creatinine 58 micromol/L), and she has good health literacy and dexterity for self-injection. Her booking BMI was 31 and she has gained 6 kg in pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 82 bpm.
data_table.Examination: BMI 31. Fundal height appropriate.
data_table.Investigations: BSL diary: fasting 5.6-6.2; postprandial within target after all meals. HbA1c 5.6%. Renal function normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence basal-bolus insulin with rapid-acting analogue at meals plus bedtime NPH
options.rationale: Full basal-bolus is reserved for patients with BOTH elevated fasting and postprandial values. This patient has isolated elevated fasting glucose only, so basal alone is sufficient. The anti-pattern is over-treating with multi-injection regimens. Basal-bolus is correct when both fasting and postprandial fail despite stepwise titration (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Commence rapid-acting prandial insulin (aspart or lispro) before each meal only, no basal
options.rationale: Prandial insulin would not address the predominant fasting hyperglycaemia and would risk daytime hypoglycaemia. The anti-pattern is mismatching insulin profile to the glucose pattern. Prandial-only insulin is correct for isolated postprandial elevations with normal fasting (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures only and review fasting glucose again in four weeks
options.rationale: Fasting glucose remains above target despite four weeks of optimised lifestyle; pharmacotherapy is indicated to reduce risk of macrosomia and stillbirth. The anti-pattern is continued lifestyle-only when targets are not met. Continued lifestyle is correct only when targets are achieved (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Commence empagliflozin 10 mg orally daily as monotherapy in pregnancy
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity concerns and lack of safety data (TGA category D). The anti-pattern is using non-pregnancy diabetes drugs in pregnancy. SGLT2i is never correct in current pregnancy management (TGA; AMH).

options.text: Commence isophane (NPH) insulin at bedtime, titrated to fasting capillary glucose target
options.rationale: For isolated fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM, the ADIPS-recommended first-line pharmacotherapy is bedtime intermediate-acting (isophane/NPH) insulin titrated to achieve fasting glucose <5.1 mmol/L. Metformin is an acceptable alternative or adjunct in suitable patients but insulin is the established first-line agent (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

explanation.summary: When lifestyle fails in GDM, pharmacotherapy is matched to the pattern of hyperglycaemia: isolated fasting hyperglycaemia -> bedtime isophane (NPH); isolated postprandial -> prandial rapid-acting insulin; both -> basal-bolus regimen. Metformin is an option after counselling about placental transfer.
key_points: ADIPS targets: fasting <5.1, 1 h <7.4, 2 h <6.7 mmol/L
Isolated fasting hyperglycaemia: bedtime NPH first
Isolated postprandial: prandial rapid-acting analogue
Both: basal-bolus
Metformin: alternative or adjunct; crosses placenta; not for severe hyperglycaemia
SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists: contraindicated in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Match the insulin to the glucose pattern. Don't reach for basal-bolus when only the fasting value is high.

subtopic_detail: GDM - insulin titration after lifestyle failure

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with gestational hypertension (persistent BP 144/92 over a fortnight; no proteinuria; bloods normal; normal foetal growth and Dopplers) is asymptomatic. She has well-controlled severe asthma on inhaled budesonide/formoterol with two emergency presentations in the past year requiring oral prednisolone. She has no other medical conditions, no allergies, normal renal and liver function. The consultant is choosing the first-line oral antihypertensive.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 144/90 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Uncomplicated. No oedema. Fundus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L, urine PCR <30 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antihypertensive?

options.text: Labetalol 100 mg orally twice daily titrated to target
options.rationale: Labetalol is standard first-line in pregnancy hypertension, but in significant asthma (recent exacerbations on ICS-LABA) beta-blockade risks bronchospasm; SOMANZ and RANZCOG list significant asthma as a relative contraindication. Labetalol is correct in pregnancy hypertension without asthma or other beta-blocker contraindication (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Slow-release nifedipine 20 mg orally twice daily titrated to target
options.rationale: In a woman with significant asthma where labetalol is unsafe, nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) is the preferred first-line alternative; both are SOMANZ-recommended options (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Enalapril 5 mg orally once daily titrated to target
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy due to foetal renal failure, oligohydramnios, calvarial hypoplasia, and pulmonary hypoplasia. The classic absolute teratogen anti-pattern. Enalapril is never correct in current pregnancy; pre-conception switch to pregnancy-safe agent is mandatory (SOMANZ; AMH).

options.text: Methyldopa 250 mg orally three times daily titrated to target
options.rationale: Methyldopa is an acceptable second-line agent but is less effective (higher target BP achievement failure) and has higher rates of depression/sedation/postural hypotension. Nifedipine is preferred in this asthma context. Methyldopa is correct as alternative second-line when first-line agents fail (SOMANZ).

options.text: Oral hydralazine 25 mg three times daily titrated to target
options.rationale: Oral hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia and unpredictable response; IV hydralazine is used for severe acute hypertension but oral hydralazine is not first-line maintenance. The anti-pattern is using parenteral-only drugs orally. Oral hydralazine is correct only as add-on in resistant pregnancy hypertension with specialist input (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: First-line oral antihypertensives for non-severe hypertension in pregnancy in Australia: labetalol > nifedipine SR > methyldopa. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated. Treatment target BP 110-140 / 80-90 mmHg per SOMANZ.
key_points: Labetalol 100-400 mg BD-TDS (first-line)
Nifedipine SR 20-60 mg BD (second-line)
Methyldopa 250-500 mg TDS-QID (second/third-line)
ACE inhibitors and ARBs: contraindicated
Acute severe BP: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
explanation.pearls: Avoid labetalol if the patient has asthma, heart block, or significant bradycardia; switch to nifedipine SR.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 weeks presents with 3 days of fever 38.6, myalgia, headache and mild non-bloody diarrhoea after eating unpasteurised soft cheese 1 week earlier. Several other guests have had similar symptoms. CTG reactive at 145 bpm baseline. Examination: looks unwell but not septic, chest clear, abdomen soft, uterus non-tender. Blood and urine cultures taken before any antibiotics. WCC 14.5, CRP 92, blood cultures pending. She has no penicillin allergy, normal renal function, takes only pregnancy supplements. The microbiology registrar advises empirical cover for the most likely food-borne organism in this setting.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min.
data_table.Examination: Looks unwell but not septic. Chest clear. Abdomen soft. Uterus non-tender, fundus appropriate.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.5 x10^9/L (neutrophilia), CRP 92 mg/L, blood cultures taken. CTG reactive.

lead_in: Which empirical antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg daily, pending cultures
options.rationale: The clinical picture suggests listeriosis (febrile gastroenteritis-like illness with high-risk food exposure in pregnancy). Therapeutic Guidelines recommend IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) with gentamicin for synergy as empirical therapy; cephalosporins do not cover Listeria monocytogenes (eTG; RANZCOG).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Listeria is INTRINSICALLY RESISTANT to all cephalosporins, so ceftriaxone monotherapy is inadequate - a major anti-pattern. The intrinsic resistance is due to absence of penicillin-binding protein 3 affinity for cephalosporins. Ceftriaxone is correct in suspected gram-negative bacteraemia without Listeria suspicion (eTG).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily without IV cover
options.rationale: Suspected listeriosis in pregnancy requires IV therapy until cultures and clinical improvement allow step-down; oral amoxicillin alone is insufficient given placental and CNS penetration concerns. Oral amoxicillin is correct as step-down after clinical and biochemical improvement on IV (eTG).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole IV monotherapy
options.rationale: TMP-SMX is an alternative in penicillin-allergic patients but is contraindicated in pregnancy in T1 (folate antagonism, neural tube defects) and near term (kernicterus risk). The anti-pattern is using Bactrim-as-default in pregnancy. TMP-SMX is correct in penicillin-allergic non-pregnant patients (eTG).

options.text: IV vancomycin monotherapy
options.rationale: Vancomycin is not first-line for Listeria; ampicillin + gentamicin is the recommended Australian regimen because of better bactericidal synergy. The anti-pattern is using vancomycin as default broad-spectrum. Vancomycin is correct in MRSA, severe penicillin-allergy with PJI/endocarditis, or selected severe gram-positive infection (eTG).

explanation.summary: Listeria monocytogenes is a food-borne Gram-positive rod (intracellular) acquired from unpasteurised dairy, soft cheese, deli meats, smoked seafood and pre-prepared salads. Pregnancy increases susceptibility 10-20 fold. Treatment is IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin.
key_points: Cephalosporins do NOT cover Listeria
Empirical Rx: ampicillin 2 g IV q6h + gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg/day
Duration: 2-3 weeks (longer if meningitis)
Penicillin-allergic: co-trimoxazole
Notify public health (CDNA SoNG)
Foetal complications: stillbirth, preterm labour, neonatal sepsis, granulomatosis infantiseptica
explanation.pearls: Any febrile pregnant woman with a high-risk food history needs blood cultures and listeria-active empirical antibiotics before culture results return.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines - Listeriosis

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive.. Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Well. No lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Toxoplasma IgM positive, IgG positive low avidity, maternal blood PCR positive. Booking ultrasound: viable singleton at 11 weeks, normal anatomy for gestation.

lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Spiramycin 1 g TDS, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
options.rationale: Spiramycin is the first-trimester treatment of choice as it concentrates in the placenta and reduces vertical transmission without causing foetal harm. If foetal infection is confirmed (positive amniotic fluid PCR after 18 weeks), therapy switches to sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid to treat established foetal infection (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; RCOG). Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission; sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is reserved for confirmed foetal infection by amniotic fluid PCR at >=18 weeks.

options.text: Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in T1 (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the foetus is reserved for confirmed foetal infection. The anti-pattern is treating mother as if foetus is already infected. Sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is correct only after confirmed foetal infection on amniotic fluid PCR (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission and is the WHO-endorsed agent for prevention. Co-trimoxazole is correct in some non-pregnancy contexts (HIV-positive patients on prophylaxis) (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
options.rationale: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy. The anti-pattern is using a familiar antibiotic without recognising it lacks the appropriate spectrum. Azithromycin is correct only as adjunct in some refractory toxoplasmic chorioretinitis regimens (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Termination of pregnancy without treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation
options.rationale: Maternal primary T1 infection carries low vertical transmission risk (~10-15%) although severe consequences if transmitted; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not default. ToP is correct only after confirmed severe foetal infection (multiple anomalies on US/MRI) with informed maternal choice (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Suspected maternal toxoplasmosis is confirmed by serology (IgG avidity, IgM) and maternal PCR. Spiramycin reduces transmission without teratogenicity. Confirmed foetal infection (amniotic PCR after 18 weeks) is treated with sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid.
key_points: Spiramycin: first-line in pregnancy to prevent transmission (does not cross placenta well)
Sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid: for confirmed foetal infection from 14-18 weeks
Folinic acid (leucovorin) protects against pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow toxicity
Diagnose primary infection with low IgG avidity within 16-20 weeks of seroconversion
Vertical transmission risk: T1 ~10-15%, T2 ~30%, T3 ~60% - but severity decreases with gestation
Sources: cat faeces, raw/undercooked meat, unwashed produce, soil
explanation.pearls: Earlier infection equals lower transmission but more severe disease if transmitted; later infection equals higher transmission but milder disease.

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 14 weeks and 1 day gestation works as a long-day-care educator caring for children aged 12-24 months and presents to her GP with a fortnight of mild fatigue, low-grade fever, headache, mild cervical lymphadenopathy and arthralgia. She has no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no jaundice. Routine TORCH testing shows CMV IgM positive, CMV IgG positive with low avidity (consistent with infection within the past three months). Critically, her booking CMV IgG four weeks earlier was negative, providing serological evidence of seroconversion in early pregnancy. A maternal blood CMV PCR is positive at a moderate viral load. She has no prior infectious diseases history, no immunosuppression, and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a healthy term infant. She asks the maternal-foetal medicine consultant about the difference between primary and non-primary (reactivation or reinfection) maternal CMV infection in pregnancy, the next investigation step including foetal surveillance and timing of any amniocentesis, and the role and evidence base for any antiviral therapy in pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: CMV IgG positive low avidity, IgM positive, blood PCR positive. Booking CMV IgG (4 weeks prior): negative. LFTs mildly raised (ALT 65). Hb 122 g/L.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately describes the current Australian approach to maternal CMV infection in this case?

options.text: Non-primary CMV infection carries a higher vertical transmission rate than primary infection, so reassurance is appropriate here
options.rationale: Primary maternal CMV has SUBSTANTIALLY HIGHER vertical transmission rate (~30-40%) and higher severity than non-primary (~1-2%). The anti-pattern is misremembering the direction of risk. Non-primary higher-risk reassurance is never correct; the framing should always be 'primary infection = highest risk' (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: Maternal intravenous ganciclovir is the established standard of care to prevent foetal CMV infection in primary seroconversion
options.rationale: IV ganciclovir is teratogenic in animal studies and is not routinely used in pregnancy; valaciclovir trials have shown some benefit. The anti-pattern is using post-natal CMV treatment drug for maternal management. IV ganciclovir is correct for neonatal symptomatic congenital CMV, not maternal pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission of CMV in the same way that it does for Toxoplasma gondii
options.rationale: Spiramycin has NO role in CMV management; it is specific to toxoplasmosis. The anti-pattern is generalising one intrauterine infection drug to another. Spiramycin is correct only for maternal toxoplasmosis prevention of vertical transmission (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

options.text: CMV is a rare cause of congenital infection and routine foetal surveillance is unnecessary after maternal seroconversion
options.rationale: Congenital CMV is the MOST COMMON congenital infection in developed countries (~0.5-1% of live births) and the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss. Serial foetal US plus amniotic PCR after 21 weeks is recommended. Reassurance without surveillance is never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Serial foetal ultrasound, amniocentesis for CMV PCR after 21 weeks and 6 weeks post-seroconversion, and high-dose valaciclovir following MFM discussion
options.rationale: This reflects the current RANZCOG/RCOG/BMJ Best Practice approach: confirm primary infection (low avidity, seroconversion), serial foetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks and at least 6 weeks post-seroconversion to maximise sensitivity, and consider maternal high-dose valaciclovir 8 g/day which a randomised trial showed reduces vertical transmission (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; Shahar-Nissan 2020). Surveillance includes brain biometry and growth; amniocentesis sensitivity rises after 21 weeks and 6 weeks post-seroconversion; high-dose valaciclovir is the only intervention with RCT-supported reduction in vertical transmission.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection and the leading non-genetic cause of childhood sensorineural hearing loss. Primary maternal infection transmits in 30-40% of cases; non-primary in 1-2%. Management includes serial foetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks, and consideration of maternal high-dose valaciclovir.
key_points: Primary infection: 30-40% transmission; non-primary: 1-2%
Diagnose by IgG seroconversion or low IgG avidity + IgM positive
Amniotic PCR: after 21 weeks AND >=6 weeks post seroconversion
Foetal US findings: ventriculomegaly, periventricular calcifications, hyperechogenic bowel, IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly
Valaciclovir 8 g/day may reduce vertical transmission (RCT 2020)
No effective vaccine; childcare hand hygiene is key prevention
explanation.pearls: Maternal CMV is often clinically silent; hearing screening at birth is critical for affected neonates.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: CMV in pregnancy - primary vs non-primary infection

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 24 weeks and 2 days gestation with a background of mild persistent asthma on budesonide-formoterol maintenance (one inhalation twice daily as preventer plus reliever) presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of progressive wheeze, dyspnoea and chest tightness after a viral upper respiratory infection. She is too breathless to speak in full sentences. Her best personal PEFR is 420 L/min; in ED it is measured at 210 L/min (50% of personal best). SpO2 is 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally and no focal crackles or signs of consolidation. She is afebrile at 37.1 degC, has no calf swelling, no chest pain, the foetus is moving normally and the CTG is reactive with a baseline of 140 bpm. She has stopped her preventer inhaler for the past four days because she was worried about possible effects on the baby and asks whether she should continue inhaled corticosteroids and whether oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy, given a friend has told her to refuse steroids.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 28 /min, HR 118 bpm, SpO2 93% RA, BP 124/78 mmHg, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Speaking in short phrases, accessory muscle use, widespread polyphonic wheeze. CTG reactive.
data_table.Investigations: PEFR 210 L/min (50% of personal best 420). ABG not yet performed. CXR not indicated initially.

lead_in: Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue maintenance inhaled corticosteroid but withhold all systemic corticosteroids in pregnancy due to perceived foetal teratogenic risk
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy and is essential for moderate/severe asthma exacerbation; withholding it risks maternal AND foetal hypoxia, which is FAR more harmful than transient corticosteroid exposure. The anti-pattern is treating pregnancy as steroid-contraindication. Steroid withholding is essentially never correct (Therapeutic Guidelines; National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium if severe, continue ICS, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg for 5-10 days, plus controlled oxygen
options.rationale: This is the standard exacerbation algorithm. Salbutamol bronchodilation, oxygen to target SpO2 94-98% (95% to maintain foetal oxygenation), maintenance ICS continued, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for short course are all safe and indicated. Maternal hypoxia harms the foetus far more than these medications (National Asthma Council; Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG). ICS and oral prednisolone are safe in pregnancy and reassurance about safety is part of the management.
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Switch the patient to a leukotriene receptor antagonist (oral montelukast) alone as monotherapy maintenance throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: LTRAs (montelukast) are adjuncts, not first-line, and have NO role in acute exacerbation management. The anti-pattern is using maintenance-only drugs in acute exacerbations. Montelukast is correct as adjunct maintenance in exercise-induced or aspirin-sensitive asthma (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 minutes given as the immediate first-line therapy in pregnancy
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for severe exacerbations refractory to initial bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy. The anti-pattern is leading with adjunctive therapy before standard care. IV magnesium is correct after 1 hour of maximal nebulised bronchodilator + systemic steroid with inadequate response (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

options.text: Discharge home with a short-acting beta-2 agonist alone once PEFR has reached 60% of personal best
options.rationale: PEFR 60% of best with a recent severe exacerbation does not meet safe discharge criteria; she needs systemic corticosteroids, observation, and a written asthma action plan. The anti-pattern is treating PEFR-alone as discharge criterion. Discharge is correct at PEFR >75% best with sustained response 1 hour after last salbutamol (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Acute asthma in pregnancy is treated identically to non-pregnant patients with the additional priority of avoiding maternal hypoxia. Continue ICS, give salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone for moderate/severe exacerbations, and supplemental oxygen.
key_points: Maintenance ICS: safe and essential in pregnancy
Salbutamol: safe; first-line bronchodilator
Oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for 5-10 days for exacerbation
Target SpO2 94-98% (or >=95% in pregnancy)
IV magnesium for severe refractory cases
Step up therapy if severity criteria escalate; involve obstetric medicine
explanation.pearls: Maternal asthma control is more important than any medication risk; uncontrolled asthma causes IUGR, preterm birth and pre-eclampsia.

sources.label: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 7 weeks is referred for Graves disease. She was diagnosed 6 months ago with tremor, weight loss and palpitations; currently on carbimazole 15 mg daily with normal TFTs and TRAb 5.2 IU/L (positive). She has no goitre and no ophthalmopathy. She is asymptomatic. Her endocrinologist asks the obstetric medicine team to advise on antithyroid drug choice, dosing strategy by trimester, and foetal/neonatal surveillance given the positive TRAb.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Clinically euthyroid. No tremor. No goitre. No ophthalmopathy.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L, free T3 4.2 pmol/L (all within trimester-specific reference). TRAb 5.2 IU/L (positive).

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil (PTU) in trimester 1 then back to carbimazole in trimester 2-3; monitor maternal TFTs monthly; arrange foetal growth and goitre surveillance scans and neonatal TFTs/TRAb at birth
options.rationale: Carbimazole/methimazole is associated with aplasia cutis and embryopathy in T1; PTU is preferred in T1 but switched back in T2-T3 due to PTU hepatotoxicity. Positive TRAb requires foetal surveillance for goitre/tachycardia and neonatal monitoring (RANZCOG; ATA; AMH).

options.text: Continue carbimazole at current dose throughout pregnancy; foetus is not at risk
options.rationale: Carbimazole in T1 risks teratogenicity (methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy: aplasia cutis, oesophageal/choanal atresia); positive TRAb crosses placenta with foetal goitre/tachycardia/hydrops risk requiring surveillance. Continuing carbimazole in T1 is the classic anti-pattern. Continuing carbimazole is correct in T2-T3 after T1 PTU switch (RANZCOG).

options.text: Stop antithyroid drugs as pregnancy is naturally immunosuppressive and Graves usually remits
options.rationale: Graves may improve in T2-T3 (immune modulation) but T1 has highest activity; abrupt cessation risks thyroid storm and miscarriage. The anti-pattern is assuming pregnancy 'cures' autoimmune disease. Stopping antithyroid drugs is correct only when biochemically euthyroid for >=6 months and TRAb negative (RANZCOG).

options.text: Switch to radioactive iodine ablation now
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in pregnancy (foetal thyroid ablation, cretinism). The classic teratogen-by-mechanism anti-pattern. RAI is correct only post-partum if breastfeeding ceased and 6-month conception avoidance is acceptable (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Switch to PTU throughout pregnancy without switching back
options.rationale: PTU causes severe hepatotoxicity (FDA black box); it should be limited to T1 and switched back to carbimazole in T2-T3. The anti-pattern is assuming one regimen suits all trimesters. PTU throughout is correct only in carbimazole hypersensitivity or thyroid storm (RANZCOG; AMH).

explanation.summary: Graves in pregnancy: PTU in T1 (lower embryopathy risk), then carbimazole/methimazole in T2/T3 (lower hepatotoxicity risk). Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated. TRAb positivity confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk; neonatal TFTs are mandatory.
key_points: T1: propylthiouracil (PTU)
T2-T3: carbimazole/methimazole (PTU = severe liver injury risk)
Conversion: 1 mg carbimazole ~ 10-20 mg PTU (use 10:1 conventionally)
Target: maternal fT4 at or just above upper limit of trimester-specific range
Radioactive iodine: contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation
TRAb positive: foetal/neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk - neonatal TFTs day 4-7
explanation.pearls: Methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy includes aplasia cutis and a distinctive choanal/oesophageal atresia phenotype; switch off it before or by 6-10 weeks gestation.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

subtopic_detail: Combined first-trimester screen - next-step after high risk
stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 woman attends her general practitioner with the result of her combined first-trimester screen, performed at 12 weeks and 4 days gestation with the recommended ultrasound markers (crown-rump length 62 mm, nuchal translucency 3.2 mm, present nasal bone) and maternal serum PAPP-A and free beta-hCG. The laboratory adjusted risk for trisomy 21 has come back as 1 in 80, with trisomy 18 risk 1 in 950 and trisomy 13 risk 1 in 1100. Her first child is well at age 4 with no chromosomal abnormality. There is no family history of aneuploidy or congenital structural malformations. Her booking bloods are unremarkable. She and her partner have read about NIPT and diagnostic testing online and want to know the most appropriate immediate next step. She is keen to avoid procedure-related miscarriage risk if reasonable. The pregnancy is otherwise progressing well, foetal heart was 162 bpm on a brief ultrasound today and she has no bleeding, pain or symptoms of concern.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft, non-tender abdomen. Uterus not yet palpable abdominally.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS at 12+4: NT 3.2 mm, nasal bone present, PAPP-A 0.4 MoM, free beta-hCG 2.8 MoM. Adjusted T21 risk 1 in 80, T18 risk 1 in 950, T13 risk 1 in 1100.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in counselling and investigation for this woman?

options.text: Reassure that the cFTS result represents a low-risk pregnancy and proceed to a routine 18 to 20 week morphology scan
options.rationale: Risk of 1 in 80 is well above the high-risk threshold (commonly 1 in 300 in Australian labs) and NT >=3.0 mm independently warrants further evaluation. The anti-pattern is treating any individual risk as 'small enough to ignore'. Low-risk reassurance is correct only with cFTS risk <1:1000 or NIPT low-probability result (RANZCOG Prenatal screening).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Repeat the combined first-trimester screen at 13+6 weeks to see if the risk falls before any further action is taken
options.rationale: cFTS is a single screening event; repeating it will not move risk meaningfully and delays definitive evaluation. The anti-pattern is using screening tests as serial monitoring tools. Repeat cFTS is essentially never correct as a follow-up of high risk (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Proceed directly to chorionic villus sampling today without further screening, since maternal age above 35 makes diagnostic testing mandatory
options.rationale: Maternal age alone is no longer an indication for invasive diagnostic testing; risk is individualised by cFTS or NIPT. The anti-pattern is using maternal age as standalone trigger when modern combined screens are available. CVS-by-age was the pre-2000 practice and is now superseded (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Offer NIPT as a contingent screening test, or diagnostic testing (CVS now or amniocentesis from 15 weeks) after counselling about procedure-related miscarriage risk
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends that after a high-risk cFTS the woman is offered counselling about both contingent NIPT (high sensitivity for T21) and definitive diagnostic testing (CVS from about 10 weeks or amniocentesis from 15 weeks). NIPT remains a screening test - a positive must be confirmed diagnostically. Procedure-related miscarriage risk for CVS and amniocentesis is approximately 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Arrange amniocentesis today at 12+4 weeks, given the high-risk result and the woman's preference to avoid further screening tests
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is not performed before 15 completed weeks because of higher procedure-related foetal loss and limb defect risk (Canadian Early Amniocentesis Trial showed talipes equinovarus excess at 11-12 weeks). CVS is the T1 diagnostic option. Amniocentesis is correct from 15 weeks (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: A high-risk combined first-trimester screen (commonly >=1 in 300) should trigger counselling about contingent NIPT or definitive diagnostic testing. CVS is available from about 10 weeks; amniocentesis from 15 weeks. NIPT is a screening test - positive results require diagnostic confirmation.
key_points: cFTS window: 11+0 to 13+6 weeks (CRL 45 to 84 mm)
High-risk threshold typically 1 in 300 in Australian labs
NT >=3.5 mm independently increases risk of aneuploidy AND congenital heart disease - foetal echo at 20 weeks
NIPT sensitivity ~99% for T21, ~97% for T18, lower for sex chromosome aneuploidies
Procedure-related miscarriage CVS/amnio ~0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units
explanation.pearls: Never report an NIPT positive result as diagnostic - always confirm with CVS or amniocentesis before any irreversible decision.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

stem: A 41-year-old G3P2 woman attends a tertiary maternal foetal medicine clinic at 13 weeks and 2 days gestation. She declined combined first-trimester screening and instead self-funded non-invasive prenatal testing at 11 weeks. The result has been telephoned through by her general practitioner this morning as a high-probability positive for trisomy 21, with foetal fraction reported at 8.2% and no comment on sex chromosome or other autosomal findings. Trisomy 18 and 13 results were low risk. Her two previous children were chromosomally normal. She has hypothyroidism on thyroxine 100 micrograms daily, has had a previous lower-segment caesarean for breech, and there is no significant family history of inheritable disease. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. An ultrasound today shows a viable singleton pregnancy with CRL consistent with dates, a nuchal translucency of 2.1 mm, present nasal bone, no other structural concerns, and a normally implanted placenta on the posterior uterine wall. She is distressed and asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant for clear advice on what the NIPT result actually means and what the next step should be.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen. Uterus 13-14 week size.
data_table.Investigations: NIPT at 11 weeks: high probability trisomy 21, foetal fraction 8.2%, T18/T13 low risk. Today's USS: viable singleton, CRL 70 mm, NT 2.1 mm, nasal bone present, no structural anomalies, posterior placenta.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for counselling and management?

options.text: Counsel that NIPT has very high positive predictive value at her age and proceed directly to termination of pregnancy if she wishes, without further confirmatory testing
options.rationale: Even at advanced maternal age, NIPT is a SCREENING test not diagnostic. False positives occur (confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal mosaicism, maternal malignancy). Irreversible decisions should not be made on screening alone. The anti-pattern is treating high PPV as 100% PPV (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Proceed directly to amniocentesis today at 13+2 weeks for the most accurate confirmatory result
options.rationale: Amniocentesis before 15 completed weeks is associated with higher foetal loss and talipes (early amniocentesis); CVS is the correct first-trimester diagnostic test. Amniocentesis is correct after 15 weeks if she prefers to wait, with the trade-off of later decisional timing (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Repeat the NIPT on a fresh maternal sample to confirm the result before any consideration of diagnostic testing
options.rationale: Repeating NIPT does not resolve the screening-diagnostic distinction; the same biological mechanism producing the positive (e.g., CPM) will produce the same result. Diagnostic testing (karyotype on tissue) is required. Repeat NIPT is correct only with technical failure (low fetal fraction), not for confirming positive results (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Reassure her because the nuchal translucency today is normal at 2.1 mm and there are no structural anomalies; defer further testing to the 20 week morphology scan
options.rationale: Normal NT and morphology scan reduce but do not exclude T21; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have normal NT and ultrasound is not diagnostic. The anti-pattern is using sonographic 'normality' to rule out aneuploidy. Reassurance from normal scan is correct only with low-risk screening and no flagged findings (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Counsel that NIPT remains a screening test and offer confirmatory diagnostic testing by chorionic villus sampling now, or amniocentesis from 15 weeks, before any irreversible decision is made
options.rationale: RANZCOG and HGSA both emphasise that a positive NIPT requires diagnostic confirmation by CVS or amniocentesis before any decisions about pregnancy continuation. PPV for T21 with high-probability NIPT is high in older women but not 100%, and false positives occur through placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal copy number variants and rarely maternal malignancy. CVS from 10 to 13+6 weeks is the earliest diagnostic option (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

explanation.summary: A positive NIPT is a high-probability screening result that must be confirmed by diagnostic testing (CVS or amniocentesis) before any irreversible decision. Foetal fraction above 4% is technically adequate; the test still has false positives through confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin and maternal causes.
key_points: NIPT sensitivity ~99% for T21, specificity ~99.9%; PPV varies with prior risk
Foetal fraction below 4% is a no-call/low-confidence result; 8.2% is adequate
Causes of false positive NIPT: confined placental mosaicism, vanishing twin, maternal CNV, rarely maternal malignancy
CVS 10 to 13+6 weeks; amniocentesis from 15 weeks
Procedure-related miscarriage 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units
explanation.pearls: When telephoning a positive NIPT result, always say 'high-probability screening positive - we need a diagnostic test to confirm'. Never imply diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic testing - CVS vs amniocentesis timing and risk
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman is reviewed in a maternal foetal medicine clinic at 11 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain dates and a confirmatory ultrasound. Her combined first-trimester screen returned a risk of 1 in 60 for trisomy 21, with a nuchal translucency of 3.6 mm and PAPP-A of 0.3 MoM. The foetus has a present nasal bone and no structural anomaly is visible on this morning's ultrasound. She has reviewed both contingent NIPT and definitive diagnostic options with her midwife and consultant and has decided that she wants a definitive answer in the shortest time possible because she has finite leave from work and significant anxiety. Her previous child has Down syndrome diagnosed postnatally and she does not want to wait until 15 weeks. The placenta is anterior and easily accessible by transabdominal route. She is RhD positive, has no allergies, and is fit and well. She asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what its principal procedure-related risk is.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, anterior accessible placenta on ultrasound today.
data_table.Investigations: cFTS adjusted T21 risk 1 in 60. NT 3.6 mm. PAPP-A 0.3 MoM. USS today: CRL consistent with 11+5, no structural anomalies, anterior placenta.

lead_in: Which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what is the approximate procedure-related miscarriage risk in an experienced Australian unit?

options.text: Transcervical chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 2 to 3%
options.rationale: Transcervical CVS has slightly higher infection and miscarriage rates than transabdominal in most series and is used when placenta is posterior and inaccessible transabdominally. 2-3% overstates the risk in experienced units; modern figure is 0.1-0.2%. The anti-pattern is using old textbook risk figures (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Amniocentesis today at 11+5 weeks, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.5 to 1%
options.rationale: Amniocentesis before 15 weeks (early amniocentesis) is no longer offered because of higher foetal loss and talipes equinovarus per the Canadian Early Amniocentesis Trial. CVS is the correct T1 option. Early amniocentesis is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Defer diagnostic testing and arrange NIPT today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero
options.rationale: NIPT is non-invasive but remains a SCREENING test; she has already had high-risk cFTS and explicitly wants a definitive answer. Offering only NIPT does not meet her stated need. NIPT is correct as contingent screen if she preferred to defer diagnostic testing (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Transabdominal chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.1 to 0.2%
options.rationale: CVS is performed from 10 to 13+6 weeks (most commonly 11 to 13 weeks); transabdominal CVS is preferred when the placenta is accessible. Australian and international meta-analyses estimate procedure-related miscarriage in experienced units at 0.1 to 0.2%, similar to amniocentesis (RANZCOG; HGSA).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

options.text: Defer diagnostic testing until 18 to 20 weeks and proceed to morphology scan only, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero
options.rationale: Morphology scan is a screening tool, not diagnostic for aneuploidy, and does not satisfy her wish for early definitive information; up to 50% of T21 foetuses have a normal morphology scan. Deferring to morphology-only would be correct only if she explicitly declined diagnostic testing (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: CVS (10 to 13+6 weeks) and amniocentesis (from 15 weeks) are the two diagnostic options for foetal chromosomal analysis. Procedure-related miscarriage risk is approximately 0.1 to 0.2% in experienced units for both. Early amniocentesis (before 15 weeks) is contraindicated due to higher foetal loss and talipes.
key_points: CVS window: 10 to 13+6 weeks, transabdominal preferred if placenta accessible
Amniocentesis from 15 weeks; never before 15 due to Canadian Early Amnio Trial findings
Procedure-related miscarriage 0.1 to 0.2% for either in experienced units
Anti-D 625 IU IM after any procedure in an RhD-negative woman
QF-PCR results in 24 to 48 hours for common aneuploidies; full karyotype 1 to 2 weeks; microarray 1 to 2 weeks
explanation.pearls: If the placenta is posterior and inaccessible at 11 to 13 weeks, wait two weeks for amniocentesis rather than attempting transcervical CVS routinely.

sources.label: RANZCOG Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing
HGSA/RANZCOG Joint statement on prenatal screening

subtopic_detail: Anti-D prophylaxis - sensitising event before 12 weeks
stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman presents to a regional Australian emergency department with a single episode of moderate per-vaginal bleeding requiring two pads in two hours, associated with mild lower abdominal cramping. Her last menstrual period dates her at 9 weeks and 1 day, and a transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine pregnancy with CRL consistent with 8+6 weeks, present foetal cardiac activity at 158 bpm, no retained products in the uterus and a small subchorionic haematoma. There is no cervical motion tenderness, the os is closed and no products of conception are visible. Her booking blood group from the GP last week is documented as O RhD negative with a negative antibody screen, and she had no prior pregnancies carried beyond miscarriage at 6 weeks one year ago. The general practice doctor in ED asks the obstetric registrar whether routine anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin is indicated for this episode and at what dose, given Australian National Blood Authority guidance.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, mild suprapubic tenderness. Speculum: small amount of fresh blood, os closed, no products. No CMT.
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O RhD negative, antibody screen negative. TV USS: viable IUP at 8+6, FHR 158 bpm, small subchorionic haematoma, no retained products. Beta-hCG 28 000 IU/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin management today?

options.text: Administer 625 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now, since any bleeding in an RhD-negative woman after 9 weeks is a sensitising event
options.rationale: Australian NBA guideline states anti-D is not routinely indicated for a sensitising event before 12 completed weeks, EXCEPT for CVS, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy, or termination of pregnancy. The anti-pattern is reflexive anti-D for any bleed regardless of gestation. Anti-D in T1 is correct for the specific exceptions only (NBA Anti-D guidelines).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 1500 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now as a single antenatal dose covering both 28 week and 34 week prophylaxis until delivery
options.rationale: 1500 IU is the UK single-dose antenatal regimen at 28 weeks; Australia uses TWO-dose 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks, not single 1500 IU. The anti-pattern is conflating UK and Australian regimens. 1500 IU single dose is correct only in UK practice (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer 250 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now and order a Kleihauer-Betke test today to titrate any further required dose
options.rationale: 250 IU is the UK dose; the Australian dose for first-trimester sensitising events (when indicated, e.g., after CVS) is 250 IU pre-12 weeks per NBA. Kleihauer testing is recommended for events from 20 weeks, not first-trimester bleeding. The anti-pattern is using Kleihauer in T1 (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Anti-D is not indicated today; she continues routine 28 and 34 week prophylaxis of 625 IU IM with 625 IU post-event from 12 weeks onwards
options.rationale: Per the National Blood Authority guidelines, sensitising events before 12 completed weeks (threatened or complete miscarriage with no instrumentation, minor uterine bleeding) do not require anti-D. Routine antenatal anti-D 625 IU IM is given at 28 and 34 weeks, and 625 IU IM is given for any sensitising event from 12 weeks onwards (with Kleihauer-Betke if >20 weeks to quantify additional dose) (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Defer all anti-D decisions until 12 weeks gestation, then administer 625 IU IM only if vaginal bleeding persists or recurs at that time
options.rationale: Anti-D is given for sensitising events when they occur, not prophylactically deferred; routine antenatal prophylaxis begins at 28 weeks, not 12. The anti-pattern is conflating event-based dosing with routine prophylaxis schedules. Deferral is essentially never correct in anti-D management (NBA).
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Australian anti-D guidance (NBA) does not recommend anti-D for sensitising events before 12 completed weeks except CVS, ectopic, molar pregnancy or termination. From 12 weeks, give 625 IU IM after a sensitising event within 72 hours. Routine antenatal prophylaxis is 625 IU IM at both 28 and 34 weeks, and 625 IU IM after birth if the baby is RhD positive (Kleihauer if >20 weeks event to titrate).
key_points: Pre-12 week sensitising events: anti-D only for CVS, ectopic, molar, ToP
12 to 20 weeks: 625 IU IM after sensitising event (within 72 hours)
>20 weeks: 625 IU IM plus Kleihauer-Betke to quantify additional dose
Routine antenatal: 625 IU IM at 28 AND 34 weeks (two-dose Australian regimen)
Postnatal: 625 IU IM if baby RhD positive within 72 hours
explanation.pearls: Australian 625 IU IM is not interchangeable with the UK 1500 IU or 250 IU regimens - always cite the NBA guideline in Australian practice.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

subtopic_detail: GBS screening - intrapartum prophylaxis with penicillin allergy
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman presents to the birth suite at 38 weeks and 5 days gestation in established labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes and intact membranes. Her vaginal-rectal swab taken at 36 weeks for universal group B Streptococcus screening was positive for Streptococcus agalactiae, sensitive to penicillin and reported as resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin. Her chart documents an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (generalised urticaria and tongue swelling without anaphylaxis) following oral amoxicillin in childhood, judged by the allergy team as high-risk for severe penicillin allergy. Her previous baby is well at age 3 with no neonatal sepsis. Vital signs are normal, the baby is cephalic and engaged, foetal heart rate is reassuring on intermittent auscultation, and the obstetric registrar wants to commence intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis. The midwife asks which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate given the patient's penicillin allergy and the documented GBS antibiotic sensitivities.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, fully effaced, vertex at -1 station. Contractions 3 in 10 strong.
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks positive, sensitive to penicillin, resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin. FBC normal. Group O RhD positive.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regimen?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth as standard first-line intrapartum prophylaxis
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin is first-line in non-allergic women but is contraindicated by her documented immediate hypersensitivity reaction (anaphylaxis-spectrum). The anti-pattern is overlooking documented severe allergy. Benzylpenicillin is correct in non-allergic GBS-positive women (RANZCOG GBS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until birth as the recommended alternative for low-risk penicillin allergy
options.rationale: Cefazolin is the alternative in women with LOW-RISK penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic, non-immediate). Her immediate hypersensitivity reaction with mucosal involvement is high-risk and cephalosporins are not recommended due to cross-reactivity risk. Cefazolin is correct only in low-risk allergy (rash without mucosal/anaphylaxis features) (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) loading then 15 mg/kg every 12 hours until birth
options.rationale: For high-risk penicillin allergy with GBS resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin, IV vancomycin is the recommended intrapartum prophylaxis in Australia. Dosing is weight-based loading then maintenance, with at least 4 hours before birth ideally for adequate cord blood levels (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours until birth, given the documented high-risk penicillin allergy and inability to use beta-lactams
options.rationale: Clindamycin is an option in high-risk penicillin allergy only when GBS sensitivity to clindamycin is CONFIRMED on testing; her isolate is resistant. The anti-pattern is using clindamycin without sensitivity testing (which is required for GBS isolates). Clindamycin is correct with documented sensitivity (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral erythromycin 500 mg every 6 hours until birth, given documented penicillin allergy and need to avoid intravenous antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Oral therapy does not achieve adequate cord blood levels for intrapartum prophylaxis, and her GBS isolate is resistant to erythromycin. The anti-pattern is using oral antibiotics in labour. Oral erythromycin has no role in intrapartum GBS prophylaxis (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Australia uses universal vaginal-rectal GBS screening at 35 to 37 weeks. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is given to GBS-positive women, prior GBS-affected baby, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, intrapartum fever, preterm labour <37 weeks with unknown status, or ROM >=18 hours with unknown status.
key_points: First line: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours (or ampicillin 2 g load then 1 g every 4 hours)
Low-risk penicillin allergy: IV cefazolin 2 g load then 1 g every 8 hours
High-risk penicillin allergy with clindamycin-sensitive GBS: IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours
High-risk penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant GBS: IV vancomycin
Aim for >=4 hours of antibiotic before birth for adequate cord levels
explanation.pearls: Always check the GBS isolate sensitivities at the time of the positive swab - inducible clindamycin resistance is common and is the reason vancomycin is the safe fallback.

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practice for review of her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test performed at 26 weeks gestation as routine ADIPS-recommended screening. She has no prior diabetes and a body mass index of 28 kg/m2. Her parents both have type 2 diabetes on metformin. She has had no symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia or weight loss. Her morphology scan at 19 weeks was normal, with foetal biometry on the 60th centile. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. The OGTT was performed after an overnight 10-hour fast at a local pathology collection centre with serial venous plasma glucose samples at 0, 60 and 120 minutes after the 75 g glucose load. Her fasting venous plasma glucose was 5.3 mmol/L, the 1-hour value was 9.6 mmol/L and the 2-hour value was 7.9 mmol/L. She has no acute symptoms, blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg and the foetal heart rate today is 142 bpm on bedside auscultation. She asks the general practitioner whether the test indicates gestational diabetes by Australian (ADIPS) criteria.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 28 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: Fundus 26 cm. Foetal heart 142 bpm. No oedema.
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT at 26 weeks: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.6 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.9 mmol/L. HbA1c 5.5%.

lead_in: Which best describes the interpretation of this OGTT result against current ADIPS criteria?

options.text: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed because the fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: Current ADIPS diagnostic thresholds for the 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks are fasting >=5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour >=10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; any one threshold met or exceeded is diagnostic. Her fasting value of 5.3 meets the fasting criterion (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because no single value reaches the WHO threshold of 7.0 mmol/L fasting or 11.1 mmol/L at 2 hours
options.rationale: WHO 7.0/11.1 thresholds are for OVERT diabetes diagnosis outside pregnancy; Australian ADIPS thresholds for GDM are 5.1/10.0/8.5. The anti-pattern is applying non-pregnancy thresholds to pregnancy. WHO 7.0 cut-off is correct for diagnosing overt diabetes in early pregnancy (a separate diagnostic category from GDM) (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because at least two values must meet thresholds under ADIPS criteria
options.rationale: ADIPS adopts the IADPSG single-value rule: any single value at or above threshold is diagnostic; two abnormal values are NOT required. The anti-pattern is requiring multiple abnormalities (old WHO/Carpenter-Coustan two-step criteria). One-value rule is the Australian standard (ADIPS).

options.text: Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because the 1-hour value of 9.6 mmol/L is below the 10.0 mmol/L threshold; the fasting value of 5.3 is within the normal pregnancy range
options.rationale: 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1; meeting ANY ONE threshold is diagnostic regardless of the other two values. The anti-pattern is focusing on the most-abnormal-looking value rather than applying threshold rules. All three values >= thresholds are not required (ADIPS).

options.text: The diagnosis should be deferred and HbA1c used to confirm; HbA1c of 5.5% rules out gestational diabetes
options.rationale: HbA1c is not used as the primary diagnostic test for GDM (lacks sensitivity in T2 due to increased red cell turnover); OGTT remains the standard. HbA1c is correct for early-pregnancy screening for overt T2DM (>=6.5%), not as GDM diagnostic test (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: Australia uses ADIPS thresholds for the 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L. Any one value meeting threshold diagnoses GDM. Early OGTT at booking is offered to women with risk factors (prior GDM, BMI >=30, previous macrosomic baby, family history, certain ethnicities).
key_points: ADIPS OGTT thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0, 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L
Any single value meeting threshold = GDM
Standard screening at 24 to 28 weeks; early OGTT at booking for high-risk women
Management ladder: diet/exercise, then metformin and/or insulin; insulin is first-line agent if pharmacotherapy needed in many AU centres
Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6 to 12 weeks to detect persistent diabetes; lifelong screening every 1 to 3 years
explanation.pearls: Memorise 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 for ADIPS; do not use older Australian thresholds (e.g. ADIPS 1998 5.5/8.0) or international cut-offs.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman attends an early pregnancy assessment service with a referral from her general practitioner. She is at 10 weeks and 3 days gestation by certain last menstrual period, confirmed by a dating ultrasound at 7 weeks that showed CRL of 9 mm with present foetal cardiac activity. She has had minimal brown vaginal spotting for the last week but no heavy bleeding or pain, and has noticed that breast tenderness and nausea (previously prominent) have resolved over the past 10 days. She has no fever, no haemodynamic instability and no significant past medical history. Examination shows a soft, non-tender abdomen with a closed cervical os, no active bleeding and no products of conception visible. The transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine gestational sac with mean sac diameter 28 mm, a foetal pole with crown-rump length of 12 mm and no foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on careful M-mode assessment by an accredited sonographer. There is no retained tissue, no subchorionic haematoma and the cervix is closed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen. Speculum: minimal brown discharge, os closed, no products. No CMT.
data_table.Investigations: TV USS: single IUP, MSD 28 mm, CRL 12 mm, no foetal cardiac activity on M-mode. Cervix closed. No retained tissue.

lead_in: Which classification best describes this clinical and ultrasound picture?

options.text: Threatened miscarriage - viable intrauterine pregnancy with bleeding and closed cervix
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage requires confirmation of foetal VIABILITY; the foetal pole has CRL 12 mm with no cardiac activity, diagnostic of foetal demise. The anti-pattern is calling any bleeding-with-closed-os 'threatened' without checking viability. Threatened is correct with confirmed viable IUP plus bleeding plus closed os (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Missed miscarriage - foetal demise diagnosed on ultrasound with retained pregnancy tissue and a closed cervical os
options.rationale: Missed (silent) miscarriage is defined as ultrasound confirmation of foetal demise (CRL >=7 mm with no cardiac activity, or MSD >=25 mm with no foetal pole on transvaginal scan) with retained pregnancy tissue and a closed cervix. Her CRL of 12 mm with no cardiac activity meets the diagnostic ASUM/RCOG criteria (RANZCOG; ASUM).

options.text: Inevitable miscarriage - bleeding with open cervix and foetal demise on ultrasound
options.rationale: Inevitable miscarriage requires an OPEN cervical os; her os is closed. The anti-pattern is conflating clinical labels. Inevitable is correct with open os, viable or non-viable pregnancy retained, often with bleeding (RANZCOG).

options.text: Incomplete miscarriage - retained products of conception following partial expulsion of pregnancy tissue
options.rationale: Incomplete miscarriage requires that pregnancy tissue has been partially passed, with retained products on US; she has had no significant bleeding and the pregnancy tissue is intact. The anti-pattern is over-using 'incomplete' label. Incomplete is correct after partial expulsion with retained POC (RANZCOG).

options.text: Complete miscarriage - all products of conception passed with an empty uterus and resolving symptoms
options.rationale: Complete miscarriage requires an empty uterus on US following passage of all products; her uterus contains the foetal pole and gestational sac. The anti-pattern is calling tissue-retained scenarios 'complete'. Complete miscarriage is correct with empty uterus and resolving symptoms (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Miscarriage classification is based on clinical features, cervical os status and ultrasound findings. Missed miscarriage is foetal demise with retained tissue and closed os, often with resolution of pregnancy symptoms. Diagnostic ultrasound criteria are CRL >=7 mm with no cardiac activity OR MSD >=25 mm with no foetal pole on TV scan.
key_points: Threatened: viable IUP, bleeding, closed os
Inevitable: open os, bleeding, often POC at os
Incomplete: partial passage, retained POC on USS
Complete: empty uterus on USS, resolving symptoms/hCG
Missed: foetal demise, closed os, retained tissue
Diagnostic USS: CRL >=7 mm + no FH, or MSD >=25 mm + no foetal pole on TV
explanation.pearls: Always confirm foetal demise with a second opinion or repeat scan 7 to 14 days later if the CRL is borderline (close to 7 mm) before offering definitive management.

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis - severity assessment and admission criteria
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman at 10 weeks and 2 days gestation presents to an emergency department with three weeks of progressively worsening nausea and vomiting. Over the last 48 hours she has been unable to keep down oral fluids, has vomited at least eight times per day, and has lost 4 kg from her booking weight of 62 kg (>5%). She is exhausted, intermittently tearful, and has missed two days of work. Her partner reports she has had episodes of postural light-headedness. Examination shows dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 to 3 seconds, mild ketotic breath, HR 108 bpm, BP 100/62 mmHg lying and 88/55 mmHg standing, T 36.7 degC. Urinalysis shows ketones 3+, specific gravity 1.030, no nitrites or leucocytes. Her PUQE-24 score today is 14 out of 15 (severe). Bloods reveal sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, urea 7.8 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. Beta-hCG and TFTs are pending. A transabdominal scan shows a viable singleton pregnancy at 10+2 with no molar features. She has tried oral pyridoxine 25 mg TDS at home with no relief.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 100/62 lying, 88/55 standing, HR 108 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, CR 2 to 3s, ketotic breath. Soft abdomen. Uterus not yet palpable.
data_table.Investigations: PUQE-24 14/15 (severe). Weight loss 4 kg (>5%). Urine ketones 3+, SG 1.030. Na 132, K 3.1, urea 7.8, creat 92. USS: viable singleton, no molar features.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Admit for IV normal saline rehydration, IV thiamine before any dextrose, IV potassium replacement, and stepwise antiemetics with serial electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: She meets admission criteria for hyperemesis gravidarum: severe PUQE, >5% weight loss, dehydration with postural hypotension, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and failed oral therapy. Treatment is IV rehydration (commonly normal saline with KCl), thiamine 100 mg IV/IM daily before any dextrose to prevent Wernicke's, a stepwise antiemetic (pyridoxine-doxylamine, metoclopramide or prochlorperazine, ondansetron), and serial electrolyte and weight monitoring (RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hyperemesis).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Discharge with oral doxylamine-pyridoxine and a metoclopramide PRN script with a written safety-net to return for ongoing vomiting
options.rationale: Severe PUQE, postural hypotension, hypokalaemia, ketonuria, and >5% weight loss are explicit admission indications. Outpatient antiemetics will fail. The anti-pattern is under-treating hyperemesis as 'morning sickness'. Outpatient management is correct in mild-moderate hyperemesis without dehydration or weight loss (RANZCOG; SOMANZ Hyperemesis).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Admit for total parenteral nutrition via a central venous catheter to bypass the gut entirely while severe vomiting resolves over coming days
options.rationale: TPN is reserved for women who fail standard rehydration and antiemetic regimens with persistent weight loss; it carries significant infection/thromboembolism risk (PICC complications). The anti-pattern is escalating to TPN before exhausting stepwise antiemetics. TPN is correct only after failure of standard escalation (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).

options.text: Admit for IV dextrose 5% rehydration to provide immediate energy substrate and commence IV ondansetron monotherapy without giving prior thiamine
options.rationale: Dextrose-first rehydration without thiamine cover risks WERNICKE'S ENCEPHALOPATHY in hyperemesis - a critical preventable iatrogenic injury. Use saline; give thiamine BEFORE any dextrose. The classic 'glucose before thiamine' anti-pattern is essentially never correct in malnourished/hyperemesis patients (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Arrange urgent termination of pregnancy at 10 weeks as the only definitive treatment for refractory severe vomiting unresponsive to oral therapy
options.rationale: Termination is rarely indicated and only after exhausting medical management with the woman's informed wish; the great majority of hyperemesis resolves by 16-20 weeks. The anti-pattern is offering ToP without trial of escalating therapy. ToP is correct only after refractory disease with maternal informed decision (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
