explanation.summary: Admission criteria for hyperemesis: severe PUQE-24, >5% weight loss, persistent vomiting despite oral antiemetics, ketonuria, dehydration with postural hypotension or tachycardia, electrolyte derangement (hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia), or inability to tolerate oral therapy. Treatment is IV rehydration with thiamine before dextrose, antiemetic ladder and electrolyte correction.
key_points: PUQE-24: 12+ = severe
Antiemetic ladder: pyridoxine-doxylamine -> metoclopramide / prochlorperazine -> ondansetron -> corticosteroids (refractory)
Thiamine 100 mg IV/IM daily BEFORE any dextrose (Wernicke prevention)
VTE prophylaxis on admission
Investigate for molar pregnancy, multiple pregnancy, thyroid storm if atypical features
explanation.pearls: Always check TFTs and exclude molar/multiple pregnancy in severe hyperemesis; suppressed TSH from beta-hCG cross-reactivity is common and rarely requires treatment.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her general practitioner at an estimated 11 weeks gestation by uncertain last menstrual period with three weeks of persistent nausea, intermittent vaginal spotting and one episode of passing what she describes as small grape-like tissue. She has no significant medical history. Examination shows a uterus palpable above the symphysis to a size consistent with 14 weeks, soft and non-tender. Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg (previously 110/70 at GP visit at 8 weeks). She has dry mucous membranes and reports mild postural symptoms. A transvaginal ultrasound at the local imaging service today shows a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with multiple small cystic spaces (described as 'snowstorm' appearance), no foetal pole, no foetal cardiac activity, and bilateral theca lutein cysts on the ovaries measuring 4 to 5 cm. The serum beta-hCG is 285 000 IU/L. Thyroid function tests show TSH 0.05 mU/L and free T4 22 pmol/L (mildly elevated). She has been referred to the local gynaecology team and her GP wants to confirm the recognition and what the next steps are.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus size at 14 weeks (LMP-dated 11 weeks). Dry mucosa. Mild postural light-headedness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 285 000 IU/L. TSH 0.05 mU/L, fT4 22 pmol/L. TV USS: heterogeneous 'snowstorm' mass, no foetal pole, bilateral theca lutein cysts 4 to 5 cm.

lead_in: Which sequence of management most accurately reflects current Australian (RANZCOG) guidance for this presentation?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as outpatient with weekly hCG until undetectable, then 6-monthly surveillance for the following 12 months
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line for persistent gestational trophoblastic NEOPLASIA, not for primary evacuation of a hydatidiform mole. Surgical evacuation is the primary treatment. The anti-pattern is using GTN treatment as primary mole management. MTX is correct for persistent GTN after evacuation with rising hCG (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Suction curettage in theatre with histology, weekly hCG until three negative samples then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial), with reliable contraception
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the Australian/New Zealand Gestational Trophoblastic Disease group recommend suction curettage as the primary treatment for hydatidiform mole. Histopathology confirms complete (no foetus, diploid androgenetic) vs partial (foetus or foetal parts, triploid) mole. Surveillance: weekly beta-hCG until three consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete mole) or until one month after normal hCG (partial). Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout because pregnancy obscures rising hCG that would indicate persistent GTN (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG only, given that many molar pregnancies will resolve spontaneously without surgical or medical intervention
options.rationale: Molar pregnancies do not reliably resolve spontaneously; surgical evacuation with histological confirmation is required to confirm diagnosis and remove trophoblastic tissue. The anti-pattern is expectant management of confirmed mole. Expectant management is correct only for very small failed early pregnancies, not molar (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as primary treatment given the high reported risk of choriocarcinoma associated with this histology
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed their family AND have a complete mole, or for refractory disease; suction curettage is standard first-line for women wishing to preserve fertility. Hysterectomy is correct in completed family with high-risk complete mole or persistent disease (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Medical evacuation of the mole with methylergometrine and misoprostol, followed by weekly serum beta-hCG until undetectable for three consecutive weekly samples
options.rationale: Medical evacuation with uterotonics/prostaglandins is associated with HIGHER rates of incomplete evacuation, embolisation of trophoblastic tissue, and persistent GTN. Suction curettage is preferred. The anti-pattern is treating molar pregnancy like missed miscarriage (RANZCOG GTD).

explanation.summary: Hydatidiform mole is treated by suction curettage in theatre with histopathological confirmation. Beta-hCG surveillance follows: weekly until three consecutive negative samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or one month (partial). Reliable contraception is required throughout because pregnancy obscures the rising hCG signal of persistent GTN.
key_points: Complete mole: no foetus, 'snowstorm' USS, hCG often >100 000, 15 to 20% progress to GTN
Partial mole: foetus/foetal parts, triploid, 1 to 5% progress to GTN
Primary management: suction curettage in theatre with histology
Surveillance: weekly hCG until 3 consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial)
Contraception throughout surveillance; avoid pregnancy until cleared
Refer to GTD multidisciplinary service if hCG plateaus, rises, or new metastatic features develop
explanation.pearls: Suspect mole in any first-trimester pregnancy with hyperemesis, early pre-eclampsia, hyperthyroidism, very high hCG, or uterus large for dates - get a TV scan.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 12 weeks is asymptomatic with a normal dipstick today. The booking MSU MC&S sent a week ago reports pure growth of GROUP B STREPTOCOCCUS 10^8 CFU/L (sensitive to penicillin). No symptoms, no costovertebral tenderness, observations normal, no allergies. The GP asks the obstetric registrar what management this asymptomatic finding warrants.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no costovertebral angle tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: MSU MCS: pure growth E. coli 10^8 CFU/L, sensitive to amoxicillin, cefalexin, nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim. Dipstick today negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Treat with oral antibiotics now (penicillin V or amoxicillin) per Therapeutic Guidelines, and arrange intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin GBS prophylaxis at delivery
options.rationale: GBS bacteriuria at any colony count in pregnancy is significant: (1) treat antenatally (asymptomatic bacteriuria) AND (2) it is an indication for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regardless of vaginal-anorectal swab result. Both steps are required (RANZCOG GBS; eTG).

options.text: No treatment as patient is asymptomatic and dipstick is negative
options.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is treated regardless of dipstick (5-10% progress to pyelonephritis if untreated). GBS bacteriuria additionally mandates intrapartum prophylaxis. The anti-pattern is using dipstick negativity to defer treatment. No treatment is correct only outside pregnancy with no symptoms (RANZCOG).

options.text: Treat with antibiotics now but no intrapartum prophylaxis required
options.rationale: GBS bacteriuria is an INDEPENDENT indication for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis at delivery; antenatal treatment does not eliminate this need (the rectovaginal colonisation persists). The anti-pattern is confusing antenatal treatment with intrapartum prophylaxis - they are distinct interventions (RANZCOG GBS).

options.text: Repeat MSU in 4 weeks before any treatment to confirm the finding
options.rationale: Pregnant ASB warrants prompt empirical treatment, not deferral; growth at 10^8 CFU/L is diagnostic. The anti-pattern is repeat testing for confirmation in a clear-cut diagnosis. Repeat MSU is correct only for borderline results <10^5 CFU/mL or contamination concern (eTG).

options.text: Commence continuous suppressive nitrofurantoin until delivery
options.rationale: Continuous suppression is reserved for recurrent UTI in pregnancy after multiple confirmed episodes, not single ASB. Nitrofurantoin is also avoided near term (haemolysis risk). Continuous suppression is correct after >=2 confirmed UTIs in pregnancy (eTG).

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic bacteriuria affects 2 to 10% of pregnancies and carries a 20 to 30% risk of progression to pyelonephritis if untreated, plus an increased risk of preterm birth and low birthweight. Treat with a short course of a culture-directed agent and confirm clearance with a test-of-cure MSU.
key_points: Significant bacteriuria: single MSU with pure growth >=10^8 CFU/L
Dipstick has poor sensitivity (~50%); MSU MCS at booking is the standard
First-line agents (AU): cefalexin, nitrofurantoin (not at term), amoxicillin
Avoid trimethoprim in first trimester (folate antagonist); avoid nitrofurantoin at term
Test of cure 1 week post-treatment; recurrence -> investigate and consider suppression
explanation.pearls: Always check the booking MSU result actively at the 12 to 16 week visit - it is the commonest treatable cause of preterm birth and pyelonephritis that gets missed by dipstick.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 attends her 28-week antenatal visit. Her partner attends every visit, answers questions for her, and asked the midwife last visit to keep him informed of all her results. Today she is quiet, has a fading bruise on her left forearm she attributes to a kitchen accident, and SFH/foetal heart are appropriate. The clinic has a private room, an interpreter line and an on-site social worker. The midwife steps out and asks the GP registrar what to do next; the partner is in the room with her.

data_table.Examination: SFH 28 cm, foetal heart 142, longitudinal lie, cephalic presentation; healing 4 cm bruise left forearm
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, MCV 84 fL, urinalysis NAD
data_table.Social: Lives with partner and 3-year-old son; partner attends all visits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to enable safe domestic violence enquiry?

options.text: Ask the screening questions in front of the partner to demonstrate transparency and gather his perspective
options.rationale: Asking in the presence of the suspected perpetrator places the woman at increased risk of escalation and produces unreliable answers; the classic IPV-screening anti-pattern. Partner-present enquiry is essentially never correct in IPV screening (RACGP IPV; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

options.text: Find a clinical reason to see her alone (e.g. urine sample, listen to baby in another room) and enquire privately using a validated framework such as HARK or RADAR
options.rationale: Routine antenatal IPV enquiry must occur in private without the partner; a face-saving clinical reason to separate them, in an environment with social-work backup, is the recommended approach (RACGP; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

options.text: Defer the enquiry to the postnatal home visit, where the partner is less likely to attend
options.rationale: Antenatal enquiry is recommended specifically because pregnancy is a high-risk period for IPV escalation; deferring to postnatal misses the engagement window. Postnatal-only enquiry is correct only when antenatal opportunity is missed (RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

options.text: Avoid direct enquiry today and place a 1800RESPECT leaflet in her hand-held antenatal record for her to see
options.rationale: Visible leaflets in shared records are unsafe if the partner reviews them; safe enquiry in private is recommended. Written information is correct only when given discreetly during private consultation, not via shared documents (RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

options.text: Contact NSW Police immediately because the forearm bruise constitutes evidence of an assault
options.rationale: Mandatory police reporting against the woman's wishes endangers her and damages the therapeutic alliance; she must consent to any external action. Police involvement is correct only with the woman's consent or where mandatory child-protection thresholds apply (RACGP; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

explanation.summary: Safe DV enquiry requires the woman be alone, a validated framework be used, the disclosure be documented in a non-shared place, and immediate referral pathways be activated. Antenatal pregnancy is a peak-risk window for escalation (RANZCOG C-Obs 57; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).
key_points: Never enquire with the partner present (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
Use validated tool: HARK, AAS, or abbreviated WAST (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
Document outside shared/hand-held notes (NSW Health)
Warm referral to 1800RESPECT and on-site social worker (1800RESPECT)
Pregnancy is a peak risk window for first-time and escalation DV (RANZCOG C-Obs 57)
explanation.pearls: If the partner refuses to leave, use a urine sample request or imaging excuse to create a private window (NSW Health).

tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 Aboriginal woman from a regional NSW community books for antenatal care at 11 weeks. She has no medical conditions, BMI 24, supportive partner, lives with her mother and cousins. She tells the booking midwife she felt unsafe at a previous gynaecology appointment because she did not feel listened to and had to bring her own information; she has missed two prior follow-up appointments at other services for the same reason. The hospital offers: standard midwifery group practice (MGP); a local Aboriginal Maternal and Infant Health Service (AMIHS) co-led by an Aboriginal Health Worker and continuity midwife linked with the regional ACCHO; obstetrician-led clinic; GP shared care; and telehealth. All five pathways can access morphology USS, OGTT and specialist obstetric review.

data_table.Booking bloods: Hb 122 g/L, blood group O Rh+, rubella immune, syphilis EIA non-reactive, HIV negative, HBsAg negative
data_table.Social: Lives with mother and cousins; supportive partner; previous disengagement from non-culturally-safe services

lead_in: Which model of care is most likely to optimise engagement and outcomes for this woman?

options.text: AMIHS continuity-of-carer model integrating midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage
options.rationale: Birthing-on-Country and AMIHS-type continuity-of-carer models have evidence for improved engagement, reduced preterm birth and improved cultural safety for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women. Given her history of disengagement from non-culturally-safe services, this is the best option (RANZCOG; AHMAC Birthing on Country).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

options.text: Standard midwifery group practice with referral to a separate Aboriginal Health Worker as needed
options.rationale: Bolting on an AHW as a separate referral is less effective than an integrated model where AHW and midwife share continuity from start; the anti-pattern is treating cultural safety as 'add-on'. Standard midwifery with AHW referral is correct only when AMIHS is unavailable (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

options.text: Obstetrician-led care because all Aboriginal pregnancies are inherently high-risk and require specialist supervision
options.rationale: Generalising all Aboriginal pregnancies as high-risk is racist and contradicts evidence; risk should be individually assessed. Obstetrician-led care is correct based on individual clinical risk factors, not ethnicity (RANZCOG; AHMAC).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

options.text: GP shared care with the regional GP to minimise hospital contact
options.rationale: GP shared care is a valid model but does not provide the integrated cultural and clinical continuity that AMIHS offers. GP shared care is correct in low-risk pregnancies where cultural safety is met by other means (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

options.text: Telehealth-only antenatal care to reduce her travel burden regardless of her preference
options.rationale: Telehealth is an adjunct, not a substitute for face-to-face culturally safe care; removing in-person AHW/midwife contact reduces engagement. Telehealth is correct as supplement to face-to-face care, not replacement (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

explanation.summary: AMIHS / Birthing on Country / Bubup-Wilam type continuity-of-carer models combining midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage, have Australian evidence for improved antenatal attendance, reduced preterm birth, increased breastfeeding, and improved cultural safety (NSW Health AMIHS Evaluation; RANZCOG W-WH 22; NACCHO).
key_points: AMIHS = NSW model; analogous services exist in Vic (Koori Maternity), Qld (Birthing in Our Community)
Continuity-of-carer is the active ingredient (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Aboriginal identity is not itself a risk factor (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Closing the Gap target 2 measures healthy birthweight (Closing the Gap)
Offer choice; do not force into Aboriginal-specific service if the woman prefers standard care
explanation.pearls: Birthing on Country is a movement returning birthing services to community control; it is not a single model but a principle (NACCHO).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander antenatal care - culturally safe model

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 Aboriginal woman from a remote NT community presents for booking at 12 weeks. She has a documented history of acute rheumatic fever aged 10 with subsequent moderate mitral regurgitation on echo last year and is on monthly intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin (BPG) 1.2 million units secondary prophylaxis through the local clinic. Her last dose was 2 weeks ago. She has had one previous uncomplicated SVD at term with no cardiac decompensation, breastfed for 12 months, and is currently well with no exertional dyspnoea, no orthopnoea, and NYHA class I. Examination: BP 108/64, HR 82, soft pansystolic murmur at apex unchanged from previous, no peripheral oedema, clear chest. Repeat booking echo shows unchanged moderate MR, mild LA dilation, normal LV function, and pulmonary artery pressure 28 mmHg. She asks whether she should continue the monthly injections during pregnancy because a friend told her to stop because the penicillin might harm the baby.

data_table.Examination: Soft PSM apex unchanged; no signs of failure; SFH appropriate; foetal heart 156
data_table.Investigations: Echo: moderate MR, mild LA dilation, normal LVEF 60%, PASP 28 mmHg; Hb 120 g/L; ECG sinus
data_table.Medications: Benzathine benzylpenicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 28 days; pregnancy multivitamin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding her secondary prophylaxis during pregnancy?

options.text: Continue 4-weekly IM BPG at the standard dose throughout pregnancy and the postpartum period
options.rationale: Benzathine benzylpenicillin does not cross the placenta in clinically meaningful amounts and is safe and recommended throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding; the dosing interval is unchanged. Multidisciplinary cardiac-obstetric review is required for any rheumatic heart disease in pregnancy (RHDAustralia Guideline; eTG Antibiotic; RANZCOG; NACCHO).

options.text: Switch to oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg BD for the duration of pregnancy to avoid IM injections
options.rationale: Oral prophylaxis has higher failure rates due to adherence and absorption; IM BPG is preferred. Oral PenV is correct only when IM cannot be tolerated and adherence is supervised (RHDAustralia Guideline).

options.text: Cease benzathine benzylpenicillin prophylaxis until after delivery because penicillin readily crosses the placenta
options.rationale: Penicillin is safe across all trimesters; cessation risks ARF recurrence with permanent valve worsening. Cessation is essentially never correct in active RHD secondary prophylaxis (RHDAustralia Guideline; AMH).

options.text: Shorten the BPG interval to every 3 weeks because pregnancy increases drug clearance and lowers troughs
options.rationale: Standard 4-weekly interval is retained for routine prophylaxis; 3-weekly is reserved for documented breakthrough ARF or selected high-risk situations per specialist advice, not pregnancy alone. 3-weekly is correct after documented breakthrough recurrence (RHDAustralia Guideline).

options.text: Convert to azithromycin 1 g monthly as an alternative because penicillin allergy is more common in pregnancy
options.rationale: Macrolides are only used for documented penicillin allergy; pregnancy does not cause allergy. Azithromycin monthly is correct only in confirmed penicillin allergy (RHDAustralia Guideline).

explanation.summary: Continuation of 4-weekly IM BPG through pregnancy and lactation is the standard of care; multidisciplinary cardiac-obstetric review is recommended for moderate or severe RHD (RHDAustralia Guideline; RANZCOG W-WH 22).
key_points: BPG is Pregnancy Category A and safe in lactation (AMH)
Cessation risks ARF recurrence and progressive valve damage (RHDAustralia)
Moderate/severe RHD requires combined obstetric-cardiac team (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
Echocardiography baseline + each trimester for moderate or severe disease (RHDAustralia)
Anaesthetic and intrapartum planning at 28-32 weeks (RANZCOG W-WH 22)
explanation.pearls: ARF/RHD remains a leading cardiac cause of maternal morbidity in Aboriginal women; antenatal interruption of prophylaxis is a recurring contributor to adverse outcome (RHDAustralia).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - RHD secondary prophylaxis in pregnancy

stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman in regional Victoria presents at 23+1 weeks by dating ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. Morphology ultrasound at 21 weeks showed a foetal cardiac anomaly initially read as a ventricular septal defect, but on tertiary review at 23 weeks was confirmed as hypoplastic left heart syndrome with associated coarctation. She has discussed the diagnosis with paediatric cardiology, including the staged Norwood pathway and its long-term survival and morbidity, and feels unable to proceed with the pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities, her support network is intact, she has accessed counselling, and her first pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage. She specifically asks about the legal pathway in Victoria, whether she must travel interstate, and whether an ethics committee or panel needs to approve the procedure before she can be referred to the tertiary service She has no contraindication to mifepristone or prostaglandin induction.

data_table.Examination: SFH 24 cm; uterus consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: Tertiary foetal echo: hypoplastic left heart syndrome with coarctation; karyotype normal; Hb 120 g/L

lead_in: Which statement best describes the lawful pathway for termination of pregnancy in Victoria at this gestation?

options.text: Termination is lawful on request without any additional consultation up to 24 weeks gestation in Victoria
options.rationale: Victorian Act permits TOP on request up to 24 weeks; this is correct on the law, but BEST practice for advanced gestation with foetal anomaly is the two-doctor tertiary pathway (option C). Option A understates the appropriate referral pathway. On-request alone is correct for early gestations without anomaly (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination is unlawful after 20 weeks in Victoria and the patient must travel interstate to obtain care
options.rationale: Victoria has no 20-week prohibition; TOP is on-request to 24 weeks and lawful after 24 weeks with two doctors. The anti-pattern is misremembering interstate variations. The 20-week limit applies in some other states (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination is lawful: she is under the 24-week on-request limit and is referred to a tertiary service
options.rationale: Under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008, TOP is lawful on request up to 24 weeks; after 24 weeks it requires two medical practitioners. At 23+1 weeks she is within the on-request limit. Standard practice for a confirmed major foetal anomaly at this gestation is referral to a tertiary service, which will offer feticide and induction of labour with multidisciplinary support (RANZCOG; Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination requires approval by a formal hospital ethics committee at any gestation in Victoria
options.rationale: Victoria does not require ethics committee approval; the framework is the Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 with practitioner-led decision making. Ethics committee approval was the pre-2008 NSW practice (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

options.text: Termination is only lawful where the foetal anomaly is incompatible with life as defined by statute
options.rationale: No statutory list of qualifying anomalies; the test for TOP after 24 weeks is two-doctor reasonable belief considering all relevant circumstances. A statutory-list framework would be correct only in jurisdictions with such legislation (none in Australia) (Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008).

explanation.summary: Victoria: TOP on-request to 24 weeks; after 24 weeks two doctors must reasonably believe it appropriate considering all relevant circumstances (Abortion Law Reform Act 2008). Late TOP for foetal anomaly is provided in tertiary services with feticide and IOL (RANZCOG; The Royal Women's).
key_points: Victoria: on-request to 24+0 (Abortion Law Reform Act 2008)
NSW: on-request to 22+0; after 22 weeks two specialists must agree (Abortion Law Reform Act 2019 NSW)
Qld: on-request to 22+0; after 22 weeks two doctors agree (Termination of Pregnancy Act 2018 Qld)
SA: on-request to 22+6; after 22+6 two doctors agree (Termination of Pregnancy Act 2021 SA)
WA: on-request to 23+0; after 23 weeks Ministerial panel of two specialists (Public Health Amendment Act 2023 WA)
Tasmania: on-request to 16+0; after 16 weeks two doctors agree (Reproductive Health Act 2013 Tas)
explanation.pearls: All states require conscientious objectors to refer to a non-objecting practitioner (RANZCOG TOP).

subtopic_detail: Termination of pregnancy - Australian state-specific gestational limits

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 presents at her general practice at 7+2 weeks by transvaginal ultrasound, having confirmed an unintended intrauterine pregnancy. She requests medical termination. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, BMI 23, takes no regular medications, has a confirmed intrauterine location with single live embryo CRL consistent with 7+2 weeks, has been screened and consented for medical TOP, and has discussed contraception. The GP is MS-2 Step certified. The patient lives 25 minutes from a hospital with 24-hour emergency services, has a supportive partner who can stay with her for 48 hours, and has clear arrangements for follow-up serum beta-hCG and a 2-week review. She understands the success rate, expected pain and bleeding, warning signs prompting ED return, and the need for confirmatory follow-up. She has read the MS-2 Step consumer information and asks the GP to confirm the exact dosing and interval she should expect.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen; cervix closed; small mobile uterus
data_table.Investigations: TVS: single live IUP, CRL 7+2 weeks; blood group O Rh positive; Hb 128 g/L
data_table.Counselling: TOP options discussed, contraception plan agreed (etonogestrel implant at follow-up)

lead_in: Which medical regimen is correct under the MS-2 Step Australian protocol for this gestation?

options.text: Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed 36 to 48 hours later by misoprostol 800 microg buccal
options.rationale: The MS-2 Step Australian regimen for medical TOP up to 9+0 weeks is mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed 36-48 hours later by misoprostol 800 microg (buccal, sublingual or vaginal). Follow-up at 1-2 weeks with serum hCG or ultrasound (MS-2 Step Product Information; Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Mifepristone 600 mg oral, followed 24 hours later by misoprostol 400 microg oral
options.rationale: 600 mg mifepristone is the older regimen replaced because 200 mg is equally effective. 400 microg oral misoprostol has lower completion rates than 800 microg buccal/sublingual/vaginal. Higher mifepristone dose was the original Roussel-Uclaf regimen, now obsolete (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM, followed 7 days later by misoprostol 800 microg vaginal
options.rationale: Methotrexate-misoprostol is used for medical management of ectopic pregnancy and historically for very early TOP, but is not the registered Australian protocol; MS-2 Step is standard. MTX-miso is correct for ectopic pregnancy management (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg vaginal alone, repeated every 3 hours up to 3 doses
options.rationale: Misoprostol-only regimens have lower success (~85%) than mifepristone-misoprostol (~95-98%); used only when mifepristone is unavailable. Miso-alone is correct in resource-limited settings without mifepristone access (RANZCOG TOP; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed immediately by misoprostol 800 microg buccal in the same visit
options.rationale: Simultaneous administration has lower efficacy than the 36-48 hour interval; the gap is required for mifepristone-induced cervical priming and progesterone receptor antagonism. Same-visit administration is correct only in selected emergency contexts where 36-48 hours wait is impossible (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

explanation.summary: MS-2 Step Australian medical TOP regimen: mifepristone 200 mg oral, then misoprostol 800 microg (buccal/sublingual/vaginal) 36-48 hours later, with follow-up at 1-2 weeks. Effective up to 9+0 weeks (MS-2 Step PI; Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG TOP).
key_points: Up to 9+0 weeks for MS-2 Step (Product Information)
Mifepristone 200 mg oral (not 600) (MS-2 Step PI)
Misoprostol 800 microg buccal/SL/PV at 36-48 h (MS-2 Step PI)
Follow-up at 1-2 weeks: serum hCG (>=80% drop) or USS (RANZCOG TOP)
Give anti-D 250 IU if Rh negative > 10 weeks per Australian schedule (Australian Red Cross)
Discuss contraception at the same visit (LARC same-day option) (Family Planning Australia)
explanation.pearls: MS-2 Step prescribers must be certified; pharmacy supply requires the certified dispenser network (MS Health).

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman presents to ED 10 days after a medical TOP at 8 weeks using mifepristone-misoprostol from her GP. The procedure was reported as successful with passage of products on day 1 and tapering bleeding for 6 days. Over the last 36 hours she has had heavy bleeding soaking 1 large pad per hour, lower abdominal cramping, low-grade fever (37.8 deg C), foul-smelling discharge, and feels lightheaded on standing. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, blood group O Rh positive, and breastfed her two prior children. On arrival: BP 96/58, HR 116, T 37.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%. Abdomen tender suprapubically with guarding but no peritonism. Speculum: open external os with brisk bleeding and brown malodorous discharge, products visible at the os. Bedside USS: heterogeneous endometrial collection 38 mm with vascularity on colour Doppler and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Bloods: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6, group-and-hold sent.

data_table.Examination: Tender suprapubic with guarding; open cervical os with brisk bleed, products visible, foul discharge
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4, CRP 142, beta-hCG 1820 IU/L, lactate 1.6; USS endometrial collection 38 mm vascular

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Outpatient repeat misoprostol 800 microg vaginal plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Haemodynamic compromise plus sepsis signs plus active bleeding makes outpatient management unsafe with inadequate source control. The anti-pattern is treating a septic patient as outpatient. Outpatient miso + doxy is correct only in stable patients with incomplete TOP and no sepsis (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, group-and-hold, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, then proceed to urgent surgical evacuation of retained products
options.rationale: Septic incomplete abortion with haemorrhage requires resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam per eTG), and source control by urgent surgical evacuation. Delaying evacuation prolongs the source of infection and bleeding (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; RANZCOG PPH/Gynae emergencies).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion with close observation, deferring evacuation pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Oxytocin has limited effect on a non-puerperal uterus; antibiotics should not wait for cultures in sepsis (ANZICS 1-hour rule); source control must not be delayed. Oxytocin-only is correct in atonic PPH at term, not in septic abortion (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV with observation and serial Hb without antibiotic cover until culture results
options.rationale: TXA is adjunctive but does not address source; delaying antibiotics breaches the 1-hour sepsis standard. TXA is correct as adjunct to source control plus antibiotics, not as sole therapy (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 for presumed ongoing intrauterine pregnancy and discharge home to GP
options.rationale: Methotrexate addresses neither retained products nor sepsis, and is contraindicated in active infection. The anti-pattern is using MTX for any pregnancy-related bleeding. MTX is correct only for unruptured ectopic without infection (AMH; eTG).

explanation.summary: Septic incomplete abortion requires concurrent resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, and surgical source control by evacuation of retained products. This is a gynaecological emergency (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).
key_points: Antibiotics within 1 hour for sepsis (ANZICS Sepsis)
Source control: surgical evacuation of RPOC (RANZCOG TOP)
Antibiotic regimen: amp + gent + metronidazole or pip-taz (eTG Antibiotic)
Anti-D 250 IU if Rh negative and unsensitised (Australian Red Cross)
Major haemorrhage protocol if Hb continues to fall (RANZCOG PPH)
Inform original prescriber and arrange contraception/follow-up
explanation.pearls: Beware Clostridium sordellii / perfringens infection after medical TOP: profound shock without fever, leucocytosis and haemoconcentration (RANZCOG TOP).

stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman in NSW discloses ongoing intimate partner violence during her postnatal home visit at day 14. There have been two physical assault episodes in the past 2 weeks, both witnessed by her 5- and 8-year-old children. The youngest child has begun bedwetting and the older child has been refusing school. The newborn is feeding well and gaining weight; there are no injuries on the infant. She is asking that nothing be reported to child protection because she fears the four children will be removed from her care. She has agreed to a safety plan, accepted a warm referral to a specialist DV service, and confirmed she is safe to remain at her sister's house tonight. The midwife is a mandated reporter under the NSW Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998, and exposure of children to intimate partner violence meets the threshold of Risk of Significant Harm under the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

data_table.Examination: Maternal: healing perineal repair, no acute injury, EPDS 16. Infant: alert, no injuries, weight gaining
data_table.Social: Two children witnessing assault; behavioural change in both; partner unaware of disclosure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding child protection reporting?

options.text: Honour the woman's request not to report because reporting would breach therapeutic trust and engagement
options.rationale: Mandated reporters cannot suspend the legal duty at the patient's request; the threshold ('risk of significant harm') has been reached. The anti-pattern is conflating therapeutic alliance with legal obligation. Therapeutic-trust framing is correct in informing patients about the report, not in declining to make it (NSW MRG; NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998).
source_refs: NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998

options.text: Report only the children's behavioural change to the GP and do not lodge any child protection report
options.rationale: Behavioural change in the context of witnessed IPV meets the NSW Risk of Significant Harm threshold and triggers mandated reporting. Reporting-only-to-GP is correct only for concerns below RoSH threshold (NSW MRG).

options.text: Lodge a Risk of Significant Harm report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline and continue the safety plan
options.rationale: Exposure of children to intimate partner violence meets the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide threshold for Risk of Significant Harm. Reporting does not equal removal; the report triggers a structured response that can include in-home support. The midwife should explain her legal duty, continue the safety plan and warm DV referrals, and document the woman's agreement to ongoing engagement (NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998; NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Contact NSW Police directly to seek an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order on behalf of the woman
options.rationale: ADVO applications are typically made by police or the protected person; clinicians can encourage and support but the immediate mandated step is child protection reporting because children are involved. Police-direct contact is correct in immediate physical-threat situations (NSW MRG; NSW Police ADVO guidance).

options.text: Wait until the next clinic visit in a fortnight to allow the woman more time to consider
options.rationale: Delaying a RoSH report once threshold is met breaches the mandated reporter duty. Delay is essentially never correct once RoSH threshold is crossed (NSW MRG).

explanation.summary: In NSW, witnessing repeated domestic violence with behavioural change in the children meets the Risk of Significant Harm threshold and mandates a report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline, alongside continued safety planning and specialist DV referral. Transparency with the mother is best practice (NSW MRG; NSW Health DV Routine Screening).
key_points: Risk of Significant Harm threshold triggers mandatory reporting (NSW MRG)
All states have mandatory reporter pathways; thresholds and scope differ (each state Act)
Witnessing IPV counts as exposure to harm (NSW MRG)
Report does not replace DV safety planning - both proceed (NSW Health)
Tell the parent (when safe) that a report is being made
explanation.pearls: Use the NSW MRG decision tree on the spot if uncertain about threshold (NSW MRG).

sources.label: NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - mandatory reporting of child exposure

stem: A 26-year-old woman disclosed intimate partner violence 4 weeks ago and you drew up a written safety plan (code words, packed bag at her sister's, 1800RESPECT number). Today she reports her partner has been kind, brought flowers, and agreed to attend couples counselling; she is reconsidering whether she still needs the plan. No acute injuries. The previous incident involved choking, which she had not initially disclosed. She is calm, oriented, has no children, joint rental, independent income, family interstate. She asks whether the change means the cycle of abuse is over and whether joint counselling would now be safe.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury; old healed bruising right upper arm
data_table.Social: No children; joint rental; independent income; family interstate; partner currently in 'reconciliation' phase per her description

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response?

options.text: Validate her experience, explain the reconciliation/honeymoon phase of the cycle, advise against joint couples counselling (which is unsafe in active IPV with prior strangulation), reinforce the safety plan and offer ongoing review
options.rationale: Joint couples counselling is contraindicated in active IPV (especially with non-fatal strangulation, a major homicide risk marker) because it places the victim at risk and reinforces coercive control. Validation, education about the cycle and safety planning are the recommended steps (RACGP; WHO; ANROWS).

options.text: Reassure her that the apology phase usually means violence will not recur and discard the safety plan
options.rationale: The honeymoon phase predictably gives way to renewed tension and incident phases; reassurance and abandoning the plan is unsafe. Reassurance is correct only when the abuser has engaged with intervention and tracked behavioural change is sustained (RACGP; WHO).

options.text: Insist she leave the relationship today as the only safe option
options.rationale: Leaving is the highest-risk time for homicide; her autonomy and timing must be respected with safety planning. Insistence-on-leaving is correct only with consent-based safety planning toward that goal (ANROWS).

options.text: Recommend joint couples counselling because the partner has agreed to attend
options.rationale: Joint counselling is contraindicated in active IPV; it provides the perpetrator a platform and worsens risk. Joint counselling is correct only in non-violent relationship conflict, never in active IPV (WHO; RACGP).

options.text: Discharge her from clinical follow-up because she now feels safe
options.rationale: Ongoing follow-up is recommended through cycles; discharging removes support at the highest-risk time. Discharge is correct only when patient is consistently safe with established external support (RACGP).

explanation.summary: Cycle of abuse (tension, incident, reconciliation) means that the apology/honeymoon phase commonly precedes recurrence. Safety plans should be preserved and updated; couples counselling is contraindicated during active IPV; leaving is itself a high-risk transition requiring planning (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).
key_points: Walker cycle: tension -> incident -> reconciliation -> repeat (1800RESPECT)
Leaving is the highest-risk period (ANROWS)
Couples therapy contraindicated in active IPV (1800RESPECT)
Safety plans: code word, packed bag, important documents, escape route (1800RESPECT)
Document outside hand-held notes; offer 1800RESPECT contact
explanation.pearls: Most women leave 5-7 times before final departure; do not measure success by single-visit resolution (ANROWS).

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - cycle and safety planning

stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to ED 18 hours after a night out, with no memory of several hours of the evening, having woken in an unfamiliar bed beside someone she did not know, with disarranged clothing. She is concerned that her drink was spiked. She last urinated on waking 1 hour ago and has had three sips of water since. She has had no alcohol or illicit drugs to her knowledge other than two drinks she ordered and saw poured. She has no known medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable and a forensic medical examiner is available within the hour. She has not decided whether to report to police. The forensic examiner will offer toxicology and asks which sample to collect first given the long window since exposure and the short half-lives of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents such as GHB and benzodiazepines.

data_table.Examination: No acute injury identified; speculum to be performed by forensic examiner if she consents
data_table.Investigations: Pending forensic samples, baseline STI screen, beta-hCG
data_table.Timing: 18 hours since suspected event; first void available; minimal hydration

lead_in: Which sample provides the highest yield for detection of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents at this presentation?

options.text: A clotted serum sample taken now for benzodiazepine and GHB quantification analysis
options.rationale: Many DFSA agents (notably GHB) have short serum half-lives (~30-60 min); by 18 hours serum is typically negative even when urine remains positive. Serum is correct for early-window forensic testing (<6 hours), not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

options.text: First-catch urine sample now and a repeat urine sample at the next void
options.rationale: Urine is the highest-yield matrix for drug-facilitated sexual assault toxicology because most agents (GHB, benzodiazepines, ketamine) have short serum half-lives but concentrate in urine and can be detected for 12 to 96 hours depending on the agent. A first-catch sample now plus a repeat at the next void maximises detection (RANZCOG; NSW Health Sexual Assault Services; ANZFSS forensic toxicology guidance).

options.text: Scalp hair sample taken now for later segmental analysis after a 4-week growth window
options.rationale: Hair toxicology has a role but optimal timing is 3-5 weeks after exposure to allow the dosed segment to grow above the scalp. Hair is correct for retrospective single-dose detection, not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

options.text: Gastric aspirate via nasogastric tube to recover any residual ingested drug from the stomach
options.rationale: NG aspirate is not standard in DFSA workup and is invasive without yield. NG aspirate is correct only in known recent acute oral overdose with bowel decontamination intent (RANZCOG sexual assault).

options.text: Oral mucosal swab to recover any drug residues from saliva and the buccal mucosa surface
options.rationale: Oral fluid has a shorter detection window than urine and is not the preferred forensic toxicology matrix for DFSA at 18 hours. Oral fluid is correct for very recent (1-12 hour) exposures in some testing contexts, not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

explanation.summary: Urine is the highest-yield forensic toxicology matrix in suspected drug-facilitated sexual assault within the first 24 hours; collect first void plus serial samples. Hair analysis is reserved for late presentations 3-5 weeks later (RANZCOG sexual assault; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Urine: best matrix to ~24 h (RANZCOG)
Serum: short window (~12 h for benzo, <6 h for GHB) (RANZCOG)
Hair: collect at 3-5 weeks (RANZCOG)
Always handle via chain of custody (RANZCOG)
Concurrent EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment, support
explanation.pearls: Encourage the patient to not pass urine without forensic collection if she presents within 12-24 h of suspected event (RANZCOG sexual assault).

subtopic_detail: Sexual assault - drug-facilitated and toxicology evidence

stem: A 33-year-old G1P1 is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum following a left lower-limb deep vein thrombosis diagnosed 2 weeks ago and now on therapeutic warfarin (target INR 2 to 3) after initial enoxaparin. She is bottle feeding by choice (lactation suppressed), normotensive, BMI 28, non-smoker, with no history of migraine and no other medical history. She asks for the most reliable contraception she can use. She has decided she does not want further children at this stage but is not ready to consider permanent sterilisation. She is happy with all reversible options, including LARCs, and asks the GP to confirm which methods are unsafe given the recent DVT, particularly the combined hormonal options that her sister uses. She has read about UKMEC categories online and wants to understand which method she should avoid She is on warfarin for at least 3 more months and may extend to 6 months.

data_table.Examination: Healing left calf, no swelling; uterus involuted; non-lactating

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is contraindicated (UKMEC category 4) in this woman?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) inserted at this 6-week review
options.rationale: LNG-IUD is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; acceptable but Mirena insertion in anticoagulated patient requires care for procedural bleeding. LNG-IUD is correct in VTE history, but the question seeks the contraindicated option (LNG-IUD is not it) (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; usable with local bruising precautions. The implant is correct (acceptable) in VTE, so not the contraindicated option (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken daily at the same time
options.rationale: POP is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE - acceptable. POP is correct (usable) in VTE; not contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraception (COCP, vaginal ring, or transdermal patch) at any dose
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception (oestrogen-containing) is UKMEC 4 (unacceptable) in current/past VTE because the prothrombotic effect of synthetic oestrogen further increases recurrence risk; anticoagulation does not negate the contraindication (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia; RCOG VTE in pregnancy guidance).

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted as a non-hormonal LARC at this review
options.rationale: Copper IUD is UKMEC 1 in current/past VTE; non-hormonal so does not affect thrombotic risk. Copper IUD is the safest choice in VTE; not contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC 4 in current or past VTE because oestrogen increases recurrence risk; concurrent anticoagulation does not change this. Progestogen-only methods and IUDs are acceptable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
key_points: Current/past VTE: CHC UKMEC 4 (FSRH)
Acceptable: copper IUD (UKMEC 1), LNG-IUD/POP/implant/DMPA (UKMEC 2) (FSRH)
Insert IUD with INR confirmed in range and consider haemostatic precautions (FSRH)
Warfarin and DOACs do not interact with hormonal contraception efficacy (FSRH)
Counsel re return of fertility - prompt with most methods on cessation
explanation.pearls: First-degree family history of unprovoked VTE plus known thrombophilia raises CHC to UKMEC 4 even without personal history (FSRH UKMEC).

stem: A 30-year-old G1P1 woman with well-controlled HIV (CD4 720, viral load <50 copies/mL on tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir) is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum. She is formula feeding per current Australian guidance, an option chosen after counselling once viral suppression was sustained, and the infant has completed post-exposure prophylaxis per ASHM. She is normotensive, BMI 25, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and no other comorbidity. She wants reliable contraception for at least 2 years, preferably long-acting and not requiring daily adherence. Her HIV physician confirms she will remain on bictegravir-based therapy and is not on any enzyme-inducing antiretroviral. She specifically asks whether the implant will still work on her HIV regimen and whether she needs efavirenz to be added for the implant to be effective Her infectious-diseases physician has cleared any contraceptive method Her HIV physician noted no enzyme-inducing antiretrovirals on her regimen.

data_table.Examination: Well; uterus involuted
data_table.Medications: Tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir daily; no other medication
data_table.Investigations: CD4 720, HIV viral load <50 copies/mL

lead_in: Which contraceptive option is most appropriate without significant interaction concerns?

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Bictegravir/integrase inhibitor-based regimens (including tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir) do not significantly affect hormonal contraceptive efficacy; the etonogestrel implant is suitable. NNRTI-based regimens (efavirenz) and protease inhibitor/ritonavir-boosted regimens can reduce hormonal contraceptive levels (ASHM HIV Management; FSRH Drug Interactions; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill in the immediate postpartum period because she is not breastfeeding
options.rationale: CHC is contraindicated <6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk regardless of breastfeeding (UKMEC 4 <3 weeks, UKMEC 3 from 3-6 weeks). Efficacy with bictegravir is preserved but postpartum VTE concern dominates. COCP is correct >6 weeks postpartum without other contraindications (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant only if efavirenz is added to her antiretroviral regimen
options.rationale: Efavirenz (NNRTI) is enzyme-inducing and REDUCES etonogestrel efficacy; adding it would worsen interactions. The anti-pattern is conditioning the implant on an interacting drug. Bictegravir as her current regimen preserves implant efficacy (FSRH Drug Interactions; ASHM).

options.text: Avoid all hormonal methods because of HIV-related immune effects on contraceptive efficacy
options.rationale: Well-controlled HIV does not alter hormonal contraceptive efficacy; the relevant issue is specific drug interactions, not HIV. Hormonal avoidance is correct only with documented interacting ARV regimen (FSRH UKMEC; ASHM).

options.text: Permanent surgical sterilisation as the only safe contraceptive option in women with HIV
options.rationale: No contraceptive method is unsafe purely on basis of well-controlled HIV; reproductive autonomy and full method choice apply. Sterilisation is correct only when family complete and patient-initiated, never as 'only safe option' (ASHM HIV Management; UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Modern integrase inhibitor regimens (bictegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir) do not significantly interact with hormonal contraception; the etonogestrel implant and other LARCs are appropriate. NNRTIs (efavirenz, nevirapine) and ritonavir-boosted protease inhibitors reduce contraceptive efficacy. CHC has the usual <6-week postpartum VTE restrictions (ASHM HIV Management; FSRH Drug Interactions; FSRH UKMEC).
key_points: Integrase inhibitors (bictegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir): no major interaction (FSRH)
NNRTIs (efavirenz, nevirapine): reduce hormonal contraception (FSRH)
Ritonavir-boosted PIs: reduce hormonal contraception (FSRH)
IUDs (LNG and copper) are UKMEC 1 in HIV with no AIDS-defining illness (FSRH UKMEC)
Australian infant feeding guidance: formula feeding currently advised even if maternal U=U (ASHM)
explanation.pearls: Always check the specific ART regimen before assuming interactions; integrase-only era has changed the rules (FSRH Drug Interactions).

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health
stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a regional town presents at 11 weeks for booking with her local ACCHO. Her two previous pregnancies were complicated by gestational diabetes managed with diet and metformin, and both babies were macrosomic (birth weights 4.3 kg and 4.5 kg with shoulder dystocia in the second). Between pregnancies she lost touch with primary care and has not had her glucose checked. Her BMI is 32 and BP is 122/76. There is a strong family history of type 2 diabetes (mother, two siblings). At booking, the GP arranges fasting venous plasma glucose and HbA1c as part of the early antenatal panel recommended by ADIPS and RANZCOG for high-risk women. Results: fasting venous plasma glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.7% (50 mmol/mol), urine ketones negative. She is otherwise well, on folic acid and iodine, and asks whether she has GDM or something different.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no glycosuria today
data_table.Investigations: Fasting plasma glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.7% (50 mmol/mol), urine ketones negative
data_table.Past obstetric: Two prior GDM, macrosomic infants 4.3 and 4.5 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation and next step?

options.text: Reassure that values are normal in pregnancy and re-screen with 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks
options.rationale: FPG >=7.0 and HbA1c >=6.5% in early pregnancy meets ADIPS criteria for diabetes in pregnancy (likely pre-existing T2DM), not GDM; treating as normal misses pre-existing disease. Re-screen at 24-28 weeks is correct only for FPG <5.1 with no diabetes-range values (ADIPS Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy 2022).

options.text: Diagnose diabetes in pregnancy, commence lifestyle measures and metformin, refer to multidisciplinary clinic
options.rationale: FPG >=7.0 or HbA1c >=6.5% at any time in pregnancy meets criteria for diabetes in pregnancy (likely pre-existing type 2), not GDM. Management mirrors pre-existing T2DM in pregnancy: lifestyle intervention, metformin, early multidisciplinary diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic review, a detailed foetal anomaly scan, and early ophthalmology and renal screening for undiagnosed end-organ damage (ADIPS; RANZCOG; NACCHO).

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes on the FPG threshold of >=5.1 and commence dietary advice only
options.rationale: FPG 5.1-6.0 with no diabetes-range result is GDM; this woman exceeds the diabetes threshold and HbA1c is also in diabetes range. GDM diagnosis is correct only when values are sub-diabetes-threshold (FPG 5.1-6.9 with HbA1c <6.5%) (ADIPS).

options.text: Repeat the fasting plasma glucose in 4 weeks before initiating any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Delaying intervention by 4 weeks misses critical early-pregnancy organogenesis-period glycaemic control. Delay is correct only for clearly borderline single-test results with no clinical concerns (ADIPS; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy).

options.text: Commence insulin glargine immediately as monotherapy without any metformin or lifestyle intervention being trialled
options.rationale: First-line pharmacotherapy in pregnancy for overt T2DM is usually metformin with stepwise insulin addition; insulin-only without lifestyle and metformin is not standard. Insulin-first is correct in severe hyperglycaemia (FPG >11) or metformin contraindication (ADIPS; RANZCOG; AMH).

explanation.summary: Early-pregnancy FPG >=7.0 mmol/L OR HbA1c >=6.5% OR random >=11.1 = overt diabetes in pregnancy (typically previously undiagnosed T2DM). Management = treat as pre-existing diabetes with MDT clinic, tight targets, early anomaly screening, retinal and renal baseline. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women have higher T2DM prevalence and benefit from integrated culturally safe care (ADIPS Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy; RACGP).
key_points: FPG >=5.1, 1h >=10.0, 2h >=8.5 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT = GDM (ADIPS)
FPG >=7.0 or HbA1c >=6.5% (48 mmol/mol) or random >=11.1 = overt diabetes in pregnancy (ADIPS)
Treat overt diabetes in pregnancy as pre-existing T2DM (RANZCOG)
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander-specific early screening recommended at booking (ADIPS; RACGP)
Metformin + insulin if needed; glargine and aspart commonly used (AMH)
explanation.pearls: Macrosomia + prior GDM + family history of T2DM mandates early-booking FPG/HbA1c, not waiting for 24-28 weeks (ADIPS).

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - hyperglycaemia in pregnancy

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 woman in Perth, Western Australia presents at 24+2 weeks gestation by ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities and no foetal anomaly. The pregnancy is unwanted but she had previously been undecided. She is certain of her decision now and asks her GP to refer her. The GP knows the new WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023 was enacted in early 2024 with different rules above and below 23 weeks.

data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound singleton live IUP 11+0 weeks; blood group O Rh positive

lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the legal pathway for her TOP in WA?

options.text: Lawful on request up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner; at her current gestation (24+2) she needs assessment and agreement by two medical practitioners, at least one being an obstetrician/gynaecologist or in consultation with one
options.rationale: Under the WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023, TOP is lawful on request up to 23 weeks (with one practitioner), and after 23 weeks requires assessment by two medical practitioners with specialist input. Partner consent is not required (WA Department of Health; SHQ WA).

options.text: Unlawful in WA at this gestation under any circumstances; must travel interstate
options.rationale: TOP after 23 weeks is lawful in WA with two-practitioner assessment; interstate travel is not required. The anti-pattern is treating all advanced-gestation TOP as illegal. Interstate transfer is correct only when local provider declines (WA Act 2023).

options.text: Partner consent is required before the procedure
options.rationale: No Australian state or territory requires partner consent for TOP. Partner consent is never legally required in Australia (WA Act 2023; equivalent state legislation).

options.text: A mandatory 7-day waiting period applies from first consultation to procedure
options.rationale: WA's 2023 Act removed the previous mandatory waiting period and counselling requirement. 7-day waiting is correct only in jurisdictions with such legal requirements (WA Act 2023; SHQ WA).

options.text: Must be performed surgically; medical TOP is not available in WA
options.rationale: Both medical and surgical TOP are available in WA; choice depends on gestation and clinical factors. Surgical-only is correct only when medical TOP is contraindicated or gestation is too advanced (SHQ WA).

explanation.summary: The WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023 decriminalised TOP and allows on-request access up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner. After 23 weeks a Ministerial-approved panel of two specialists must agree. Other jurisdictional comparators are in the key points (WA Public Health Amendment Act 2023; RANZCOG TOP).
key_points: WA: on-request to 23 weeks; >23 weeks panel of two specialists (WA Act 2023)
Vic: 24 weeks on-request (Vic Act 2008)
NSW: 22 weeks on-request (NSW Act 2019)
Qld: 22 weeks on-request (Qld Act 2018)
SA: 22+6 on-request (SA Act 2021)
Tas: 16 weeks on-request (Tas Act 2013)
No state requires partner consent
Conscientious objectors must refer (RANZCOG TOP)
explanation.pearls: Cross-jurisdictional travel still occurs for late TOP due to capacity rather than legality (RANZCOG TOP).

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 woman is 34 weeks pregnant and has an active Apprehended Domestic Violence Order in NSW against her former partner, who is the father of the unborn child. The order prohibits him contacting her or approaching within 100 metres of her residence and workplace. She presents to the maternity day-assessment unit for routine review and is anxious because her former partner has been ringing the hospital switchboard asking for her appointment times and 'information about his child'. She is fully engaged in antenatal care, has a written safety plan, is supported by a hospital social worker, and has a planned hospital birth. She asks specifically whether the ADVO applies inside the hospital, whether her former partner has any right to be told her appointment times because he is the father, and what the hospital can do to ensure he cannot find out when she will be in the building.

data_table.Examination: SFH 34 cm, foetal heart 142, cephalic
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal bloods normal
data_table.Social: Active ADVO; ex-partner attempting information access via switchboard; social worker engaged

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next action with respect to her hospital information access and safety?

options.text: Provide the former partner with appointment times because he has a paternal right to information about the unborn child
options.rationale: No automatic paternal right to maternal health information; the woman is the patient and consent governs disclosure. Disclosure would breach NSW Health privacy and ADVO protective intent. Paternal disclosure is correct only with the patient's explicit consent (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Health Privacy Manual).

options.text: Flag the medical record as confidential, instruct switchboard and reception not to release any information, and notify security
options.rationale: Hospital information access is a critical component of the safety plan in active intimate partner violence with an ADVO. The medical record should be flagged as confidential, the ADVO documented, switchboard and reception instructed not to release any information, the social worker and hospital security notified, and the safety plan for arrival and discharge confirmed. Paternity does not override the ADVO or confer a right to maternal appointment information (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Crimes (Domestic and Personal Violence) Act 2007).

options.text: Transfer her care to a different hospital to break the trail without involving her in the decision
options.rationale: Transfer without patient agreement breaks continuity, undermines relationship she has built with the team. Transfer is correct only with patient agreement and clear safety benefit (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Tell the partner the ADVO does not apply to the hospital so he may attend appointments
options.rationale: ADVOs apply across all locations as worded by the magistrate; hospital is not exempt. Inviting attendance contradicts the order. Attendance is correct only when the ADVO explicitly permits and the patient consents (NSW Police ADVO guidance; NSW DV Routine Screening).

options.text: Restrict her from public maternity care until the ADVO is formally upgraded by the court
options.rationale: Restricting care is unsafe and inappropriate; the response is to make care safe, not withdraw it. Care restriction is essentially never correct in IPV (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

explanation.summary: When an active ADVO/DVO is in place, hospital response is to restrict-flag the record, brief switchboard and reception, brief security, coordinate with the social worker, and plan safe arrival and discharge for the birth. There is no automatic paternal right to maternal health information (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Health Privacy Manual).
key_points: Patient consent governs disclosure of her health information (NSW Health Privacy)
ADVO applies across all locations including hospital (NSW Police)
Restricted record flag is a routine NSW Health mechanism
Coordinate with hospital security and social work for arrival/discharge (NSW Health)
Maintain access to care, not withdraw it
explanation.pearls: Document the ADVO conditions in the safety plan section of the record; review at each contact (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

subtopic_detail: DV - DVO/AVO interaction with antenatal care planning

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes elective surgical TOP at 13 weeks by dilation and evacuation in a day-procedure unit. The procedure itself is uneventful but in recovery 30 minutes later she is found to be soaking the pad with bright red blood. She has no significant past medical history, is on no anticoagulation, has no known bleeding diathesis, BMI 26, and no prior caesarean section. On urgent review: BP 88/52, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 99%, T 36.7, alert but pale and clammy. Abdominal palpation reveals a soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis. Speculum: brisk red bleeding from a parous cervical os, no obvious cervical laceration, no visible retained products at the os. Estimated blood loss is already 800 mL. The major haemorrhage protocol is being activated, two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ, and a group-and-hold has been sent. The on-call gynaecologist is en route; pharmacy has misoprostol, ergometrine and carboprost available The anaesthetic team has secured the airway adequately for now.

data_table.Examination: Soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between umbilicus and symphysis; brisk bleeding from os, no cervical laceration
data_table.Investigations: Hb pre-op 124 g/L, EBL 800 mL and ongoing; group and hold sent
data_table.Procedure: Surgical TOP at 13 weeks; no obvious tissue retained at speculum

lead_in: What is the single best initial uterotonic intervention while ongoing resuscitation continues?

options.text: IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus followed by oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L saline
options.rationale: Postabortal uterine atony is managed identically to postpartum atony: oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic, given as a slow 5 IU IV bolus followed by an infusion. Second-line agents (ergot alkaloids, carboprost, misoprostol) and tranexamic acid are added if atony persists; mechanical and surgical measures escalate from there (RANZCOG PPH; WHO PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg per rectum as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian PPH/TOP haemorrhage protocols, used when first-line agents are unavailable or contraindicated. Misoprostol-first is correct only in low-resource settings without IV oxytocin (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole haemorrhage agent, withholding any uterotonic for now
options.rationale: TXA is a useful adjunct (1 g IV within 3 hours per WOMAN trial) but does not address atony; uterotonics are the primary mechanism-targeted therapy. TXA-alone without uterotonic is correct only when atony is excluded (rare) (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial).

options.text: Carboprost 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Carboprost is second-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; contraindicated in asthma. Carboprost-first is correct only after oxytocin failure in non-asthmatic patients (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Methylergometrine 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Ergometrine/methylergometrine is standard second-line after oxytocin and contraindicated in hypertension/pre-eclampsia. Ergometrine-first is correct only when oxytocin is unavailable in normotensive patients (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Post-TOP atonic haemorrhage is managed in parallel with major-haemorrhage resuscitation: bimanual compression, IV oxytocin bolus then infusion as first-line uterotonic, then ergometrine and carboprost if needed, plus TXA within 3 hours. Re-examine for retained tissue and cervical/uterine trauma; escalate to balloon tamponade, embolisation or laparotomy if uncontrolled (RANZCOG TOP; RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
key_points: First-line: IV oxytocin 5 IU bolus then 40 IU/L infusion (RANZCOG PPH)
Second-line: ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (avoid in HTN, PET) (RANZCOG PPH)
Third-line: carboprost 250 microg IM q15min, max 8 doses (avoid asthma) (RANZCOG PPH)
Backup: misoprostol 800 microg PR (RANZCOG PPH)
Adjunct: TXA 1 g IV within 3 h of onset (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial)
Always re-examine for retained tissue, cervical laceration, uterine perforation (RANZCOG TOP)
explanation.pearls: After surgical TOP haemorrhage that does not respond promptly to uterotonics, suspect retained tissue or uterine perforation and arrange urgent USS and theatre re-examination (RANZCOG TOP).

stem: A 49-year-old G3P3 presents with a 9-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Cycles have shortened from 28 to 22 days, last 8 to 10 days, and she changes a fully soaked pad every 90 minutes on the worst two days with passage of 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She reports two episodes of intermenstrual bleeding in the last 3 months. Her BMI is 34, she has type 2 diabetes on metformin, and she has never been on hormonal contraception. There is no postcoital bleeding, no dyspareunia, and her last cervical screen 18 months ago was HPV negative. She is not sexually active currently and uses no contraception. On examination her abdomen is soft with no palpable mass; speculum shows a healthy cervix and no cervical lesion; bimanual reveals a bulky but mobile uterus, no adnexal mass. Pregnancy test is negative. FBC shows Hb 98 g/L with MCV 74 fL and ferritin 6 microg/L. TSH and coagulation screen are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/84 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 34. Bulky mobile uterus. Cervix healthy. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 microg/L, TSH normal, coags normal, HPV negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Commence tranexamic acid 1 g tds during menses and review
options.rationale: TXA is first-line medical therapy for HMB without structural cause, but empirical treatment in a woman >45 with IMB and unopposed-oestrogen risk factors without first excluding malignancy is inappropriate. TXA is correct after endometrial sampling excludes hyperplasia/cancer (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test with HPV partial genotyping today
options.rationale: She is in routine 5-yearly screening interval with recent negative HPV; bleeding pattern points to endometrial rather than cervical source. Repeat CST is correct with new cervical symptoms or visible cervical lesion (NCSP; RANZCOG).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to characterise the bulky uterus before further management
options.rationale: MRI is second-line imaging used to map fibroids prior to surgery or when TVUS is inconclusive; not first investigation. MRI is correct after TVUS identifies a complex finding requiring better characterisation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound and outpatient endometrial biopsy at this visit
options.rationale: Age over 45 with HMB plus intermenstrual bleeding and obesity/diabetes (unopposed oestrogen risk factors) mandates endometrial assessment to exclude hyperplasia/carcinoma; TVUS plus Pipelle biopsy is the recommended first-line workup (RANZCOG AUB statement; Therapeutic Guidelines Gynaecology).

options.text: Outpatient hysteroscopy without prior endometrial sampling or pelvic imaging
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is indicated when TVUS shows focal pathology or when blind sampling is non-diagnostic; initial workup is TVUS plus Pipelle. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only when TVUS already shows focal lesion (e.g., polyp) (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

explanation.summary: In HMB, age over 45, intermenstrual bleeding, persistent symptoms despite treatment, or risk factors for unopposed oestrogen exposure (obesity, diabetes, PCOS, tamoxifen, nulliparity, late menopause) require endometrial assessment before empirical medical therapy. TVUS measures endometrial thickness and identifies structural PALM lesions; Pipelle biopsy samples the endometrium for hyperplasia/carcinoma.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
Endometrial sampling indicated in HMB >45, IMB, failed medical Rx, or risk factors
Management ladder if benign: tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, LNG-IUS, COCP, oral progestogen, ablation, hysterectomy
LNG-IUS is first-line long-term medical therapy for HMB without structural cause
explanation.pearls: A bulky uterus on bimanual examination in a perimenopausal woman with IMB is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise, regardless of how plausible adenomyosis or fibroids seem.

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - investigation in a perimenopausal woman

stem: A 16-year-old presents with primary amenorrhoea. She has never had a menstrual period. Her height is 148 cm (below 3rd centile), weight 48 kg, BMI 22. On Tanner staging her breast development is Tanner 1 and pubic hair is Tanner 3. She has a wide carrying angle at the elbows, a short fourth metacarpal, and a low posterior hairline. There is no anosmia and no visual field defect. She reports no cyclical pelvic pain, no abdominal mass, and no headaches. Her past medical history includes recurrent otitis media in childhood requiring grommets and a known bicuspid aortic valve picked up incidentally at age 8 on a school murmur referral. She has no sisters; her mother went through menarche at 13 and is 165 cm tall. There is no family history of consanguinity, autoimmune disease, or premature ovarian insufficiency. On examination her external genitalia are prepubertal Tanner stage; the vaginal opening is patent and a normal-appearing cervix is visible on careful inspection. Pelvic ultrasound shows a small infantile uterus and streak ovaries bilaterally with no follicular activity. A renal ultrasound performed in childhood was normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Height 148 cm (<3rd centile). Breast Tanner 1. Wide carrying angle. Short 4th metacarpal. Low posterior hairline.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS abdo: small uterus, streak ovaries bilaterally. External genitalia normal female.

lead_in: Which is the single most useful next investigation to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Karyotype to exclude Turner syndrome and structural abnormalities
options.rationale: Short stature with somatic features (wide carrying angle, short fourth metacarpal, low posterior hairline, bicuspid aortic valve), streak ovaries and absent breast development is Turner syndrome (45,XO); karyotype confirms the diagnosis and detects mosaicism (RANZCOG; ESHRE Turner 2017).

options.text: Serum prolactin to exclude a pituitary cause
options.rationale: Hyperprolactinaemia typically causes secondary amenorrhoea with normal breast development and galactorrhoea, not primary amenorrhoea with dysmorphic features. Prolactin is correct in secondary amenorrhoea with galactorrhoea (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to characterise the uterus and adnexa
