options.rationale: Ultrasound has already shown streak ovaries and a small uterus; MRI is reserved for suspected Mullerian anomalies when ultrasound is equivocal. MRI is correct for MRKH syndrome workup with uncertain US (RANZCOG).

options.text: GnRH stimulation test to localise the axis defect
options.rationale: GnRH testing differentiates pituitary from hypothalamic causes of hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism; the streak ovaries and dysmorphic features point to primary (gonadal) cause that karyotype diagnoses definitively. GnRH stimulation is correct in suspected hypothalamic-pituitary disease (ESHRE).

options.text: Olfactory testing for anosmia to identify Kallmann syndrome
options.rationale: Anosmia raises Kallmann syndrome (hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism with normal ovaries and uterus); streak ovaries and short stature with somatic features are not Kallmann features. Olfactory testing is correct in suspected Kallmann (anosmia + delayed puberty + normal MRI brain) (StatPearls).

explanation.summary: Primary amenorrhoea is investigated by stratifying on breast development (oestrogen exposure) and presence of uterus. Absent breasts with elevated FSH and streak ovaries indicates gonadal dysgenesis (Turner 45,XO most common). Absent breasts with low FSH indicates hypothalamic-pituitary cause (Kallmann if anosmia). Normal breasts with absent uterus suggests Mullerian agenesis (MRKH) or androgen insensitivity (46,XY). Imperforate hymen presents with cyclical pain and a bulging bluish membrane.
key_points: Turner 45,XO: short stature, streak ovaries, bicuspid aortic valve, renal anomalies, lymphoedema in infancy
Kallmann: anosmia + hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism
MRKH: 46,XX, normal ovaries, absent/rudimentary uterus, normal breast development
Complete androgen insensitivity: 46,XY, female phenotype, blind-ending vagina, intra-abdominal testes
Imperforate hymen: cyclical pelvic pain, primary amenorrhoea, bluish bulging hymen
explanation.pearls: Short stature plus primary amenorrhoea is Turner syndrome until karyotype proves otherwise; remember to screen for aortic root dilatation and renal anomalies in every Turner patient.

subtopic_detail: Primary amenorrhoea with absent breast development - workup pathway

stem: A 24-year-old marathon runner presents with 11 months of amenorrhoea. Her menarche was at 13 and cycles were regular at 28 days until she increased training to 90 km per week 18 months ago in preparation for an ultramarathon. Her BMI has dropped from 22 to 18.5 in the last 12 months and she reports restricting carbohydrates and skipping meals on training days. She works as a primary school teacher and has noticed fatigue, low mood and cold intolerance for the last 4 months. She has no galactorrhoea, no hirsutism, no acne, no hot flushes, no vaginal dryness, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no recent dilatation and curettage or termination of pregnancy, and no history of pelvic infection. She has had no live births and uses condoms with her long-term male partner. A pregnancy test is negative. Her FSH is 2.8 IU/L, LH 1.6 IU/L, oestradiol 110 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, AMH 22 pmol/L, total testosterone 0.6 nmol/L. A bone density scan shows a Z-score of -2.3 at the lumbar spine and -1.8 at the femoral neck.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 48 bpm, BMI 18.5.
data_table.Examination: Thin, well. No acne or hirsutism. Normal external genitalia.
data_table.Investigations: FSH 2.8 IU/L, LH 1.6 IU/L, E2 110 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, AMH 22 pmol/L. DXA Z-score -2.3 lumbar spine. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Polycystic ovary syndrome with anovulation
options.rationale: PCOS requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria; she has no hyperandrogenism and LH is low rather than the elevated LH:FSH typical of PCOS. PCOS is correct with oligo/anovulation + hyperandrogenism or polycystic morphology (CREPCOS 2023; Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea from stress
options.rationale: Low FSH, low LH and low oestradiol in the setting of low energy availability (high training volume, restrictive eating, weight loss to BMI 18.5) and low bone density is classic functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea, part of relative energy deficiency in sport (REDs); management is restoring energy balance (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society 2017).

options.text: Premature ovarian insufficiency before age forty
options.rationale: POI requires FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart in a woman <40; her FSH is low and AMH preserved. POI is correct with elevated FSH and reduced AMH (Australasian Menopause Society POI).

options.text: Hyperprolactinaemia from a microprolactinoma adenoma
options.rationale: Her prolactin is within normal range (typically <500 mIU/L); microprolactinoma usually causes elevations of 1000-5000 mIU/L with galactorrhoea. Microprolactinoma is correct with elevated prolactin plus galactorrhoea (Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology).

options.text: Asherman syndrome from prior uterine instrumentation
options.rationale: Asherman follows intrauterine instrumentation (D&C, especially postpartum or post-infection) with amenorrhoea despite a normal hormonal axis. She has no such history and gonadotrophins are suppressed. Asherman is correct with prior uterine instrumentation and normal hormone profile (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Secondary amenorrhoea after pregnancy is excluded is worked up with prolactin, TSH, FSH/LH, oestradiol and androgens. Low FSH/LH/E2 with low energy availability defines functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea (FHA), a component of REDs. PCOS gives elevated LH:FSH and androgens. POI gives FSH >25 IU/L. Hyperprolactinaemia gives elevated prolactin with normal/low FSH/LH. Asherman gives amenorrhoea with a normal hormonal axis after uterine instrumentation.
key_points: FHA = energy deficit, stress, excessive exercise -> suppressed GnRH
REDs replaces female athlete triad and includes male athletes
FHA bone health: low E2 causes accelerated bone loss
Manage FHA by restoring energy balance, not by giving COCP for bone density alone
POI cutoff: FSH >25 IU/L x 2 occasions, age <40
explanation.pearls: Giving the COCP to mask amenorrhoea in FHA does not reliably protect bone and removes the most important marker of recovery; energy restoration is the treatment.

subtopic_detail: Secondary amenorrhoea in an athlete - hypothalamic differential

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with her fifth episode of vulvovaginal candidiasis in the past 12 months. Each episode features intense vulval pruritus, dyspareunia, thick white curd-like discharge, and erythematous vulval skin, and resolves with a single oral fluconazole 150 mg dose. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. She is in a stable monogamous relationship for 5 years, uses copper IUD for contraception, has no history of diabetes, and a recent HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She does not use scented soaps, wears cotton underwear, and has not used antibiotics in the last 6 months. She has no immunosuppression. Today on speculum examination there is thick white adherent discharge with vulval erythema and excoriation. Vaginal pH is 4.2. KOH microscopy shows numerous pseudohyphae and budding yeast. A high vaginal swab for fungal culture and speciation is collected and confirms Candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Thick white discharge, vulval erythema and excoriation.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 4.2. KOH: pseudohyphae and budding yeast. HVS culture: Candida albicans, fluconazole sensitive. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management?

options.text: Single dose fluconazole 150 mg orally and reassure this is an isolated acute episode
options.rationale: She has had at least 4 episodes in 12 months, meeting criteria for recurrent VVC; isolated single-dose treatment without induction and suppression has high relapse rate. Single-dose fluconazole is correct for sporadic acute episodes only (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Induction fluconazole 150 mg orally days 1, 4, 7, then suppressive fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
options.rationale: Recurrent VVC (>=4 episodes/year) is managed with an induction regimen of fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for 3 doses to clear, followed by weekly suppressive fluconazole 150 mg for at least 6 months; this halves recurrence rates (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; CDC STI Treatment 2021).

options.text: Empirical oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for a 7-day course without further investigation
options.rationale: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not candidiasis; KOH confirms fungal infection. Metronidazole is correct in BV and trichomoniasis, never in confirmed candidiasis (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Refer her for an oral glucose tolerance test before any further antifungal therapy
options.rationale: Diabetes is a risk factor but HbA1c 5.4% effectively excludes it; OGTT not warranted as a precondition for treatment. OGTT is correct only with HbA1c borderline or unexplained recurrent infections persisting after suppression (RACGP Red Book; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Long-term intravaginal boric acid 600 mg nightly indefinitely as continuous suppressive antifungal therapy regimen
options.rationale: Intravaginal boric acid is reserved for non-albicans species (C. glabrata) or azole-resistant infection; her isolate is fluconazole-sensitive C. albicans. Boric acid is correct after speciation confirms non-albicans Candida (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Recurrent VVC is defined as 4 or more symptomatic episodes per year, confirmed by microscopy or culture. Management combines an induction phase (fluconazole 150 mg orally on days 1, 4 and 7) with suppression (fluconazole 150 mg weekly for at least 6 months). Address modifiable factors: optimise glycaemic control, avoid douching and irritants, and consider non-albicans Candida if treatment-resistant. Liver function should be checked before prolonged azole therapy.
key_points: RVVC: >=4 culture-confirmed episodes per year
Induction: fluconazole 150 mg PO days 1, 4 and 7
Suppression: fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
Test for non-albicans Candida if resistant (glabrata responds to topical boric acid)
Screen for poorly controlled diabetes and consider HIV in immunocompromised picture
explanation.pearls: Routine OGTT is not needed when HbA1c is well below the diabetes threshold; the diagnostic role of HbA1c here is to exclude diabetes as the driver, not to gatekeep treatment.

stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 presents with 18 months of urinary leakage. She leaks small volumes when she coughs, laughs, sneezes, or lifts her grandchildren, has no urgency, no nocturia, and no leakage on the way to the toilet. She empties her bladder 6 times a day, never wakes overnight to void, and does not feel incomplete emptying. There is no haematuria, no dysuria, no recurrent UTI, and no faecal incontinence. She had three vaginal births with two episiotomies and a forceps delivery for her second child. Her last period was 14 months ago. BMI 31. She smokes 10 cigarettes/day. On examination, abdomen soft, no palpable bladder. On speculum a first-degree anterior wall prolapse is noted. A cough stress test in semi-recumbent and standing positions reproduces leakage of urine from the urethral meatus. Urinalysis is clear and post-void residual on bladder scan is 35 mL.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/82 mmHg, BMI 31.
data_table.Examination: First-degree anterior wall prolapse. Positive cough stress test. No pelvic mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear. PVR 35 mL. Bladder diary not yet completed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer for a mid-urethral sling (transobturator tape) surgical procedure now
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for women who have failed adequate conservative therapy including supervised pelvic floor training and lifestyle modification; first-line is conservative. Surgery is correct only after >=3 months supervised PFMT with persistent symptoms (RANZCOG; Continence Foundation Australia).

options.text: Commence oral oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily as first-line monotherapy for symptoms
options.rationale: Oxybutynin is antimuscarinic indicated for urgency UI; history is pure stress incontinence so antimuscarinics will not help and may cause cognitive/anticholinergic side effects. Oxybutynin is correct in urgency-predominant UI (AMH).

options.text: Three-day bladder diary, pelvic floor training for 3 months, weight loss and smoking cessation
options.rationale: First-line for stress urinary incontinence is supervised pelvic floor muscle training for at least 3 months alongside weight loss (5 to 10% reduction halves leakage episodes) and smoking cessation (reduces cough-driven leakage); a bladder diary characterises voiding and is recommended before further intervention (Continence Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a ring pessary for the management of stress urinary incontinence
options.rationale: Continence pessary may be useful for SUI but is second-line conservative after PFMT; first-degree prolapse is not the primary driver here. Pessary is correct as adjunct after failed PFMT or with prolapse contribution (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urodynamic studies as the first step before any conservative therapy is trialled
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not routinely required before conservative management of uncomplicated SUI; indicated when surgery is considered or diagnosis uncertain. Urodynamics is correct before surgical planning (NICE NG123).

explanation.summary: Urinary incontinence is classified as stress (leakage on cough/sneeze/exertion), urge (preceded by urgency, part of overactive bladder), mixed, or overflow (chronic retention with leakage, raised PVR). Initial assessment includes history, bladder diary, urinalysis, PVR, and cough stress test. First-line management for stress and mixed UI is conservative: pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) for at least 3 months, weight loss, smoking cessation, and treatment of constipation. Failure of conservative therapy escalates to mid-urethral sling for stress UI or antimuscarinics/beta-3 agonists for urge UI.
key_points: Stress UI: leak with cough/sneeze, no urgency
Urge UI: leak preceded by urgency, often nocturia and frequency
Overflow UI: raised PVR, chronic retention
PFMT first-line for at least 3 months supervised
Antimuscarinics/mirabegron for urge; sling for stress that fails conservative Rx
explanation.pearls: If you cannot reproduce leakage with a cough stress test and the post-void residual is high, you are not dealing with stress incontinence - rethink the diagnosis before recommending a sling.

subtopic_detail: Urinary incontinence - stress vs urge distinction and initial management

stem: A 68-year-old G4P4 woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day. She also has bothersome stress urinary incontinence on coughing/sneezing and needs to splint to void fully. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. Sexually active with husband, BMI 27, hypertension on perindopril, no other comorbidities. POP-Q: Ba 0, C -2, Bp -3 (stage 2 anterior compartment). Post-void residual 80 mL. Urodynamic studies confirm urodynamic stress incontinence with no detrusor overactivity and good bladder capacity. She wants the option with the best evidence for both prolapse and urinary symptoms; she is prepared to consider surgery but would also consider a pessary.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 136/82 mmHg, BMI 27.
data_table.Examination: POP-Q: Ba = 0 (anterior wall at hymen on Valsalva), C = -2, Bp = -3. Stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse.
data_table.Investigations: PVR 80 mL. Urinalysis clear.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Trial vaginal ring pessary and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy, with reassessment of urinary symptoms after prolapse reduction
options.rationale: Conservative management (pessary + pelvic floor physiotherapy) is first-line for symptomatic POP including in women with concurrent stress incontinence. Pessary may unmask or improve SUI; reassessment after reduction guides whether surgery for incontinence is needed (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: Anterior colporrhaphy with concomitant retropubic midurethral sling
options.rationale: Mesh midurethral sling implantation has been restricted by TGA in Australia following safety concerns; combined surgery is not first-line. Mesh sling is correct only after conservative failure with formal consent and TGA-compliant device choice (RANZCOG; TGA).

options.text: Sacrocolpopexy with synthetic mesh under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Abdominal mesh sacrocolpopexy is reserved for apical/post-hysterectomy prolapse, not first-line for stage-2 anterior compartment; restricted in Australia. Sacrocolpopexy is correct for refractory apical prolapse after conservative failure (TGA mesh).

options.text: Indwelling urinary catheter to manage the elevated post-void residual long-term
options.rationale: PVR of 80 mL is mildly raised and likely improves with prolapse reduction; long-term catheter risks infection. IDC is correct only with refractory retention not improved by other measures (RANZCOG).

options.text: Topical oestrogen as monotherapy with no other intervention
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen is an adjunct to pessary tolerance but does not treat prolapse or SUI alone. Topical oestrogen is correct as adjunct to pessary use or for atrophic symptoms (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: POP-Q stages prolapse by the most distal point relative to the hymen: stage 0 no prolapse; stage 1 most distal point >1 cm above hymen; stage 2 within 1 cm of hymen; stage 3 >1 cm beyond hymen but not complete eversion; stage 4 complete eversion. Conservative management (pessary, pelvic floor physiotherapy, topical oestrogen if postmenopausal) is first-line for symptomatic prolapse, particularly in women who wish to avoid surgery or have surgical contraindications. Surgical options include native tissue anterior/posterior repair, sacrocolpopexy for apical defect, and colpocleisis in non-sexually-active women with comorbidities.
key_points: POP-Q stage 2 = leading point within 1 cm of hymen (-1 to +1)
Conservative: pessary + PFMT + topical oestrogen if postmenopausal
Pessary types: ring (mild-moderate), Gellhorn (advanced), cube (refractory)
Sacrocolpopexy = gold standard apical repair
Mesh use restricted in Australia following TGA review; native tissue preferred
explanation.pearls: Stage 2 anterior wall prolapse with urinary symptoms often masks occult stress incontinence; reducing the prolapse with a pessary can unmask leakage that the patient did not previously notice.

subtopic_detail: Pelvic organ prolapse - conservative management of symptomatic stage 2 cystocele

stem: A 29-year-old G1P1 presents for preconception counselling. Her first child was born 18 months ago at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal birth weighing 3450 g. She takes sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for generalised tonic-clonic epilepsy (last seizure 4 years ago) and is co-managed by neurology. She is otherwise well. She has no personal or family history of neural tube defects, no diabetes, no malabsorption, and a BMI of 24. She is up to date with her cervical screening (HPV negative 8 months ago) and her booking bloods from her last pregnancy showed she was rubella immune and varicella immune. She smokes 5 cigarettes a day, drinks 4 standard drinks twice a week on weekends, and does not use any other supplements. She would like to start trying for a second child in 3 months and asks what she should do to prepare.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Well, no abnormal findings.
data_table.Investigations: Rubella IgG immune (last pregnancy). Varicella IgG immune. Cervical screen HPV negative 8 months ago. HbA1c 5.2% (33 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most important pre-conception intervention?

options.text: Folic acid 0.5 mg orally daily for 3 months pre-conception and the first trimester only
options.rationale: Standard 0.5 mg is for low-risk women; sodium valproate is major teratogen requiring 5 mg daily. Low-dose folate is correct for women without teratogenic medications, prior NTD, diabetes, malabsorption, or BMI >30 (RANZCOG; NHMRC).

options.text: Iodine 150 microg daily plus vitamin D level testing only without any medication change overall
options.rationale: Iodine 150 microg/day is recommended pre-pregnancy but does not address dominant teratogenic risk from sodium valproate. Iodine alone is correct as standard pre-conception supplement, but inadequate as single intervention here (RANZCOG; NHMRC).

options.text: Reassure her that no change to current AED therapy is needed because she is presently seizure-free
options.rationale: Seizure control does not mitigate teratogenic risk of valproate; the drug itself is the harm irrespective of seizure control. No-change is correct only when alternative AEDs have failed and informed decision is documented (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Administer MMR and varicella booster immunisations now before any pregnancy attempt is made by this patient
options.rationale: She is already immune to rubella and varicella; live vaccines not indicated and contraindicated within 28 days of attempting conception. MMR/varicella is correct only with sub-protective titres and 4-week conception delay (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Refer to neurology to switch to a safer anti-seizure medication and prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily
options.rationale: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk of any anti-seizure medication (10% major congenital malformation rate, neurodevelopmental effects in 30 to 40%); switching to a safer agent (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) before conception is mandatory and high-dose folate 5 mg is indicated because of the elevated neural tube defect risk (RANZCOG; NHMRC; AMH).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception counselling covers folate (0.5 mg/day low-risk; 5 mg/day high-risk: previous NTD, diabetes, anti-epileptic drugs, malabsorption, family history NTD, obesity BMI >30 in some guidelines), iodine 150 microg/day, rubella and varicella serology with vaccination if non-immune (4 weeks before conception), cervical screening, STI screening, optimisation of chronic disease (diabetes, hypertension, thyroid, epilepsy), medication review for teratogens (valproate, ACE inhibitors, warfarin, retinoids, methotrexate), smoking and alcohol cessation, weight optimisation, mental health, and partner factors.
key_points: Folate low-risk 0.5 mg; high-risk 5 mg from 1 to 3 months pre-conception through 12 weeks
Iodine 150 microg/day pre-conception and throughout pregnancy and lactation
Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) at least 4 weeks before conception attempts
Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic AED; switch pre-conception
Other teratogens: ACEi/ARB, warfarin, isotretinoin, methotrexate, mycophenolate
explanation.pearls: If a woman of reproductive age is taking sodium valproate, the bigger intervention is changing the drug, not adding folate; high-dose folate is mitigation, not protection.

stem: A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, no pelvic surgery, no PID, no STI history, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative). BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, no varicocele symptoms, no testicular trauma, no mumps orchitis, no inguinal surgery, no cryptorchidism, no chronic illness, no anabolic steroid use, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic examination.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screen HPV negative 6 months ago. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.text: Semen analysis, day-21 progesterone, TSH and transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after initial workup
options.rationale: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35 (6 months if 35 or over). First-line investigations are semen analysis (assesses 40 to 50% of contributory causes), confirmation of ovulation (mid-luteal progesterone), thyroid function, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HSG or HyCoSy) is performed after initial workup if no abnormality is found (RANZCOG Infertility; NICE CG156).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation of the couple
options.rationale: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor. IVF is correct after workup identifies indications (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation of the couple
options.rationale: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces chances of timely treatment. Continued attempts are correct only if duration is below the 12 month (or 6 month if >=35) threshold (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Hysterosalpingogram and diagnostic laparoscopy as the first-line investigations of the couple
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment is part of workup but follows initial investigations; laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified. HSG/laparoscopy is correct after initial workup is unrevealing (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure her she can wait given young age
options.rationale: AMH is part of broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes. AMH-only is correct as adjunctive ovarian reserve assessment, never as sole investigation (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35, or 6 months at age 35 or older, or earlier if there is a known cause. Initial workup is parallel investigation of both partners: semen analysis (according to WHO criteria), ovulation assessment (mid-luteal progesterone, cycle history, ultrasound follicle tracking), ovarian reserve markers (AMH, antral follicle count, FSH on day 2 to 5), thyroid, prolactin, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HyCoSy or HSG) follows. Laparoscopy is reserved for suspected endometriosis or adhesive disease.
key_points: <35: investigate after 12 months; >=35: investigate after 6 months
Male factor accounts for 40 to 50% - always do semen analysis
Mid-luteal (day 21 in 28-day cycle) progesterone >25 nmol/L = ovulation
Tubal patency: HyCoSy preferred (no radiation) or HSG
AMH for ovarian reserve, not as a predictor of spontaneous conception
explanation.pearls: The most common omission in infertility workup is the semen analysis - both partners must be investigated in parallel from the outset.

subtopic_detail: Infertility workup in a couple under 35

stem: A 42-year-old G2P2 woman presents with 4 months of postcoital bleeding, small-volume bright red, after most episodes of intercourse. No intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, no pelvic pain. Cycles regular, COCP for 5 years. Her last cervical screening test 9 months ago: HPV non-16/18 detected with reflex LBC showing pLSIL; she did not attend her recommended 12-month repeat. On speculum today: a 1.5 cm fungating lesion on the anterior cervical lip that bleeds on contact, with surrounding pale tissue. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass on bimanual examination.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: To be performed.
data_table.Investigations: Last HPV test 12 months ago: HPV negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent referral to gynaecological oncology for biopsy of the cervical lesion
options.rationale: A visible suspicious cervical lesion overrides any cytology pathway; the cervical screening program states a clinically suspicious cervix must be referred for biopsy by a gynaecologist (preferably gynae-oncology). Repeat HPV testing is not appropriate (NCSP; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in clinic today
options.rationale: Screening is for asymptomatic women without visible lesions; a fungating lesion mandates biopsy not re-screen. Re-screen is correct for asymptomatic women in screening interval (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Stop the COCP and review in 3 months as it may be the cause of bleeding
options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding with suspicious cervical lesion is not attributable to COCP; this delays cancer diagnosis. COCP cessation is correct only after cervical pathology excluded (NCSP; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Take chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT swabs and treat empirically while awaiting results
options.rationale: STI screen is reasonable but does not replace urgent biopsy of a visible suspicious lesion. STI swabs are correct as adjunctive workup, not as substitute for biopsy (NCSP; eTG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Reassure and arrange clinical review in 6 months
options.rationale: Reassurance is unsafe given visible lesion and missed previous follow-up; cancer must be excluded. Reassurance is correct only after biopsy excludes malignancy (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

explanation.summary: Postcoital bleeding in a woman of reproductive age has a differential of cervical ectropion (common in COCP users and pregnancy), cervicitis (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, Mycoplasma genitalium, trichomonas), endocervical polyp, atrophic changes, and rarely cervical or vaginal cancer. Workup is speculum examination, cervical screening if not up to date, STI NAAT swabs (endocervical or self-collected vaginal), and colposcopy if a suspicious lesion is identified on inspection. A recent negative HPV test substantially reduces but does not exclude cancer.
key_points: PCB differential: ectropion, cervicitis, polyp, atrophy, cancer
Speculum + chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT is first-line
Colposcopy if visible suspicious lesion or screen positive
Recent HPV-negative test does not rule out polyp or cervicitis
Ectropion = columnar epithelium on ectocervix, often physiological in COCP users
explanation.pearls: Cancer is a rare cause of postcoital bleeding in women under 35 with recent negative HPV screening, but speculum examination remains mandatory - imaging cannot replace direct visual inspection of the cervix.

stem: A 42-year-old Aboriginal woman attends an ACCHO. Her last Pap smear was 9 years ago (reported normal). She declines a clinician-collected speculum, citing childhood sexual trauma and discomfort with previous examinations. She has no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, and no pelvic pain. She is G3P3 (all vaginal), in a monogamous relationship, no immunosuppression, BMI 27. She received Gardasil as an adolescent via catch-up. The clinic is accredited to offer self-collection. She wants to participate in screening in a way she can tolerate. There is no visible lesion at external genital examination (which she has agreed to).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. External genital examination declined.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab today as her routine cervical screening test
options.rationale: Under the NCSP since July 2022, self-collected vaginal HPV swab is available to ALL eligible screen-age women aged 25-74 as an equivalent option to clinician-collected sampling. It is particularly important for under-screened groups including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women and those with trauma histories (NCSP; Cancer Council Australia).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines
Australian Government Department of Health: NCSP

options.text: Refer to gynaecology for examination under anaesthesia and cervical sampling
options.rationale: EUA is reserved for situations where examination is essential and cannot be tolerated; self-collection makes EUA unnecessary for screening. EUA is correct for symptomatic presentations requiring complete examination under sedation (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Decline screening today and rebook for clinician-collected sample at a later visit
options.rationale: Deferral perpetuates under-screening; self-collection answers this exact barrier. Deferral to clinician collection is correct only when clinician sampling is patient's preference (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Send a self-collected urine sample for HPV testing in lieu of vaginal sampling
options.rationale: Urine HPV testing is not part of the Australian NCSP; validated self-collection is vaginal. Urine HPV is not currently approved in Australia (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Reassure her that prior Gardasil vaccination removes the need for screening
options.rationale: Vaccination does not eliminate the need for screening, which is recommended every 5 years from age 25 regardless of vaccination status. Vaccinated-no-screen is essentially never correct (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

explanation.summary: Self-collection (vaginal flocked swab for HPV) became universally available within the NCSP in July 2022 and is particularly important for under-screened groups. It has equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected sampling for oncogenic HPV detection (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Universal self-collection eligibility since 1 July 2022
Approved sample is a vaginal flocked swab (not urine)
Equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected HPV testing
Reflex LBC requires clinician-collected sample - patient is recalled if HPV non-16/18 detected on self-collected sample
Patients screened directly by colposcopy if HPV 16/18 is detected on a self-collected sample
explanation.pearls: If a self-collected sample detects non-16/18 oncogenic HPV, the patient must return for a clinician-collected sample for reflex LBC. If HPV 16/18 is detected, the patient is referred directly to colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: NCSP - non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with pLSIL reflex cytology
stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 returns for her cervical screening result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible HIGH-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is asymptomatic; cervix appeared normal at the time of the sample. She is in a monogamous relationship of 12 years, last screened 7 years ago (normal Pap), non-smoker, BMI 24, no immunosuppression. She received Gardasil as a teenager. She uses the LNG-IUS. She is anxious and asks specifically whether 'non-16/18' means she should just have another screen in 12 months rather than colposcopy.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. Cervix appeared normal at speculum at the time of sample with no visible lesion and no contact bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer directly to colposcopy now
options.rationale: Under the NCSP, HPV non-16/18 detected with HIGH-grade reflex cytology (pHSIL or HSIL) mandates direct colposcopy, the same as any HPV 16/18 result. The 12-month repeat pathway applies only to HPV non-16/18 with NEGATIVE or pLSIL reflex cytology (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Repeat clinician-collected HPV test in 12 months from today
options.rationale: 12-month repeat is for non-16/18 with NEGATIVE or pLSIL cytology; pHSIL escalates to direct colposcopy. 12-month repeat is correct for non-16/18 + negative/pLSIL only (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Repeat HPV test in 6 months as an interim safety check
options.rationale: No 6-month repeat interval in NCSP for screening results; colposcopy is required. 6-month interval was the old pathway; current NCSP uses 12-month surveillance only (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly screening as cytology is described as only 'possible'
options.rationale: pHSIL is treated as actionable and triggers colposcopy referral, not routine return. Routine 5-yearly return is correct only with negative HPV (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Refer to colposcopy at 6 months if she develops symptoms, otherwise routine screening
options.rationale: Symptom-based delay is unsafe; pHSIL on screening requires prompt colposcopy regardless of symptoms. Symptom-based escalation is essentially never correct in screening-detected pHSIL (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

explanation.summary: Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative/pLSIL/LSIL reflex cytology is intermediate-risk and managed by repeat HPV at 12 months. Most transient infections clear in this interval; persistent infection or progression to HSIL on cytology prompts colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Intermediate risk pathway: non-16/18 HPV + negative/pLSIL/LSIL LBC -> 12-month repeat HPV
Higher risk: non-16/18 HPV + possible HSIL/HSIL/glandular abnormality -> colposcopy
Highest risk: any HPV 16/18 -> direct colposcopy regardless of LBC
Immunosuppressed patients have a different pathway with closer surveillance
Symptomatic patients always co-tested and referred regardless of result
explanation.pearls: The 12-month repeat is HPV-based, not cytology-based. Many transient HPV infections clear in this interval; only persistent infection requires colposcopy (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: HPV vaccination - Australian schedule (Gardasil 9, single-dose schedule from 2023)
stem: A 14-year-old female adolescent attends a GP clinic with her mother for a general check after recently arriving in Australia from overseas. She has not received any HPV vaccination previously. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is otherwise well, with menarche at age 12 and regular cycles. She has never been sexually active. Routine childhood vaccinations were completed in her country of origin but did not include HPV. She is up to date with school enrolment. She has no immunosuppression, no organ transplant, and is not on long-term corticosteroids. Her mother specifically asks about the HPV vaccine schedule because she has read conflicting information online about whether one or two doses are required. The GP confirms the patient is generally well, has had no recent febrile illness, and is up to date with all other Australian National Immunisation Program vaccines. She is keen to receive the vaccine today if it is offered for free.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Tanner stage 4. Otherwise unremarkable.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate HPV vaccination plan for this patient under the National Immunisation Program?

options.text: Three doses of Gardasil 9 at 0, 2 and 6 months because she is starting late
options.rationale: Three-dose schedule is reserved for immunocompromised individuals; immunocompetent people up to age 25 receive a single dose under 2023 schedule. Three-dose is correct only in immunocompromised patients (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Two doses of Gardasil 9 at 0 and 6 months because she is over 14
options.rationale: Two-dose schedules were used prior to February 2023; current schedule is single dose for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25. Two-dose was correct pre-Feb 2023 (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Single dose of Gardasil 9 today, funded under the National Immunisation Program
options.rationale: Since February 2023 the Australian NIP HPV schedule is a single dose of Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25, including catch-up. Routine school-based delivery is in year 7 (around age 12-13) but free catch-up is funded up to and including age 25 (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Defer HPV vaccination until age 18 when sexual debut is more likely
options.rationale: HPV vaccination is most effective before HPV exposure; deferring removes protection during highest-risk period. Deferral is essentially never correct in HPV vaccination strategy (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Single dose of bivalent Cervarix today as she is over 14 years old
options.rationale: Cervarix (bivalent HPV 16/18) is not the funded NIP vaccine in Australia; Gardasil 9 (9-valent) is the program vaccine with broader genotype coverage. Cervarix is correct only in jurisdictions where it remains the program vaccine (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

explanation.summary: Since February 2023, the Australian NIP HPV schedule has been a single dose of Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25 years, including catch-up. Three doses remain recommended for the severely immunocompromised. School delivery is in year 7 (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
key_points: Single dose Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9-25
Three-dose schedule (0, 2, 6 months) for severe immunocompromise
Routine school-based program delivers in year 7 (ages 12-13)
Catch-up free under NIP up to and including age 25
Vaccination does not change cervical screening requirements
explanation.pearls: Australia was the first country to move to a single-dose HPV schedule on the National Immunisation Program (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

subtopic_detail: Symptomatic cervical cancer - postcoital bleeding workup overrides routine screening
stem: A 35-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP with a 4-month history of intermittent postcoital bleeding occurring after most episodes of intercourse, with one episode of intermenstrual bleeding lasting 2 days. She has had no abnormal discharge, no dyspareunia and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 18 months ago and showed HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She received Gardasil as a teenager and is otherwise well. She is a current smoker (15 pack-years) and uses the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has one child after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. There is no significant family history. She has been treated empirically by another clinician for presumed chlamydial cervicitis with azithromycin 1 g stat 6 weeks ago, with chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT both negative; symptoms have persisted unchanged. BMI is 24. She has no fevers, no weight loss, no urinary symptoms and no bowel symptoms. She is asking for a 'repeat HPV swab to make sure'.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Speculum: cervix has a friable area at 4 o'clock with contact bleeding on swab; no obvious exophytic lesion. Bimanual: uterus normal size, no adnexal mass, no cervical motion tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. Chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT negative 6 weeks ago. HPV screening test 18 months ago: HPV not detected.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the routine NCSP HPV cervical screening test and act on the result
options.rationale: Routine HPV screening is for asymptomatic women; symptomatic patients require co-test PLUS direct referral for colposcopy regardless of HPV result, because negative HPV does not exclude all cervical pathology in a symptomatic patient. Routine HPV alone is correct for asymptomatic women (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Empirical treatment for cervicitis with doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not appropriate when STI testing is negative and symptoms have already failed empirical therapy; persistent postcoital bleeding with friable cervix requires structural assessment. Empirical antibiotics are correct in confirmed/strongly-suspected STI cervicitis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Reassure patient that symptoms will likely resolve and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a smoker with 4 months of postcoital bleeding and visibly friable cervix risks missing cervical cancer. Reassurance is correct only after biopsy excludes malignancy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Perform a co-test (HPV + LBC) and refer for colposcopy regardless of result
options.rationale: Symptomatic cervical bleeding (postcoital, intermenstrual or postmenopausal) is an indication for a co-test PLUS direct referral to colposcopy regardless of HPV or LBC results, because cytology can be falsely negative in invasive cancer and a recent negative HPV does not exclude pathology. A visibly abnormal cervix in a symptomatic patient is colposcopy-urgent (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Discontinue the combined oral contraceptive pill as likely cause, review 3 months
options.rationale: Breakthrough bleeding on COCP rarely presents with reproducible postcoital bleeding and friable cervix; structural pathology must be excluded first. COCP cessation is correct only after cervical pathology excluded (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Persistent postcoital bleeding, particularly with a visibly abnormal cervix, mandates a co-test PLUS colposcopy regardless of the HPV result, because cervical cancer can present with cytology- and HPV-negative findings. A recent negative HPV screen does not exclude invasive disease in a symptomatic patient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Symptomatic cervical bleeding: co-test AND colposcopy regardless of result
Routine HPV screening assumes the patient is asymptomatic
Smoking is a co-factor for cervical cancer progression
Friable, contact-bleeding cervix warrants direct visualisation
Recent negative HPV does not exclude pathology in a symptomatic patient
explanation.pearls: The biggest pitfall in cervical cancer diagnosis is over-reliance on a recent negative screen in a symptomatic patient. Symptoms override screening (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Advanced cervical cancer - recognising locally invasive symptoms
stem: A 52-year-old woman who has never participated in cervical screening attends ED with a 6-week history of intermittent vaginal bleeding (now soaking through several pads daily), foul-smelling watery discharge, deep right-sided pelvic and lower-back pain, new right leg swelling extending to the thigh, and frank haematuria for the past week. She has lost 6 kg unintentionally over 3 months and feels exhausted. She is a former heavy smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago), G3P3 with all vaginal deliveries, and has had three lifetime partners. She received no HPV vaccination. She has no significant past medical history. She emigrated to Australia from a country without an organised cervical screening program at age 30 and has had no screening since. She takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Bowels are regular and there is no rectal bleeding. She is otherwise afebrile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Right leg pitting oedema to thigh, non-tender, no calf cord. Abdomen: tender suprapubic mass, no peritonism. Speculum: large exophytic friable mass replacing the cervix, contact bleeds easily, foul discharge. Bimanual: fixed irregular mass extending into right parametrium.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (115-165), MCV 76 fL, ferritin 8 mcg/L. Urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline unknown). Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 1+. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which combination of clinical features most strongly suggests locally advanced cervical cancer with parametrial / pelvic sidewall involvement?

options.text: Heavy menstrual bleeding with iron-deficiency anaemia and a bulky uterus
options.rationale: HMB with IDA and bulky uterus is non-specific and characterises benign causes (fibroid uterus) rather than locally advanced cervical cancer. This pattern is correct for fibroid disease, not for advanced cervical cancer (RANZCOG; Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Postcoital bleeding with a small, mobile cervix and normal renal function
options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding with mobile cervix and normal renal function is more consistent with early-stage cervical disease or benign causes (ectropion, polyp); lacks features of local extension. Early-stage features are correct for FIGO IA-IB1 disease (RANZCOG).

options.text: Unilateral lower-limb oedema, haematuria, rising creatinine and a fixed parametrial mass
options.rationale: Unilateral leg oedema reflects iliac/pelvic-sidewall lymphatic or venous obstruction, haematuria with rising creatinine suggests bladder invasion or ureteric obstruction with hydronephrosis, and a fixed parametrial mass indicates extracervical spread. Together these are features of locally advanced cervical cancer (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Postmenopausal bleeding with an endometrial thickness of 5 mm on TVUS
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with thickened endometrium is the syndrome of endometrial cancer, not cervical; cervical cancer typically presents with cervix-based symptoms. PMB+endometrial thickening is correct for endometrial pathology (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bilateral lower abdominal pain with adnexal tenderness, pyrexia and elevated WCC
options.rationale: Pattern fits PID or tubo-ovarian abscess; locally advanced cervical cancer is typically afebrile unless superimposed pyometra. PID features are correct for pelvic infection, not cervical cancer (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Locally advanced cervical cancer extends laterally into the parametrium (palpable as a fixed cervix), can occlude ureters (hydronephrosis, rising creatinine), invades bladder (haematuria) or rectum (rectal bleeding, fistula), and causes lymphatic and venous obstruction in the pelvic sidewall (unilateral leg oedema). Recognising these features prompts urgent gynae-oncology referral (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Parametrial extension presents as a fixed cervix on bimanual examination
Ureteric obstruction -> hydronephrosis, rising creatinine, AKI
Bladder invasion -> haematuria; rectal invasion -> rectal bleeding/fistula
Pelvic sidewall lymphatic/venous obstruction -> unilateral leg oedema
Under-screened women (migrants, refugees, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women) over-represented in advanced presentations
explanation.pearls: Cervical cancer remains a disease of inequitable access - 80% of Australian cervical cancers occur in under-screened or never-screened women (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness >4 mm threshold
stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 woman attends her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks. She experienced natural menopause at age 51 with no further bleeding until now. She has no postcoital bleeding, no discharge and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 4 years ago, HPV not detected. She has type 2 diabetes (metformin 1 g BD, last HbA1c 7.4%), essential hypertension on perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 34, and a 25-year history of regular menses prior to menopause. She has never used hormone therapy or tamoxifen. Family history is unremarkable for gynaecological malignancy and Lynch syndrome screening criteria are not met. She does not smoke. She walks 3 km daily and has no urinary or bowel red flags. She has had no recent intercourse and is not on anticoagulation. She is not anaemic on point-of-care testing. She is anxious because her neighbour was recently diagnosed with endometrial cancer.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Speculum: atrophic vaginal mucosa, no cervical lesion, small amount of blood at the os, no friability. Bimanual: uterus normal size, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (115-165). Urinalysis negative. Transvaginal ultrasound: endometrium 8 mm with heterogeneous echotexture; no focal mass identified; both ovaries normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial thickness >4 mm on TVUS requires histological assessment; outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle biopsy is the recommended pathway, with the higher diagnostic yield than blind Pipelle alone when the endometrium is thickened or heterogeneous (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer).

options.text: Trial topical vaginal oestrogen for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Vaginal atrophy is common cause of PMB but cannot be assumed when endometrium is >4 mm; histological exclusion of endometrial pathology is required first. Topical oestrogen is correct after histology excludes pathology (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure as a single episode and review in 3 months if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Any PMB warrants investigation; reassurance without histology is unsafe given endometrial cancer prevalence ~10% in PMB. Reassurance is essentially never correct in PMB without histology (RANZCOG; eviQ).

options.text: Order a serum CA-125 to risk-stratify endometrial pathology before further imaging
options.rationale: CA-125 is used in ovarian cancer risk stratification, not endometrial; histology directly addresses the diagnostic question. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound in 6 weeks to assess for any change
options.rationale: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without altering the indication for histology in PMB with endometrium >4 mm. Repeat US is correct only with inadequate initial imaging (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

explanation.summary: Any episode of postmenopausal bleeding must be investigated. TVUS endometrial thickness of >4 mm in a postmenopausal woman not on HRT, or any focal endometrial abnormality, mandates histological assessment via hysteroscopy and biopsy (RANZCOG; eviQ).
key_points: Any PMB requires investigation - approximately 10% have endometrial cancer
TVUS endometrial thickness threshold for biopsy: >4 mm (postmenopausal, no HRT)
Persistent or recurrent PMB requires biopsy even if endometrium <4 mm
Hysteroscopy + directed biopsy outperforms blind Pipelle when endometrium is thickened/focal
Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, nulliparity, late menopause, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
explanation.pearls: If a postmenopausal woman has bleeding but endometrium is <=4 mm with otherwise reassuring TVUS, reassure but warn that recurrent bleeding mandates histology regardless of thickness (RANZCOG).

subtopic_detail: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia - management in postmenopausal woman
stem: A 58-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman is referred to gynaecology after 4 weeks of intermittent vaginal bleeding. TVUS demonstrated an endometrial thickness of 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance and no focal mass. Outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle biopsies reports endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (also called endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia, EIN); no invasive disease seen on the available tissue. She has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.6%, metformin 1 g BD), hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 38, and natural menopause at age 53. She has no family history of Lynch-associated cancers. She has completed her family. She has no contraindications to surgery, ASA 2, and is keen to 'sort this out properly'. She does not wish to continue hormonal therapy long term. She has no thromboembolic history and a normal cardiac assessment on preoperative review. She asks specifically about the risk that this finding already represents cancer.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 140/84 mmHg, HR 78 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus normal size, mobile, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Pipelle + hysteroscopic directed biopsy: endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (EIN). Hb 122 g/L. TVUS: ET 12 mm heterogeneous, both ovaries normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as definitive surgical management
options.rationale: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN) carries a concurrent endometrial carcinoma rate of approximately 30-40% on final hysterectomy specimens and a substantial future progression risk; in a postmenopausal woman who has completed her family, total hysterectomy with BSO is the recommended definitive treatment (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer; RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system with 6-monthly endometrial sampling for surveillance
options.rationale: LNG-IUS with surveillance is reserved for premenopausal patients wishing to retain fertility or unfit for surgery; in postmenopausal woman with completed family, definitive surgery is preferred. LNG-IUS is correct in fertility-sparing context (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Oral medroxyprogesterone acetate with repeat biopsy in 12 months
options.rationale: Oral progestogens are less effective than LNG-IUS for hyperplasia regression and not first-line in surgical candidate with completed family. Oral MPA is correct only in non-surgical candidates (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Endometrial ablation to control bleeding and reduce cancer risk
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is CONTRAINDICATED with hyperplasia + atypia or suspected malignancy because residual buried endometrium can harbour or develop cancer. Ablation is correct for benign HMB without hyperplasia (RANZCOG; RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Watchful waiting with repeat TVUS and endometrial biopsy in 6 months
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe for atypical hyperplasia given the high concurrent (~30-40%) and progression risk to invasive cancer. Watchful waiting is correct only in hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia in selected reversible scenarios (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

explanation.summary: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (EIN) carries a 30-40% concurrent endometrial cancer rate. Total hysterectomy with BSO is the standard of care in postmenopausal women or those who have completed their family. Fertility-sparing LNG-IUS is reserved for younger patients or those unfit for surgery (RCOG/BSGE).
key_points: Hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia: LNG-IUS first-line, repeat biopsy at 6 months
Hyperplasia WITH atypia (EIN): hysterectomy + BSO if postmenopausal or completed family
Fertility-sparing option in atypia: LNG-IUS with 3-monthly biopsy until regression
Endometrial ablation contraindicated when atypia/malignancy is present or suspected
Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
explanation.pearls: Even with formal hysteroscopy plus Pipelle, 30-40% of atypical hyperplasia cases harbour an undetected concurrent endometrial cancer at hysterectomy (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

subtopic_detail: Ovarian cancer recognition - vague abdominal symptoms + CA-125
stem: A 62-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman attends her GP with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, mild lower abdominal discomfort, increased urinary frequency without dysuria, and unintentional 4 kg weight loss. She initially thought she had developed IBS. There is no rectal bleeding, no change in bowel habit beyond mild constipation, and no postmenopausal bleeding. She has no significant past medical history, no family history of ovarian, breast or bowel cancer, and is not known to carry BRCA1/2. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol minimally. She has had no abdominal surgery. Her last cervical screening test was 2 years ago, HPV not detected. She has not used hormone therapy. Examination reveals mild abdominal distension and a vaguely palpable left adnexal fullness. There is no overt ascites but a low-volume effect cannot be excluded clinically. She is afebrile, with stable observations. The GP has already started her on a fibre supplement which has not relieved symptoms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 80 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen mildly distended. Vague left lower-quadrant fullness, no peritonism. Bimanual: left adnexal mass approximately 7 cm, partly fixed; uterus normal size.
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal. Urea, creatinine, LFTs within normal limits. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Colonoscopy as the initial investigation for presumed bowel pathology and constipation
options.rationale: Colorectal cancer is on the differential, but palpable adnexal mass in postmenopausal woman shifts priority to gynaecological assessment. Colonoscopy is correct after gynaecological workup excludes ovarian pathology or in dominant bowel symptoms (RANZCOG; Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Serum HE4 alone as a more specific tumour marker than CA-125
options.rationale: HE4 is not established Australian first-line marker; CA-125 is incorporated in RMI and Australian pathway. HE4 is correct in ROMA-based international pathways (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Trial of antispasmodic and follow up if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks
options.rationale: Antispasmodics in postmenopausal woman with palpable adnexal mass risks delaying ovarian cancer diagnosis; symptoms beyond 4 weeks warrant urgent investigation. Antispasmodic trial is correct only without red flags (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as first-line imaging investigation
options.rationale: CT has a role in staging once malignancy is suspected, but pelvic US is recommended first-line imaging to characterise the lesion. CT is correct after US suggests malignancy (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Pelvic transvaginal and transabdominal ultrasound with serum CA-125 for RMI/IOTA risk stratification
options.rationale: Persistent abdominal bloating, early satiety, urinary frequency or pelvic pain in a postmenopausal woman, particularly with a palpable adnexal mass, requires urgent gynaecological workup. The Australian pathway is pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal + transabdominal) plus serum CA-125; together these enable Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) or IOTA-based triage to a gynaecological oncology MDT (RANZCOG; Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

explanation.summary: Ovarian cancer typically presents with vague abdominal symptoms (bloating, early satiety, urinary frequency, abdominal/pelvic pain) lasting more than a few weeks; in postmenopausal women these symptoms must trigger TVUS plus CA-125. The combination feeds RMI/IOTA scoring and triages referral to a gynaecological oncology MDT (Cancer Australia; RANZCOG).
key_points: Symptoms: persistent bloating, early satiety, urinary urgency/frequency, pelvic/abdominal pain
First-line investigation: TVUS + transabdominal US + serum CA-125
Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) = U x M x CA-125, with US features, menopausal status
IOTA simple rules / ADNEX model for ultrasound-based malignancy risk
All suspicious adnexal masses in postmenopausal women -> gynae oncology MDT
explanation.pearls: Ovarian cancer is often labelled the 'silent killer' but a careful history elicits 3-6 months of persistent symptoms in most patients; the key skill is taking these complaints seriously in postmenopausal women (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

subtopic_detail: Test of cure after LLETZ for HSIL
stem: A 34-year-old G0 woman had a LLETZ procedure 11 months ago for biopsy-proven HSIL (CIN3) detected after a screening HPV test reported HPV 16 with possible HSIL on reflex cytology. The LLETZ histology confirmed CIN3 with clear excision margins (ectocervical, endocervical and deep margins all clear of dysplasia, no glandular involvement, no microinvasion). Post-LLETZ recovery was uncomplicated with no secondary haemorrhage and no cervical stenosis on subsequent review. She is asymptomatic, with no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding and no discharge. She is in a monogamous relationship of 2 years and is using the etonogestrel implant for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, has no immunosuppression, takes no regular medications and has no family history of cervical or other gynaecological malignancy. She received Gardasil as a teenager and completed her course. She attends her GP today asking what surveillance she now needs and when she can return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft. Cervix at speculum: smooth, no obvious lesion, no contact bleeding.

lead_in: Which surveillance pathway should she follow under the Australian NCSP?

options.text: Annual HPV cervical screening for 5 years, then return to routine 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: Annual HPV testing is not the NCSP test-of-cure pathway; structured ToC uses co-test at 12 months then yearly until two consecutive negatives. Annual HPV-only is correct in some immunocompromised surveillance, not post-LLETZ (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Single co-test at 24 months and return to routine 5-yearly screening if negative
options.rationale: Single co-test is not adequate; two consecutive negative co-tests at 12 and 24 months are required before returning to routine screening. Single co-test is essentially never sufficient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening now because excision margins were clear
options.rationale: Clear excision margins reduce but do not eliminate the risk of recurrent HSIL; structured surveillance is required because residual HPV is strongest predictor. Routine 5-yearly is correct only after two negative ToC co-tests (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: 6-monthly colposcopy for 2 years regardless of cytology or HPV result
options.rationale: Routine 6-monthly colposcopy is not the NCSP pathway after successfully treated CIN3 with clear margins; cytological/HPV co-test surveillance is sufficient. 6-monthly colposcopy is correct only with positive ToC results or persistent disease (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Co-test at 12 and 24 months; both negative returns to 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: After treatment of HSIL (CIN2/3), the NCSP test-of-cure protocol is a co-test (HPV + LBC on the same sample) at 12 and 24 months post-treatment. If both are HPV not detected and LBC negative, the patient returns to routine 5-yearly HPV screening. Any positive result triggers colposcopy referral (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: After treatment of CIN2/3 (HSIL), the NCSP test-of-cure pathway is a co-test (HPV + LBC) at 12 and 24 months. Two consecutive negative co-tests return the patient to routine 5-yearly screening. Any positive result triggers colposcopy referral. Persistent HPV positivity is the strongest predictor of recurrent disease (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Test of cure after HSIL treatment: co-test at 12 and 24 months
Two consecutive negative co-tests -> return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening
Any positive at test of cure -> colposcopy referral
Adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS) treatment requires longer surveillance
Immunosuppressed patients have a more intensive surveillance protocol
explanation.pearls: Persistent HPV is the strongest single predictor of recurrent CIN2/3 after LLETZ - more important than margin status alone (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

stem: A 44-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP with an 8-month history of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Cycles are regular at 28 days but bleeding now lasts 9 days with flooding through super tampons hourly on days 2-3 and passage of large clots up to 3 cm in size. She reports increasing tiredness, palpitations on minor exertion, and exertional shortness of breath climbing one flight of stairs. Her work as a primary school teacher has been disrupted and she has had to change pads during teaching sessions. She is not currently sexually active, has had no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, and no postcoital spotting. There is no abdominal or pelvic pain. There is no easy bruising, no gum bleeding, no epistaxis, no family bleeding history and her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. BMI is 34. She has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and takes no anticoagulants, no antiplatelets and no over-the-counter NSAIDs. She has had two normal vaginal deliveries with no postpartum haemorrhage. She has not yet had pelvic imaging or endometrial sampling in this presentation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. Bimanual: uterus bulky, mobile, anteverted, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L (115-165), MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 mcg/L. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: In addition to a transvaginal ultrasound and full blood count with ferritin, which further investigation is most important at this visit?

options.text: FSH and oestradiol to assess for perimenopausal anovulation as cause
options.rationale: Random FSH/oestradiol in a 44-year-old with regular cycles is unhelpful; cycle regularity argues against anovulation. Hormonal testing is correct only with established cycle irregularity (RANZCOG HMB).

options.text: Coagulation screen and von Willebrand panel as the first-line bleeding work-up
options.rationale: Inherited bleeding disorder screen is for HMB since menarche or personal/family bleeding cues; her 8-month history without bleeding red flags does not prioritise this. Coag/vWF panel is correct with bleeding history clues (RANZCOG HMB).

options.text: Endometrial Pipelle biopsy at this visit, with TSH and coagulation screen as adjuncts
options.rationale: RANZCOG and NICE NG88 recommend endometrial sampling in women with HMB who are aged 45 or over OR who have risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer; obesity (BMI 34) is a major risk factor and TVUS alone is insufficient to exclude endometrial pathology in this setting (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy as the sole investigation in place of TVUS today
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is appropriate where TVUS suggests focal pathology or sampling is unsuccessful, but TVUS remains first-line. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only with TVUS showing focal lesion (NICE NG88).

options.text: Serum CA-125 to screen for occult ovarian malignancy in this patient
options.rationale: CA-125 is non-specific in premenopausal women and has no role in HMB workup. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Workup of HMB follows PALM-COEIN. TVUS plus FBC/ferritin are universal. Endometrial sampling is indicated at age >=45, persistent intermenstrual bleeding, failed medical therapy, or risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, anovulation, tamoxifen, Lynch). TSH and coagulation screen are adjuncts based on history.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
FBC + ferritin in all; TSH if cycle change or clinical features; coags if bleeding history since menarche
Biopsy if age >=45 OR risk factor for endometrial cancer (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen)
TVUS first-line imaging; saline-infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy for focal lesions
explanation.pearls: Obesity lowers the age threshold for endometrial sampling. Don't anchor on age 45 if other risk factors are present.

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - structured PALM-COEIN workup

stem: A 39-year-old G1P1 woman has a 2-year history of heavy regular menstrual bleeding with cycle-related bulk symptoms including urinary frequency, mild lower-abdominal pressure, and occasional nocturia. She passes large clots during menses and floods through super tampons every 1-2 hours on the heaviest days. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm intramural fibroid (FIGO type 4, not distorting the cavity) and a 3 cm subserosal fibroid; the endometrial cavity is regular and the endometrium is 6 mm. Endometrial biopsy returns benign proliferative endometrium with no atypia. She has completed her family, is in a monogamous relationship using condoms for contraception, and would prefer to avoid surgery if possible. She does not want to lose her fertility surgically and is open to a long-acting hormonal device. She has tried tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses with only modest improvement and has not tolerated mefenamic acid. BMI 26. She has no contraindications to oestrogen or progestogen, no migraines and no history of VTE.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, lower-uterine mass palpable just above pubis. Bimanual: bulky irregular uterus approx 12-week size, mobile.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 101 g/L (115-165), ferritin 14 mcg/L. TVUS as above. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?

options.text: GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly) with add-back oestrogen
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are used short-term (3-6 months) to shrink fibroids pre-surgery or to bridge perimenopause; not first-line ongoing therapy due to vasomotor symptoms and bone loss. GnRH is correct as bridge therapy, not chronic (RANZCOG).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion now
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is first-line medical therapy for HMB including fibroid-related HMB where the uterine cavity is not distorted by submucosal fibroids; it reduces blood loss by 70-95% and is acceptable for a woman who has completed her family (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: TXA reduces blood loss by 40-50% but does not address bulk symptoms and is less effective than LNG-IUS overall. TXA is correct for acute heavy periods or as adjunct (NICE NG88).

options.text: Mefenamic acid 500 mg orally three times daily during menses only
options.rationale: NSAIDs reduce blood loss by ~25-30% and help dysmenorrhoea; useful adjuncts but not first-line for moderate-severe HMB with anaemia. NSAIDs are correct as adjunctive symptomatic therapy (NICE NG88).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy under GA
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but should be reserved for failed/declined medical therapy; she has not yet trialled LARC. Hysterectomy is correct after failed conservative options or with patient preference (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: For fibroid-related HMB without significant cavity distortion: LNG-IUS first-line. Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs are useful adjuncts. GnRH analogues are short-term pre-surgical or perimenopausal bridging agents. Myomectomy preserves fertility; hysterectomy is definitive when family complete and other options failed.
key_points: LNG-IUS: first-line for HMB without cavity distortion (FIGO types 3-7 generally OK)
Submucosal fibroids (FIGO 0-2) distorting cavity: hysteroscopic myomectomy
GnRH agonist: short-term shrinkage pre-op or perimenopausal bridge
Uterine artery embolisation: alternative when fertility not desired
Myomectomy: preserves fertility; hysterectomy: definitive cure
explanation.pearls: Mirena requires a cavity that the device can sit in. A large submucosal fibroid distorting the cavity contraindicates Mirena and instead triggers hysteroscopic myomectomy.

subtopic_detail: Uterine fibroids - choice of medical therapy

stem: A 27-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 4 years of cyclical pelvic pain (peaks day 1-2, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia during menses, cyclical back pain). She has tried regular ibuprofen and heat-pack regimen with minimal benefit. She is not planning conception in the next 18-24 months but does want children eventually.
