She has no migraine with aura, no smoking, no VTE history, BMI 24. Pelvic USS normal (no endometrioma). Bimanual examination shows tender uterosacral nodularity. She is now keen for the most evidence-based next step that does not commit her to surgery if it can be avoided.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus anteverted, mobile, mildly tender. No palpable nodularity in the pouch of Douglas. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal-appearing ovaries, no endometrioma, no deep infiltrating lesion visualised.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial continuous combined oral contraceptive pill with NSAID for breakthrough pain, with review at 3-6 months and laparoscopy if refractory or fertility planning advances
options.rationale: RANZCOG and ESHRE endometriosis guidance support empirical hormonal therapy (continuous COCP or progestogen) as first-line in suspected endometriosis without requiring laparoscopic confirmation, especially if fertility is not imminently sought. Laparoscopy is reserved for refractory cases or fertility planning (RANZCOG; ESHRE 2022).

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before any medical therapy
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy is now preferred first-line over diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis. Laparoscopy is correct after failed medical therapy or for surgical planning (ESHRE 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Serum CA-125 to support the working diagnosis
options.rationale: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology, not endometriosis diagnosis (ESHRE 2022).

options.text: MRI pelvis as the next investigation to confirm the working diagnosis
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis with surgical planning, not primary diagnosis. MRI is correct for deep endometriosis surgical mapping (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: GnRH agonist with add-back HRT as first-line therapy
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second/third line after COCP/progestogens due to bone density and menopausal symptoms. GnRH is correct after first-line hormonal failure (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Endometriosis diagnosis is clinical; TVUS often normal in superficial peritoneal disease. Laparoscopy is gold standard for visual/histological confirmation but not required to commence empirical therapy. First-line medical mgmt: continuous COCP/POP/LNG-IUS + NSAID.
key_points: Classic features: cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria
Empirical hormonal therapy precedes laparoscopy in straightforward presentations
TVUS first-line imaging; MRI for suspected deep disease
Laparoscopy: failed medical therapy, suspected deep disease, infertility, severe symptoms
Refer to gynaecologist if no response in 3-6 months or red flags
explanation.pearls: Normal pelvic ultrasound does not exclude endometriosis. Trial hormonal therapy empirically.

stem: A 33-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea following a 12-month history of progressively irregular cycles. She reports new hot flushes (around 6-8 per day), troublesome night sweats with disturbed sleep, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia, low mood, reduced libido and increased fatigue. She is in a stable long-term relationship and is open to future fertility consideration, although they have not actively been trying to conceive. There is no recent significant weight change, no excessive exercise, no medication changes, no eating disorder behaviours, no galactorrhoea, no headaches or visual field symptoms. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no previous ovarian surgery. She had menarche at age 12 with previously regular 28-day cycles. Family history: her mother had menopause at age 41 and her maternal aunt had menopause in her early forties; one cousin has Hashimoto's thyroiditis. BMI 23. She is not using contraception currently. Beta-hCG is negative today. Initial bloods 5 weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L, oestradiol low, prolactin normal, TSH normal, total testosterone normal. Today's repeat FSH and oestradiol have been collected and are pending.

data_table.Examination: BMI 23. No goitre. No visual field defect. Speculum: pale dry vaginal mucosa.
data_table.Investigations: Five weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L (premenopausal <10), oestradiol low, LH 38, prolactin normal, TSH 1.8, beta-hCG negative. Today: bloods repeated, pending.

lead_in: If today's repeat FSH is similarly elevated (>25 IU/L), which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure and recheck FSH in 6 months as a single elevated FSH is non-diagnostic alone
options.rationale: POI diagnosis requires two FSH >25 IU/L at least 4 weeks apart; her first sample was 5 weeks prior, so confirmatory second now is appropriate. Reassurance with re-check at 6 months is correct only with isolated borderline FSH and no amenorrhoea (Australasian Menopause Society POI).

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill solely for menopausal symptom control without further workup needed
options.rationale: COCP can be used in POI but HRT provides more physiological replacement and better bone protection. COCP is correct when contraception desired alongside replacement (BMS).

options.text: Bone density scan and fertility counselling referral, but defer hormonal therapy until age 40 years
options.rationale: Delaying oestrogen replacement until 40 risks cardiovascular, bone, and neurocognitive harm; replacement should commence at diagnosis. Defer HRT is essentially never correct in confirmed POI (ESHRE).

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for an adrenal failure work-up before initiating any hormonal therapy in her
options.rationale: Adrenal autoimmune screen (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is part of POI workup but does not need to precede HRT commencement. Adrenal screen is correct concurrently, not before HRT (ESHRE).

options.text: Systemic HRT, fertility counselling, screen for adrenal/thyroid antibodies and karyotype, plus baseline bone densitometry
options.rationale: POI confirmed by FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions at least 4 weeks apart with amenorrhoea >=4 months in a woman <40 mandates HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause to protect bone and cardiovascular health; concurrent work-up includes karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal antibodies, thyroid antibodies, and a fertility discussion (ESHRE 2016; Jean Hailes).

explanation.summary: POI: amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea >=4 months + FSH >25 IU/L on 2 readings 4+ weeks apart, in a woman aged under 40. Work-up: karyotype, fragile X premutation, adrenal (21-OH) and thyroid antibodies, baseline DXA. Treat with HRT until at least the average age of natural menopause (~51) for bone, CV and neurocognitive protection. Spontaneous pregnancy possible in ~5-10%; HRT is not a contraceptive.
key_points: Diagnosis: amenorrhoea >=4 months + 2 x FSH >25 IU/L, 4+ weeks apart, age <40
Causes: idiopathic, genetic (Turner, fragile X), autoimmune, iatrogenic (chemo/radiotherapy/surgery)
HRT (or COCP) until ~age 51
Discuss contraception separately - HRT not contraceptive
Refer for fertility counselling early
explanation.pearls: POI is not just early menopause - women may still ovulate intermittently and conceive; HRT does not prevent pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Premature ovarian insufficiency - diagnosis and HRT

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman presents to ED with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began 90 minutes ago while she was walking briskly home. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, sharp, with intermittent stabbing exacerbations, and is associated with two episodes of bilious vomiting and one episode of diarrhoea-free loose stool. She is sexually active with her regular partner of 18 months, uses condoms consistently, and her last menstrual period began 14 days ago and was normal in flow and duration. Her cycles are regular at 28 days and she has no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. There is no fever, no rigors, no dysuria, no frequency, no haematuria, no diarrhoea, no recent travel and no recent change in bowel habit. She had a similar but milder episode three months ago that resolved spontaneously after 4 hours; at that time she did not present for medical review. She has no significant past medical history, no previous abdominal surgery, no allergies, and takes no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Pale and distressed. Abdomen: marked tenderness right iliac fossa with guarding but no rebound; bowel sounds present. Bimanual: marked right adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness equivocal, no cervical discharge. No palpable adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. FBC: WCC 12.1, neutrophils 8.9. CRP 8 mg/L. UEC normal. Urinalysis: trace blood, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside USS in ED: enlarged right ovary 7 cm with a 5 cm complex cyst, minimal arterial Doppler flow within the ovary.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum and shock
options.rationale: Negative urine beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic; sensitivity approaches 99%. Ectopic is correct with positive hCG (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ovarian torsion of a right ovarian cyst
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting, an enlarged ovary with a cyst and reduced/absent arterial Doppler flow on TVUS in a haemodynamically stable woman with negative beta-hCG points to ovarian torsion; the prior self-resolving episode is typical of intermittent torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
options.rationale: PID presents with bilateral pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever, raised inflammatory markers; the sudden unilateral severe pattern fits torsion. PID is correct with sexually active patient with discharge, fever, bilateral pain (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Ruptured haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with pelvic pain
options.rationale: Ruptured cyst produces pain improving over hours; US shows free fluid without persistent enlarged ovary and Doppler is preserved. Ruptured cyst is correct with improving pain and free fluid alone (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute appendicitis with right iliac fossa peritonism
options.rationale: Appendicitis progresses over 12-24 hours with central-to-RIF migration, anorexia, rising inflammatory markers; the abrupt severe onset with prior identical self-resolving episode points to torsion. Appendicitis is correct with progressive migrating pain (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pain, nausea/vomiting, enlarged ovary +/- cyst on USS, reduced or absent Doppler flow (though preserved flow does not exclude torsion). Prior self-resolving episodes (intermittent torsion) are a major clue. Treatment is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation.
key_points: Negative beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic
Doppler flow: reduced/absent suggests torsion but normal flow does not exclude it (dual blood supply)
Ovarian mass >5 cm increases torsion risk
Treatment: urgent laparoscopy with detorsion; oophorectomy only if necrotic/malignant
Cystectomy of underlying cyst at the time, or interval if oedematous
explanation.pearls: Pregnancy, mass >5 cm, prior similar episodes, and right > left predominance are torsion risk hooks. Do not wait for absent Doppler flow before operating.

stem: A 21-year-old sexually active woman presents with 4 days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, mucopurulent vaginal discharge, fever 38.4 C, HR 110, BP 100/64. Examination: bilateral lower-quadrant tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, AND a 6 cm tender right adnexal mass with bedside USS demonstrating a multilocular cystic mass with internal debris consistent with tubo-ovarian abscess. She is unable to tolerate oral intake due to nausea and vomiting. Urine beta-hCG negative. WCC 18, CRP 180. NAAT swabs sent. She has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen: bilateral lower abdominal tenderness. Bimanual: cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, no palpable adnexal mass.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit and commence IV ceftriaxone, doxycycline (or azithromycin if intolerant) and metronidazole, with surgical/interventional drainage if no improvement at 48-72 hours
options.rationale: Severe PID with tubo-ovarian abscess, systemic features (fever, tachycardia, vomiting) requires hospital admission and IV combination therapy. Drainage is added if there is no response in 48-72 hours or the abscess is large (RANZCOG PID; eTG).

options.text: Outpatient ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + oral doxycycline + oral metronidazole for 14 days
options.rationale: Outpatient regimen is for mild-moderate PID without abscess or systemic features; her TOA, fever, tachycardia, vomiting require IV in hospital. Outpatient is correct in mild PID (RANZCOG PID; eTG).

options.text: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone and discharge home with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Single-dose monotherapy is inadequate for any PID, let alone severe with TOA. Single-dose IM is essentially never sufficient in PID (eTG).

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparoscopy and salpingectomy as the primary intervention
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, ruptured TOA, or failure of antibiotic therapy at 48-72 hours. Surgery is correct after antibiotic failure or rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin monotherapy for 14 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is not first-line PID therapy and does not cover gonorrhoea, anaerobes, or atypicals. Amoxicillin is correct in confirmed susceptible Listeria or strep infections, not PID (eTG).

explanation.summary: Empirical outpatient PID regimen per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat + doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Take partner notification, STI screen, and HIV/hepatitis screening at presentation.
key_points: Ceftriaxone covers gonorrhoea
Doxycycline covers chlamydia and M. genitalium (test/retest if treatment failure)
Metronidazole covers anaerobes
Admit and treat IV if pregnant, severe, TOA, sepsis, or failed oral therapy
Partner notification and contact tracing essential
explanation.pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis - don't wait for swab results before starting antibiotics.

stem: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP for contraceptive counselling. She has been using condoms only since her last relationship ended and would now like a more reliable method as she is in a new monogamous relationship of 9 months. She has a 6-year history of migraine with aura: a visual scotoma with shimmering edge develops in one visual field for 20-30 minutes preceding a moderate-to-severe unilateral throbbing headache that lasts up to 24 hours. These attacks occur 1-2 times per month and are usually associated with sleep deprivation or menstruation. She has been reviewed by a neurologist who confirmed the diagnosis of migraine with aura. She is otherwise well, non-smoker, BMI 24, blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, and has no personal or family history of VTE, ischaemic heart disease or stroke. She has had no episodes of focal neurology between migraine attacks. She has not had children and is open to long-acting reversible methods. She would prefer a method she does not need to remember daily. She has no allergies and takes sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for acute migraine attacks; she is not on any prophylactic agent.

data_table.Examination: BP 118/76 mmHg. BMI 24. No focal neurology between attacks.
data_table.Investigations: None required for routine contraceptive counselling.

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is contraindicated in this patient?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) for 5 years
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 in migraine with aura; local progestogen does not raise stroke risk. Mirena is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant is UKMEC 2 in migraine with aura - acceptable. Implant is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Copper intrauterine device for up to 10 years
options.rationale: Cu-IUD is UKMEC 1 in migraine with aura. Copper IUD is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg with levonorgestrel 150 mcg)
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception (COCP, patch, vaginal ring) is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) in migraine with aura due to a 2-4-fold increase in ischaemic stroke risk; absolute contraindication (FSRH UKMEC 2016; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Progestogen-only desogestrel 75 mcg pill taken once daily continuously
options.rationale: POP is UKMEC 2 in migraine with aura - acceptable. POP is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Migraine with aura is UKMEC category 4 for combined hormonal contraception (COCP, patch, ring) - an absolute contraindication. All progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS, DMPA) and the copper IUD are safe (UKMEC 1-2). The increased stroke risk is driven by the oestrogen component.
key_points: Migraine WITH aura + CHC = UKMEC 4 (do not use)
Migraine WITHOUT aura + CHC = UKMEC 2 (initiation), 3 (continuation if aura develops)
All POP methods and IUDs are UKMEC 1-2 in migraine with aura
Smoking + age >=35 + COCP = also UKMEC 3-4
Aura = focal neurological symptoms preceding headache, typically 5-60 min
explanation.pearls: If migraines develop aura while on COCP, stop the COCP immediately and switch to a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method.

subtopic_detail: Contraception - migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 for COCP)

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia, no migraines and has a BMI of 25. She does not smoke. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Well. BP 118/74. BMI 25. Soft well-involuted uterus, no lochia, perineum healed. Lactating breasts, no mastitis.
data_table.Investigations: None required today; routine 6-week postnatal review.

lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as BP has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 <6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding, UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months. CHC is correct >6 months postpartum or not breastfeeding (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole contraceptive method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
options.rationale: LAM requires all three: amenorrhoea, exclusive/near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND <6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter. LAM is correct when all three criteria met (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted today, and etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
options.rationale: Intrauterine contraception (LNG-IUS or Cu-IUD) can be safely inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (UKMEC 1-2); progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA) are also suitable in breastfeeding women from any time postpartum (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an adverse effect on milk supply
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant does not adversely affect milk supply and can be inserted at any time postpartum. Implant delay is essentially never required (FSRH; WHO MEC).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding and should not be used at all here
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 1 from 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 before). DMPA is correct from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception in breastfeeding women: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, LNG-IUS) are all safe at 6 weeks. Cu-IUD or LNG-IUS can be inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (or immediately postpartum within 48 h of birth). CHC is avoided <6 weeks (UKMEC 4) and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months while breastfeeding. LAM is ~98% effective only when ALL three criteria are met: <6 months, amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding.
key_points: POP can start day 21 postpartum without need to wait
IUD/IUS: insert immediately postpartum (within 48 h) or from 4-6 weeks
CHC: avoid <6 weeks if breastfeeding; from 6 weeks UKMEC 2; from 6 months UKMEC 1
DMPA: UKMEC 2 before 6 weeks if breastfeeding, then UKMEC 1
LAM strict criteria: amenorrhoea + exclusive BF + <6 months postpartum
explanation.pearls: Day 21 postpartum is the earliest fertility could return in non-breastfeeding women - that's why POP and CHC have day 21 as the earliest start if other criteria allow.

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception in a breastfeeding woman

stem: A 38-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her general practice. She is not pregnant, has had two previous CSTs at ages 28 and 33 which were both negative for oncogenic HPV, and has had no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding and no pelvic pain. She is in a stable monogamous relationship of 11 years and uses the LNG-IUS 52 mg (Mirena, inserted 4 years ago for both contraception and HMB control) and is currently amenorrhoeic. She has had a total of 4 lifetime sexual partners and has never had a sexually transmitted infection identified. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14 through the school-based program. Her practitioner-collected sample returns: oncogenic HPV detected (non-16/18 type); reflex liquid-based cytology performed automatically by the laboratory shows no intraepithelial lesion or malignancy. She is immunocompetent and has no history of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cervical excisional treatment. She does not smoke.

data_table.Examination: Routine speculum normal at time of collection.
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: oncogenic non-16/18 detected. Reflex LBC: negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy.

lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Direct referral for colposcopy with cervical biopsy now
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy is for HPV 16/18 regardless of cytology, or any oncogenic HPV with pHSIL/HSIL, or persistent HPV at 24 months. Direct colposcopy is correct in those higher-risk pathways (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 12 months (intermediate-risk pathway)
options.rationale: Oncogenic non-16/18 HPV with reflex LBC negative or low-grade abnormality places the patient in the intermediate-risk group; repeat HPV at 12 months. If still HPV-positive then refer to colposcopy; if negative, return to 5-yearly screening (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly cervical screening immediately
options.rationale: 5-yearly screening resumes only after HPV becomes negative; any oncogenic HPV detection triggers 12-month repeat or colposcopy. Routine 5-yearly is correct only with negative HPV (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Repeat HPV test for partial genotype in 6 months
options.rationale: 6-month repeat is not NCSP-recommended; intermediate-risk repeat is at 12 months. 6-month repeat was the old pathway, now obsolete (NCSP 2022).

options.text: Refer for excisional treatment (LLETZ) without further investigation
options.rationale: Treatment is based on histological diagnosis from colposcopy/biopsy, not on HPV status with negative cytology. LLETZ is correct after histology confirms high-grade dysplasia (NCSP 2022).

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP: HPV 16/18 detected -> direct colposcopy regardless of LBC. Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with negative or low-grade reflex LBC -> repeat HPV in 12 months (intermediate risk). Oncogenic HPV non-16/18 with possible high-grade or worse on LBC -> colposcopy. Persistent oncogenic HPV at 24 months -> colposcopy.
key_points: HPV 16/18 (any LBC): direct colposcopy
Other oncogenic HPV + negative/low-grade LBC: repeat HPV at 12 months
Other oncogenic HPV + possible high-grade or worse LBC: colposcopy
If 12-month repeat HPV negative -> return to 5-yearly screening
Self-collected vaginal swab is now universally available since July 2022
explanation.pearls: Reflex LBC is performed automatically by the lab if oncogenic HPV is detected; the practitioner does not need to order it separately.

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening - oncogenic non-16/18 HPV management

stem: A 62-year-old G3P3 woman presents to her general practice with a 6-week history of intermittent painless vaginal bleeding. She has been menopausal since age 51 (last menstrual period at 51) and has had no episodes of bleeding in the intervening 11 years until now. The current bleeding is light to moderate, requires a pad most days, and has been ongoing intermittently for the last 6 weeks. There is no associated abdominal or pelvic pain, no urinary symptoms, no bowel change and no weight loss. She has never been on menopausal hormone therapy, has never used tamoxifen, and has no family history of endometrial, ovarian, breast or bowel cancer (specifically no features of Lynch syndrome on family history). Her BMI is 33, she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1 g twice daily (HbA1c 6.6%), and her hypertension is well controlled on perindopril 5 mg daily. She has had three previous spontaneous vaginal births, none complicated, and no postpartum haemorrhage. Her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. She is on no anticoagulants and no antiplatelets. A transvaginal ultrasound is being arranged today.

data_table.Examination: BMI 33. Speculum: small amount of dark blood at cervix, no visible cervical lesion. Bimanual: uterus mobile, normal size, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240. UEC and LFTs normal. Beta-hCG not indicated. Cervical screening negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Regarding TVUS endometrial thickness in this clinical setting, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: If TVUS endometrial thickness is <=2 mm then endometrial sampling is mandatory regardless of other factors
options.rationale: Endometrial thickness <=4 mm has very low cancer risk and TVUS alone may be sufficient initial triage; sampling not mandatory below this cut-off. Mandatory sampling at <=2 mm is over-treatment (RANZCOG).

options.text: A TVUS endometrial thickness of 3 mm essentially excludes endometrial cancer and no further investigation is needed even if recurrent bleeding
options.rationale: Although thin endometrium has high NPV, recurrent or persistent PMB requires sampling regardless of TVUS thickness. Reassurance without sampling is correct only for single PMB episode with thin endometrium and resolution (RCOG).

options.text: Tamoxifen use makes TVUS thickness measurement unreliable due to subepithelial cysts; saline sonohysterography or hysteroscopy is preferred
options.rationale: True but not directly relevant - she is not on tamoxifen. This option is a red herring (RANZCOG).

options.text: She should proceed directly to hysteroscopy without prior TVUS, given her constellation of risk factors
options.rationale: TVUS is appropriate first-line imaging; hysteroscopy is for thickened endometrium >=4 mm, recurrent PMB, or non-diagnostic Pipelle. Direct hysteroscopy is correct after TVUS shows thickened or focal endometrium (RCOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Endometrial thickness >=4 mm on TVUS warrants endometrial sampling (Pipelle), with hysteroscopy if focal lesions or non-diagnostic sampling
options.rationale: The widely used 4 mm cut-off has a sensitivity of ~95% for endometrial carcinoma in postmenopausal bleeding; endometrial sampling is indicated above this threshold, with hysteroscopy +/- directed biopsy if focal pathology is suspected or Pipelle is non-diagnostic (RCOG Green-top 67; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: TVUS endometrial thickness <=4 mm has a high negative predictive value (~99%) for endometrial cancer in PMB; sampling not mandatory if bleeding is a single episode. Thickness >=4 mm triggers Pipelle biopsy +/- hysteroscopy. Recurrent PMB warrants sampling regardless of TVUS. Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, nulliparity, late menopause, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome.
key_points: TVUS endometrial thickness >=4 mm -> Pipelle biopsy
Recurrent PMB -> sample regardless of TVUS thickness
Hysteroscopy if focal lesion, non-diagnostic Pipelle, or thickened endometrium
Risk factors: obesity, DM, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen, Lynch, unopposed oestrogen
Approximately 10% of PMB has endometrial cancer
explanation.pearls: Any PMB warrants investigation. A thin endometrium does not equal a benign outcome if bleeding recurs.

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness threshold

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour, admitted 6 hours ago at 5 cm, is re-examined and is now 6 cm at +0 station in direct OP position with mild caput and no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 4 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions: palpate 3 in 10 lasting 40 seconds, mild intensity. CTG normal. Hb 124. Her mother is reminding the registrar that her own labour had to be 'started with the drip' and asks them to do the same.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: 6 cm, +0, direct OP, mild caput, no moulding. Contractions 3 in 10, 40 s, mild.
data_table.Investigations: CTG normal. GBS negative. Hb 124 g/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to achieve 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
options.rationale: Membranes are already ruptured and there is inadequate contraction frequency/strength causing slow progress in the active first stage. Oxytocin augmentation at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes is the correct Australian protocol; faster titration risks hyperstimulation (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Perform ARM and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
options.rationale: Membranes already spontaneously ruptured; ARM not applicable. ARM is correct in intact membranes with poor progress (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress
options.rationale: Failure to progress requires demonstration after adequate uterine activity (after oxytocin); proceeding to caesarean before oxytocin is premature. Cat 2 CS is correct after oxytocin trial fails (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
options.rationale: Starting at 6 mU/min risks hyperstimulation and foetal compromise; standard starts at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes. High-dose start is correct only with maximum-dose review and continuous fetal monitoring in selected protocols (RANZCOG).

options.text: Site an epidural and re-examine her in 6 hours without commencing any augmentation now
options.rationale: Pain relief is reasonable but does not address slow progress with inadequate contractions; delay risks foetal compromise. Epidural without augmentation is correct when contractions are adequate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Primary dysfunctional labour in a nullipara at 6 cm with inadequate contractions and intact membranes is managed with oxytocin augmentation (after ARM if membranes intact). Start at 1-2 mU/min and double every 30 minutes to a usual maximum of 32 mU/min, titrated to 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes. Diagnose labour dystocia only after at least 4 hours of adequate contractions with no progress, then proceed to CS.
key_points: Active phase: >=6 cm with regular contractions (Zhang/ACOG)
Adequate progress nullipara: ~0.5-1 cm/h after 6 cm
Oxytocin start 1-2 mU/min, double every 30 min, max ~32 mU/min
Target: 4-5 contractions per 10 min, 45-60 s, moderate-strong
Diagnose dystocia after >=4 h adequate contractions without progress
explanation.pearls: Direct OP at 6 cm is a common cause of slow progress; many will rotate spontaneously with adequate contractions plus mobility/epidural.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Augmentation of labour
Royal Women's Hospital - Oxytocin in labour

subtopic_detail: Failure to progress in active first stage - oxytocin augmentation

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 40+5 with a previous unassisted vaginal birth (3.6 kg, no shoulder dystocia documented) is now in second stage. She has gestational diabetes diagnosed at 26 weeks and managed on metformin 1 g twice daily with a last HbA1c of 5.6% and recent fasting BGLs in target range. Her booking BMI was 31. The estimated foetal weight at the 38-week growth scan was 4.1 kg with a normal amniotic fluid index. After 35 minutes of active pushing the head delivers with a turtle sign immediately recognised on the perineum. The midwife immediately calls for help, places the mother in McRoberts position with her hips hyperflexed and applies suprapubic pressure from the side of the foetal back for two consecutive contractions. The anterior shoulder remains impacted behind the symphysis pubis. The obstetric registrar arrives within one minute and the multidisciplinary team is now organised at the bedside, including neonatal staff. The mother has an epidural in situ with adequate block, the bladder is empty after recent in/out catheter, no episiotomy has been performed yet, and there is no PV bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 96 bpm, BP 124/76, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Head delivered, turtle sign present. Anterior shoulder impacted. Epidural in situ. Bladder empty.
data_table.Investigations: EFW at 38 weeks 4.1 kg. GDM on metformin, HbA1c 5.6%.

lead_in: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed, what is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Perform Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and proceed to immediate emergency caesarean section in theatre
options.rationale: Zavanelli is last-resort manoeuvre after failed internal manoeuvres and posterior arm delivery; not next step after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure. Zavanelli is correct as final salvage (RANZCOG; ALSO).
source_refs: ALSO (Advanced Life Support in Obstetrics) Course Manual

options.text: Perform internal rotation (Rubin II or Woods screw) or delivery of the posterior arm, with episiotomy if access is inadequate
options.rationale: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure, internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw or reverse Woods) or delivery of the posterior arm are the recommended next steps. Episiotomy is performed only if it improves vaginal access for the operator's hand (RANZCOG; ALSO; PROMPT).
source_refs: ALSO (Advanced Life Support in Obstetrics) Course Manual
PROMPT (Practical Obstetric Multi-Professional Training) Course Manual

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure during the next contraction to dislodge the impacted anterior shoulder now
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia; worsens impaction and increases uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury. Fundal pressure is essentially never correct in shoulder dystocia (RANZCOG).

options.text: Deliberately fracture the foetal clavicle by direct outward pressure on the mid-clavicle bone immediately now
options.rationale: Deliberate cleidotomy is salvage manoeuvre reserved for failure of all other techniques. Cleidotomy is correct only when all other manoeuvres exhausted (RANZCOG; ALSO).

options.text: Cut a generous mediolateral episiotomy alone and ask the mother to push harder with the next contraction
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not relieve bony dystocia; only creates space for internal manoeuvres. Episiotomy is correct as adjunct to internal manoeuvres (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: RANZCOG/ALSO/PROMPT algorithm for shoulder dystocia: call for help, McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, then internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, reverse Woods) or delivery of the posterior arm. If these fail consider all-fours (Gaskin), then last-resort options (Zavanelli, symphysiotomy, deliberate cleidotomy). Episiotomy only if it aids access for internal manoeuvres.
key_points: HELPERR mnemonic: Help, Evaluate for Episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll (all-fours)
Fundal pressure is contraindicated
Document the time of head delivery, manoeuvres used, and head-to-body delivery interval
Brachial plexus injury occurs in 4-16% of shoulder dystocias; permanent in <1%
Risk factors: macrosomia, GDM, prior shoulder dystocia, prolonged second stage
explanation.pearls: If the head delivers with a turtle sign, do not wait to see if a contraction will dislodge the shoulder. Diagnose dystocia, call for help and start the algorithm.

sources.label: ALSO (Advanced Life Support in Obstetrics) Course Manual
PROMPT (Practical Obstetric Multi-Professional Training) Course Manual

subtopic_detail: Shoulder dystocia - third-line manoeuvre after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour at 10 cm dilatation has just had a spontaneous rupture of membranes with a large gush of clear liquor. The midwife performing a vaginal examination immediately feels a pulsating loop of cord in the vagina at +0 station. The foetal head is at -2 station and direct OA, with 3/5 of the head still palpable abdominally. The CTG was previously reassuring (baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations) but now shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm starting within the last minute. The mother is comfortable on her right side with an epidural in situ; her observations are BP 122/78, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99%. She is otherwise well, has no obstetric or medical comorbidities, and her 36-week growth scan was unremarkable. The emergency buzzer has been pulled and the obstetric, anaesthetic and neonatal teams are en route. She has had no previous abdominal surgery, no caesarean section, and is otherwise low risk for surgical complications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 10 cm, -2 station, direct OA, pulsating cord at +0 in vagina. Liquor clear. Bladder catheterised.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm, starting within the last minute.

lead_in: Which immediate set of actions is most appropriate?

options.text: Reduce the cord back into the uterus through the cervix and proceed to instrumental vaginal delivery in the room
options.rationale: Cord replacement is no longer recommended due to cord trauma and vasospasm; manual elevation of presenting part is preferred. Cord replacement is essentially obsolete (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

options.text: Continue oxytocin augmentation, reposition the mother to left lateral and reassess CTG again in 5 minutes
options.rationale: Oxytocin must be STOPPED immediately to reduce contractions compressing cord; 5-minute observation is unsafe with prolonged deceleration. Continued oxytocin is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously and proceed immediately to assisted vaginal birth using a ventouse now
options.rationale: Tocolysis with terbutaline may be considered for hyperstimulation or transfer delay, but ventouse at -2 station is contraindicated (station must be >=+2). Tocolysis + ventouse combination is never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, knee-chest or exaggerated Sims, fill bladder 500 mL if delay, category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: At -2 station vaginal delivery is not imminent. Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline if transfer delay) plus knee-chest/Sims position plus category 1 CS within 30 minutes is the standard pathway (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 50).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

options.text: Wait for the next contraction at full dilatation and perform a fundal pressure delivery to expedite vaginal birth
options.rationale: Fundal pressure contraindicated; head is at -2 station with vaginal delivery not feasible. Category 1 CS required. Pushing-fundal pressure is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with a non-imminent vaginal delivery requires manual elevation of the presenting part (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline for transfer delay), knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position, stop oxytocin, consider tocolysis if hyperstimulation, and category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval.
key_points: Diagnosis: pulsating cord palpated below the presenting part
Manual elevation preferred over cord replacement
Bladder filling 500-700 mL saline if transfer delay
Stop oxytocin; consider terbutaline 250 microg SC if hyperstimulation
Category 1 CS, decision-to-delivery target 30 min (RANZCOG/NICE)
Instrumental vaginal birth only if fully dilated AND station >=+2 AND operator confident
explanation.pearls: Keep the examiner's hand in the vagina elevating the presenting part during transfer; do not handle the cord more than necessary because handling causes vasospasm.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

subtopic_detail: Cord prolapse - immediate management at full dilatation

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 with an epidural is in active second stage. She has been pushing for 110 minutes without descent of the presenting part beyond +1 station. The foetal head is at +1, direct OA, with no caput or moulding palpable, and 0/5 of the head is palpable abdominally on Crichton assessment. The maternal pelvis was clinically adequate on assessment by both registrar and consultant. The CTG shows a baseline of 145 with variability 5-10 bpm, no accelerations and variable decelerations with most contractions but no late or prolonged decelerations and no sinusoidal pattern. Membranes are ruptured with clear liquor of normal volume. Contractions are 4 in 10 with oxytocin running at 16 mU/min. The mother is exhausted, asking for help to deliver, and consented antenatally to instrumental birth in the room with the obstetric registrar present. The bladder is empty after recent catheter in/out. Adequate epidural top-up has been given and the block is dense to ice. The neonatal team has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: +1 station, direct OA, 0/5 head palpable abdominally, no caput, no moulding. Bladder empty. Epidural adequate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG suspicious (variable decelerations, reduced variability), not pathological.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate instrument and approach for this delivery?

options.text: Kielland's rotational forceps for a deep transverse arrest under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Position is direct OA, not transverse arrest; Kielland's restricted to experienced operators for rotational deliveries. Kielland's is correct in deep transverse arrest by experienced operators (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

options.text: Low-cavity non-rotational outlet forceps (Neville-Barnes or Simpson) under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Direct OA at +1 station with maternal exhaustion and adequate epidural is suitable for low-cavity non-rotational forceps. Forceps have a lower failure rate than ventouse in maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (but not pathological) CTG (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 26).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

options.text: Sequential ventouse then forceps if the initial vacuum extraction attempt fails to descend
options.rationale: Sequential instrument use significantly increases neonatal morbidity (ICH, subgaleal); generally avoided. Sequential is correct only when head on perineum and primary instrument fails (RCOG).

options.text: Kiwi OmniCup soft ventouse with traction synchronous with maternal contractions only and pulls
options.rationale: Ventouse has higher failure rate than forceps in maternal exhaustion and suspicious CTG, plus higher cephalohaematoma/subgaleal rates. Ventouse is correct in early descent and adequate maternal effort (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to a category 2 caesarean section now without attempted instrumental delivery
options.rationale: At +1 station with 0/5 palpable abdominally and direct OA, trial of instrumental is appropriate. Category 2 CS is correct after failed instrumental or with malposition not amenable to instruments (RANZCOG; NICE).

explanation.summary: For mid-cavity or low-cavity OA position with maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (not pathological) CTG, non-rotational forceps have a higher success rate than ventouse and a lower neonatal cephalohaematoma rate, but a higher rate of maternal perineal trauma. Sequential instrument use is associated with higher neonatal morbidity and should be avoided. Document foetal station, position, caput/moulding, bladder, epidural, and consent prior to commencing.
key_points: Pre-instrumental checklist: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, head <=1/5 palpable abdominally, defined station, defined position, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent
Forceps: higher success rate, more maternal trauma
Ventouse: higher failure rate, more cephalohaematoma/subgaleal haemorrhage
Sequential use significantly increases neonatal morbidity
Failed instrumental: stop after 3 contractions with no descent or after 20 minutes total
explanation.pearls: Choose the instrument most likely to succeed first. If you would normally choose ventouse but conditions are marginal (exhaustion, suspicious CTG, OP), forceps usually has the better outcome.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

subtopic_detail: Instrumental birth - forceps vs ventouse selection

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 having a trial of labour after caesarean (one previous LSCS for breech) is in active labour at 7 cm with epidural. The CTG develops a sudden prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm, not recovering after 4 minutes despite stopping oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL crystalloid and exclusion of hypotension. Fresh PV bleeding develops, scar tenderness, and the previously palpable head becomes ballotable abdominally (suspected uterine rupture). The registrar calls a category 1 caesarean section.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Scar tender, fresh PV bleeding, head ballotable abdominally.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration 60 bpm, not recovering at 4 minutes.

lead_in: Which combination most accurately describes the standards for category 1 caesarean and the immediate priorities?

options.text: Decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes; concurrent MTP activation, theatre transfer, anaesthetic preparation; do not delay for re-examination
options.rationale: The Australian/NICE standard for category 1 caesarean is delivery as soon as possible, with a 30-minute auditable threshold; bypassing further bedside investigation is appropriate when uterine rupture is suspected, and parallel resuscitation and MTP activation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 15 minutes; defer MTP activation until intraoperative blood loss is confirmed
options.rationale: 15 minutes is idealised target rarely achieved; audited standard is 30 minutes. MTP should be activated proactively given suspected rupture. 15-minute target is correct only in immediate intra-theatre rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 60 minutes; obtain confirmatory bedside ultrasound before transfer to theatre
options.rationale: 60 minutes is threshold for category 2 CS; rupture is category 1 emergency. 60 minutes is correct for category 2 (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 75 minutes if epidural is already sited and theatre is in-house
options.rationale: Epidural availability does not change category-1 timing; target is 30 minutes. 75 minutes is essentially never appropriate in suspected rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 45 minutes; abandon TOLAC pathway only after delivery is achieved
options.rationale: TOLAC is abandoned the moment rupture is suspected, not after delivery; target is 30 minutes. 45 minutes is appropriate only in category 2 status (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section (RANZCOG/NICE): Cat 1 - immediate threat (target 30 min decision-to-delivery); Cat 2 - maternal or foetal compromise, not immediately life-threatening (target 60-75 min); Cat 3 - early delivery needed but no compromise; Cat 4 - delivery timed to suit woman or staff.
key_points: Cat 1: 30 minutes (audit standard)
Cat 2: 60-75 minutes (audit standard)
30 minutes is an audit standard, not a strict outcome threshold
Common Cat 1 indications: cord prolapse, abruption, uterine rupture, scalp pH <7.20, prolonged bradycardia
explanation.pearls: Document time of decision, time of arrival in theatre, time of knife-to-skin, and time of delivery for every Cat 1 CS for departmental audit.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 presents at 32+1 weeks with a 30-minute history of moderate fresh vaginal bleeding. She had spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes before the bleeding started, with a sudden gush of clear liquor followed almost immediately by bright red blood. She has not had abdominal pain at any point and reports no abdominal trauma in the preceding week. Her two previous births were both lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 18 months ago and the first 4 years ago), both for failure to progress in the first stage; she has not been counselled about VBAC this pregnancy and the antenatal plan was for an elective repeat CS at 39 weeks. Her 20-week morphology scan showed a low-lying anterior placenta (3 cm from the os) and a velamentous cord insertion with a vessel coursing close to the internal os, but a repeat 32-week transvaginal scan to characterise the cord vessel and re-measure placental edge was scheduled for next week. On arrival she is mildly tachycardic at 102 with a normal BP of 124/76. The CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with a baseline of 145 bpm and reduced variability. The uterus is soft and non-tender between contractions and there is no maternal pain on speculum examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, non-tender. Speculum: moderate fresh blood at os, cervix appears closed. SROM confirmed.
data_table.Investigations: 20-week USS: low-lying anterior placenta, velamentous cord, vessel near internal os. Two previous LSCS. CTG: sinusoidal, baseline 145 bpm, reduced variability.

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of the antepartum haemorrhage in this woman?

options.text: Placenta praevia with marginal sinus bleeding from the lower segment
options.rationale: Praevia is suggested by low-lying placenta but classically causes painless bleeding without ROM as trigger; sinusoidal CTG immediately after SROM points to vasa praevia. Praevia is correct with painless antepartum bleeding without ROM trigger (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

options.text: Placental abruption secondary to undisclosed cocaine use or trauma
options.rationale: Abruption causes painful bleeding with tender, irritable, or tonic uterus; uterus here is soft and non-tender, no trauma/stimulant history. Abruption is correct with painful uterine activity (RANZCOG).

options.text: Vasa praevia with ruptured foetal vessel at the internal os
options.rationale: Velamentous cord insertion with a vessel near the internal os, bleeding triggered immediately by SROM, and a sinusoidal CTG (indicating foetal anaemia) is the classic triad of vasa praevia rupture with foetal exsanguination. Requires immediate category 1 CS (RCOG GTG 27a; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

options.text: Uterine rupture along the previous lower-segment caesarean scar from prior delivery
options.rationale: Uterine rupture causes severe maternal pain, scar tenderness, maternal haemodynamic compromise, loss of foetal station; soft non-tender uterus and not in labour. Rupture is correct in TOLAC patient with these features (RANZCOG).

options.text: Heavy bloody show from established early preterm labour onset now
options.rationale: Show is blood-stained mucous in small volume; moderate fresh bleeding after SROM with sinusoidal trace is not consistent. Show is correct for small mucus-blood loss (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia rupture is rare but devastating: bleeding is foetal blood, not maternal, so the foetus exsanguinates rapidly while the mother appears haemodynamically intact. Classic triad: ROM, vaginal bleeding, foetal compromise (often sinusoidal CTG). Requires immediate category 1 CS. Antenatal detection at 20-week morphology scan is the key prevention strategy.
key_points: Vasa praevia: ROM-triggered foetal bleeding, sinusoidal CTG, mother haemodynamically stable
Praevia: painless maternal bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, recurrent
Abruption: painful bleeding (or concealed), tender/tonic uterus, may have DIC
Rupture: severe pain, scar tenderness, haemodynamic collapse, loss of station
Vasa praevia detected antenatally requires elective CS at 34-36 weeks after steroids
explanation.pearls: If the mother looks well but the baby looks terrible (sinusoidal CTG) after ROM, think vasa praevia and get to theatre immediately.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 40+2 of a 4.0 kg neonate after a 9-hour first stage and a 45-minute second stage with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress in the first stage. Active management of the third stage with IM oxytocin 10 units has been given and the placenta is delivered intact 8 minutes after the baby. There is now ongoing brisk PV bleeding estimated at 1200 mL over 10 minutes with a soft, boggy uterus that is poorly contracted at the umbilicus. The bladder has been catheterised and is empty. Bimanual compression has been commenced. IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/h is already running through a 16G cannula. Her observations are BP 96/58, HR 118, T 36.7. She has a history of asthma (mild, intermittent, no recent exacerbations) but no hypertensive disorder of pregnancy and no other medical comorbidities. Blood has been sent for FBC, coagulation, fibrinogen and group and crossmatch.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft and boggy, fundus at umbilicus. Bladder empty. No genital tract trauma visible. Placenta delivered intact.
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1200 mL. FBC/coags/fibrinogen/X-match sent. Asthma history (mild, intermittent).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in addition to ongoing IV oxytocin infusion?

options.text: PR misoprostol 800 microg with antiemetic precaution and continue the IV oxytocin infusion at current rate
options.rationale: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian guidance because of slower onset and higher pyrexia/shivering; ergometrine and carboprost are preferred when available and not contraindicated. Misoprostol is correct when ergometrine/carboprost contraindicated (RANZCOG PPH).

options.text: IM carboprost 250 microg, repeated every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses in total
options.rationale: Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma; ergometrine is preferred second-line here. Carboprost is correct in non-asthmatic PPH (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 microg (no hypertension contraindication) plus IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Atonic PPH with no contraindication to ergometrine (no hypertension/pre-eclampsia/cardiac disease): ergometrine 250 microg IM (or IV slowly) is second-line, plus IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial). Asthma is a relative contraindication to carboprost, not ergometrine (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Increase IV oxytocin infusion to 60 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 500 mL/h and observe response for 15 minutes
options.rationale: Maximum oxytocin is 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours; doses above this risk water intoxication. Oxytocin escalation beyond max is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert an intrauterine balloon tamponade with 500 mL warmed saline immediately and withhold further uterotonics
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is appropriate after failure of medical management; uterotonics and TXA should be optimised first. Balloon is correct after first/second-line uterotonic failure (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Atonic PPH stepwise: massage and compression, oxytocin (10 IU IM/IV bolus then 40 IU/1 L IV infusion), ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (if no contraindication), carboprost 250 microg IM q15min up to 8 doses (avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 microg PR if other agents unavailable. Give IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth. Escalate to balloon tamponade, B-Lynch, internal iliac ligation, hysterectomy.
key_points: Ergometrine contraindicated: hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease, Raynaud's
Carboprost contraindicated: asthma (relative)
Misoprostol side effects: pyrexia, shivering
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial 2017)
Max IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours
explanation.pearls: If hypertensive: avoid ergometrine, use carboprost (if no asthma). If asthma: avoid carboprost, use ergometrine (if no hypertension). If both: misoprostol.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum haemorrhage - second-line uterotonic after oxytocin

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of Twin 1 (cephalic, 2.6 kg) with intact membranes of the second sac. She has an epidural in situ. After delivery of Twin 1 her uterus relaxes and bedside ultrasound now shows Twin 2 in a transverse lie with the back inferior and the membranes still intact. The estimated foetal weight of Twin 2 at the 36-week scan was 2.8 kg. The CTG of Twin 2 is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations. The cervix remains fully dilated. The obstetric consultant is in theatre attached to the room, and the anaesthetist confirms a working epidural with a dense block. There is no PV bleeding and the uterus is currently soft. The team has been briefed and the mother consented antenatally to internal manoeuvres and breech extraction for a non-cephalic second twin.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 88 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated. Twin 2 transverse, back inferior, membranes intact. Uterus relaxed.
data_table.Investigations: USS at bedside: transverse lie, back inferior. CTG reassuring.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of Twin 2 at this point?

options.text: External cephalic version under tocolysis followed by ARM when cephalic and longitudinal
options.rationale: ECV for second twin has lower success and higher failure than internal podalic version with breech extraction; with back inferior and relaxed uterus, internal podalic version is preferred. ECV is correct with back-up presentation and superior position (RANZCOG).

options.text: ARM immediately to allow the head to descend by gravity
options.rationale: ARM with transverse lie is CONTRAINDICATED due to high cord prolapse risk; version must occur first. ARM-first in transverse lie is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Immediate emergency caesarean section for delivery of the second twin
options.rationale: Caesarean for second twin after vaginal birth of first is associated with higher maternal morbidity than internal podalic version with breech extraction in experienced hands. CS for twin 2 is correct only after failed manoeuvres (RANZCOG).

options.text: Wait 60 minutes for spontaneous version before any intervention
options.rationale: Delay risks cord prolapse on subsequent ROM, abruption from uterine retraction, and closing cervix; prompt management within 30 minutes is recommended. Waiting 60 minutes is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Internal podalic version with breech extraction performed under continued epidural anaesthesia
options.rationale: Transverse lie of the second twin with intact membranes, a relaxed uterus and a working epidural is the classic indication for internal podalic version with breech extraction by a skilled operator. This avoids caesarean for the second twin and outcomes are equivalent or better when performed by experienced operators (RANZCOG; ACOG).
source_refs: Twin Birth Study (Barrett et al. NEJM 2013)

explanation.summary: For a non-cephalic second twin after delivery of Twin 1, internal podalic version with breech extraction is the preferred approach by a skilled operator with a working epidural and a relaxed uterus. ECV is an alternative but has higher failure rates. Caesarean for the second twin is reserved for cord prolapse, foetal compromise unresponsive to manoeuvres, or operator inexperience.
key_points: Twin Birth Study (NEJM 2013): planned vaginal birth non-inferior to planned CS at >=32 weeks if Twin 1 cephalic
IPV preferred with intact membranes, relaxed uterus, working epidural
ECV alternative; higher failure rate
Always have theatre ready and the consultant available
Deliver Twin 2 within 30 minutes of Twin 1 to reduce abruption and cord prolapse risk
explanation.pearls: Do not break the membranes of Twin 2 until you have a cephalic or breech longitudinal lie. ROM with a transverse lie is a category 1 emergency.

sources.label: Twin Birth Study (Barrett et al. NEJM 2013)

subtopic_detail: Twin delivery - second twin with non-cephalic lie

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 was admitted in spontaneous labour 22 hours ago with intact membranes; she had a SROM 20 hours ago and is now at 7 cm with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress. She has just developed a temperature of 38.4 degC sustained on two readings 30 minutes apart, with a maternal tachycardia of 122 bpm and a foetal tachycardia on CTG of 175 bpm with reduced variability. She reports a mild uterine ache between contractions and the liquor on examination is now offensive and meconium-stained (Grade 2). She has no productive cough, no dysuria, no headache and no diarrhoea. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. She has no penicillin allergy. Her observations are BP 102/64, HR 122, RR 22, T 38.4. The decision is made to commence empirical intrapartum antibiotics for suspected chorioamnionitis after IV access, two sets of peripheral blood cultures, a high vaginal swab and a midstream urine sample are obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, RR 22 /min, T 38.4 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Uterus mildly tender between contractions. Offensive Grade 2 meconium-stained liquor. 7 cm dilatation.
data_table.Investigations: GBS negative at 36 weeks. Cultures + HVS + MSU sent. No penicillin allergy.

lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen is most appropriate per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly alone as monotherapy until delivery
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone is GBS intrapartum prophylaxis regimen and does not cover gram-negative or anaerobic organisms implicated in chorioamnionitis. BPG alone is correct in GBS prophylaxis only (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy until delivery
options.rationale: Pip-tazo is reserved for treatment failure or severe sepsis with broader gram-negative concerns; not first-line empirical. Pip-tazo is correct in severe sepsis or treatment failure (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone + metronidazole is reasonable in penicillin allergy but not first-line in non-allergic. Ceftriaxone + metro is correct in severe penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines first-line empirical regimen for chorioamnionitis is IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily; metronidazole is added at caesarean section for anaerobic cover (Therapeutic Guidelines; KEMH).

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy and adds little against typical chorioamnionitis pathogen mix. Vancomycin is correct in severe penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Chorioamnionitis empirical therapy per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily. Add IV metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly if caesarean section is required. Penicillin allergy (non-immediate): IV cefazolin plus gentamicin. Immediate hypersensitivity: IV clindamycin plus gentamicin. Take blood cultures, HVS and MSU before antibiotics if possible without delaying treatment.
key_points: Diagnostic criteria (Gibbs): maternal fever PLUS >=2 of: maternal tachycardia, foetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, offensive liquor, maternal leucocytosis
First-line: IV ampicillin + gentamicin; add metronidazole if CS
Penicillin allergy (non-severe): cefazolin + gentamicin
Penicillin allergy (immediate): clindamycin + gentamicin
Expedite delivery once antibiotics commenced; do not delay for prolonged labour
Notify paediatrics; neonate will need early-onset sepsis workup
explanation.pearls: Take cultures before antibiotics if you can, but do not delay antibiotics by more than the time it takes to draw blood and put up the giving set.

stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 at 41+5 is undergoing induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She has received two doses of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel 6 hours apart, the second 8 hours ago. Cervix today: 1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, head at -3 (Bishop score 3). No regular contractions. CTG reassuring, membranes intact. EFW 3.4 kg, no contraindications to mechanical methods, no previous caesarean, no antepartum haemorrhage, no foetal compromise. Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses of dinoprostone gel in 24 hours and prefers mechanical ripening when prostaglandins fail. She has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Bishop score 3 (1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, -3 station). Membranes intact.
data_table.Investigations: CTG reassuring. EFW 3.4 kg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for up to 24 hours for mechanical cervical ripening
options.rationale: After failure of prostaglandins at maximum local dose with an unfavourable cervix, mechanical methods (Foley catheter or balloon) are the recommended next step. They are safe, effective and avoid hyperstimulation; ARM with oxytocin is inappropriate at Bishop 3 (RANZCOG IOL; NICE).

options.text: Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel today
options.rationale: Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses dinoprostone in 24 hours; exceeding risks hyperstimulation. Third dose is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform artificial rupture of membranes and commence IV oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: ARM at Bishop 3 with high station is technically difficult and carries cord prolapse risk; cervix must first be ripened. ARM is correct when Bishop favourable (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a membrane sweep at the bedside and reassess in 24 hours
options.rationale: Membrane sweep is for low-Bishop cervices >=1 cm dilated, but insufficient as single intervention after failed prostaglandins. Sweep is correct in latent labour with favourable cervix (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to category 4 elective caesarean section for failed induction of labour
options.rationale: Caesarean is for genuine failed induction after both pharmacological and mechanical methods; she has not failed mechanical. CS is correct after both methods exhausted (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: When the cervix remains unfavourable (Bishop <=6) after the maximum dose of prostaglandins, the next step is mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley catheter (or double balloon Cook catheter). This is comparable in efficacy to repeat prostaglandins with less tachysystole and is preferred for women with a previous CS. Caesarean for failed induction is reserved for women who fail mechanical ripening or who are not in active labour after ARM and oxytocin.
key_points: Bishop score: dilatation, length/effacement, station, position, consistency (max 13)
Bishop <=6 = unfavourable cervix; mechanical or pharmacological ripening required
Dinoprostone max 2 doses (Australian protocols)
Foley catheter 16 Fr with 30-60 mL balloon, leave 12-24 h or until expelled
Failed induction diagnosed only after ARM + adequate oxytocin trial without progress
explanation.pearls: Mechanical methods are particularly useful for women with a previous CS because they avoid the increased rupture risk of prostaglandins.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour
Cochrane review: Mechanical methods for induction 2012

subtopic_detail: Failed induction - unfavourable cervix after prostaglandins

stem: A 38-year-old G5P4 has had a postpartum haemorrhage after spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg neonate following oxytocin augmentation. Despite 40 IU oxytocin infusion, IM ergometrine, three doses IM carboprost, IV tranexamic acid 1 g, and a Bakri balloon at 500 mL, she continues to bleed. Total EBL 3500 mL. BP 88/52, HR 134, peripheries cool. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 68. MTP active, packed cells/FFP/platelets running. She has completed her family. Consultant obstetrician is at the bedside, gynae-oncology is in-house, interventional radiology is on site, anaesthetic team is in theatre.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 134 bpm, T 36.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: Atonic uterus despite Bakri 500 mL. Cool peripheries. In theatre, laparotomy imminent.
data_table.Investigations: Total EBL 3500 mL. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L. Platelets 68 (x10^9/L). Massive transfusion protocol active.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed to laparotomy with uterine-sparing measures first (B-Lynch compression suture +/- stepwise uterine artery ligation), escalating to peripartum hysterectomy if bleeding persists
options.rationale: RANZCOG PPH algorithm: after failure of medical therapy and balloon tamponade, the recommended surgical sequence is conservative interventions (B-Lynch compression, uterine artery ligation) before hysterectomy. She has completed her family so hysterectomy is acceptable if conservative steps fail, but should not be the first surgical step (RANZCOG PPH).

options.text: Proceed directly to peripartum hysterectomy without trialling uterine-sparing surgery
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is last resort even with completed family; conservative surgical measures recommended first if stabilisable. Direct hysterectomy is correct in life-threatening haemorrhage when uterine-sparing measures cannot be performed safely (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a second balloon alongside the Bakri and arrange transfer to interventional radiology for embolisation
options.rationale: Haemodynamically unstable; transfer to IR outside theatre is unsafe. IR is correct in stable patients (RANZCOG).

options.text: Re-inflate Bakri to 750 mL and observe for 30 minutes before further intervention
options.rationale: Bakri has maximum fill ~500 mL; overinflation does not increase efficacy. Observation in shock is unsafe. Re-inflation is essentially never correct in failed Bakri (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation as the first surgical step
options.rationale: Internal iliac artery ligation is technically demanding, takes time, and reserved for after uterine artery ligation or by IR/vascular if available. Internal iliac is correct after B-Lynch and uterine artery ligation fail (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Surgical escalation for intractable atonic PPH after failed medical and balloon therapy: (1) compression suture such as B-Lynch, (2) stepwise uterine devascularisation (uterine artery ligation, then ovarian artery ligation), (3) internal iliac artery ligation or interventional radiology embolisation, (4) peripartum hysterectomy as last resort. Hysterectomy is performed earlier if the patient is unstable, the uterus is severely damaged, or in placenta accreta spectrum.
key_points: Uterine-sparing options first unless contraindication
B-Lynch suture: vertical compression, leaves uterine cavity accessible
Stepwise uterine devascularisation: uterine arteries then ovarian arteries
Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation: technically demanding
Peripartum hysterectomy: last resort, but do not delay if unstable
Always involve a senior obstetrician early; consider IR if available and patient stable enough
explanation.pearls: The clock matters: do not let pride in uterine-sparing surgery delay hysterectomy in an exsanguinating patient. The right operation is the one that stops the bleeding before the patient dies.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Postpartum haemorrhage clinical guideline

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+5 in spontaneous labour has been at full dilatation with a working epidural for 2 hours. She has been pushing actively for 90 minutes. The foetal head is at 0 station in occiput transverse with moderate caput and 1+ moulding. CTG: baseline 145, variability 5-10, no accelerations, occasional early decelerations. Contractions 3 in 10 lasting 45 seconds, palpate moderate. Liquor clear. EFW 3500 g, gynaecoid pelvis. She is exhausted but cooperative.

data_table.Examination: Full dilatation, +1 station, direct OA, mild caput, no moulding.
data_table.CTG: Baseline 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations.
data_table.Contractions: 4 in 10, 60 seconds, palpate moderate to strong.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue active pushing for a further hour as delay is defined only after 3 hours in nulliparous women with epidural
options.rationale: Although 3 hours is the threshold, descent has stalled at 0 station with malposition, caput, and moulding; continued pushing risks foetal compromise. Continued pushing is correct only with ongoing descent (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Obstetric review for assessment of arrest of descent with consideration of rotational instrumental or caesarean
options.rationale: After 90 minutes of pushing with no descent below 0 station, malposition (OT), caput, moulding and inadequate contractions, an experienced obstetrician must assess for instrumental delivery (rotational) versus second-stage caesarean rather than continuing routine pushing (RANZCOG; NICE intrapartum).

options.text: Immediate Kielland's rotational forceps in the room without senior review
options.rationale: Rotational instrumental delivery requires experienced operator, often in theatre with trial-of-instrumental protocols; bypassing senior review is unsafe. Kielland's without senior review is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Commence oxytocin augmentation and continue pushing for another 2 hours
options.rationale: Augmentation may be considered with descent and inadequate contractions, but with malposition at 0 station, caput, and moulding, oxytocin without resolution risks rupture. Augmentation is correct when descent + inadequate contractions (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Stop pushing and observe passively for spontaneous descent for the next 4 hours
options.rationale: Passive observation is reasonable BEFORE active pushing begins but not after 90 minutes with stalled descent. Passive observation is correct only at 10 cm dilatation before pushing begins (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Active second stage delay thresholds: nullipara without epidural 2 h, with epidural 3 h; multipara without epidural 1 h, with epidural 2 h. The passive second stage (from full dilatation to active pushing) is allowed up to 2 h in nullipara, 1 h in multipara to allow descent.
key_points: Stage 1: latent (<4 cm) and active (4-10 cm); active progress benchmark ~0.5-1 cm/h in nullipara
Stage 2: passive (full dilatation to active pushing) + active (pushing)
Stage 3: delivery of baby to delivery of placenta; active management <=30 min
Epidural extends acceptable second stage duration by 1 h
Multiparas progress faster in all stages
explanation.pearls: If you cannot remember the four numbers, anchor on nullipara-no-epidural = 2 h and add 1 h for epidural, subtract 1 h for multipara.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Labour and Birth

subtopic_detail: Normal labour - duration of second stage in nulliparous woman with epidural

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 41+2 with an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy attends the day assessment unit for induction of labour for post-dates. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+4 weeks with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a small first-degree tear. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, no uterine surgery and no previous caesarean. The foetus is cephalic with a reassuring CTG today, an estimated foetal weight on the 60th centile at her 36-week scan and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers at that time. Liquor volume is normal. Membranes are intact and there has been no PV bleeding, no PV loss and no offensive odour.
