On vaginal examination the cervix is 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm in consistency, posterior in position, and the presenting part is at -3 station, giving a Bishop score of 3. She has not had any contractions and reports normal foetal movements. She is keen to maximise her chance of vaginal birth and to remain as ambulant as possible, and she asks about the safest first agent to use to begin induction at her cervical state.

data_table.Examination: Cervix: 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm consistency, posterior position. Station -3. Cephalic, membranes intact.
data_table.Bishop score: Dilatation 1 (1), length 1 cm (2), consistency firm (0), position posterior (0), station -3 (0) = 3.
data_table.Past obstetric: One previous SVD at 39+4, no perineal trauma, no PPH, no instrumental.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial induction method?

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes followed by an IV oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: ARM is not feasible with closed cervix at -3 station and unfavourable Bishop 3; ripening required first. ARM is correct with favourable Bishop and accessible cervix (RANZCOG IOL 2021).

options.text: Mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley balloon catheter as priming
options.rationale: A Bishop score of 3 indicates an unfavourable cervix and a mechanical Foley balloon is an appropriate first-line ripening agent, with comparable efficacy to prostaglandins but lower rates of hyperstimulation and CTG abnormalities. PGE2 is also acceptable; in this multipara with intact membranes Foley is widely preferred in Australian units to reduce hyperstimulation risk and allow outpatient ripening when local protocols permit (RANZCOG Induction of Labour 2021; Cochrane mechanical methods for IOL 2019; KEMH IOL guideline).

options.text: Oxytocin infusion commenced at 2 mU/min and titrated to contractions
options.rationale: Oxytocin alone with unripe closed cervix is ineffective and associated with prolonged induction, exhaustion, and increased CS. Oxytocin is correct after ripening (RANZCOG IOL 2021).

options.text: Misoprostol 200 microg oral every 2 hours until contractions established
options.rationale: Oral misoprostol IOL dosing is 25-50 microg every 2-4h; 200 microg is for PPH or TOP. 200 microg miso for IOL is essentially never correct (RANZCOG IOL).

options.text: Stretch and sweep at the bedside and discharge home for review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Sweep alone unlikely to initiate labour with Bishop 3 at 41+2; post-dates beyond 41+0 warrants formal induction. Sweep is correct in earlier post-dates with favourable cervix (RANZCOG IOL).

explanation.summary: Bishop score guides IOL method: <=6 = unfavourable, ripening needed (Foley balloon or PGE2); >=7 = favourable, proceed to ARM and oxytocin. Mechanical Foley and prostaglandins are equivalent for vaginal delivery rates with less hyperstimulation with Foley.
key_points: Bishop components: Dilatation, Effacement (length), Consistency, Position, Station
Foley balloon: 30-60 mL, left in situ up to 24 h, no foetal monitoring needed during ripening (outpatient option in many AU units)
PGE2 gel/pessary (Cervidil/Prostin): inpatient with CTG, risk of hyperstimulation
Misoprostol 25-50 microg orally or vaginally for IOL
Previous CS: Foley preferred; PGE2 and misoprostol contraindicated (rupture risk)
explanation.pearls: If the cervix is closed and posterior, you cannot ARM. Ripen first. Bishop <=6 means ripen, >=7 means ARM/oxytocin.

subtopic_detail: Bishop score and selection of induction agent in an unfavourable cervix

stem: A 39-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 with a BMI of 34 attends antenatal clinic. EDD has been confirmed by an 8-week scan. The foetus is cephalic, growth on the 50th centile, normal AFI and Dopplers, reassuring CTG today. Bishop score 4 (cervix 1 cm, 30%, firm, posterior, station -2). Booking BP 118/74, today 122/76 with no proteinuria. She has gestational diabetes diet-controlled with normal home glucose monitoring. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 40 weeks with no complications. She is anxious about a long pregnancy but feels well. She asks about timing of induction.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 78, T 36.6, urinalysis negative.
data_table.Examination: Bishop 6. SFH 38 cm at 41+0. Reassuring CTG.
data_table.Investigations: 8-week dating scan confirms gestation. 36-week growth scan: 50th centile, normal AFI, normal Dopplers.

lead_in: Which evidence-based threshold best supports a recommendation to induce labour for this woman?

options.text: Maternal age >=35 alone independently justifies induction at 39 weeks regardless of other factors
options.rationale: Maternal age >=39-40 with multiple risk factors is used, but age alone is not strong stand-alone trigger at 39+0. Age alone is correct only with advanced age + other factors (RANZCOG; NICE).

options.text: Diet-controlled GDM with appropriate growth supports induction at 39-40+6 weeks to reduce stillbirth risk
options.rationale: Australian/RANZCOG guidance supports IOL between 39 and 40+6 weeks for diet-controlled GDM to reduce stillbirth and shoulder dystocia, particularly with BMI elevation and age. This is the strongest evidence-based reason at this gestation (RANZCOG; ADIPS).

options.text: BMI 34 alone is an evidence-based indication for induction before 39+0 weeks
options.rationale: Class I obesity without other risk factors does not by itself mandate induction before 39 weeks; supportive but not stand-alone. BMI 34 alone is correct only in research-grade evidence base (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bishop score >=8 is required before induction can be offered at any gestation
options.rationale: Unfavourable cervix does not preclude IOL; ripening precedes oxytocin. Bishop >=8 requirement is essentially never correct (RANZCOG IOL).

options.text: Induction at 39+0 reduces caesarean section rates compared with expectant management to 41+0
options.rationale: ARRIVE suggested small CS reduction in low-risk nulliparas, but not primary evidence base in this high-risk multipara. ARRIVE evidence is correct in low-risk nulliparas (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Australian practice offers IOL between 41+0 and 42+0 for low-risk women to reduce stillbirth and perinatal mortality. Other common indications: term PROM (after 18-24 h), GDM at 38-40 weeks depending on control, pre-eclampsia >=37 weeks, FGR with adverse surveillance, maternal request after 39+0.
key_points: Stillbirth risk: ~0.5/1000 at 40 weeks, ~1/1000 at 41 weeks, ~3/1000 at 42 weeks
SWEPIS 2019 stopped early for perinatal mortality benefit of IOL at 41+0
GDM diet-controlled: deliver by 40+6; on metformin/insulin: 38+0 to 39+6
PE >=37+0: deliver
PROM at term: IOL within 24 h reduces chorioamnionitis (Hannah TermPROM)
explanation.pearls: The reason 41+0 IOL exists is stillbirth, not convenience and not CS rates.

sources.label: Cochrane Induction of labour at or beyond 37 weeks 2020

subtopic_detail: Indications for induction of labour - post-dates threshold in Australian practice

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has a continuous CTG sited for thick meconium-stained liquor at ARM 90 minutes ago. She has no medical comorbidities, the foetus is appropriately grown with normal Dopplers, and oxytocin has not been used. The foetus is cephalic, head at -1 station, direct OA. Contractions are 4 in 10 lasting 50 seconds, palpating moderate with normal resting tone. Maternal observations are stable. Over the last 30 minutes the CTG shows a baseline of 135 bpm, variability of 10 to 15 bpm, occasional accelerations and recurrent decelerations occurring with most contractions. The decelerations have a sharp drop from the baseline beginning at the start of the contraction, reach a nadir of 90 bpm within 20 seconds, last around 40 seconds, often have a small acceleration immediately before the drop and a small overshoot before returning to baseline, and recover by the end of the contraction. There are no decelerations between contractions.

data_table.CTG features: Baseline 135, variability 10-15, accelerations occasional, recurrent decelerations with sharp drop, nadir within 20 sec, shoulders present, return to baseline by end of contraction.

lead_in: Which type of deceleration is most consistent with these features and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Early decelerations from foetal head compression
options.rationale: Early decelerations are shallow, symmetric, mirror contraction, no shoulders; sharp drop within 20 seconds with shoulders is variable, not early. Early decelerations are correct with head-compression pattern (FIGO 2015).

options.text: Late decelerations from uteroplacental insufficiency
options.rationale: Late decelerations have gradual onset (>30s to nadir), nadir after peak of contraction, return to baseline after contraction ends. Sharp drop with recovery before end is not late. Late is correct in uteroplacental insufficiency pattern (FIGO 2015).

options.text: Typical variable decelerations from umbilical cord compression
options.rationale: Sharp drop with nadir within 20-30 seconds, duration <60 seconds, shoulders (small accelerations either side), and recovery within the contraction are typical variables. The mechanism is umbilical cord compression triggering a baroreceptor-mediated vagal response. Typical variables with preserved variability and shoulders are not pathological (FIGO Intrapartum Foetal Monitoring 2015; RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Atypical variable decelerations indicating foetal hypoxia
options.rationale: Atypical variables show loss of shoulders, slow return, biphasic, or duration >60s; this description retains shoulders and recovers within contraction. Atypical variables are correct with concerning features added (FIGO 2015).

options.text: Prolonged decelerations from acute foetal compromise
options.rationale: Prolonged decelerations last >3 minutes; these recover within 60 seconds. Prolonged is correct for >3 minute duration (FIGO 2015).

explanation.summary: FIGO 2015 deceleration types: early (mirror contraction, gradual, head compression, benign); late (gradual, nadir after peak, uteroplacental insufficiency, concerning); variable (sharp, V-shaped, cord compression, typical vs atypical); prolonged (>3 min, acute compromise).
key_points: Early: gradual onset >=30 sec to nadir, mirrors contraction, no clinical action
Late: gradual onset, nadir after contraction peak, recovery after contraction ends, uteroplacental insufficiency
Variable typical: sharp, <60 sec, shoulders preserved, variability preserved, cord compression
Variable atypical: loss of shoulders, slow recovery, biphasic, duration >60 sec, overshoots without shoulders
Prolonged: >3 min; abruption, rupture, cord prolapse, hypotension
explanation.pearls: Shape and timing: gradual + late = uteroplacental; sharp + variable timing = cord. Shoulders are reassuring, overshoots without shoulders are not.

sources.label: NICE NG229 Intrapartum care for women at risk
NSW Health Maternity - Foetal heart rate monitoring

subtopic_detail: CTG terminology - distinguishing variable from late decelerations

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 39+6 is in spontaneous labour, currently 7 cm dilated, with oxytocin augmentation at 8 mU/min for slow progress between 5 and 7 cm. Contractions are 5 in 10 lasting 60 seconds, palpate strong with no tachysystole. She has an epidural with good analgesia. Maternal BP 110/68, HR 88, T 36.8. Liquor clear, ARM 4 hours ago. The 36-week scan was unremarkable and GBS swab was negative. The CTG over the last 25 minutes shows a baseline of 150 bpm, variability of 5 to 8 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions. The decelerations begin gradually after the peak of each contraction, reach a nadir 60 seconds after the contraction peak and recover slowly after the contraction ends. There has been no prolonged deceleration and no vaginal bleeding. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available within 15 minutes.

data_table.CTG: Baseline 150, variability 5-8, no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations with >50% of contractions for 25 min.
data_table.Contractions: 5 in 10, 60 sec, strong on palpation. Oxytocin 8 mU/min.
data_table.Examination: 7 cm, +0 station, direct OA, clear liquor, no PV bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Continue oxytocin at current rate and repeat assessment in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability are pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens acidaemia. Continued oxytocin is essentially never correct in pathological trace (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Proceed immediately to a category 1 caesarean section in theatre now
options.rationale: Category 1 CS is for acute decompensation (prolonged bradycardia, abruption, cord prolapse); pathological without acute decompensation warrants intrauterine resuscitation + scalp lactate first. Cat 1 is correct in acute compromise (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Increase oxytocin to expedite delivery before further foetal compromise worsens further now
options.rationale: Increasing oxytocin in pathological trace is contraindicated and worsens uteroplacental hypoperfusion. Oxytocin increase is essentially never correct in pathological trace (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Stop oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL IV crystalloid, exclude reversible causes, consider foetal scalp lactate
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability indicate uteroplacental insufficiency and warrant intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV fluid bolus, exclude hypotension and tachysystole) followed by foetal scalp blood sampling for lactate to guide ongoing management (RANZCOG IFSM 2019; FIGO Intrapartum Foetal Monitoring 2015; NSW Health Maternity).
source_refs: NSW Health Maternity - Foetal heart rate monitoring

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for acute uterine tocolysis in this setting now
options.rationale: Acute tocolysis is for tachysystole-related compromise; contractions are 5 in 10 (not tachysystolic >5/10/30min). Tocolysis is correct in tachysystolic patterns (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

explanation.summary: Late decelerations reflect uteroplacental insufficiency. Management: stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV crystalloid bolus, correct maternal hypotension, exclude tachysystole (consider terbutaline if present), and perform foetal scalp blood sampling for lactate if the pattern persists.
key_points: Intrauterine resuscitation mnemonic: SPOILT (Stop oxytocin, Position, Oxygen no longer routinely recommended, IV fluid, Lower BP correct, Tocolysis if tachysystole)
Foetal scalp lactate: <4.2 normal, 4.2-4.7 borderline (repeat in 30 min), >=4.8 abnormal (deliver)
Reduced variability is the most predictive single feature of acidaemia
Tachysystole = >5 contractions in 10 minutes averaged over 30 min
Category 1 CS target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Late + flat = act. But act in the right order: resuscitate first, then sample, then deliver if not corrected.

sources.label: NSW Health Maternity - Foetal heart rate monitoring

subtopic_detail: Management of recurrent late decelerations with intrauterine resuscitation

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 40+0 is in the active second stage at full dilatation following a 25-minute push, with an epidural providing good analgesia. The previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD. The foetus is cephalic with normal growth, and the GBS swab was negative. The CTG shortly before crowning showed a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10, and mild variable decelerations recovering well. Liquor is clear. The foetal head crowns and is delivered slowly with perineal support. A cord is palpated wrapped tightly around the neck. An attempt to slip the cord over the head fails because it is too tight and the loop cannot be loosened either way. The shoulders have not yet delivered and the head is in restitution to LOT. No shoulder dystocia is recognised and the woman is pushing effectively with the next contraction expected within 30 to 60 seconds. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what to do.

data_table.Examination: Head delivered, restituting to LOT. Tight nuchal cord, cannot be slipped over the head. No shoulder dystocia recognised.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Double-clamp and divide the umbilical cord immediately before delivery of the foetal shoulders begins
options.rationale: Routine clamp-and-cut of tight nuchal cord before shoulder delivery is no longer recommended; converts contained circulation into acutely interrupted one if shoulder dystocia occurs. Clamp-and-cut is correct only as last resort with prolonged failure (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Apply firm traction to the foetal head to deliver the shoulders quickly before the cord tightens further
options.rationale: Forceful traction risks brachial plexus injury and does not address the cord; controlled somersault is correct. Firm traction is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Ask the woman to stop pushing now and prepare for an emergency caesarean section
options.rationale: Head is delivered; CS not feasible or indicated. Remaining task is to deliver body safely. Stop-pushing + CS is essentially never correct after head delivery (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Cut the cord between two clamps only when shoulder dystocia is clearly diagnosed clinically
options.rationale: Cutting cord during shoulder dystocia is hazardous - removes foetoplacental reservoir while foetus wedged. Cord cutting during dystocia is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Perform somersault manoeuvre, keep the head close to maternal thigh as the body delivers, then unwind the cord
options.rationale: When a nuchal cord is too tight to slip over the head, the somersault manoeuvre (flexing the foetal head towards the maternal thigh while the body delivers in a forward roll) keeps the cord intact and the foetoplacental circulation preserved, then the loop is unwound after delivery. This is preferred over clamp-and-cut, which risks foetal hypovolaemia and HIE if shoulder dystocia complicates delivery (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; Schorn and Blanco 2014).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Schorn MN, Blanco JD 2014 Somersault manoeuvre

explanation.summary: Nuchal cord occurs in ~25% of deliveries. If loose: slip over head. If tight and cannot be slipped: somersault manoeuvre (flex head to maternal thigh, deliver body in forward roll, then unwind). Avoid routine clamp-and-cut before shoulder delivery.
key_points: Loose nuchal cord: slip over head, deliver normally
Tight nuchal cord: somersault manoeuvre preserves cord integrity
Clamp-and-cut before shoulder delivery is no longer recommended
If shoulder dystocia complicates and the cord has been cut, foetal hypovolaemia and HIE risk rise sharply
Nuchal cord is rarely the cause of poor neonatal outcome on its own
explanation.pearls: Somersault first, snip last. If you cut the cord and then meet a shoulder dystocia, the baby is on a clock without circulation.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Schorn MN, Blanco JD 2014 Somersault manoeuvre
KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Labour and Birth

subtopic_detail: Management of a tight nuchal cord at delivery of the head

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+2 is at full dilatation, head at +3 station, direct OA, distending the perineum. CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 12 minutes. The consultant has decided to perform a non-rotational Neville-Barnes forceps delivery in the room. Bladder empty, epidural topped up, consent obtained, paediatric team notified. EFW 3500 g, pelvis gynaecoid, head fully engaged. The registrar suggests cutting a midline episiotomy at the 12 o'clock position to maximise space.

data_table.Examination: Full dilatation, +3, direct OA, head distending perineum, no abdominal palpable head.
data_table.Indication: Non-rotational forceps for pathological CTG at +3.
data_table.Maternal: Epidural topped up, bladder empty, consent obtained, BMI 26.

lead_in: Which response is most appropriate regarding episiotomy in this birth?

options.text: Decline midline episiotomy; cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 60 degrees from the midline at crowning
options.rationale: Mediolateral episiotomy at approximately 60 degrees from the midline at crowning is recommended for operative vaginal birth in Australia/UK practice to reduce obstetric anal sphincter injury (OASIS). Midline episiotomy is associated with markedly higher OASIS risk and should not be used (RANZCOG OVB; RCOG).

options.text: Cut the midline episiotomy as the registrar suggests to maximise outlet space
options.rationale: Midline episiotomy increases risk of third- and fourth-degree perineal tears, especially with forceps - classic anti-pattern in Australian practice. Midline is correct in some US practices but not Australian (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 30 degrees from the midline before crowning
options.rationale: Recommended angle is 60 degrees from midline AT crowning; 30 degrees and pre-crowning is incorrect. 30 degrees pre-crowning is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Avoid episiotomy entirely as routine episiotomy is not recommended
options.rationale: Routine episiotomy is not recommended for spontaneous birth, but selective mediolateral is recommended for forceps to reduce OASIS. Avoiding episiotomy in forceps is correct only in selected low-risk cases (RANZCOG OVB).

options.text: Cut a J-shaped episiotomy starting midline then curving laterally at crowning
options.rationale: J-shaped episiotomy is not standard practice and risks OASIS extension before the curve. J-shape is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).

explanation.summary: Episiotomy: not routine in spontaneous vaginal birth but indicated for operative vaginal delivery (particularly forceps), suspected imminent OASI, and some cases of shoulder dystocia. Mediolateral technique at 60 degrees from midline, cut at crowning, originating at the fourchette.
key_points: Mediolateral 60 degrees preferred over midline in Australia/UK
Midline episiotomy is standard in North America but has higher OASI rates
Cut at crowning, not before
Right-handed operators cut to the right; aim away from the anus
Episiotomy reduces OASI in forceps deliveries (NNT ~12 in nulliparas)
explanation.pearls: Sixty degrees to the side, at the fourchette, at crowning. Routine for forceps, considered for ventouse, never routine for spontaneous birth.

stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 39+5 has just had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3500 g infant. The baby is on the maternal chest, Apgar 9 at 1 minute, with delayed cord clamping at 90 seconds. Booked BP at 36 weeks was 138/86 and her booking BP today 1 hour ago was 145/92; she has been on labetalol for gestational hypertension since 32 weeks. She has no proteinuria and was not on magnesium sulfate. Maternal observations are now BP 150/95, HR 90, EBL so far 200 mL, uterus palpable at the umbilicus. Active management of the third stage is planned per unit protocol.

data_table.Maternal: BP 118/74, HR 88, EBL so far 200 mL, uterus palpable at umbilicus.
data_table.Neonate: Apgar 9 at 1 min, on maternal chest, delayed cord clamping at 90 sec.
data_table.Risk factors for PPH: None: parity 3, no APH, no prolonged labour, no induction, no chorioamnionitis.

lead_in: Which uterotonic is the most appropriate component of active management for this woman?

options.text: Oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder, controlled cord traction and uterine massage
options.rationale: Oxytocin 10 IU IM is the first-line uterotonic for active management of the third stage in Australian practice and is safe in hypertensive women. Ergometrine and Syntometrine are contraindicated in hypertension (RANZCOG; WHO).

options.text: Syntometrine (ergometrine 500 microg + oxytocin 5 IU) IM with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
options.rationale: Syntometrine contains ergometrine, CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension due to severe vasoconstriction and stroke risk. Syntometrine is correct in normotensive women (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Ergometrine 500 microg IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder and uterine massage
options.rationale: Ergometrine alone is contraindicated in hypertension - classic obstetric pharmacology anti-pattern. Ergometrine is correct in normotensive women (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Misoprostol 600 microg orally with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
options.rationale: Misoprostol is second-line where oxytocin unavailable (home birth, resource-limited); oxytocin remains first-line in Australian hospitals. Misoprostol is correct as backup (RANZCOG; WHO).

options.text: Carbetocin 100 microg IV bolus with immediate cord clamping and uterine massage
options.rationale: Carbetocin is used selectively for elective CS in Australia, not routine vaginal birth. Carbetocin is correct in elective CS (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Active management of third stage: 1) oxytocin 10 IU IM with anterior shoulder (or after birth); 2) controlled cord traction (Brandt-Andrews) with suprapubic counter-pressure; 3) uterine massage after placental delivery. Reduces PPH ~60%. Delayed cord clamping (1-3 min) is now incorporated.
key_points: Oxytocin 10 IU IM is first-line; carbetocin 100 microg IM/IV alternative
Ergometrine 500 microg IM avoided in hypertension/PE
Syntometrine (oxytocin 5 IU + ergometrine 500 microg) increases nausea/hypertension and is second-line
Delayed cord clamping 1-3 min unless asphyxia or maternal indication
Active management reduces PPH from ~13% to ~5% (Begley Cochrane 2019)
explanation.pearls: Three components: drug, traction, massage. Memorise the order; it is exam-favoured.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

subtopic_detail: Active management of the third stage of labour

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 has had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3400 g infant 45 minutes ago after a 7-hour labour with no oxytocin augmentation and no instrumental delivery. Active management of the third stage was performed with oxytocin 10 IU IM at delivery of the anterior shoulder, and controlled cord traction has been attempted multiple times by the experienced midwife and registrar without placental delivery. There is no significant ongoing bleeding, total EBL is 350 mL. The uterus is palpable and contracted at the umbilicus and there is no apparent uterine inversion. The cord is intact, appears at the introitus, and shows no signs of having broken on traction. The woman has an epidural in situ that is providing good analgesia and remains haemodynamically stable. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD with a normal third stage 3 years ago. She had one D&C for a missed miscarriage at 10 weeks two years ago and an LLETZ for CIN2 four years ago. No previous caesarean. The placenta is therefore retained at 45 minutes despite active management with no third-stage haemorrhage. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what should happen next, and the on-call consultant has been informed.

data_table.Maternal: BP 118/72, HR 86, EBL 350 mL, uterus contracted at umbilicus.
data_table.Third stage: 45 min, oxytocin 10 IU IM given, controlled cord traction unsuccessful.
data_table.Past history: One vaginal birth, one D&C for missed miscarriage, one LLETZ for CIN2.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue expectant management for a further 30 minutes given no significant bleeding evident
options.rationale: Retained placenta at 30 minutes with active management is the trigger for definitive intervention. Continued expectant is correct only with physiological management and no bleeding (RANZCOG; NICE).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Inject 20 IU oxytocin diluted in 20 mL saline into the umbilical vein
options.rationale: Umbilical vein oxytocin has been studied but Cochrane and RANZCOG do not recommend as standard. UV oxytocin is correct only when manual removal cannot be arranged (Cochrane 2011; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Administer ergometrine 500 microg intramuscularly now and reattempt controlled cord traction in theatre
options.rationale: Ergometrine causes tetanic uterine contraction trapping rather than expelling placenta - contraindicated for retained placenta. Ergometrine is correct in atonic PPH, not retained placenta (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Perform manual removal at the bedside without anaesthesia since the woman has a working epidural
options.rationale: Even with epidural, manual removal should occur in theatre due to haemorrhage, inversion, and accreta risks. Bedside manual is correct only in extreme emergency without theatre access (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Transfer to theatre for manual placental removal under regional or general anaesthesia with IV antibiotics and oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: Retained placenta with active management at >=30 minutes requires manual removal in theatre under regional (or general if regional not feasible) anaesthesia. Manage with IV access (two large-bore cannulae, FBC and crossmatch), single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. cefazolin and metronidazole or co-amoxiclav), oxytocin infusion during and after removal, and assessment for placenta accreta spectrum particularly with a history of uterine instrumentation (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; NICE NG235; Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline

explanation.summary: Retained placenta: >=30 min with active management or >=60 min with physiological management. Move to theatre for manual removal under regional or general anaesthesia. IV access, group and hold/crossmatch, IV antibiotics, oxytocin infusion. Suspect placenta accreta if removal is difficult or in women with previous CS, uterine surgery, or instrumentation. PPH and infection are the main complications.
key_points: Active management threshold: 30 min
Physiological management threshold: 60 min
Theatre, regional anaesthesia preferred, single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotics
Oxytocin infusion during and after manual removal
Accreta risk: previous CS, D&C, myomectomy, placenta praevia - have escalation pathway ready
explanation.pearls: 30 minutes with active management means theatre, not another bedside pull. Always check for accreta clues in the history.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotics for obstetric procedures

subtopic_detail: Retained placenta - timing and approach to manual removal

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 attends in spontaneous labour at 5 cm. Her vaginorectal GBS screening swab at 36+2 was positive. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+5 and the infant had no early-onset GBS sepsis and was discharged home well. She has a documented allergy to penicillin recorded in detail in her record as widespread urticarial rash starting 2 days into a course of amoxicillin for a dental infection three years ago, without angioedema, without wheeze, without hypotension and without anaphylaxis or hospitalisation. Susceptibility testing on the GBS isolate from this pregnancy's swab is documented in the pathology report as susceptible to clindamycin and erythromycin. She has no other comorbidities, is afebrile, has a reassuring CTG, normal observations and no other risk factors for intrapartum infection. Membranes ruptured 30 minutes ago with clear liquor and CTG is reassuring with baseline 138, variability 10-15, accelerations and no decelerations. The midwife asks the resident which antibiotic regimen should be commenced now for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis.

data_table.Microbiology: GBS positive (vaginorectal swab 36+2). Isolate susceptible to clindamycin and erythromycin.
data_table.Allergy: Penicillin - widespread urticarial rash, no angioedema/wheeze/hypotension (non-severe, non-anaphylactic).
data_table.Labour: 5 cm, SROM 30 min ago, clear liquor, reassuring CTG.
data_table.Other risk factors: None: afebrile, no prolonged ROM, no preterm labour.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen for GBS prophylaxis?

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g IV every 4 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
options.rationale: She has documented non-severe penicillin allergy; cefazolin is appropriate. BPG is correct in non-allergic women (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
options.rationale: Clindamycin is for severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy with documented isolate susceptibility; non-severe allergy uses cefazolin. Clindamycin is correct in severe allergy with susceptible isolate (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Vancomycin 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
options.rationale: Vancomycin is third-line, only with severe penicillin allergy and clindamycin resistance or unknown susceptibility. Vancomycin is correct only after first/second-line agents excluded (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDC).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g IV loading then 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Australia and RANZCOG recommend cefazolin for women with non-severe (non-anaphylactic) penicillin allergy because cross-reactivity with first-generation cephalosporins is low (<2%). Loading dose 2 g IV with a maintenance dose of 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery. Clindamycin is reserved for severe (anaphylactic) allergy with isolate susceptibility (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2019; CDC GBS prevention 2019).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours until delivery as intrapartum prophylaxis
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve reliable intrapartum levels for GBS prophylaxis; IV mandatory. Amoxicillin is also penicillin, contraindicated in allergy. Oral PCN-class is essentially never correct (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

explanation.summary: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g IV q4h until birth (first-line). Non-severe penicillin allergy: cefazolin 2 g IV load then 1 g IV q8h. Severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h if susceptible, vancomycin 20 mg/kg IV q8h if not. Adequate prophylaxis = >=4 h before birth.
key_points: Australian screening: vaginorectal swab at 35-37 weeks OR risk-based (preterm, prolonged ROM, intrapartum fever, previous infant with GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy)
Severe allergy = anaphylaxis, angioedema, respiratory distress, hypotension; non-severe = rash without systemic features
Cefazolin is safe in non-severe penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity <2%)
Clindamycin resistance varies 13-20%; always check susceptibility
Adequate prophylaxis is >=4 h before birth
explanation.pearls: Allergy severity drives choice: non-severe = cefazolin, severe + susceptible = clindamycin, severe + resistant/unknown = vancomycin.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis - regimen and penicillin allergy

stem: A 30-year-old G2P2 woman, three weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her well term infant, presents to her GP with a 24-hour history of a tender, hot, wedge-shaped erythematous area in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. She feels feverish and shivery, has been continuing frequent feeds and effective expression after feeds, and the area has continued to enlarge over the last 12 hours. There is no fluctuance, no skin breakdown and no axillary adenopathy. She is otherwise well. Her recorded drug allergy is to phenoxymethylpenicillin during childhood, when she developed an urticarial rash and lip swelling within 1 hour of a dose; she has never tolerated a penicillin or cephalosporin since. She has no other allergies, has not had antibiotics in the past 12 months, has no recent hospital contact and is not from a community with known high MRSA prevalence. She wishes to continue breastfeeding throughout treatment and her infant has no medical conditions and was born at term with no neonatal complications.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, HR 104 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Wedge-shaped erythema upper outer left breast, hot and tender, no fluctuance, no skin breakdown, no axillary lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear. Bedside ultrasound not yet performed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin would be first-line but is contraindicated with immediate hypersensitivity reaction to penicillin. Flucloxacillin is correct in non-allergic women (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cefalexin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Cephalosporins are avoided when penicillin reaction was immediate (Type 1) hypersensitivity with angioedema due to unpredictable cross-reactivity. Cefalexin is correct in non-immediate penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Clindamycin 450 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Clindamycin is the eTG-recommended alternative for lactational mastitis in patients with immediate penicillin hypersensitivity; it covers S. aureus including most CA-MRSA, is compatible with breastfeeding per LactMed and concentrates well in breast tissue (Therapeutic Guidelines; LactMed).

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate contains penicillin and is contraindicated in immediate hypersensitivity. Augmentin is correct in non-allergic women with suspected polymicrobial infection (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: TMP-SMX is reserved for confirmed MRSA and avoided in mothers of infants <1 month due to theoretical kernicterus risk. TMP-SMX is correct in MRSA mastitis in older infants (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis with systemic features warrants oral antibiotics for 5-7 days plus effective milk removal. First-line is flucloxacillin; with immediate penicillin hypersensitivity the eTG alternative is clindamycin, which is breastfeeding-compatible (LactMed).
key_points: Most common pathogen: Staphylococcus aureus
Continue breastfeeding/expressing - drainage is therapeutic
Immediate (Type 1) penicillin allergy excludes cephalosporins as well
Clindamycin covers most CA-MRSA and concentrates in breast tissue
TMP-SMX avoided in mothers feeding infants <1 month (kernicterus risk)
explanation.pearls: If the mother fails to improve at 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics, image with ultrasound for abscess before broadening cover.

subtopic_detail: Lactational mastitis - antibiotic choice in penicillin allergy

stem: A 27-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, exclusively breastfeeding her thriving term infant, attends with a 14-hour history of a discrete tender lump in the lower inner right breast. Overlying skin has faint pink mottling but no defined erythema. T 36.8, no rigors, no flu-like symptoms, infant feeding effectively. Recent change: baby has slept a longer 5-hour stretch the past two nights. No fluctuance, no axillary adenopathy. She has read online that 'aggressive deep massage' and 'pump-and-dump' are recommended.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 78 bpm, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16.
data_table.Examination: Tender 3 cm lump lower inner right breast, faint overlying pink mottling, no defined erythema, no fluctuance, no axillary adenopathy.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue feeding from both breasts on demand, apply cold packs between feeds, ibuprofen for pain, and review in 24 hours; avoid deep massage and pump-and-dump
options.rationale: The Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine 2022 protocol describes this as ductal narrowing/inflammatory mastitis spectrum, not bacterial mastitis. Management is on-demand feeding, cold packs, NSAIDs, and AVOIDANCE of deep massage and pumping (both can worsen inflammation/oedema). Antibiotics are reserved for bacterial mastitis (RANZCOG; ABM 2022).

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg qid for 7 days now
options.rationale: No fever, systemic symptoms, or defined erythema. Antibiotics are not indicated for inflammatory mastitis/ductal narrowing and may worsen dysbiosis. Flucloxacillin is correct in bacterial mastitis with fever/erythema (ABM 2022).

options.text: Aggressive deep breast massage every 2 hours with hot compresses for 48 hours
options.rationale: Aggressive massage worsens tissue oedema and risks abscess - the major anti-pattern that ABM 2022 specifically warns against. Aggressive massage is essentially never correct (ABM 2022).

options.text: Cease feeding from the affected breast and pump-and-dump for 48 hours
options.rationale: Cessation worsens engorgement and inflammatory cycle; on-demand feeding remains essential. Pump-and-dump is correct only with documented contraindications (ABM 2022).

options.text: Urgent breast ultrasound and surgical referral for incision and drainage
options.rationale: No fluctuance, abscess features, or failure of conservative management; surgical referral premature. Surgical referral is correct after failed conservative management or abscess (ABM 2022).

explanation.summary: The lactation inflammation spectrum runs from ductal narrowing/blocked duct (focal lump, no systemic features) through inflammatory mastitis (erythema, pain) to bacterial mastitis (systemic features) to abscess. Current evidence-based care de-emphasises deep massage and heat; the cornerstones are physiological feeding, lymphatic drainage and cold for symptom control.
key_points: Blocked duct: focal lump, no fever, no defined erythema
Inflammatory mastitis: erythema and pain, no systemic features
Bacterial mastitis: above plus fever/tachycardia, or non-resolution
Abscess: fluctuant collection on USS
Cold preferred over heat per ABM 2022 protocol
explanation.pearls: A long overnight feed gap is the classic precipitant of a blocked duct in an established 6-week breastfeeding dyad.

subtopic_detail: Blocked duct vs inflammatory mastitis vs bacterial mastitis discrimination

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman, 4 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, was diagnosed with right-breast bacterial mastitis 5 days ago and commenced flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily with continued feeding and expression. Her fevers settled within 48 hours but the erythematous area has now developed a discrete, fluctuant tender lump over the past 24 hours despite ongoing antibiotics and effective drainage. She is afebrile today, haemodynamically stable, and the skin overlying the lump is intact, non-necrotic and not under tension. Bedside ultrasound performed by the GP demonstrates a well-defined, unilocular hypoechoic collection measuring 38 mm x 24 mm at 1.5 cm depth, with no overlying skin necrosis. She has no recurrent abscess history, no immunosuppression, no diabetes and wishes to continue breastfeeding. She is comfortable being managed in primary care if the lesion is amenable to office-based drainage and the local Breast Centre offers ultrasound-guided procedures on a same-day basis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 84 bpm, BP 122/76 mmHg.
data_table.Examination: Right breast, 4 cm fluctuant tender mass, overlying skin intact and non-necrotic, no axillary adenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: unilocular 38 x 24 mm hypoechoic collection, 1.5 cm depth, no skin necrosis. Hb 124 g/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, CRP 48 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current oral flucloxacillin and review in 5 days without drainage
options.rationale: Drained collection requires drainage to resolve; antibiotics alone are inadequate and risk chronicity/fistula. Antibiotics-alone is correct only in early cellulitic phase without collection (ABM).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with culture; continue antibiotics, reaspirate if needed
options.rationale: For a lactational abscess under approximately 5 cm with intact non-necrotic skin, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration (often serial) with cultured aspirate and continued antibiotics is first-line; it preserves duct architecture, allows ongoing breastfeeding and avoids a surgical wound that complicates feeding (ABM; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Refer for theatre incision and drainage with packing under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Surgical I&D is reserved for very large abscesses, multiloculated, or thinned/necrotic skin; leaves wound that may interfere with feeding. I&D is correct for large/complex abscesses (ABM).

options.text: Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast and continue antibiotics only
options.rationale: Cessation worsens milk stasis and slows resolution; current guidance is to continue feeding or express. Cessation is essentially never correct in mastitis/abscess (ABM).

options.text: Switch to oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily and observe without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic broadening without drainage is inadequate; established collection needs source control. Clindamycin alone is correct only without abscess collection (ABM).

explanation.summary: Lactational breast abscesses require source control. Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with cultured aspirate is first-line for abscesses up to around 5 cm with intact skin and is repeated as needed; surgical drainage is reserved for larger, multiloculated, or skin-compromised collections.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding throughout treatment
USS-guided aspiration preserves duct anatomy
Send aspirate for culture to guide ongoing antibiotics
Surgical I&D for large, multiloculated, or skin-necrosed abscesses
Antibiotics alone are inadequate once a collection has formed
explanation.pearls: An apparently improving mastitis that develops a new fluctuant area at day 5 is an abscess until ultrasound proves otherwise.

stem: A 28-year-old primiparous woman attends with her 3-week-old term infant for a same-day appointment because she is convinced her milk supply has dropped. The baby is now feeding for 60-80 minutes per feed, 10-12 times in 24 hours, and is unsettled between feeds. She produces only 30 mL when she expresses after a feed and feels her breasts are softer than they were at 10 days. The infant had a birthweight of 3.4 kg, was discharged at 3.15 kg on day 4 (regained birthweight by day 12), and today weighs 3.55 kg on the practice scales. The infant is alert, has 6-8 heavy wet nappies and 3-4 mustard-yellow stools per day, has no jaundice, and feeds with audible swallowing during feeds. She has been advised by family to start formula top-ups. She is not on any medications, has no breast surgery history, has normal-appearing breasts that grew through pregnancy, and breastfed her older nieces' children briefly without difficulty.

data_table.Infant assessment: Birthweight 3.4 kg; day 4 discharge 3.15 kg; today 3.55 kg (mean gain ~30 g/day from nadir).
data_table.Feeding: 10-12 feeds/24h, audible swallowing, 6-8 heavy wet nappies, 3-4 mustard stools/day.
data_table.Maternal: Soft breasts, no surgery, infant alert.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence formula top-ups of 60 mL after each breastfeed to meet caloric requirements
options.rationale: Supplementation without indication suppresses prolactin-driven supply and undermines breastfeeding; growth and output are normal. Formula top-ups are correct with documented poor growth/output (ABA; RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure: growth, nappy output and feeding behaviour indicate adequate transfer; review in 1 week
options.rationale: Soft breasts after the early hyperlactation phase, low expressed volumes (a poor proxy for transfer), and cluster-feeding behaviour are normal at 3 weeks. Adequate weight gain (>20-30 g/day after nadir), regaining birthweight by 2 weeks, and 6+ heavy wet nappies confirm adequate intake; perceived low supply without objective markers is the most common cause of unnecessary supplementation (ABA; RCH; ABM).

options.text: Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily as a pharmacological galactagogue
options.rationale: Galactagogues are considered only after structured assessment confirms inadequate supply, identifies reversible cause, and addresses technique. Domperidone is correct after structured supply assessment failure (ABM).

options.text: Arrange measured pre-feed and post-feed test-weighs over 24 hours at home
options.rationale: Test-weighing is anxiety-provoking, prone to error, and rarely changes management with reassuring growth/output. Test-weighing is correct only in specific research/clinical questions about transfer (ABA; ABM).

options.text: Refer for maternal pituitary MRI to exclude Sheehan syndrome as the cause
options.rationale: No clinical suspicion for Sheehan (no severe PPH, no other pituitary dysfunction); imaging not indicated. Pituitary MRI is correct in suspected Sheehan or other pituitary disease (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Perceived low supply is far more common than true low supply. Assess transfer using objective markers (weight gain >20-30 g/day after nadir, regaining birthweight by 2 weeks, 6+ heavy wet nappies, mustard stools, audible swallowing) before introducing supplementation or galactagogues.
key_points: Expressed volume is a poor proxy for milk transfer
Cluster feeding and softer breasts are normal after 2-3 weeks
Confirm adequate output with weight + nappies + stools before intervening
Premature top-ups suppress supply (negative feedback)
Galactagogues are second-line after a structured assessment
explanation.pearls: Counsel that breasts feeling 'less full' often reflects normal calibration of supply to demand, not failing supply.

subtopic_detail: Low milk supply - first step in assessment

stem: A 30-year-old primiparous woman attends the GP at 3 weeks postpartum with her term infant. She has persistent bilateral cracked, bleeding nipples that have not improved despite three lactation consultant reviews focused on positioning and attachment; the latch has been optimised across multiple holds and she has tried nipple shields without lasting benefit. Feeds last 50-70 minutes and the infant frequently breaks suction with a clicking sound. Infant weight gain has fallen below the expected trajectory: birthweight 3.5 kg, discharge weight 3.25 kg, today's weight 3.4 kg at 3 weeks (mean gain ~10 g/day from nadir, below the 20-30 g/day threshold). On examination by an experienced lactation consultant, the infant has a thin anterior tongue frenulum that restricts tongue elevation to less than midline when crying, a heart-shaped tongue tip when the tongue is lifted, and a Hazelbaker tongue-function score that meets criteria for ankyloglossia with impaired function. The infant has no other oral, craniofacial or neurological abnormalities and is otherwise well.

data_table.Infant assessment: Birthweight 3.5 kg, nadir 3.25 kg, today 3.4 kg at day 21 (~10 g/day from nadir, below threshold).
data_table.Feeding: 50-70 min feeds, audible clicking, nipple trauma persists.
data_table.Examination: Restricted tongue elevation, heart-shaped tongue tip, Hazelbaker confirms functionally significant anterior tongue tie.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to an experienced clinician for assessment and frenotomy of the anterior tongue tie
options.rationale: When ankyloglossia is associated with functionally significant feeding problems persisting despite optimised positioning and attachment (nipple trauma + impaired transfer + objective tongue-function deficit), frenotomy by an experienced clinician is appropriate and improves both maternal pain and infant transfer in RCT-level evidence (RANZCOG; RCH; ABM Protocol 11).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Continue positioning and attachment optimisation for another 4 weeks before considering frenotomy
options.rationale: Positioning already optimised with three lactation consultant reviews and infant growth faltering; further delay risks supply failure and breastfeeding cessation. Continued positioning is correct only with adequate transfer and growth (RCH; RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence formula top-ups and discontinue breastfeeding given the persistent nipple trauma
options.rationale: Mother wishes to continue and reversible structural cause identified; cessation not first-line when treatable lesion present. Formula transition is correct only after failed intervention or maternal preference (RCH; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Refer for posterior tongue tie and upper-lip tie release out of hours regardless of findings
options.rationale: RANZCOG and RCH caution against indiscriminate posterior tongue tie or upper-lip tie release; evidence is weak and over-diagnosis documented. Posterior/lip-tie release is correct only with rigorous criteria and specialist assessment (RCH).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

options.text: Prescribe long-term nipple shields and review at the 6-week postnatal check
options.rationale: Shields already failed and do not address underlying restriction; reliance can worsen transfer. Nipple shields are correct as short-term adjunct, not long-term solution (RCH).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

explanation.summary: Frenotomy for ankyloglossia is indicated only when feeding is functionally impaired AND optimised positioning/attachment has not resolved the issue AND examination confirms restricted tongue function. RANZCOG and RCH caution against routine posterior tongue or lip tie release.
key_points: Frenotomy indicated only when function is impaired
Optimise positioning and attachment with IBCLC support first
RCT evidence supports anterior tongue-tie frenotomy when functionally significant
Posterior tongue tie and lip tie release evidence base is weak
Refer to an experienced clinician (paediatrician, ENT, paediatric dentist with training)
explanation.pearls: If the latch has been optimised across multiple reviews and the mother still has nipple trauma, look in the baby's mouth - not at the mother's technique.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Tongue tie

subtopic_detail: Tongue tie - indications for frenotomy referral

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed in the GP-IBCLC combined clinic at 5 weeks postpartum with confirmed insufficient milk supply contributing to faltering infant growth despite four weeks of structured input. Positioning and attachment have been optimised, frequency of feeds increased, post-feed expression added, and a structural cause has been excluded (no tongue tie, no breast surgery, no retained placenta, normal thyroid). Infant weight gain remains below 20 g/day with documented inadequate transfer on test-weighs over 24 hours. The mother wishes to use a galactagogue as adjunctive therapy to maintain breastfeeding while continuing the non-pharmacological measures. She has no personal cardiac history, but on review of her medications she takes citalopram 20 mg daily for anxiety. Her resting baseline ECG today shows sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with QTc 460 ms (Bazett) and normal electrolytes including K+ 4.4 mmol/L and Mg2+ 0.85 mmol/L. She has no family history of sudden cardiac death and no syncope. The IBCLC asks specifically whether domperidone is appropriate in her situation.

data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm 78 bpm, QTc 460 ms (Bazett), no AV block.
data_table.Electrolytes: K+ 4.4 mmol/L, Mg2+ 0.85 mmol/L, Ca2+ corrected 2.35 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding domperidone?

options.text: Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily for 7 days as a galactagogue
options.rationale: TGA and ABM safety guidance contraindicate domperidone with concurrent QT-prolonging medication and QTc >=450 ms; citalopram is QT-prolonging. Domperidone is correct only with normal QTc and no QT drugs (TGA).

options.text: Avoid domperidone; review citalopram with her psychiatrist and intensify non-pharmacological measures
options.rationale: Domperidone is contraindicated when QTc is prolonged or when a patient is on concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (including citalopram); the safe step is to avoid it, address the modifiable risk (review citalopram) and continue evidence-based milk-removal optimisation (TGA; ABM).

options.text: Increase domperidone dose to 20 mg four times daily to maximise the milk yield
options.rationale: TGA limits dosing to no more than 10 mg three times daily; cardiac risk dose-dependent. Plus active contraindications here. Higher dose is essentially never correct (TGA).

options.text: Substitute metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily as a long-term galactagogue
options.rationale: Metoclopramide has weaker galactagogue evidence and significant risks of extrapyramidal effects and depression with prolonged use. Metoclopramide is correct in short-term postpartum nausea, not long-term galactagogue (TGA).

options.text: Commence fenugreek 600 mg three times daily as a herbal galactagogue without other changes
options.rationale: Fenugreek has limited inconsistent evidence and is not a substitute for addressing milk removal or QT-drug review. Fenugreek is correct only as adjunctive lay-supported intervention, not medical recommendation (ABM).

explanation.summary: Domperidone is the most-evidenced pharmacological galactagogue but carries dose-dependent QT prolongation risk. TGA advice is to avoid use with concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (including citalopram, escitalopram, ondansetron, clarithromycin, methadone) or with a baseline QTc >=450 ms.
key_points: TGA dose ceiling: 10 mg TDS
Baseline ECG indicated before starting domperidone
Avoid with concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
Avoid with QTc >=450 ms or electrolyte derangement
Galactagogues are adjuncts, not replacements, for milk removal
explanation.pearls: Always review the medication list (including SSRIs and antiemetics) before starting domperidone - the most common safety oversight is concurrent QT-prolonging drugs.

stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman attends urogynaecology with 12 months of progressive vaginal vault prolapse 9 years after a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign menorrhagia. She has previously trialled a ring pessary for 6 months with recurrent expulsion despite refit, and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 4 months with no symptomatic benefit. POP-Q examination shows vaginal vault descent to +4 with anterior wall to +2 and posterior wall to 0; TVL is 8 cm. She is sexually active and wishes to preserve coital function; she has no obesity-related surgical risk (BMI 24), no significant cardiopulmonary disease, no diabetes and is otherwise healthy with no contraindication to general anaesthetic and abdominal surgery.

data_table.History: TAH 9 years ago. Pessary failed (recurrent expulsion). PFMT 4 months no benefit. Sexually active, BMI 24, no comorbidities.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.text: Laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior sacral ligament
options.rationale: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) using type 1 macroporous polypropylene mesh is the gold-standard procedure for symptomatic vault prolapse where coital function is to be preserved; it has the lowest recurrence rate and best preserves vaginal length; abdominal (non-vaginal) mesh placement is not subject to the TGA's transvaginal mesh restriction (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Transvaginal mesh kit repair of the vaginal vault using a synthetic mesh device
options.rationale: Transvaginal mesh kits withdrawn by TGA in Australia in 2017 due to harm signals; not available. TV mesh is essentially never correct in current Australian practice (TGA mesh statement).

options.text: Sacrospinous ligament fixation of the vaginal vault through a vaginal surgical approach
options.rationale: Sacrospinous fixation is reasonable native-tissue alternative for poor abdominal surgical candidates but higher recurrence than sacrocolpopexy. SSLF is correct in patients unsuitable for abdominal surgery (RANZCOG).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Le Fort colpocleisis with obliteration of the vagina for definitive vault support
options.rationale: Colpocleisis is obliterative procedure for older, frail, non-sexually-active women who accept loss of coital function; she remains sexually active. Colpocleisis is correct in elderly non-sexually-active women (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat pessary trial with a Gellhorn pessary plus indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: Already failed conservative with recurrent pessary expulsion and ineffective PFMT; another pessary unlikely to succeed. Pessary is correct as initial trial or in patients declining surgery (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) with type 1 polypropylene mesh is the durability gold-standard for symptomatic vault prolapse where sexual function is to be preserved. Sacrospinous fixation is the principal native-tissue alternative. Colpocleisis is reserved for frail, non-sexually-active patients. Transvaginal mesh kits are not available in Australia since 2017.
key_points: TGA withdrew transvaginal mesh kits in 2017; sacrocolpopexy mesh is placed abdominally and remains available
Sacrocolpopexy preserves vaginal length and axis better than vaginal approaches
Sacrospinous fixation: native tissue, vaginal route, higher recurrence
Colpocleisis: obliterative, only for non-sexually-active
Conservative measures should be trialled first unless not feasible
explanation.pearls: The Australian Senate Inquiry into transvaginal mesh shaped the current TGA stance and is why the abdominal vs vaginal mesh distinction is essential to know.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Vault prolapse after hysterectomy - surgical choice

stem: A 58-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day and a need to splint to defaecate. She also reports new-onset stress urinary incontinence on coughing, urgency without leakage, and one episode of incomplete bladder emptying requiring her to bear down. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. BMI 26, postmenopausal 8 years. Speculum and POP-Q: Ba 0 (anterior at hymen on Valsalva), Bp +1 (posterior beyond hymen), C -5 (cervix well supported), Aa 0, Ap +1. Post-void residual 90 mL. Bowel motions Bristol 4 on fibre.

data_table.Symptoms: Vaginal bulge, splints posterior wall to defaecate, no urinary symptoms.
data_table.POP-Q: Ba -2, Bp +1, C -5. Posterior wall bulges to introitus on Valsalva.

lead_in: Which compartment defect best explains her splinting-to-defaecate symptom specifically?

options.text: Posterior vaginal compartment defect (rectocele), evidenced by Bp +1 and need to splint posteriorly to evacuate stool
options.rationale: Splinting the posterior vagina to evacuate stool is the classic symptom of rectocele (posterior compartment); Bp +1 confirms posterior wall descent. The cystocele (Ba 0) explains her urinary symptoms, but the defaecatory symptom is rectocele-specific (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: Anterior vaginal compartment defect (cystocele), causing her splinting symptom
options.rationale: Cystocele causes URINARY splinting (anterior wall splinting to void), not posterior splinting to defaecate. Cystocele is correct with urinary splinting symptoms (IUGA).

options.text: Apical compartment defect of the uterine cervix, causing her splinting symptom
options.rationale: Apical descent presents as dragging/heaviness; C -5 here well-supported, ruling out apical prolapse. Apical defect is correct with cervical descent beyond -1 (IUGA).

options.text: Enterocele of the pouch of Douglas, causing her splinting symptom
options.rationale: Enterocele may coexist but requires specific clinical or radiological identification; examination more consistent with rectocele. Enterocele is correct with peritoneal sac on imaging or characteristic finding (IUGA).

options.text: Post-hysterectomy vault prolapse defect
options.rationale: G3P3 with intact cervix on examination (C -5); has not had hysterectomy. Vault prolapse is correct post-hysterectomy only (IUGA).

explanation.summary: Compartment defects map to distinctive symptoms: anterior (cystocele) - urinary hesitancy/splinting anterior wall to void; posterior (rectocele) - obstructed defaecation/splinting posterior wall to evacuate; apical - generalised vaginal heaviness/dragging.
key_points: Anterior wall splinting to void -> cystocele
Posterior wall splinting to defaecate -> rectocele
Generalised heaviness/something coming down -> apical defect
Vault prolapse = apical descent post-hysterectomy
Multiple compartments often coexist - identify the dominant symptom
explanation.pearls: Splinting direction is one of the most useful pieces of history in distinguishing compartment dominance before you examine.

stem: A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment (MoCA 24) presents to her GP with 9 months of urinary urgency, urge incontinence approximately twice daily and nocturia 3 times. She has had a structured trial of bladder training and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 12 weeks under a continence nurse, with caffeine and evening fluid restriction implemented, and reports modest but inadequate improvement. There is no stress component on her bladder diary, no haematuria, and PVR is 30 mL. Urinalysis is clear; recent FBC, UEC, glucose and Ca2+ are normal. She has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg, no untreated narrow-angle glaucoma, no severe constipation and her resting heart rate is 72 bpm with BP 134/82 mmHg. She does not have severe uncontrolled hypertension. She is concerned about adding medications that might worsen her memory. Her geriatrician and GP agree to add pharmacotherapy to her ongoing conservative measures.

data_table.Bladder diary: Urgency, urge UI x2/day, nocturia x3, no stress component. PVR 30 mL.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate pharmacological agent?

options.text: Oxybutynin immediate-release 5 mg orally twice a day
options.rationale: Oxybutynin (especially IR) has highest CNS penetration; associated with cognitive decline in older adults. Listed as potentially inappropriate. Oxybutynin is correct only in younger patients without cognitive concern (Beers Criteria; STOPP).

options.text: Mirabegron 25 mg orally daily, titrating to 50 mg if needed
options.rationale: Mirabegron is a beta-3 adrenergic agonist with no anticholinergic burden, making it preferred in older patients with cognitive impairment when conservative measures are inadequate, provided severe uncontrolled hypertension is excluded (NICE NG123; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Solifenacin 5 mg orally daily as a long-acting antimuscarinic
options.rationale: Solifenacin is more bladder-selective with lower CNS penetration than oxybutynin but still anticholinergic burden best avoided in cognitive impairment. Solifenacin is correct in moderate OAB without cognitive concern (Beers).

options.text: Duloxetine 40 mg orally twice daily for the urinary symptoms
options.rationale: Duloxetine is used for stress UI; diary shows no stress component. Duloxetine is correct in stress-predominant or mixed UI (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Desmopressin 0.1 mg orally at night for the urge incontinence
options.rationale: Desmopressin restricted in older patients due to hyponatraemia risk; not first-line for OAB. Desmopressin is correct in younger patients with nocturnal polyuria and no hyponatraemia risk (Continence Foundation; AMH).

explanation.summary: Second-line pharmacotherapy for urge UI in an older person with cognitive impairment should minimise anticholinergic burden. Mirabegron is preferred provided uncontrolled severe hypertension is excluded. Antimuscarinics carry cognitive and falls risk; oxybutynin IR is the worst offender.
key_points: First-line: bladder training, PFMT, caffeine reduction, weight loss
Mirabegron: beta-3 agonist, no anticholinergic burden, check BP
Antimuscarinics rank by CNS burden: oxybutynin (worst) > tolterodine > solifenacin/darifenacin (lowest)
Desmopressin restricted in >65 (hyponatraemia)
Duloxetine is for stress UI, not urge UI
explanation.pearls: Always check PVR before pharmacotherapy in OAB - antimuscarinics in undiagnosed chronic retention can precipitate acute retention.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

subtopic_detail: Urge incontinence pharmacological choice in older woman

stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 woman is referred to urogynaecology after 12 months of stress urinary incontinence symptoms refractory to a documented 6-month programme of supervised pelvic floor muscle training and weight loss (lost 7 kg, BMI now 28). Her bladder diary shows leakage with cough/sneeze/exertion only, no urgency or urge episodes, no voiding difficulty. PVR is 20 mL and a positive cough stress test reproduces leakage in clinic. There is no haematuria, no recurrent UTI, no prior pelvic surgery, no neurological disease and no prolapse beyond the hymen (POP-Q Ba -1). She has been counselled about a mid-urethral sling and an autologous fascial sling and is keen to proceed surgically. She has not had urodynamic studies. The clinical fellow asks whether urodynamics should be performed before sling surgery. She has not had any previous incontinence surgery, no pelvic radiotherapy and no relevant neurological disease.

data_table.Diary: Pure stress symptoms; no urgency or urge.
data_table.Tests: PVR 20 mL, positive cough stress test, no prolapse beyond hymen.
data_table.Trials: 6 months PFMT + 7 kg weight loss without resolution.

lead_in: Which approach to urodynamics is most consistent with current guidance?

options.text: Routine urodynamic studies in every woman before any incontinence surgery
options.rationale: NICE NG123 advises against routine urodynamics before primary mid-urethral sling for clinically clear uncomplicated stress UI. Routine urodynamics-for-all is essentially never correct (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamics not required before primary sling here; reserve for mixed or complex
options.rationale: Her presentation is uncomplicated pure stress UI with normal PVR, no voiding symptoms, no prior surgery and no prolapse beyond the hymen; NICE NG123 and the Continence Foundation Australia consensus indicate urodynamics in this scenario do not change management (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamic studies only after a failed primary mid-urethral sling procedure
options.rationale: Failed primary procedure is one indication but not the only one; urodynamics may be appropriate before surgery in complicated cases. Failed-primary indication is correct but not exclusive (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamic studies required only when the post-void residual exceeds 100 mL
options.rationale: PVR is one input; symptom pattern, prior surgery, neurological history, and prolapse all guide decision. PVR-alone trigger is correct only as one of multiple criteria (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: Urodynamics is mandatory whenever any pharmacotherapy is being considered
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy for urge UI does not require urodynamics; conservative measures and empirical trial first-line. Urodynamics for any pharmacotherapy is essentially never correct (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

explanation.summary: Urodynamic studies are NOT required before a primary procedure for clinically straightforward stress urinary incontinence. They are indicated when: symptoms are mixed or urge-predominant, voiding dysfunction is suspected (high PVR, hesitancy), there is previous incontinence/pelvic surgery, concurrent prolapse repair is planned (occult SUI assessment), or there is neurogenic disease.
key_points: Pure, uncomplicated SUI -> no urodynamics needed before primary sling
Mixed UI with discordant findings -> urodynamics often helpful
Prior incontinence surgery -> urodynamics before redo
Neurogenic bladder -> urodynamics needed
Suspected occult SUI before prolapse surgery -> urodynamics
explanation.pearls: The single biggest indication people forget is concurrent prolapse repair, where reducing the prolapse can unmask stress incontinence.

sources.label: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

stem: A 48-year-old G3P3 woman attends the gynaecology outpatient clinic 4 weeks after a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for symptomatic fibroids. The procedure was complicated by adhesions at the vesicouterine peritoneum requiring sharp dissection from the bladder; the surgeon documented bladder integrity at the end of the case using bladder filling without leak observed. For the past 8 days she has had continuous painless watery leakage into her underwear regardless of position or activity, with no urge, no stress trigger, no incomplete emptying sensation, and unchanged daytime voids of normal volume. There is no fever, no flank pain, no haematuria, no vaginal bleeding. Urinalysis is negative for blood, nitrites and leucocytes. Speculum examination reveals clear fluid pooling in the vaginal vault but no visible defect on direct inspection. The vault is well healed; there is no apparent vault dehiscence. She is on no medications other than recent post-operative paracetamol and is otherwise well.

data_table.History: Continuous painless watery leakage 8 days after TLH complicated by bladder dissection.
data_table.Examination: Clear fluid in vault, no visible defect, vault intact.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear, no infection, no haematuria.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Three-swab dye test: instil dilute methylene blue into the bladder and inspect vaginal swabs
options.rationale: The three-swab (tampon) test with intravesical dye distinguishes vesicovaginal from ureterovaginal and urethrovaginal fistulae and can identify a fistula site too small to see at speculum; it is a simple bedside test and is the standard first-line investigation alongside, or before, formal cystoscopy (RANZCOG; FIGO Fistula manual).
source_refs: FIGO Global Competency-Based Fistula Surgery Training Manual

options.text: Empirical oral antibiotics for 7 days then outpatient review of the leakage pattern
options.rationale: Urinalysis clear and clinical picture not UTI; antibiotics will not address fistula. Antibiotics are correct in UTI, not fistula (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Urodynamic studies as first-line to characterise the pattern of continuous leakage
options.rationale: Urodynamics characterise bladder/sphincter function and not used to diagnose fistula. Urodynamics is correct in functional incontinence workup (NICE NG123).
source_refs: NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

options.text: MRI pelvis with rectal contrast to define the suspected genitourinary tract fistula
options.rationale: MRI may help anatomical mapping for surgical planning of complex fistulae but not first step; dye testing plus cystoscopy is standard. MRI is correct in complex fistula surgical planning (RANZCOG).

options.text: Outpatient pelvic floor physiotherapy with re-review in 3 months without imaging
options.rationale: Continuous painless leakage after pelvic surgery with bladder dissection is fistula until proven otherwise; physiotherapy delays diagnosis. PT is correct in functional UI without fistula features (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Continuous painless urinary leakage after pelvic surgery is a vesicovaginal or ureterovaginal fistula until proven otherwise. The three-swab dye test (intravesical methylene blue plus tampons in upper, middle and lower vagina) localises the leak and distinguishes urethral, vesical and ureteric sources; cystoscopy and CT-IVU or CTU then characterise the defect for repair planning.
key_points: Continuous painless leakage post-pelvic surgery -> fistula until proven otherwise
Three-swab dye test: upper swab stained suggests ureterovaginal; mid/upper bladder; lower urethrovaginal
If dye test negative for vesical source, suspect ureterovaginal: oral phenazopyridine or IV indigo carmine plus CT-IVU
Cystoscopy maps the bladder defect for repair planning
Obstetric fistula remains the leading global cause; in high-income settings most fistulae are iatrogenic post-hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: If the upper vaginal tampon stains blue and the middle does not, suspect a ureterovaginal fistula and proceed to ureteric imaging.

sources.label: FIGO Global Competency-Based Fistula Surgery Training Manual
NICE NG123 Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse

stem: A 38-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 540, viral load undetectable on ART) is referred to a vulval clinic with 9 months of mild vulval pruritus. Examination shows two well-demarcated, slightly raised, pigmented and acetowhite plaques on the right labium minus measuring 8 mm and 12 mm, with no ulceration, induration or vascular atypia. There is no clitoral hood resorption, no labial fusion and no porcelain-white skin.
