options.rationale: Cefixime monotherapy is wrong because oral cefixime has higher MICs and rising resistance; IM ceftriaxone provides more reliable bactericidal pharmacokinetics, especially for pharyngeal infection. Would be correct only when IM access is not possible (ASHM; BASHH).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus azithromycin 1 g orally single dose
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus azithromycin 1 g PO is the Australian first-line dual therapy for uncomplicated gonorrhoea; ceftriaxone targets gonorrhoea (with rising MIC concerns) and azithromycin covers presumptive chlamydia plus provides synergy. Doxycycline 100 mg BD x 7 days for chlamydia is an alternative co-therapy.

options.text: Benzathine penicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single dose with no other agent
options.rationale: Benzathine penicillin is wrong because it treats syphilis; Neisseria gonorrhoeae is intrinsically less susceptible and frequently penicillin-resistant. Would be correct only for syphilis, not gonorrhoea (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated gonorrhoea in Australia is treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus azithromycin 1 g PO single dose (dual therapy); the azithromycin covers co-existing chlamydia and provides synergy against ceftriaxone-resistant strains. Culture and susceptibility must be sent before treatment whenever possible. Test of cure at 2 weeks for pharyngeal infection or any treatment failure.
key_points: Dual therapy: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + azithromycin 1 g PO
Always send culture before treatment for susceptibility
Pharyngeal: TOC at 2 weeks (NAAT) and 4 weeks (culture)
Partner notification: 2 months
Disseminated gonococcal infection: ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily for 7 days
explanation.pearls: Disseminated gonococcal infection presents with the triad of dermatitis, tenosynovitis and migratory polyarthralgia or septic arthritis; blood cultures often negative, but skin lesion or joint aspirate PCR may confirm.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a painless ulcer on the right labia majora first noticed eight days ago. She has had two new sexual contacts over the past three months including one overseas partner. The ulcer is solitary, indurated and clean-based with a rolled edge and is associated with non-tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy. She has no rash, no oral lesions, no fever and no joint pain. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no penicillin allergy, no immunosuppression, and is otherwise well with a BMI of 23. Dark-field microscopy is not available locally but a swab from the ulcer base has been sent for Treponema pallidum PCR. Treponemal (CMIA) serology returns reactive and the RPR titre is 1:32. She has never previously been diagnosed with or treated for syphilis. HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C and gonorrhoea/chlamydia screens have been sent.

data_table.Examination: Solitary 8 mm indurated painless ulcer with clean base and rolled edge on right labia majora. Non-tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy. No palmar/plantar rash, no condylomata lata, no mucous patches.
data_table.Investigations: Treponemal CMIA: reactive. RPR: 1:32. T. pallidum PCR from ulcer pending. Urine beta-hCG negative. HIV, HBV, HCV, gonorrhoea/chlamydia screens pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment regimen for this stage of syphilis?

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Doxycycline monotherapy is wrong because doxycycline 14 days is reserved for non-pregnant patients with confirmed penicillin allergy; she has no allergy and benzathine penicillin is first-line. Would be correct only with documented penicillin allergy outside pregnancy (ASHM).

options.text: Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single stat dose
options.rationale: A single dose of benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) IM is the first-line treatment for primary, secondary and early latent syphilis (acquired within the past 2 years) in Australian guidance (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly weekly for three doses
options.rationale: Three weekly doses is wrong because that regimen is for late latent syphilis or syphilis of unknown duration (where treponemal eradication takes longer); primary syphilis acquired within 2 years needs only one dose. Would be correct only for late latent or unknown duration syphilis (ASHM).

options.text: Procaine penicillin 1.5 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Procaine penicillin daily 10 days is wrong because that regimen is reserved for neurosyphilis or ocular/otic syphilis when IV benzylpenicillin is impractical; primary syphilis does not need CNS penetration. Would be correct only for neurosyphilis or eye/ear involvement (ASHM).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days total
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone 10 days is wrong because ceftriaxone is an alternative when penicillin is not available; with no allergy, single-dose benzathine penicillin remains first-line. Would be correct only with penicillin allergy or shortage (CDC; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Primary syphilis (painless indurated chancre, regional lymphadenopathy, RPR reactive, treponemal serology reactive, acquired within 2 years) is treated with a single dose of benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) IM. Late latent or unknown-duration syphilis requires 3 weekly doses. Counsel about Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, chills, myalgia 4-12 h after first dose) and notify partners (3 months for primary, 6 months for secondary, 12 months for early latent).
key_points: Primary chancre: painless, indurated, single, clean base
Primary/secondary/early latent: single benzathine 1.8 g IM
Late latent or unknown duration: 3 weekly IM doses
Neurosyphilis/ocular/otic: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly x 15 days
Jarisch-Herxheimer common 4-12 h after first dose
explanation.pearls: Adequate response = 4-fold (2-dilution) drop in RPR titre by 6-12 months. Persistent or rising titres indicate treatment failure or reinfection. Always screen for co-existing HIV.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with five days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, intermenstrual bleeding and a low-grade fever. She has had one new sexual partner over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L), has no allergies, takes only the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no recent gynaecological instrumentation and no IUD in situ. She has not had previous PID. On examination her temperature is 37.9 degrees C, abdomen is mildly tender suprapubically without peritonism, bowel sounds are normal, and on bimanual examination there is bilateral adnexal tenderness with marked cervical motion tenderness. Speculum reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge. Endocervical NAATs and gonococcal culture have been sent. There is no palpable adnexal mass, no fluctuant cul-de-sac mass on examination, and the patient tolerates oral fluids and analgesia. The treating registrar judges her safe for outpatient management with close 48-hour review.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Suprapubic tenderness without peritonism. Speculum: mucopurulent cervical discharge. Bimanual: marked cervical motion tenderness, bilateral adnexal tenderness, no mass.
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. Endocervical NAAT for gonorrhoea/chlamydia and culture sent. WCC 12.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 48 mg/L. Pelvic ultrasound: no tubo-ovarian abscess.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat plus doxycycline 100 mg bd and metronidazole 400 mg bd for 14 days
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines and Australasian STI Guidelines recommend ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat (to cover gonorrhoea) plus 14 days of doxycycline 100 mg BD (chlamydia and atypical organisms) plus metronidazole 400 mg BD (anaerobes including BV-associated organisms) for outpatient PID.

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as oral monotherapy regimen
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is wrong because it does not reliably cover N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis, the principal pathogens in PID; the anti-pattern is reaching for a broad oral beta-lactam without coverage of the actual culprits. Would never be correct as PID monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as oral monotherapy regimen
options.rationale: Doxycycline monotherapy is wrong because while doxycycline covers chlamydia and atypicals, it does not reliably cover gonorrhoea or anaerobes; PID needs combination therapy. Would be correct only as part of combination (with ceftriaxone and metronidazole), not alone (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally weekly for 2 weeks as a oral monotherapy regimen
options.rationale: Weekly azithromycin is wrong because single-agent azithromycin is inadequate for PID, risks treatment failure and contributes to macrolide resistance. Would never be correct as PID monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines; BASHH).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily plus metronidazole 500 mg IV twice daily until afebrile for 24 hours
options.rationale: IV regimen is wrong because IV therapy is reserved for inpatient PID with tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, severe sepsis, failed oral therapy or inability to tolerate oral intake; this woman meets outpatient criteria. Would be correct only with one of those inpatient indications (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Outpatient PID in Australia is treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Admit for IV therapy if pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess, severe sepsis, inability to tolerate oral intake, surgical abdomen needing exclusion, or failure of outpatient therapy at 48-72 hours. Review at 48-72 hours mandatory.
key_points: Empirical Rx: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + doxycycline 100 mg BD x 14 d + metronidazole 400 mg BD x 14 d
Admit if: pregnant, TOA, severe sepsis, vomiting, surgical abdomen, failed oral
48-72 h review essential
Treat partners empirically (last 6 months)
IUD does not need removal unless no improvement at 72 h
explanation.pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis - have a low threshold to treat young sexually active women with pelvic pain plus cervical motion or adnexal tenderness, as missed PID is a leading cause of tubal infertility and ectopic pregnancy.

stem: A 29-year-old cisgender woman attends a sexual health service requesting HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. Her current male partner of three months has HIV-1 with a viral load that he reports has been detectable on most recent testing because of treatment interruption while changing jobs. They have condomless vaginal sex 2-3 times per week. She has no symptoms of acute HIV illness in the past 6 weeks (no fever, rash, lymphadenopathy or sore throat), is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), is on the etonogestrel implant for contraception, has no history of renal disease, and is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated). She has not injected drugs. Her last STI screen 8 months ago was negative. She has no allergies and no regular medications other than the implant. She is asking whether she is eligible for PrEP, what baseline tests are required, and what monitoring she will need on therapy.

data_table.Examination: Well. No mucosal lesions, no rash, no lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. HBsAg, anti-HBc and HBsAb pending today. HIV Ag/Ab pending today.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for PrEP initiation and monitoring?

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg only after a 4-week negative HIV window-period repeat test
options.rationale: Deferring PrEP 4 weeks is wrong because a single recent negative HIV test plus absence of acute HIV symptoms is sufficient to start PrEP; deferring prolongs an unnecessary exposure window. Would be correct only if there are symptoms suggestive of acute seroconversion (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Prescribe on-demand (2+1+1) tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine, suitable for all genders in this clinical scenario today
options.rationale: On-demand (2+1+1) PrEP is wrong because on-demand dosing is only recommended for cisgender MSM with infrequent exposures; cisgender women require daily PrEP due to slower tissue drug levels in cervicovaginal tissue. Would be correct only for selected MSM, not for cisgender women (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg with baseline HIV/HBV/renal screen, then HIV/STI screening every 3 months
options.rationale: Daily oral TDF/FTC is the recommended PrEP regimen for cisgender women at substantial risk. Baseline workup includes HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis serology, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT, renal function and beta-hCG. Quarterly monitoring includes HIV testing and STI screen; renal function 6-monthly (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PrEP and recommend condom use only, as PrEP is contraindicated when the partner has detectable viral load
options.rationale: Defer PrEP because partner is detectable is wrong because a partner with detectable viral load is exactly the indication for PrEP; PrEP is strongly indicated, not contraindicated. Would never be correct (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily oral dolutegravir 50 mg as PrEP monotherapy in this scenario at first visit
options.rationale: Dolutegravir monotherapy is wrong because dolutegravir is not a PrEP agent; PrEP uses TDF/FTC (daily or, for selected MSM, on-demand). Would never be correct (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Cisgender women eligible for PrEP receive daily oral TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg. Baseline workup: HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT (multi-site), renal function, beta-hCG. Quarterly: HIV test, STI screen, adherence and side-effect review. Renal function 6-monthly. On-demand (2+1+1) dosing only for cisgender MSM with predictable infrequent exposures. PrEP can be continued in pregnancy if substantial risk persists.
key_points: Daily TDF/FTC for cisgender women (no on-demand)
MSM may use daily or on-demand (2+1+1)
Baseline: HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis, GC/CT, renal, beta-hCG
Monitoring: HIV + STI every 3 months; renal every 6 months
HBV must be screened pre-PrEP (rebound on cessation)
explanation.pearls: Exclude acute HIV before initiation: ask about fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, sore throat in the past 6 weeks. Acute HIV may have a still-negative Ag/Ab and starting PrEP could select for resistance.

subtopic_detail: HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis - eligibility and monitoring

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left iliac fossa pain that began two hours ago while exercising, associated with one episode of vomiting. The pain is colicky on a background of constant severe pain and radiates to the left flank. She has no fever, no dysuria, no diarrhoea, no anorexia and no migratory periumbilical pain. On examination temperature is 36.9 degrees C, pulse 104, BP 116/72. The abdomen is tender in the left iliac fossa with voluntary guarding but no rebound or percussion tenderness; right iliac fossa is non-tender. Bimanual examination reveals a tender left adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness and the right adnexa is non-tender. Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bedside ultrasound by the registrar shows a 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow and reduced arterial flow.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Left iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no rebound. Right iliac fossa non-tender. Bimanual: tender left adnexal fullness, no cervical motion tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocyte esterase negative. Bedside US: 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow, reduced arterial flow.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Left ovarian torsion with ischaemia
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pain with vomiting on a background of a 7 cm ovarian cyst, tender adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness, negative beta-hCG, and ultrasound showing absent venous and reduced arterial Doppler flow are classical for ovarian torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
options.rationale: Ruptured ectopic is wrong because serum beta-hCG <5 IU/L definitively excludes pregnancy; an ectopic cannot exist without pregnancy. Would never be correct with beta-hCG negative (RANZCOG).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
options.rationale: Acute PID is wrong because PID typically presents with bilateral pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge and fever, often subacute; sudden severe unilateral pain with abnormal Doppler on a cyst fits torsion. Would be correct only with bilateral CMT, mucopurulent discharge and inflammatory markers (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Acute appendicitis with pelvic peritonism
options.rationale: Appendicitis is wrong because classical appendicitis produces migratory periumbilical-to-RIF pain with anorexia and low-grade fever; left-sided pain with adnexal Doppler abnormalities points to torsion. Would be correct only with RIF localisation and characteristic features (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Renal colic from a left ureteric stone
options.rationale: Renal colic is wrong because renal colic produces flank-to-groin pain often with microscopic haematuria; urinalysis is clean and ultrasound localises the abnormality to the ovary with abnormal Doppler. Would be correct only with haematuria and a ureteric stone on imaging (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Classical features: sudden unilateral pelvic pain, often during physical activity, with nausea and vomiting; tender adnexal mass on bimanual examination; ovarian cyst (typically > 5 cm) on imaging; reduced or absent Doppler flow (venous flow lost first, then arterial). Diagnosis often requires diagnostic laparoscopy as Doppler can be normal in intermittent torsion. Detorsion with cystectomy is preferred over oophorectomy when ovarian tissue appears viable.
key_points: Torsion: sudden unilateral pain, vomiting, cyst > 5 cm, abnormal Doppler
Ectopic: beta-hCG positive, missed period, PV bleed, hypovolaemia if ruptured
PID: bilateral pain, fever, CMT, mucopurulent discharge, days-onset
Appendicitis: migratory pain, anorexia, RIF, McBurney's, Rovsing's
Doppler: venous flow lost first; intermittent torsion may show preserved flow
explanation.pearls: Beta-hCG must be checked in every woman of reproductive age with pelvic pain; missing ectopic is a never-event. A normal Doppler does not exclude torsion - if clinical suspicion is high, proceed to diagnostic laparoscopy.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 18+3 weeks gestation presents to her midwife with two weeks of a thin off-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour. She has no abdominal pain, no abnormal bleeding, no fever, no contractions and no fluid leakage. She is otherwise well, BMI at booking 25, no medical comorbidities, no previous preterm birth (the first pregnancy delivered at 39+2), no known cervical insufficiency, and the dating scan at 9 weeks confirmed a singleton intrauterine pregnancy. Her booking bloods including HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B and rubella serology were negative or immune. Speculum examination shows a thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation, a closed cervix without lesions, and no liquor pooling. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall is 5.1 and addition of 10% potassium hydroxide produces a strong amine odour. Wet mount microscopy shows clue cells and few lactobacilli with no motile trichomonads and no pseudohyphae.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge, closed cervix, no liquor pooling. No external genital lesions.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.1. KOH whiff test positive. Wet mount: clue cells present, few lactobacilli, no trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. Booking HIV, syphilis, HBV negative; rubella immune.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment for this woman's bacterial vaginosis at 18+3 weeks gestation?

options.text: No treatment required as bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is self-limiting and treatment does not reduce preterm birth
options.rationale: No treatment is wrong because symptomatic BV warrants treatment for symptom relief in pregnancy, regardless of whether treatment reduces preterm birth; Australian guidance treats symptomatic BV with oral metronidazole. Would be correct only for asymptomatic BV in low-risk pregnancies (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream applied intravaginally nightly for 7 nights as monotherapy regimen
options.rationale: Clindamycin vaginal cream is wrong because in the second half of pregnancy the oil-based cream weakens latex condoms and has been associated with adverse neonatal outcomes and preterm birth in some studies; oral therapy is preferred in pregnancy. Would be correct only in the first half of pregnancy when oral metronidazole is unsuitable (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 5 to 7 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days is the first-line treatment for symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy beyond the first trimester in Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with no further course required
options.rationale: Tinidazole is wrong because tinidazole has limited pregnancy safety data and is contraindicated in the first trimester; oral metronidazole has the established pregnancy safety profile across trimesters. Would never be correct in pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Metronidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with no further course required
options.rationale: Metronidazole 2 g single dose is wrong because single-dose 2 g has lower cure rates than 5-7 day regimens for BV; longer courses are preferred in pregnancy. Would be correct only in non-pregnant women preferring single-dose convenience over efficacy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is treated with oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days. Clindamycin vaginal cream is avoided in pregnancy (oil-based, weakens latex condoms, theoretical association with adverse outcomes). Diagnosis is by Amsel criteria (3 of 4) or Nugent score >= 7. Screening of asymptomatic women is not recommended (no benefit in low-risk women; mixed evidence in high-risk).
key_points: Treatment of choice in pregnancy: oral metronidazole 400 mg BD x 5-7 days
Single-dose 2 g and tinidazole both inferior choices in pregnancy
Avoid clindamycin vaginal cream (oil-based, weakens latex)
Amsel 3 of 4 or Nugent >= 7 for diagnosis
Routine asymptomatic screening not recommended
explanation.pearls: BV associates with preterm birth but treatment in low-risk asymptomatic women does not consistently reduce preterm birth; treat symptomatic women for symptom relief and individualise in those with previous preterm birth.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy
Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

stem: A 32-year-old woman who is breastfeeding her 4-month-old infant (exclusive breastfeeding, no formula) presents with 3 weeks of frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulval irritation and dyspareunia. Strawberry cervix on speculum, vaginal pH 5.6, KOH whiff positive, saline wet mount shows motile flagellated protozoa. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia NAAT have been sent. She has had two new male partners in the past 4 months. She has no allergies and no liver disease. She wishes to continue breastfeeding without interruption if possible.

data_table.Examination: Strawberry cervix with punctate haemorrhages. Copious frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.6. Saline wet mount: motile flagellated protozoa. Trichomonas NAAT pending. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Single 2 g oral metronidazole dose to her and her recent partners; pause breastfeeding for 12-24 hours and discard expressed milk
options.rationale: Single-dose metronidazole 2 g is first-line for trichomoniasis in adults, including breastfeeding women, with brief interruption of breastfeeding (12-24 hours) and discarding milk during peak levels to minimise infant exposure. Partner treatment is essential to prevent reinfection (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).

options.text: Intravaginal metronidazole 0.75% gel nightly for 5 nights; no partner treatment
options.rationale: Intravaginal metronidazole gel is wrong because topical metronidazole achieves <50% cure for trichomoniasis (parasites in urethra, glands, etc. not reached); systemic therapy is required and partners must be treated to prevent ping-pong reinfection. Would never be correct for trichomonas (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with continued breastfeeding throughout
options.rationale: Tinidazole with continued breastfeeding is wrong because tinidazole has a longer half-life requiring an approximately 3-day breastfeeding pause; metronidazole 2 g single dose with a 12-24h pause is preferred in breastfeeding. Would be correct only outside breastfeeding (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Permanently cease breastfeeding so that metronidazole can be given safely
options.rationale: Permanently cease breastfeeding is wrong because a brief 12-24h pause is sufficient to clear peak metronidazole levels; permanent cessation is unnecessary and harmful. Would never be correct (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg bd for 7 days to her only
options.rationale: Doxycycline to her only is wrong because doxycycline has no activity against Trichomonas vaginalis and is contraindicated in breastfeeding due to enamel staining; treating her alone also leaves partners to reinfect. Would never be correct for trichomoniasis (eTG; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Trichomoniasis in non-pregnant adults is treated with oral metronidazole 2 g single dose, or 400 mg BD for 7 days (alternative for those at higher risk of treatment failure such as HIV). In pregnancy, treatment is still recommended (oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days). Always treat sexual partners empirically. Test of cure not routine but re-test for reinfection at 3 months.
key_points: First-line: metronidazole 2 g PO stat OR 400 mg BD x 7 d
Strawberry cervix (colpitis macularis) in 2-5% but pathognomonic when present
Motile flagellated protozoa on wet mount confirms; NAAT most sensitive
Partner notification and empirical treatment essential
Pregnancy: treat with oral metronidazole; avoid topical preparations
explanation.pearls: Avoid alcohol during and for 24 hours after metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). HIV co-infection increases treatment failure risk; consider 7-day BD regimen in that group.

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with a sudden onset of constant, severe lower abdominal pain and a moderate amount of dark red vaginal bleeding. She has a history of pre-eclampsia in her previous pregnancy, which was delivered via emergency lower segment caesarean section at 36 weeks. In this pregnancy, her blood pressure has been mildly elevated but unmedicated. Her 20-week morphology ultrasound showed an anterior placenta clear of the cervical os. On examination, she is visibly distressed. Abdominal examination reveals a tense, tender uterus with a fundal height of 39 cm. Foetal parts are difficult to palpate. Speculum examination confirms dark blood in the vaginal vault with no active cervical lesions. A continuous CTG is commenced, showing a baseline of 165 bpm, reduced variability, and recurrent late decelerations.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 115 bpm, BP 155/95 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Tense, tender uterus, guarding. Speculum: dark vaginal bleeding, os closed.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: 165 bpm baseline, reduced variability, late decelerations.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Perform an urgent bedside transvaginal ultrasound.
options.rationale: Urgent transvaginal ultrasound is wrong because although TVUS rules out praevia, the 20-week scan was clear and the foetus is acutely distressed; delaying delivery for confirmatory imaging in placental abruption with foetal compromise is unsafe. Would be correct only in stable suspected praevia, not acute abruption with foetal distress (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Administer intravenous oxytocin infusion for induction.
options.rationale: IV oxytocin is wrong because oxytocin is contraindicated in a hypertonic, contracting uterus (abruption typically presents with a woody, tense uterus from concealed haemorrhage) and does not address foetal distress; the anti-pattern is augmenting a uterus that is already pathologically contracting. Would never be correct in abruption (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Perform an immediate emergency caesarean section.
options.rationale: The clinical picture of constant pain, woody uterus, bleeding, and foetal compromise strongly suggests a severe placental abruption requiring category 1 caesarean section. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes and internal monitoring.
options.rationale: ARM and internal monitoring is wrong because amniotomy is not appropriate with severe foetal distress, an unengaged foetus and prior caesarean; cervical state and uterine scar limit instrumental rescue. Would be correct only in mild abruption with engaged head, low-risk uterus and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Administer intramuscular betamethasone and observe.
options.rationale: Betamethasone and observe is wrong because corticosteroids are for preterm foetuses (<35 weeks) and this foetus is term at 38 weeks; observation in the face of placental abruption with foetal distress is unsafe. Would be correct only at preterm gestations, not term (KEMH: Corticosteroids in Pregnancy).

explanation.summary: This patient presents with classic signs of a severe placental abruption (constant pain, woody uterus, dark bleeding) leading to acute foetal distress. Immediate delivery via category 1 caesarean section is required to prevent foetal demise and maternal coagulopathy. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)
key_points: Placental abruption is a clinical diagnosis characterised by painful vaginal bleeding and a hypertonic uterus.
Prior caesarean and maternal hypertension are significant risk factors.
Ultrasound is poorly sensitive for acute abruption and should not delay delivery if foetal distress is present.
explanation.pearls: Never delay emergency delivery for an ultrasound in a patient with a woody, painful uterus and a pathological CTG.

stem: A 26-year-old nulliparous woman presents to the ED with 2 days of right iliac fossa pain and scant dark vaginal spotting at LMP+7 weeks. Previous PID 3 years ago, treated. No contraception. On arrival: HR 132, BP 88/52, RR 22, SpO2 98%, peripherally cool with delayed capillary refill 4 seconds, pale, conversing but anxious. Abdominal guarding, rebound tenderness across the right iliac fossa, distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Cervical excitation, right adnexal tenderness with palpable fullness. Urine beta-hCG positive. Bedside FAST shows free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison's pouch. IV access x2 has been obtained; group and hold and crossmatch sent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 18/min, Temp 37.1 C
data_table.Examination: Lower right abdominal guarding. Cervical excitation positive, right adnexal tenderness and mass.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfer immediately to operating theatre for diagnostic laparoscopy with potential laparotomy and activate the massive transfusion protocol
options.rationale: Haemodynamic instability (shock), positive FAST and clinical features of ruptured ectopic mandate immediate operative management without delay for further imaging. Theatre transfer with MTP activation is the correct multi-step decision (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Perform an urgent transvaginal ultrasound to confirm location of pregnancy before further action
options.rationale: Urgent TVUS first is wrong because while imaging is correct for haemodynamically stable women, a shocked patient with positive FAST requires immediate operative management; TVUS would delay laparoscopy and cost the patient blood and time. Would be correct only in stable women with positive beta-hCG and no peritonism (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Administer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 and admit for monitoring
options.rationale: IM methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate requires haemodynamic stability, an unruptured ectopic and low/declining beta-hCG; it is absolutely contraindicated in suspected rupture and shock. Would be correct only for stable ectopic with beta-hCG <5000 and no foetal cardiac activity (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease and reassess in 6 hours
options.rationale: IV antibiotics for PID is wrong because the diagnostic picture is shock plus positive pregnancy test plus free fluid - ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise; treating empirically as PID would miss the surgical emergency and likely be fatal. Would be correct only with negative beta-hCG and PID picture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours and discharge home with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Repeat beta-hCG in 48h and discharge is wrong because discharging a shocked patient with positive pregnancy and free fluid is reckless and likely fatal; the anti-pattern is treating beta-hCG trending as a substitute for resuscitation. Would never be correct in shock (RANZCOG ectopic).

explanation.summary: This patient presents with classic features of an ectopic pregnancy: 7 weeks amenorrhoea, right iliac fossa pain, vaginal spotting, and risk factors (previous PID). Since her vital signs are stable, the next immediate step is a transvaginal ultrasound to confirm the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding)
key_points: Any woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain must have a pregnancy test.
Haemodynamically stable patients with suspected ectopic require urgent transvaginal ultrasound.
Haemodynamically unstable patients require immediate resuscitation and surgical intervention.
explanation.pearls: Cervical motion tenderness (cervical excitation) is a sign of peritoneal irritation from blood in the pelvis, common in both PID and leaking/ruptured ectopic pregnancy.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 41 weeks gestation is undergoing an induction of labour for post-dates. A vaginal dinoprostone (Prostin) pessary was inserted 6 hours ago. The midwife calls you urgently for a review. The patient is distressed, complaining of continuous, severe abdominal pain without relief between contractions. Palpation of the abdomen reveals that she is having 6 contractions every 10 minutes, with increased resting uterine tone. A continuous cardiotocograph (CTG) is running. The baseline foetal heart rate was 140 bpm an hour ago. For the past 4 minutes, the CTG has demonstrated a single, sudden, and prolonged deceleration down to a nadir of 80 bpm with loss of baseline variability.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 105 bpm, BP 120/75 mmHg, RR 22/min
data_table.Examination: Uterus is hypertonic. Cervix is 3 cm dilated, 1 cm long, membranes intact.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: Prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm for >3 minutes. Contractions: 6 in 10 mins.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medical intervention?

options.text: Administer an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate.
options.rationale: IV magnesium sulfate is wrong because magnesium is for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks preterm labour) or eclampsia prophylaxis; this is post-dates hyperstimulation needing tocolysis. Would be correct only at preterm gestations or for severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia (KEMH: Intrapartum Care).

options.text: Commence an intravenous oxytocin augmentation infusion.
options.rationale: Oxytocin augmentation is wrong because the uterus is already hyperstimulated; adding oxytocin would worsen tachysystole and deepen foetal hypoxia. Would never be correct in hyperstimulation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of the amniotic membranes.
options.rationale: Artificial rupture of membranes is wrong because the immediate life-threatening issue is hyperstimulation with prolonged deceleration; ARM does not relieve the contractile state and may worsen cord compression. Would be correct only in non-hyperstimulated labour augmentation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Perform an immediate emergency category one caesarean section.
options.rationale: Immediate category 1 caesarean is wrong because in-utero resuscitation with acute tocolysis is the first step; tocolysis often restores foetal heart rate within minutes and avoids caesarean. Would be correct only if tocolysis fails or further deterioration occurs (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Administer a stat dose of subcutaneous terbutaline.
options.rationale: The patient has uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole >5:10) secondary to prostaglandins, causing foetal hypoxia (prolonged deceleration). Acute tocolysis with terbutaline or salbutamol is the most appropriate immediate step. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

explanation.summary: This patient is experiencing uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole) as a complication of prostaglandin induction. The excessive contractions are occluding the spiral arteries, preventing placental reperfusion and leading to a profound, prolonged foetal deceleration. The immediate priority is acute tocolysis (typically subcutaneous terbutaline or intravenous salbutamol) to relax the uterus and restore oxygen delivery to the fetus. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)
key_points: Tachysystole is defined as >5 contractions in 10 minutes; hypertonus is an abnormally high resting tone.
Uterine hyperstimulation is the combination of excessive uterine activity WITH associated foetal heart rate abnormalities.
Immediate management includes stopping any oxytocin, removing vaginal prostaglandins, left lateral positioning, IV fluids, and acute tocolysis.
explanation.pearls: A prolonged deceleration (>3 minutes) is a hypoxic emergency. Always check for cord prolapse, abruption, and uterine hyperstimulation.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic seeking assistance with falling pregnant. She and her partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. She has a history of secondary amenorrhoea, having not menstruated for the past 12 months. Her body mass index (BMI) is 32 kg/m^2. On examination, she has moderate facial hirsutism and cystic acne along her jawline. An initial infertility workup reveals normal serum prolactin and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels, but her calculated free testosterone is elevated. Her partner's semen analysis is entirely normal. A hysterosalpingogram confirms bilateral tubal patency.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 120/80 mmHg, BMI 32 kg/m2
data_table.Investigations: Prolactin normal. TSH normal. Free testosterone elevated. Semen analysis normal. HSG: patent tubes.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction?

options.text: Prescribe oral clomiphene citrate for ovulation induction.
options.rationale: Clomiphene is wrong because the 2023 international Monash PCOS guideline supersedes the older clomiphene-first approach; PPCOS II and meta-analyses show letrozole achieves higher live birth rates with lower multiple-pregnancy risk. Would be correct only if letrozole were unavailable or contraindicated (Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Prescribe oral metformin to improve insulin sensitivity.
options.rationale: Metformin is wrong because metformin improves metabolic parameters but achieves fewer live births than letrozole; it is a second-line or adjunct agent, particularly in BMI >25 or IGT. Would be correct only as adjunct to letrozole or for metabolic indication (Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Refer for gonadotropin injections and timed intercourse.
options.rationale: Gonadotrophin injections are wrong because exogenous FSH in PCOS carries a high OHSS and multiple-pregnancy risk and needs intensive monitoring; strictly second-line. Would be correct only after failed oral ovulation induction (Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Refer for immediate in vitro fertilisation treatment.
options.rationale: Immediate IVF is wrong because IVF is third-line, reserved for women failing pharmacological ovulation induction or with male factor/tubal issues; partner and tubes are normal here. Would be correct only after failed letrozole and IUI cycles (Monash International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Prescribe oral letrozole to induce follicular development.
options.rationale: The Monash International PCOS Guidelines (adopted globally and in Australia) now recommend letrozole (an aromatase inhibitor) as the first-line pharmacological treatment for ovulation induction in women with PCOS. (Source: Monash International PCOS Guideline)

explanation.summary: This patient meets the Rotterdam criteria for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) presenting with anovulatory infertility. Based on the widely adopted International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS, letrozole is now the recommended first-line pharmacological agent over clomiphene citrate, as it results in higher ovulation, pregnancy, and live birth rates with a lower risk of multiple gestations. (Source: Monash International PCOS Guideline)
key_points: PCOS diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound.
Lifestyle modification (weight loss of 5-10%) is the first-line overall management for overweight women with PCOS.
Letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) is the first-line pharmacological treatment for anovulatory infertility in PCOS.
Metformin is useful for metabolic protection but is not the primary drug for ovulation induction.
explanation.pearls: Unlike clomiphene, letrozole does not deplete estrogen receptors in the endometrium, avoiding the 'thin endometrium' side effect that impedes implantation.

stem: A 28-year-old woman attends for routine cervical screening. She has no history of abnormal results and last screened 6 years ago overseas. She is asymptomatic, not pregnant, and has no immunosuppression. Her current test shows oncogenic HPV type 16 detected with no visible abnormality on speculum examination. She asks whether she should simply repeat screening in 12 months because she has never had an abnormal smear before. She also wants to know whether self-collection would have been acceptable. She is anxious because her mother had cervical cancer at age 52, but she has no postcoital bleeding or pelvic pain. Her only medication is the combined oral contraceptive pill.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, pulse 76/min, afebrile
data_table.Examination: Normal vulva and cervix on speculum examination, no visible lesion
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: oncogenic HPV 16 detected
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 12 months only
options.rationale: 12-month HPV repeat is wrong because HPV 16 (a high-risk genotype) skips the 12-month repeat pathway and goes directly to colposcopy in the Australian NCSP. Would be correct only for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative reflex cytology (National Cervical Screening Program).

options.text: Arrange immediate colposcopic assessment
options.rationale: In the Australian NCSP, detection of oncogenic HPV 16 or 18 leads to direct referral for colposcopy rather than routine repeat screening. Her lack of symptoms and normal appearance do not change the pathway. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.

options.text: Return to 5-yearly routine screening because the cervix looks normal
options.rationale: 5-yearly screening is wrong because normal cervical appearance does not override a positive HPV 16 result; routine 5-year screening applies only when the current test is low risk. Would be correct only with negative HPV result, not positive HPV 16 (National Cervical Screening Program).

options.text: Collect a reflex liquid-based cytology specimen and await that result
options.rationale: Reflex cytology and await is wrong because reflex cytology is the NCSP pathway for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV, not for HPV 16/18 which go directly to colposcopy. Would be correct only for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV (National Cervical Screening Program).

options.text: Defer screening until age 30 because she is asymptomatic
options.rationale: Defer screening to age 30 is wrong because Australian NCSP starts at age 25 and asymptomatic status does not exempt; her current result is already abnormal. Would never be correct (National Cervical Screening Program).

explanation.summary: This patient has oncogenic HPV 16 detected on cervical screening, which in the Australian program warrants direct colposcopic assessment. Routine repeat screening or reflex cytology is not the correct pathway for HPV 16. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.
key_points: Routine cervical screening in Australia is HPV-based every 5 years from age 25. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.
HPV 16 or 18 detected leads to colposcopy. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.
Reflex liquid-based cytology is reserved for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV results. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.
explanation.pearls: A normal-looking cervix does not neutralise a high-risk HPV result. Source: National Cervical Screening Program.

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with 2 days of light vaginal bleeding and intermittent left iliac fossa pain. She is 7 weeks from her last menstrual period and conceived spontaneously. She has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease 4 years ago and laparoscopic appendicectomy as a teenager. She feels mildly light-headed when standing but has no syncope. Pregnancy test is positive. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine gestational sac, a 19 mm left adnexal heterogeneous mass, and a small volume of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Serum beta-hCG is 2350 IU/L and was 2280 IU/L 48 hours earlier.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/72 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Mild left adnexal tenderness, no guarding or rebound
data_table.Investigations: beta-hCG plateau over 48 hours, TVUS suspicious left adnexal mass

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG and ultrasound in 7 days because this is likely an early viable intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Repeat in 7 days assuming viable IUP is wrong because plateauing beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone with a suspicious adnexal mass is highly suggestive of ectopic; expectant reassessment risks rupture. Would be correct only with rising appropriately doubling beta-hCG and definite IUP on scan (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Offer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate after confirming suitability for medical management
options.rationale: She is haemodynamically stable with likely unruptured ectopic pregnancy, plateauing beta-hCG, and imaging suspicion, making medical management appropriate if no contraindications exist. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to emergency laparotomy because free fluid confirms rupture
options.rationale: Emergency laparotomy is wrong because small free fluid can be physiological or limited bleeding; she is haemodynamically stable without peritonism. Would be correct only with shock, large haemoperitoneum or peritonism (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

options.text: Arrange dilation and curettage to exclude missed miscarriage before treatment
options.rationale: D&C to exclude missed miscarriage is wrong because the adnexal mass and plateauing beta-hCG already strongly support ectopic; D&C risks delaying treatment and unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only when discriminating PUL between failing IUP and ectopic with no adnexal mass (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Commence oral progesterone and repeat beta-hCG after 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral progesterone is wrong because progesterone has no role in suspected ectopic and risks delaying definitive treatment; the anti-pattern is treating any pregnancy bleeding with progesterone reflexively. Would never be correct in suspected ectopic (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

explanation.summary: This is a probable stable tubal ectopic pregnancy: plateauing beta-hCG above the discriminatory threshold plus an adnexal mass. Stable patients meeting criteria can be considered for methotrexate after confirming suitability and follow-up reliability. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
key_points: Plateauing beta-hCG is concerning for non-viable or ectopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)
Haemodynamic instability or rupture signs would mandate surgery. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Trend interpretation matters more than any single beta-hCG value. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 27 weeks gestation attends antenatal clinic for review of her oral glucose tolerance test. Her BMI at booking was 33 kg/m2 and her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Foetal morphology scan at 20 weeks was normal. She feels well and reports normal foetal movements. Fundal height measures slightly ahead of dates but there is no polyhydramnios on bedside scan. The OGTT was performed because of risk factors and showed fasting glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour glucose 9.6 mmol/L, and 2-hour glucose 7.8 mmol/L after a 75 g glucose load. A medical student states that the result is normal because only the fasting level is mildly elevated and the post-load values are below older diagnostic thresholds.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 29 cm, otherwise unremarkable examination
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 9.6 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these results?

options.text: The OGTT is normal because only the fasting value is abnormal and the 2-hour value remains below 8.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: Normal OGTT is wrong because under ADIPS criteria a single abnormal value diagnoses GDM; the fasting threshold (>=5.1 mmol/L) is met regardless of post-load values. Would be correct only if all three values were below threshold (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).

options.text: The OGTT confirms gestational diabetes because the fasting glucose meets the diagnostic threshold
options.rationale: ADIPS criteria diagnose gestational diabetes if fasting glucose is at least 5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour at least 10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour at least 8.5 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: The OGTT is indeterminate and should be repeated at 32 weeks because post-load glucose levels are normal
options.rationale: Indeterminate, repeat at 32 weeks is wrong because diagnostic criteria are already met on this OGTT; repeat testing is unnecessary and delays management. Would be correct only with borderline values not meeting any threshold (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).

options.text: The OGTT suggests pre-existing type 2 diabetes because fasting glucose exceeds 5.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Pre-existing T2DM is wrong because overt diabetes in pregnancy requires fasting >=7.0 mmol/L or HbA1c >=6.5% or random >=11.1 mmol/L; her fasting 5.4 is well below those thresholds. Would be correct only with much higher fasting glucose or elevated HbA1c (RANZCOG Gestational Diabetes).

options.text: The OGTT should be interpreted as impaired fasting glucose rather than gestational diabetes in pregnancy
options.rationale: Impaired fasting glucose label is wrong because Australian pregnancy-specific ADIPS criteria classify this as GDM, not the non-pregnancy IFG category; pregnancy thresholds differ. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (ADIPS Consensus Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Australian ADIPS criteria diagnose gestational diabetes if any single value on a 75 g OGTT exceeds the threshold. This patient's fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L is diagnostic despite normal post-load values. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)
key_points: ADIPS fasting threshold for gestational diabetes is at least 5.1 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)
Only one abnormal OGTT value is required for diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Diabetes)
explanation.pearls: Many exam questions exploit confusion between older non-pregnancy glucose thresholds and pregnancy-specific ADIPS criteria. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

stem: A 27-year-old woman attends her GP for review of cervical screening results. She had her first cervical screening test at age 25, which was negative for oncogenic HPV. She has no abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, or postcoital symptoms. She received partial HPV vaccination during adolescence. Her current test result shows oncogenic HPV 16 detected with negative liquid-based cytology. She asks whether she can simply repeat the test in 12 months because the cytology is normal and she feels well. She has no history of immunosuppression or previous cervical procedures.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: No examination abnormalities reported
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test positive for HPV 16, negative liquid-based cytology

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat cervical screening in 12 months because cytology is negative and she is asymptomatic
options.rationale: 12-month repeat is wrong because HPV 16 positivity triggers direct colposcopy in the Australian NCSP regardless of cytology; the anti-pattern is treating HPV 16 like an intermediate-risk result. Would be correct only for non-16/18 HPV with negative reflex cytology (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).

options.text: Refer for colposcopy because oncogenic HPV 16 carries higher cervical neoplasia risk
options.rationale: Australian cervical screening guidelines recommend direct colposcopy referral for HPV 16 or 18 positivity regardless of reflex cytology results. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 5 years because prior screening at age 25 was normal
options.rationale: 5-year repeat is wrong because a previous normal result does not override current HPV 16 detection; the screening interval applies only when current result is low risk. Would be correct only with current negative HPV result (NCSP Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange immediate excisional treatment because HPV 16 confirms high-grade dysplasia
options.rationale: Immediate excisional treatment is wrong because HPV 16 increases risk but does not confirm histologic high-grade disease; treatment decisions require colposcopy and biopsy. Would be correct only after histological HSIL confirmation (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).

options.text: Commence HPV vaccination booster doses before deciding whether colposcopy is required
options.rationale: HPV vaccination booster first is wrong because vaccination is preventive and does not alter management of current HPV 16 positivity; colposcopy is required regardless of vaccination status. Would never substitute for colposcopy (NCSP Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, HPV 16 or 18 positivity mandates direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology findings because of increased risk of significant cervical pathology. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)
key_points: HPV 16 and 18 positivity bypasses routine repeat testing pathways. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)
Negative cytology does not exclude clinically important cervical disease in HPV 16 positive patients. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)
explanation.pearls: Many students incorrectly over-prioritise cytology and under-recognise the risk implications of HPV genotyping in the Australian screening pathway. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

stem: A 27-year-old woman, G1P0, presents with 6 hours of right-sided lower abdominal pain and light vaginal spotting at 7 weeks by last menstrual period. She had a positive urine pregnancy test 10 days ago. Her cycles are usually regular. She is haemodynamically stable but uncomfortable. On examination her abdomen is mildly tender in the right iliac fossa, and there is mild cervical motion tenderness. The cervix is closed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, temp 36.9 deg C, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Mild right lower quadrant tenderness, mild cervical motion tenderness, closed cervix, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG 1800 IU/L, Hb 126 g/L, WCC 8.4 x10^9/L
data_table.Medications: Prenatal vitamin only; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Urgent transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: This is the best next investigation in a stable patient with early pregnancy pain and spotting, because transvaginal ultrasound can assess for intrauterine pregnancy or ectopic pregnancy. Her beta-hCG is in the discriminatory zone where ultrasound is useful. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

options.text: Repeat serum beta-hCG in 48 hours only
options.rationale: Repeat beta-hCG only is wrong because serial hCG is helpful when initial ultrasound is non-diagnostic, but in a stable patient with hCG in the discriminatory zone (~1500 IU/L for TVUS), TVUS provides direct visualisation. Would be correct only when initial ultrasound is uninformative (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).

options.text: Dilation and curettage to confirm a failed pregnancy
options.rationale: D&C is wrong because there is no confirmed intrauterine pregnancy loss; uterine evacuation without localising the pregnancy risks destroying a viable IUP or missing an ectopic. Would be correct only after failing PUL is confirmed (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).

options.text: Abdominal CT scan with contrast
options.rationale: CT with contrast is wrong because TVUS is the appropriate first-line imaging in early pregnancy and avoids ionising radiation exposure to a potentially viable pregnancy. Would be correct only if non-pregnancy intra-abdominal pathology dominates and ultrasound is non-diagnostic (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).

options.text: Discharge with reassurance and routine antenatal booking
options.rationale: Discharge with reassurance is wrong because pain and bleeding plus positive beta-hCG require exclusion of ectopic before discharge; the anti-pattern is treating early pregnancy bleeding as low risk without localisation. Would never be correct without exclusion of ectopic (RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance).

explanation.summary: In a stable woman with early pregnancy pain and spotting, the best next investigation is transvaginal ultrasound to localise the pregnancy and assess for ectopic gestation. Her beta-hCG level makes ultrasound particularly useful now. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.
key_points: Pain plus bleeding in early pregnancy always requires exclusion of ectopic pregnancy. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.
Transvaginal ultrasound is the preferred initial imaging test in stable patients. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.
Serial beta-hCG is mainly useful when ultrasound is nondiagnostic. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.
explanation.pearls: A closed cervix does not rule out ectopic pregnancy or threatened miscarriage, so localisation is still the key step. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.

stem: A 23-year-old woman G2P0 presents to ED with 2 days of increasing vaginal bleeding and crampy abdominal pain at 13+0 weeks gestation by ultrasound 1 week ago that showed a live intrauterine pregnancy. She is haemodynamically stable, cervix closed on speculum, uterus appropriate for dates. She is blood group O Rh(D) negative, antibody screen negative, partner Rh positive. She has not yet received any anti-D in this pregnancy. She previously had a TOP at 10 weeks last year and was given anti-D 250 IU IM at that time.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/66 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, temp 36.8 deg C, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness, closed cervix, moderate vaginal bleeding, no adnexal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Known Rh-negative mother; prior ultrasound with live intrauterine pregnancy
data_table.Medications: Prenatal vitamin only; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anti-D management now?

options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
options.rationale: Anti-D 250 IU is wrong because from 13 weeks onwards the NBA sensitising-event dose is 625 IU IM; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose. Would be correct only before 13 weeks (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Administer anti-D 625 IU IM within 72 hours now
options.rationale: At 13 weeks with a sensitising event (significant vaginal bleeding), Australian NBA guidance recommends 625 IU IM anti-D within 72 hours in non-sensitised RhD-negative women (NBA Anti-D Ig Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Administer routine antenatal anti-D 1500 IU IM now to cover this event and the 28-week dose
options.rationale: 1500 IU is wrong because the 1500 IU dose is reserved for routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks (the dual-dose schedule); sensitising events use 625 IU at 13+ weeks. Would be correct only as 28/34-week routine prophylaxis (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Withhold anti-D as the cervix is closed and bleeding is settling
options.rationale: Withholding because cervix closed is wrong because sensitising events after 12 weeks require anti-D regardless of bleeding pattern or cervical state; foeto-maternal haemorrhage can occur silently. Would never be correct (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Withhold anti-D as she received it last year and the half-life provides ongoing protection
options.rationale: Withholding because of last year's dose is wrong because anti-D protection lasts approximately 12 weeks; protection from a year ago has long since lapsed. Would never be correct (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

explanation.summary: This Rh-negative patient with first-trimester bleeding should receive anti-D prophylaxis now. The presence of a closed cervix or prior live intrauterine pregnancy does not remove the need for sensitisation prevention. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance; KEMH anti-D guideline.
key_points: Bleeding in an Rh-negative pregnant patient is an indication to consider anti-D prophylaxis. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance; KEMH anti-D guideline.
A confirmed intrauterine pregnancy does not change the Rh prophylaxis issue. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance.
Methotrexate is only relevant when treating ectopic pregnancy, not a confirmed IUP. Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy guidance.
explanation.pearls: In early pregnancy bleeding, one of the most important management questions is not just viability, but whether Rh sensitisation needs prevention. Source: RANZCOG anti-D guidance.

stem: A 39-year-old multiparous woman with gestational diabetes managed on insulin is in the second stage of labour at 40 weeks gestation. Estimated foetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.4 kg. She has been pushing effectively for 35 minutes and the foetal head delivers spontaneously. Immediately afterwards the head retracts tightly against the perineum and fails to restitute. Gentle downward traction does not deliver the shoulders.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 126/78 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, Temp 36.9 C
data_table.Examination: Turtle sign present, anterior shoulder impacted behind pubic symphysis
data_table.Investigations: CTG prior to delivery showed intermittent variable decelerations
data_table.Medications: Insulin therapy during pregnancy, epidural analgesia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Apply stronger downward traction because urgent delivery is required to reduce hypoxic injury risk
options.rationale: Stronger downward traction is wrong because excessive traction is the principal cause of brachial plexus injury in shoulder dystocia; the anti-pattern is overpowering rather than disimpacting. Would never be correct (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure while avoiding fundal pressure
options.rationale: McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure is recommended first-line management for shoulder dystocia and may resolve many cases without further intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to increase expulsive force and facilitate anterior shoulder delivery
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is wrong because fundal pressure worsens impaction by driving the anterior shoulder onto the symphysis and risks uterine rupture and worsened brachial plexus injury; suprapubic pressure (downward and lateral) is the correct adjunct. Would never be correct (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).

options.text: Perform immediate episiotomy before attempting any positional or internal manoeuvres
options.rationale: Immediate episiotomy is wrong because shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction (anterior shoulder behind pubic symphysis); episiotomy facilitates internal manoeuvres but does not itself relieve impaction. Would be correct only as adjunct to internal manoeuvres, not as first step (RCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline).

options.text: Proceed directly to posterior arm delivery because foetal bradycardia confirms failed basic manoeuvres
options.rationale: Direct posterior arm delivery is wrong because basic manoeuvres (McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure) resolve most shoulder dystocias and should be tried first; foetal bradycardia is an expected feature of shoulder dystocia, not a marker of failed first-line manoeuvres. Would be correct only after first-line manoeuvres fail (RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia).

explanation.summary: This is classic shoulder dystocia with the turtle sign after delivery of the head. The key reasoning point is prioritising evidence-based manoeuvres rather than panic-driven traction or fundal pressure. McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure is first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)
key_points: Fundal pressure should not be used in shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)
McRoberts manoeuvre changes pelvic geometry and can resolve many cases rapidly. (Source: RCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)
explanation.pearls: An episiotomy may create space for internal manoeuvres but does not itself relieve the impacted shoulder. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe lower abdominal pain and collapse at home. She is approximately 8 weeks pregnant based on her last menstrual period and had attended a GP 3 days earlier for light vaginal spotting. At that time her serum beta-hCG was 2100 IU/L and transvaginal ultrasound showed no intrauterine pregnancy but a possible right adnexal lesion. She was scheduled for repeat testing today. Instead, she developed sudden right-sided abdominal pain radiating to the shoulder tip followed by dizziness and near-syncope. On arrival she is pale, diaphoretic, and intermittently confused. Abdominal examination reveals marked lower abdominal tenderness with guarding. A bedside FAST scan demonstrates free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison pouch.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 72/40 mmHg, HR 142 bpm, RR 30/min, Temp 36.2 C, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Diffuse abdominal tenderness with guarding, cervical motion tenderness, pallor
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L, beta-hCG pending, FAST positive for intraperitoneal fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Administer methotrexate because haemodynamic instability does not alter management when beta-hCG is low
options.rationale: Methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and suspected rupture; the criterion is stable patient with unruptured ectopic, low/declining hCG. Would be correct only in stable unruptured ectopic with appropriate hCG (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound because FAST scanning may overestimate physiologic pelvic free fluid
options.rationale: Repeat TVUS is wrong because the patient is in haemorrhagic shock with clinical evidence of rupture; delaying for imaging would be fatal. Would be correct only in haemodynamically stable women with unclear initial scan (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Commence urgent resuscitation and proceed to emergency surgical management
options.rationale: This presentation is classic for ruptured ectopic pregnancy causing haemorrhagic shock. Immediate resuscitation and operative intervention are required. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Observe after fluid resuscitation because spontaneous tubal abortion may stabilise without intervention
options.rationale: Observation after fluids is wrong because intraperitoneal bleeding plus haemodynamic instability defines ongoing rupture; spontaneous resolution is not a viable strategy when the patient is shocked. Would never be correct in shock (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Arrange urgent CT abdomen because appendicitis remains an important differential diagnosis
options.rationale: CT for appendicitis is wrong because the clinical picture (positive pregnancy test, shock, intraperitoneal free fluid) overwhelmingly indicates ruptured ectopic; CT delays surgery and exposes a potentially viable pregnancy elsewhere to radiation. Would be correct only if pregnancy is excluded and appendicitis is the leading differential (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

explanation.summary: This patient has ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemorrhagic shock. The key reasoning challenge is recognising that prior low beta-hCG values do not reduce urgency once rupture and instability occur. Immediate operative management is required. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
key_points: Methotrexate is only suitable in stable selected patients. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)
Shoulder tip pain and free intraperitoneal fluid suggest diaphragmatic irritation from haemoperitoneum. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Ectopic rupture can occur at relatively low beta-hCG levels. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)

stem: A 31-year-old multiparous woman undergoes spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy infant at 39 weeks gestation after induction for gestational diabetes. The placenta delivers intact 10 minutes later. Shortly afterwards she develops ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding with large clots. Uterine massage is attempted while additional staff are called. The registrar notes the uterus feels enlarged and boggy above the umbilicus. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on initial inspection.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/56 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 24/min, Temp 36.7 C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Boggy enlarged uterus, ongoing brisk vaginal bleeding, pallor
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1400 mL so far

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.text: Perform immediate hysterectomy because severe postpartum bleeding rarely responds to medical management
options.rationale: Immediate hysterectomy is wrong because uterine atony usually responds to medical management (oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost, misoprostol, tranexamic acid) and tamponade (balloon, B-Lynch); the anti-pattern is skipping reversible interventions. Would be correct only with refractory bleeding after all conservative measures fail (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Commence uterotonic therapy while continuing uterine massage and haemodynamic resuscitation
options.rationale: The boggy enlarged uterus strongly suggests uterine atony, which requires prompt uterotonics, massage, and resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Observe for spontaneous uterine contraction because the patient remains conscious and responsive
options.rationale: Observe for spontaneous contraction is wrong because ongoing heavy bleeding with haemodynamic compromise demands active resuscitation and uterotonics; consciousness does not equal stability. Would never be correct in active PPH (RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Arrange urgent CT angiography because retained products are the most likely cause of bleeding
options.rationale: CT angiography for retained products is wrong because the immediate postpartum boggy uterus is atony until proven otherwise; imaging delays critical intervention. Would be correct only after atony is treated and retained products remain suspected (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Prioritise broad-spectrum antibiotics because postpartum endometritis commonly causes major haemorrhage
options.rationale: Antibiotics for endometritis is wrong because endometritis typically presents days postpartum with fever and pelvic pain, not immediate massive haemorrhage from a boggy uterus; the anti-pattern is treating PPH as a sepsis problem. Would never be correct in primary PPH (RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: This patient has major postpartum haemorrhage due to uterine atony, evidenced by a boggy enlarged uterus and heavy bleeding after placental delivery. Immediate uterotonics, uterine massage, and haemodynamic resuscitation are essential first-line interventions. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)
key_points: Uterine atony is the most common cause of postpartum haemorrhage. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Management requires simultaneous haemorrhage control and resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)
explanation.pearls: A patient may initially appear conversational despite life-threatening postpartum haemorrhage because young healthy patients compensate physiologically early. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour has SROM during VE (cervix 6 cm) and the midwife palpates a pulsatile cord at the introitus. CTG shows sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm despite left lateral position. The on-call obstetric registrar and anaesthetist are 5 minutes away, theatre is being prepared and an emergency category-1 caesarean section has been called. The woman is in a single delivery room (no nearby toilets, no easy access to Foley-bag elevation kit). Maternal vital signs are stable.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 102 bpm, BP 125/80 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Visible umbilical cord at introitus, pulsatile, no presenting part visible, cervix fully assessed at 6 cm, station -2
data_table.Investigations: CTG: sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm for 90 seconds, no recovery with position change
data_table.Medications: Terbutaline 250 mcg SC administered 2 minutes ago

lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate immediate action while awaiting theatre?

options.text: Place the woman in knee-chest position, elevate the presenting part manually with a vaginal hand, and instil 500-700 mL normal saline into the bladder via Foley catheter to maintain elevation; administer tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC)
options.rationale: Cord prolapse management is: lift presenting part off cord (manual elevation +/- bladder filling, knee-chest or Trendelenburg), tocolysis if contractions, minimise cord handling, immediate category-1 caesarean. Bladder filling allows safe transfer to theatre (RANZCOG cord prolapse).

options.text: Instruct active maternal pushing to expedite vaginal delivery before theatre arrives
options.rationale: Active pushing is wrong because at 6 cm dilation with foetal bradycardia and prolapsed cord, vaginal delivery is impossible; pushing accelerates cord compression and worsens hypoxia. Would never be correct in cord prolapse with incomplete dilation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Manually replace the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
options.rationale: Manual cord replacement is wrong because manipulation triggers umbilical vessel spasm and arterial vasoconstriction; the cord is left in situ while the presenting part is elevated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat digital VE every 5 minutes to assess for cervical progress before theatre
options.rationale: Repeat digital VE is wrong because repeated examinations stimulate the cord, worsen vasospasm and delay theatre transfer; the focus is presenting-part elevation, not progress assessment. Would never be correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

options.text: Wrap the cord in warm saline-soaked gauze outside the vagina and await spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: Wrap cord and await resolution is wrong because warm saline gauze can be applied if cord is exposed externally, but spontaneous resolution is not a viable strategy; immediate operative delivery is mandatory.
