Would be correct only as adjunct alongside immediate caesarean transfer (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: This woman has a cord prolapse following spontaneous rupture of membranes at 6 cm dilation with a high presenting part (station -2). The sustained foetal bradycardia indicates cord compression. While awaiting emergency caesarean section, the priority is continuous manual elevation of the presenting part to decompress the cord, combined with bladder filling to maintain that elevation. Tocolysis has already been given to reduce uterine activity.
key_points: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring continuous elevation of the presenting part off the cord until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Bladder filling with 500–700 mL normal saline via catheter is an effective adjunct to manual elevation while preparing for theatre. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines: Cord Prolapse)
Digital examination or manipulation of the cord should be avoided as it can provoke vasospasm and worsen foetal hypoxia. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Decision-to-delivery interval target is <30 minutes when foetal compromise is present, but cord decompression measures must continue throughout. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
explanation.pearls: The hand on the presenting part stays in the vagina until the surgeon is ready to make the uterine incision; do not remove it for transfer, catheterisation, or any other intervention.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 weeks presents in spontaneous labour at 4 cm dilated. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her GBS status is unknown - the screening swab at 36 weeks was not performed because she was travelling interstate. Her previous pregnancy 4 years ago was uncomplicated, and she delivered vaginally at term. Her first infant did not develop GBS disease. On direct questioning, she reports no symptoms of a urinary tract infection during this pregnancy and no history of a positive GBS swab. Her temperature is 36.7°C, and observations are normal. The membranes are intact at present. The CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm and normal variability. The woman has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7°C, HR 80 bpm, BP 115/72 mmHg, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, membranes intact, cephalic presentation, station -1
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 140 bpm, normal variability, accelerations present; GBS swab at 36 weeks: NOT DONE
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis plan?

options.text: No intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is required; treat only if the infant develops clinical signs of sepsis
options.rationale: No IAP, treat infant if symptomatic is wrong because the purpose of IAP is preventing early-onset GBS disease before clinical signs develop; waiting for infant signs misses the prevention window. Would be correct only with very low risk and confirmed negative GBS (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose, then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery, commencing immediately as she is in labour with unknown GBS status
options.rationale: IAP for all unknown-status labour is wrong because IAP for unknown status is reserved for those with risk factors (preterm labour <37, ROM >=18h, temp >=38, prior GBS infant, GBS bacteriuria); this woman currently has none. Would be correct only if risk factors emerged (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Take a low vaginal swab for GBS culture and PCR now, and commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose only if the swab returns positive or membranes rupture
options.rationale: Swab in labour and treat if positive is wrong because culture results will not be available in time to guide intrapartum decision-making; the anti-pattern is using a too-slow test as a decision tool. Would be correct only as preconception or antenatal screening, not as intrapartum decision-making (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Monitor for the development of clinical risk factors - if membranes rupture for ≥18 hours or maternal temperature reaches ≥38.0°C, commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly
options.rationale: For women with unknown GBS status at term, IAP is indicated ONLY if clinical risk factors develop: ROM ≥18 hours, maternal temperature ≥38.0°C, preterm labour <37 weeks, GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy, or previous infant with GBS disease. This woman currently has none of these, so the plan is expectant management with monitoring for risk factors. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy; KEMH: GBS Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g IV loading dose then 1 g IV 8-hourly as the preferred antibiotic for unknown GBS status with no risk factors
options.rationale: Cefazolin for unknown status with no risk factors is wrong because cefazolin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy with confirmed indication for IAP; she has no allergies and no current indication for IAP. Would be correct only with non-severe penicillin allergy and an indication for IAP (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

explanation.summary: This term woman with unknown GBS status and no clinical risk factors does not require immediate IAP. The standard Australian approach is to monitor for the development of risk factors during labour: ROM ≥18 hours, intrapartum fever ≥38.0°C, or preterm birth. If none develop, IAP is not indicated. This differs from some international approaches that recommend universal IAP for unknown GBS status, but Australian guidance uses a risk-factor-based approach for women whose screening status is unknown.
key_points: IAP is indicated for known GBS-positive women AND for unknown-GBS women with clinical risk factors: ROM ≥18 hrs, temp ≥38.0°C, <37 wks, GBS bacteriuria, previous infant with GBS disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
For unknown GBS status at term with NO risk factors, IAP is not required. (Source: KEMH: GBS Guideline)
The standard IAP regimen is benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
Clindamycin is used for women with severe penicillin allergy only if GBS sensitivities are known; vancomycin is used if sensitivities are unknown. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: IAP is considered adequate if the loading dose is given at least 4 hours before delivery. Document the time of first dose clearly to guide neonatal management.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 41+3 weeks gestation presents for a scheduled induction of labour for post-dates pregnancy. She has been well throughout the pregnancy with normal foetal movements, a reactive CTG, and an amniotic fluid index of 10 cm on yesterday's ultrasound. She has no significant past medical history. On vaginal examination, the cervix is posterior, 2 cm long, firm in consistency, closed at the external os, and the presenting part is at station -3. Her Bishop score is calculated as 2. The CTG on admission is normal with a baseline of 140 bpm and moderate variability. She is counselled about the need for cervical ripening prior to oxytocin induction. She has no contraindications to prostaglandin use (no previous uterine surgery, no severe asthma, no known allergy).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Cervix posterior, 2 cm long, firm, closed, station -3; Bishop score = 2
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 140 bpm, moderate variability, reactive; AFI 10 cm; no contraindications to prostaglandins

lead_in: What is the most appropriate cervical ripening agent and initial dose?

options.text: Dinoprostone (PGE2) 3 mg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
options.rationale: Dinoprostone 3 mg tablet (Prostin E2) is wrong because in Australian practice the standard first-line agent for primigravidae with unfavourable cervix is dinoprostone 2 mg gel 6-hourly (max 2 doses); the tablet regimen has different dosing and is not the standard first-line. Would be correct only with the appropriate tablet schedule and where tablets are local practice (KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline).

options.text: Dinoprostone (PGE2) 2 mg vaginal gel inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
options.rationale: Dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel (Prostin E2) is the standard cervical ripening agent for primigravidae with an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score ≤6) in Australian practice. It is administered into the posterior fornix and can be repeated after 6 hours if the Bishop score remains unfavourable, to a maximum of two doses (4 mg total) in 24 hours. A CTG is performed after administration and prior to each repeat dose. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Induction of Labour)

options.text: Misoprostol (PGE1) 25 mcg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 4 hours if needed
options.rationale: Vaginal misoprostol 25 mcg is wrong because Australian first-line for unfavourable cervix in low-risk primigravidae is dinoprostone gel; misoprostol may be used in specific settings (PPROM, intrauterine death) but is not the established first-line agent. Would be correct only in those specific settings or where local protocols specify misoprostol (KEMH).

options.text: Foley catheter balloon inserted through the cervix and inflated with 30 mL sterile water, left in situ for 12 hours
options.rationale: Foley catheter is wrong because mechanical ripening is reserved for women with contraindications to prostaglandins (prior caesarean, severe asthma); this low-risk primigravida is suitable for prostaglandin. Would be correct only when prostaglandins are contraindicated (KEMH).

options.text: Oxytocin IV infusion starting at 2 mU/min and titrated every 30 minutes according to contractions without prior cervical ripening
options.rationale: Oxytocin without ripening is wrong because oxytocin is less effective with unfavourable cervix (Bishop <=6); skipping ripening increases failed induction and caesarean rates. Would be correct only with favourable cervix (Bishop >=7) (RANZCOG: Induction of Labour Guideline).

explanation.summary: This primigravida at 41+3 weeks has an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score 2) and requires cervical ripening before oxytocin. The standard of care in Australia is dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed (maximum two doses in 24 hours). CTG monitoring after administration is standard. This is safe in the absence of contraindications to prostaglandins (no previous uterine scar, no severe asthma).
key_points: A Bishop score ≤6 indicates an unfavourable cervix requiring cervical ripening prior to oxytocin induction. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of Labour Guideline)

Dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel (Prostin E2) is the first-line pharmacological agent for cervical ripening in Australian practice for low-risk nulliparous women. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)

The maximum total dose is 4 mg (two doses of 2 mg gel) in 24 hours. CTG monitoring 30-60 minutes after administration and prior to each repeat dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Induction of Labour)
Mechanical methods (Foley catheter) are preferred when prostaglandins are contraindicated (e.g., previous caesarean section, severe asthma). (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Bishop score components: Cervical Dilation (0-3), Effacement/Length (0-3), Station (-3 to +2 mapped 0-3), Consistency (firm/medium/soft 0-2), Position (posterior/mid/anterior 0-2). A score of 2 means you have a long, firm, closed, posterior cervix - the least favourable.

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 at 29+0 weeks gestation was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.5 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L). Her BMI is 32 kg/m². She has been managed with lifestyle modification (diet and exercise) for 3 weeks. Her fasting BGLs have consistently been 5.0-5.3 mmol/L (target <5.1 mmol/L) and her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are 6.2-6.6 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L). An ultrasound at 28 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She asks about medication options to improve her fasting glucose. She has no contraindications to metformin or insulin. She is concerned about the risk of needing insulin and the need for injections.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, fundal height 30 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal examination; no signs of macrosomia clinically
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.4, 1h 10.5, 2h 8.9 (all diagnostic); fasting BGLs 5.0-5.3 (target <5.1), 2h postprandial 6.2-6.6 (target <6.7); EFW 78th centile, AFI normal
data_table.Medications: Nil pharmacological; lifestyle measures for 3 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing her fasting hyperglycaemia?

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg orally at night, titrate up to 1500 mg daily as tolerated, and continue diet and exercise measures
options.rationale: This woman has fasting BGLs consistently above the ADIPS target of <5.1 mmol/L despite 3 weeks of lifestyle modification. Metformin is an appropriate first-line pharmacological agent in Australian practice for GDM when lifestyle measures are insufficient. It is particularly effective for fasting hyperglycaemia by reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis. It is initiated at 500 mg nocte and titrated up. Insulin remains an option if metformin is not tolerated or fails to achieve targets. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline; RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)

options.text: Commence basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at 0.1 units/kg at night, titrated according to fasting BGL, and cease metformin
options.rationale: Basal insulin first-line is wrong because ADIPS supports metformin as first-line oral pharmacotherapy when lifestyle fails, before moving to insulin; insulin is second-line or used alongside metformin if targets are not met. Would be correct only if metformin were contraindicated or refused (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures alone for a further 2 weeks, as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target and postprandial targets are met
options.rationale: Continue lifestyle 2 more weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends pharmacotherapy if targets are not achieved within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle; she has had elevated fasting BGLs for 3 weeks. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Commence glibenclamide (glyburide) 2.5 mg orally at night, titrating to a maximum of 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Glibenclamide is wrong because it is not recommended first-line in Australia: it crosses the placenta and is associated with higher rates of macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycaemia compared to metformin or insulin. Would be correct only in very limited resource-poor settings, not Australian practice (ADIPS: GDM Guideline; RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence both metformin 500 mg BD and basal insulin 0.2 units/kg at night simultaneously to rapidly achieve glycaemic control
options.rationale: Metformin plus insulin simultaneously is wrong because dual therapy as initial pharmacological step is not indicated; metformin alone should be trialled first with insulin added only if targets remain unmet after dose escalation. Would be correct only with severe baseline derangement requiring rapid control (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman has GDM diagnosed on all three OGTT time points and has fasting hyperglycaemia (target <5.1 mmol/L, her values 5.0-5.3) persisting after 3 weeks of lifestyle modification. Postprandial targets are achieved. The next step is to add metformin, which is the recommended first-line pharmacological agent in Australian guidelines for GDM. Metformin is effective for fasting hyperglycaemia, is well-tolerated, and has a good safety profile. Insulin remains the second-line option if metformin fails.
key_points: ADIPS GDM glycaemic targets: fasting <5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial <7.8 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial <6.7 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
Pharmacological therapy is indicated if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle modification. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)

Metformin is the first-line pharmacological agent for GDM in Australia, particularly effective for fasting hyperglycaemia. Start 500 mg nocte, titrate to maximum 2 g daily. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)
Glibenclamide is not recommended as first-line due to higher rates of adverse neonatal outcomes; insulin is second-line after metformin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Metformin crosses the placenta but extensive data show no increase in congenital anomalies or short-term adverse neonatal outcomes. It is category C in Australia but widely used and recommended by ADIPS for GDM.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida who delivered by emergency caesarean section for failure to progress 3 days ago presents with fever, abdominal pain, and offensive lochia. The caesarean was performed after 18 hours of labour with ruptured membranes for 14 hours. She had three vaginal examinations during labour. Her temperature is 38.8°C, HR 104 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. The abdomen is tender in the lower segment with guarding, and the uterine fundus is tender and boggy 2 cm above the umbilicus. The lochia is heavy, purulent, and malodorous. The caesarean wound is clean and intact with no erythema. She is breastfeeding. She has no known drug allergies. Her full blood count shows WCC 18.5 x 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, Hb 95 g/L (down from 112 g/L antenatally). Blood cultures are pending.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.8°C, HR 104 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Lower abdominal tenderness with guarding, fundal tenderness, heavy purulent malodorous lochia, wound clean
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.5 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 14.2, Hb 95 g/L (from 112), blood cultures pending
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric intravenous antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Amoxicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly PLUS metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly PLUS gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily dosing
options.rationale: Amoxicillin/metronidazole/gentamicin triple therapy is wrong because the Australian first-line for postpartum endometritis is clindamycin plus gentamicin; triple therapy is reserved for severe intra-abdominal sepsis or specific concerns. Would be correct only with severe sepsis or polymicrobial intra-abdominal source (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections; KEMH).

options.text: Clindamycin 900 mg IV 8-hourly PLUS gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily dosing (with subsequent dosing based on serum levels and renal function)
options.rationale: The standard empiric regimen for postpartum endometritis in Australian practice is clindamycin (providing cover for anaerobic bacteria, including Bacteroides species, and gram-positive cocci) plus gentamicin (providing gram-negative cover). This combination is recommended by Therapeutic Guidelines and KEMH and has excellent efficacy. Gentamicin dosing should be weight-based with monitoring for nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections; KEMH: Postpartum Endometritis Guideline)

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily PLUS metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone plus metronidazole is wrong because it lacks adequate gram-positive coverage (streptococci, enterococci) needed for pelvic infection; clindamycin gives the gram-positive and anaerobic coverage. Would be correct only in selected pyelonephritis or when clindamycin contraindicated (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV 6-hourly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy is wrong because while broad-spectrum, it is reserved for severe sepsis or suspected resistant organisms; standard postpartum endometritis is treated with clindamycin plus gentamicin. Would be correct only with severe sepsis or specific resistance concerns (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g IV 8-hourly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate monotherapy is wrong because anaerobic coverage may be inadequate for severe pelvic infections and it is not the Australian guideline regimen. Would be correct only as oral step-down in mild infection, not as IV first-line (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections).

explanation.summary: This woman has postpartum endometritis (fever, uterine tenderness, purulent lochia, leukocytosis) following emergency caesarean with prolonged rupture of membranes and multiple vaginal examinations - all classic risk factors. The standard empiric IV antibiotic regimen in Australia is clindamycin 900 mg IV 8-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily (adjusted for renal function). This combination provides broad-spectrum cover for the polymicrobial aetiology (gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes) that characterises pelvic puerperal infections.
key_points: Postpartum endometritis is a polymicrobial infection involving aerobes and anaerobes from the lower genital tract. Risk factors: caesarean delivery (especially emergency), prolonged ROM, multiple vaginal examinations. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Endometritis Guideline)

The recommended empiric IV antibiotic regimen is clindamycin 900 mg IV 8-hourly PLUS gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily, with monitoring of gentamicin levels and renal function. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections)

If endometritis does not respond to clindamycin/gentamicin within 48-72 hours, consider adding ampicillin for enterococcal cover or investigating for retained products of conception. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Endometritis Guideline)
Breastfeeding is compatible with both clindamycin and gentamicin; monitor the infant for diarrhoea with clindamycin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections)
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin is renally excreted and requires dose adjustment for renal impairment. Always check a pre-dose gentamicin level before the second dose (target <1 mg/L) and monitor creatinine. The 5 mg/kg dose is for standard once-daily dosing in patients with normal renal function.

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents for her routine cervical screening test as part of the National Cervical Screening Program. She has no symptoms and no previous abnormal results. Her screening test was performed as an HPV test with partial genotyping. The result shows oncogenic HPV type 16 detected, with reflex liquid-based cytology reported as possible low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pLSIL). She has never had a colposcopy before. She is nulliparous and has no significant past medical history. She is a non-smoker. She uses a levonorgestrel intrauterine device for contraception.

data_table.Vital signs: Not applicable
data_table.Examination: Cervix appears normal on speculum examination at time of screening
data_table.Investigations: HPV test: oncogenic HPV 16 detected; reflex LBC: possible LSIL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step according to the NCSP guidelines?

options.text: Refer for colposcopy within 2-4 weeks for assessment, as the presence of HPV 16 mandates direct colposcopic evaluation regardless of cytology
options.rationale: Under the 2017 NCSP guidelines (and the 2022 update), women with a positive oncogenic HPV test and reflex LBC showing LSIL or HSIL should be referred for colposcopy. HPV 16/18 positivity with any cytology abnormality (including pLSIL or LSIL) mandates colposcopy. The presence of HPV 16 is a high-risk finding and requires direct visualisation and possible biopsy, even with low-grade cytology. (Source: NCSP: Guidelines for the Management of Screen-detected Abnormalities; Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines

options.text: Repeat the HPV test in 12 months, as the finding of pLSIL in a woman under 30 with HPV 16 can be managed conservatively due to high regression rates
options.rationale: Repeat HPV in 12 months is wrong because HPV 16 positivity with any cytology abnormality (including pLSIL/LSIL) requires direct colposcopy in the NCSP, regardless of age; conservative testing misses high-risk progression. Would be correct only for non-16/18 HPV with negative reflex cytology (NCSP: Management of Screen-detected Abnormalities).

options.text: Perform a repeat liquid-based cytology test in 6 months without HPV testing, as the current result is low-grade and may resolve spontaneously
options.rationale: Repeat cytology in 6 months is wrong because once HPV 16 is detected, repeat cytology alone is insufficient; HPV 16 plus any cytology abnormality triggers colposcopy. Would be correct only when HPV is negative with LSIL cytology (NCSP).

options.text: Perform a colposcopy only if the woman develops symptoms (such as postcoital bleeding or intermenstrual bleeding), as asymptomatic low-grade abnormalities can be observed
options.rationale: Colposcopy only if symptomatic is wrong because NCSP referral criteria are not symptom-dependent; HPV 16 plus pLSIL is an indication regardless of symptoms. Would never be correct (NCSP).

options.text: Treat empirically with large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) without prior colposcopy, as HPV 16 is a high-risk type that warrants definitive treatment
options.rationale: Empirical LLETZ without colposcopy is wrong because LLETZ is treatment for histologically confirmed HSIL/CIN2-3; performing it for pLSIL on cytology alone is overtreatment. Would be correct only after colposcopic biopsy confirms HSIL (NCSP; Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines

explanation.summary: In the Australian NCSP, a positive HPV test with partial genotyping showing HPV 16 (or 18) mandates direct colposcopy irrespective of the reflex cytology result. This woman has HPV 16 and a reflex LBC showing pLSIL. The combination requires colposcopy within 2-4 weeks to assess for any high-grade lesion that might require biopsy or treatment. Conservative management with repeat testing is not appropriate when HPV 16/18 is present.
key_points: NCSP guidelines: HPV 16 or 18 positivity → direct colposcopy regardless of cytology. (Source: NCSP: Management of Screen-detected Abnormalities)

For other oncogenic HPV types (not 16/18), colposcopy is indicated if reflex LBC shows LSIL/HSIL or higher. If LBC negative or pLSIL and no HPV 16/18, repeat HPV test in 12 months. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines)
The NCSP is HPV-based: primary screening with HPV test; cytology is used as reflex triage for HPV-positive results. (Source: NCSP: Information for Health Professionals)
Low-grade cytology (pLSIL/LSIL) in the setting of HPV 16/18 warrants colposcopy, not observation. (Source: NCSP: Management of Screen-detected Abnormalities)
explanation.pearls: HPV 16 and 18 are the 'dangerous two' in the NCSP - they account for ~70% of cervical cancers and always trigger colposcopy if detected on screening, regardless of cytology. All other oncogenic HPV types are managed based on the reflex cytology result.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening Guidelines

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 36+6 weeks gestation is admitted to the antenatal ward with new-onset hypertension. She has been well throughout her pregnancy until 3 days ago when she noted mild ankle swelling and a frontal headache. Her blood pressure on arrival is 155/105 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement 30 minutes later. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein on dipstick with a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 45 mg/mmol (normal <30). She reports no visual disturbances, no epigastric pain, and no nausea. On examination, she has mild pitting ankle oedema, normal reflexes with no clonus, and a soft non-tender abdomen. The foetal CTG is reactive with a baseline of 145 bpm, normal variability, and accelerations. Ultrasound shows an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index and normal umbilical artery Dopplers. Her blood results show haemoglobin 118 g/L, platelets 195 x 10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 28 U/L, and urate 0.36 mmol/L. She has been commenced on oral labetalol 200 mg BD, and her blood pressure has improved to 140/90 mmHg after 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 155/105 (confirmed, now 140/90 on labetalol), HR 82 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Mild pitting ankle oedema, normal reflexes, no clonus, soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height = dates
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118, Plt 195, Cr 62, ALT 28, Urate 0.36; PCR 45 mg/mmol; CTG reactive, baseline 145; USS EFW 55th centile, AFI normal, UA Doppler normal
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO BD (commenced 24 hrs ago); Betamethasone course completed 12 hrs ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.text: Induction of labour within the next 24-48 hours, as delivery is recommended for this hypertensive disorder of pregnancy at or beyond 37+0 weeks
options.rationale: RANZCOG and international guidelines consistently recommend that women with pre-eclampsia at ≥37 weeks gestation should be delivered, as the risks of continuing the pregnancy (progression to severe disease, eclampsia, placental abruption) outweigh the benefits of further foetal maturation. For this woman at 36+6 (effectively term within hours), delivery should be planned within 24-48 hours. The PHOENIX trial demonstrated reduced maternal morbidity with planned early delivery at 37 weeks compared to expectant management. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Continue oral labetalol and expectant management with twice-weekly review until 39+0 weeks, as her blood pressure is now controlled and she has no severe features
options.rationale: Continue oral labetalol to 39 weeks is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term (>=37 weeks) is itself an indication for delivery; the PHOENIX trial showed reduced maternal morbidity with planned 37-week delivery vs expectant management. Would be correct only for gestational hypertension without pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; PHOENIX Trial).

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean section, as the proteinuria with a PCR of 45 mg/mmol and hypertension at 36+6 weeks meets severe-features criteria requiring urgent delivery
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia is wrong because she does not meet severe pre-eclampsia criteria (BP <170/110, platelets normal, creatinine normal, transaminases normal, no symptoms, PCR 45 mg/mmol is significant proteinuria but does not by itself denote severe disease); vaginal delivery via induction is appropriate. Would be correct only with severe features (SOMANZ).

options.text: Discharge home on labetalol 200 mg BD with instructions for daily BP monitoring and twice-weekly CTG and ultrasound, with delivery planned at 40+0 weeks
options.rationale: Discharge with delivery at 40+0 is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term mandates delivery, not outpatient observation; discharging risks deterioration outside monitoring. Would never be correct in pre-eclampsia at term (SOMANZ; Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth).
source_refs: Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth

options.text: Wait until 38+0 weeks to allow full corticosteroid effect, then induce labour if the blood pressure remains controlled on oral labetalol without development of severe features
options.rationale: Wait until 38+0 for full steroid effect is wrong because steroids are not indicated at >=37 weeks (lungs are mature) and waiting prolongs pre-eclampsia unnecessarily. Would be correct only at preterm gestations (SOMANZ; RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman at 36+6 weeks has pre-eclampsia without severe features (BP controlled on oral labetalol, normal bloods, normal foetal assessment). Guidelines consistently recommend delivery at ≥37 weeks for pre-eclampsia, regardless of severity. The PHOENIX trial demonstrated that planned delivery at 37 weeks in women with pre-eclampsia reduces maternal morbidity compared to expectant management, without increasing neonatal morbidity. Her BP is controlled, she has no severe features, and vaginal delivery with induction is appropriate. Corticosteroids are not indicated at term.
key_points: Pre-eclampsia at ≥37 weeks gestation: delivery is recommended within 24-48 hours, regardless of severity. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
The PHOENIX trial showed planned early delivery reduces maternal morbidity in late preterm pre-eclampsia without increasing neonatal morbidity. (Source: PHOENIX Trial)

Severe pre-eclampsia features include: BP ≥170/110, platelets <100, creatinine >90, transaminases >70, epigastric pain, visual disturbance, clonus. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
Antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at ≥37+0 weeks as foetal lung maturity is established. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The cutoff of 37 weeks is critical in pre-eclampsia management: before 37 weeks, expectant management may be appropriate if no severe features; at or after 37 weeks, delivery is recommended irrespective of severity.

sources.label: Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida delivered vaginally 10 days ago following induction of labour at 38 weeks for gestational hypertension. The third stage was managed actively with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU, and the placenta was delivered complete by controlled cord traction. She was discharged home on day 2 post-delivery with no concerns. She now presents to the emergency department with new-onset heavy vaginal bleeding that began 4 hours ago, soaking through 3 maternity pads. She also reports a 2-day history of dull lower abdominal pain, malaise, and a temperature of 38.2°C measured at home. The lochia has been malodorous for 3 days. On examination, she looks pale and unwell. Her HR is 106 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, and temperature is 38.4°C. The uterus is tender, boggy, and palpable 3 cm below the umbilicus. On speculum, there is heavy dark red blood with clots in the vagina, and the os is open with blood and tissue visible. Her haemoglobin is 88 g/L (was 108 g/L antenatally), WCC is 16.2 x 10^9/L, and CRP is 125 mg/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 106 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, Temp 38.4°C, RR 18, SpO2 97%
data_table.Examination: Pale, unwell, uterus tender boggy 3 cm below umbilicus, speculum: heavy dark blood with clots, os open, tissue visible
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L (from 108 antenatal), WCC 16.2 x 10^9/L, CRP 125 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV clindamycin 900 mg 8-hourly and IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily for endometritis, and administer IV oxytocin 40 units infusion for uterine atony
options.rationale: Antibiotics plus oxytocin alone is wrong because the open os with visible tissue strongly indicates retained products of conception; antibiotics and uterotonics without addressing retained tissue do not solve the bleeding source. Would be correct only for endometritis without retained products (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Perform a bimanual examination and attempt digital removal of retained tissue from the uterine cavity under sedation, followed by IV oxytocin and antibiotics
options.rationale: Digital exploration without ultrasound is wrong because blind digital exploration risks uterine perforation, especially in a tender, infected postpartum uterus; confirmation with ultrasound and evacuation under anaesthesia in theatre is safer. Would be correct only in an emergency without ultrasound access (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Perform an urgent pelvic ultrasound to assess for retained products of conception, commence IV antibiotics (clindamycin and gentamicin), administer an IV oxytocin infusion, and arrange for suction curettage in theatre if retained products are confirmed
options.rationale: The clinical picture of secondary PPH at day 10 with fever, malodorous lochia, tender boggy uterus, open os with visible tissue, and a falling haemoglobin is classic for retained products of conception with superimposed endometritis. Management includes: urgent pelvic ultrasound to confirm retained products, commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin for endometritis), administer uterotonics (oxytocin), and arrange suction curettage in theatre. Antibiotics should be started before the procedure. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: Administer IV tranexamic acid 1 g, IV ergometrine 250 mcg, and oral misoprostol 800 mcg, and observe for 4 hours; if bleeding persists, arrange a pelvic ultrasound
options.rationale: TXA, ergometrine, misoprostol then wait 4h is wrong because while these may temporarily reduce bleeding, they do not address retained products; delay risks ongoing haemorrhage and worsening sepsis. Would be correct only as bridging while awaiting theatre (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Commence oral doxycycline 100 mg BD and oral metronidazole 400 mg TDS for endometritis, and arrange a pelvic ultrasound as an outpatient within 48 hours if the bleeding settles
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient ultrasound is wrong because she is systemically unwell (tachycardic, hypotensive, febrile, anaemic) with ongoing haemorrhage; outpatient management is unsafe. Would be correct only in mild secondary endometritis without sepsis or significant bleeding (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies).

explanation.summary: This woman has secondary postpartum haemorrhage occurring 10 days post-delivery, most likely due to retained products of conception with superimposed endometritis. Key features: heavy vaginal bleeding, tender boggy uterus, open os with visible tissue, fever, malodorous lochia, and a significant drop in haemoglobin. The appropriate management is urgent pelvic ultrasound to confirm retained products, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (clindamycin and gentamicin), uterotonics, and suction curettage in theatre. Retained products are the most common cause of secondary PPH, occurring in approximately 1% of deliveries.
key_points: Secondary PPH is defined as excessive vaginal bleeding occurring between 24 hours and 6 weeks postpartum. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
The most common causes are retained products of conception, endometritis, and subinvolution of the placental site. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

Management includes: pelvic ultrasound, IV antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin for endometritis), uterotonics, and suction curettage if retained products are confirmed. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Always send evacuated tissue for histopathology to confirm retained products and exclude gestational trophoblastic disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
explanation.pearls: Secondary PPH with fever and malodorous lochia = retained products with endometritis until proven otherwise. Ultrasound is the key investigation, but surgical evacuation should not be delayed if the clinical picture is convincing and the patient is unstable.

stem: A 38-year-old G2P1 at 30+1 weeks gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 27 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.6 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.8 mmol/L, 2-hour 9.2 mmol/L). She commenced metformin 500 mg BD at diagnosis, titrated to 1 g BD over 2 weeks, and has been on this dose for the past 3 weeks with ongoing dietary modification. Her fasting BGLs have been 5.3-5.8 mmol/L consistently. Her 2-hour postprandial BGLs have been 6.5-7.2 mmol/L. An ultrasound at 29 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 82nd centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She has no contraindications to insulin and has been counselled about the need to escalate therapy. She weighs 78 kg. Her HbA1c is 5.8% (40 mmol/mol).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, fundal height 32 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal examination
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.6, 1h 10.8, 2h 9.2 (all diagnostic); fasting BGLs 5.3-5.8 (target <5.1); 2h postprandial 6.5-7.2 (target <6.7); EFW 82nd centile; HbA1c 5.8%
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g PO BD (max tolerated dose)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her glycaemic management?

options.text: Continue metformin 1 g BD and add basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at night, starting at 0.1-0.2 units/kg, titrated to achieve fasting BGL below 5.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: ADIPS guidelines recommend that if glycaemic targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle modification and metformin therapy, insulin should be commenced. This woman has persistently elevated fasting BGLs (5.3-5.8 mmol/L) despite maximum metformin for 3 weeks, and elevated postprandial values. Basal insulin at night is the appropriate escalation, targeting fasting hyperglycaemia first. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline; RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)

options.text: Cease metformin and commence basal-bolus insulin regimen with isophane at night and rapid-acting insulin analogue before each meal to target both fasting and postprandial hyperglycaemia simultaneously
options.rationale: Cease metformin and start basal-bolus is wrong because basal insulin alone is appropriate when fasting hyperglycaemia is the primary problem; basal-bolus is for failed postprandial control on optimised basal. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin to reduce insulin requirements. Would be correct only with severe postprandial spikes despite optimised basal (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Increase metformin to 1.5 g BD (total 3 g daily) and add glibenclamide 2.5 mg at night for an additional 2 weeks before considering insulin
options.rationale: Increase metformin and add glibenclamide is wrong because she is at maximum metformin (2 g/day) and glibenclamide is not recommended in Australia (higher neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia); escalation should be to insulin. Would be correct only outside Australian guideline practice (ADIPS).

options.text: Continue current metformin dose and dietary measures for a further 2 weeks as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target, then reassess with repeat HbA1c
options.rationale: Continue current regimen and reassess in 2 weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends escalation if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of maximal oral therapy; fasting BGLs have been above target for 3 weeks with EFW already on the 82nd centile, raising macrosomia risk. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS).

options.text: Commence rapid-acting insulin analogue before breakfast only, as her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are above target while fasting values are approaching acceptable levels
options.rationale: Prebreakfast bolus only is wrong because her fasting BGLs (5.3-5.8) are consistently above the 5.1 target, driving macrosomia; fasting hyperglycaemia should be tackled with basal insulin first. Would be correct only if fasting were at target and postprandial were the primary issue (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: This woman has GDM with persistent fasting hyperglycaemia (target <5.1, hers 5.3-5.8) and elevated postprandial BGLs (target <6.7, hers 6.5-7.2) despite 3 weeks of maximum metformin therapy. The foetal growth is already on the 82nd centile. ADIPS guidelines recommend escalating to insulin when targets are not achieved on oral therapy. Basal insulin (isophane or detemir) at night is the appropriate first step, targeting fasting hyperglycaemia. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin to maintain its insulin-sensitising benefits.
key_points: ADIPS GDM glycaemic targets: fasting <5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial <7.8 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial <6.7 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
If targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of metformin therapy, insulin should be commenced. Metformin should be continued. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
Basal insulin (isophane or detemir) starting at 0.1-0.2 units/kg at night is the appropriate first-line insulin regimen for predominant fasting hyperglycaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)
Glibenclamide is not recommended in Australian practice due to higher rates of adverse neonatal outcomes compared to insulin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM is a stronger predictor of macrosomia than postprandial hyperglycaemia. When fasting BGLs are above target on maximal metformin, do not delay insulin initiation - the foetus is already being exposed to hyperglycaemia.

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 37+1 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal appointment. On abdominal palpation, the foetus is found to be in a breech presentation with the foetal head ballotable at the fundus. A bedside ultrasound confirms a frank breech presentation with the foetal legs extended, a flexed foetal head, normal amniotic fluid index, and a posterior placenta not covering the os. The estimated foetal weight is on the 55th centile. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history. She has no contraindications to external cephalic version (ECV) and is keen to avoid a caesarean section if possible. She has read about breech vaginal delivery and asks whether this is a safe option for her.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, fundal height 37 cm
data_table.Examination: Foetus in frank breech on palpation; bedside USS confirms frank breech, flexed head, AFI normal, placenta posterior not covering os, EFW 55th centile
data_table.Investigations: USS: frank breech, flexed head, normal AFI, posterior placenta, EFW 55th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management recommendation?

options.text: Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful or declined, discuss planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks as the safest mode of delivery for a term breech
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends that women with a breech presentation at or near term should be informed about external cephalic version (ECV) and offered it if clinically appropriate. If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, planned caesarean section is recommended based on the Term Breech Trial which demonstrated reduced perinatal mortality and morbidity with planned caesarean compared to planned vaginal breech delivery. Vaginal breech delivery may be considered in carefully selected cases with an experienced clinician, but the default recommendation is caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful, plan a vaginal breech delivery as this primigravida has a frank breech with flexed head and normal foetal weight on the 55th centile, which are favourable factors
options.rationale: Plan vaginal breech after failed ECV is wrong because the Term Breech Trial showed reduced perinatal morbidity and mortality with planned caesarean vs planned vaginal breech delivery; vaginal breech remains a possibility only with rigorous selection and experienced operator, and primigravidity is a relative risk factor. Would be correct only in carefully selected cases with experienced clinician after counselling (RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Proceed directly to booking an elective caesarean section at 39+0 weeks without offering ECV, as primigravidae have a lower ECV success rate and ECV carries risks of foetal distress and placental abruption
options.rationale: Skip ECV entirely is wrong because RANZCOG recommends offering ECV to all women with breech at term in the absence of contraindications; ECV is worthwhile (35-40% success in primigravidae) and risks are low (foetal HR changes 5%, abruption <1%, emergency CS <0.5%). Would be correct only with contraindications to ECV (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Perform external cephalic version immediately at this visit without tocolysis, as she is 37 weeks, and if successful, discharge home to await spontaneous labour
options.rationale: Immediate ECV at clinic without tocolysis is wrong because ECV should be performed in a setting with immediate caesarean access, tocolysis (e.g. terbutaline 250 mcg SC), informed consent and appropriate facilities; clinic ECV without tocolysis lowers success and safety. Would never be correct in this setting (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Advise the woman that breech presentation at 37 weeks has a high chance of spontaneous version before term, and arrange a repeat ultrasound at 39 weeks to reassess presentation before making a delivery plan
options.rationale: Wait until 39 weeks for reassessment is wrong because spontaneous version after 37 weeks occurs in only ~8% of breech; waiting misses the optimal ECV window and risks labour with undiagnosed plan. Would be correct only when ECV has already failed and a delivery plan is in place (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

explanation.summary: This primigravida at 37 weeks with a frank breech presentation should be offered ECV, which has a success rate of approximately 35-40% in nulliparous women. If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, a planned caesarean section is the recommended mode of delivery based on evidence from the Term Breech Trial. Vaginal breech delivery may be an option in carefully selected cases with an experienced clinician, but the default recommendation is caesarean. ECV should be performed with tocolysis in a setting with immediate access to caesarean section.
key_points: RANZCOG recommends that all women with breech presentation at term should be offered ECV in the absence of contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)

ECV success rates: approximately 35-40% in nulliparous women, 60-65% in multiparous women. Tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC) improves success. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, planned caesarean section is recommended based on the Term Breech Trial (reduced perinatal mortality and morbidity). (Source: Term Breech Trial)

Contraindications to ECV: placenta praevia, abruption, non-reassuring foetal status, ruptured membranes, multiple pregnancy, uterine anomaly, hyperextended foetal head. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
explanation.pearls: The window for ECV is 37-40 weeks: too early and the foetus may spontaneously revert, too late and the foetus may be too large to turn, and spontaneous labour may intervene.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 33+5 weeks presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound at 32 weeks showed an anterior placenta completely covering the internal os (major praevia). She is haemodynamically stable, bleeding has settled, CTG reassuring, cervical exam deferred. She has had no previous corticosteroid courses this pregnancy. The plan is admission for monitoring and elective caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. She is keen on whatever course will best protect her baby's lung function.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No active bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, CTG reactive
data_table.Investigations: Known major placenta praevia on previous USS

lead_in: What is the most appropriate corticosteroid plan?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, now
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for women at risk of preterm birth before 34+6 weeks. At 33+5 with active bleeding from major praevia and likely early delivery, a single course is indicated (RANZCOG; RCOG). 'Late preterm' (34+0 to 36+6) ACS is selective rather than universal.

options.text: Administer no corticosteroids as she is now beyond 34 weeks
options.rationale: Withhold steroids because beyond 34 weeks is wrong because she is 33+5 (below 34+0), inside the standard window; the misclassification skips clear benefit. Would be correct only at >=34+6 with no late-preterm criteria (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM
options.rationale: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; single doses have reduced benefit. Would be correct only when delivery is so imminent that the second dose cannot be given (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer weekly betamethasone 11.4 mg IM until delivery
options.rationale: Weekly repeats until delivery is wrong because routine weekly repeats are not recommended due to reduced birth weight and head circumference; only a single rescue dose is considered when delivery is anticipated within 7 days and the first course was >=7 days ago. Would be correct only as a single rescue dose, not weekly (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM, four doses 12-hourly, in place of betamethasone, on the basis of superior neurodevelopmental outcomes
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 4-dose regimen is wrong because Australian practice predominantly uses betamethasone; while dexamethasone is an equivalent alternative, the question is whether to give ACS not which agent, and the option implies a regimen change without grounds. Would be correct only when betamethasone is unavailable (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation are only recommended for women at risk of preterm birth between 23+0 and 34+6 weeks. This woman is 36+0 weeks, and the bleeding is settled; delivery may be planned electively at 37-38 weeks, but steroids have no role at this gestation.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids should be given between 23+0 and 34+6 weeks when preterm delivery is anticipated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
After 34+6 weeks, the foetal lungs are sufficiently mature, and steroids are not beneficial. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
In placenta praevia with settled bleeding, delivery is typically planned for 37-38 weeks without need for steroids. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The magic number for steroids is 34+6 – after that, no corticosteroids, no matter how imminent delivery may seem.

stem: A 25-year-old primipara who had a spontaneous vaginal delivery 5 days ago presents to the postnatal clinic with fever, lower abdominal pain and heavy malodorous lochia. She is breastfeeding without difficulty. Examination: T 38.7, HR 108, BP 110/68, RR 18; the uterus is bulky and tender, lochia heavy and offensive; breasts full but no focal tenderness or erythema; perineal repair healing; calves soft. Bedside urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG negative. WCC 16.2, CRP 110, Hb 102. She had a 4th-degree tear at delivery, repaired in theatre, with a single-shot prophylactic dose of cefazolin and metronidazole. She has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.5°C, HR 100 bpm
data_table.Examination: Tender boggy uterus, heavy malodorous lochia, no breast or wound signs
data_table.Investigations: None yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily as outpatient
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate outpatient is wrong because she has features of sepsis (HR 108, T 38.7, WCC 16.2, CRP 110) requiring admission and IV antibiotics; the anti-pattern is treating septic endometritis as a UTI-equivalent. Would be correct only in mild post-discharge endometritis without sepsis features (eTG; RANZCOG postpartum infection).

options.text: Admit and commence IV ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole until clinical improvement, then de-escalate to oral
options.rationale: Postpartum endometritis with sepsis features requires admission and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Gram-positives, Gram-negatives and anaerobes. Therapeutic Guidelines recommend ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, with de-escalation once afebrile for 24-48 hours (eTG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient
options.rationale: Oral doxycycline is wrong because doxycycline is for PID outpatient regimen, inadequate for septic postpartum endometritis, and contraindicated in breastfeeding due to enamel staining. Would be correct only in non-breastfeeding outpatient PID (eTG).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy until afebrile, then discharge
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is wrong because it misses anaerobic cover for pelvic infection; combination therapy is needed (or alternative regimen with metronidazole). Would be correct only with metronidazole added (eTG).

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound to exclude retained products before treatment
options.rationale: Withhold antibiotics for ultrasound is wrong because in sepsis, empirical antibiotics must be started within the first hour (Sepsis Six); imaging follows but does not delay treatment. Would never be correct in sepsis (Sepsis Six / eTG).

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis is the most common cause of fever after vaginal delivery, presenting with uterine tenderness, foul lochia, and fever. Mastitis, UTI, and wound infection are differentiated by focal findings.
key_points: Postpartum endometritis typically occurs 2-10 days after delivery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections)
Foul-smelling lochia with uterine tenderness strongly suggests endometritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections)
Breast engorgement alone without erythema or focal tenderness is not mastitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Infections)
explanation.pearls: Always examine the breasts and perineum when evaluating a postpartum fever – absence of focal signs there, with a tender uterus, points to endometritis.

stem: A 30-year-old nullipara at 29+3 weeks presents with new hypertension 170/110 mmHg, headache, and epigastric pain. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 98 mg/mmol. Platelets 130 ×10⁹/L, ALT 110 U/L, creatinine 92 µmol/L. She has received the first dose of betamethasone. CTG is reactive with normal variability. She is commenced on labetalol and magnesium sulphate. After stabilisation, the team discusses timing of delivery. What is the most appropriate plan?

data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness, hyperreflexia, 3 beats clonus
data_table.Investigations: PCR 98, Platelets 130, ALT 110, Cr 92, CTG reactive
data_table.Medications: Labetalol commenced, MgSO₄ infusion, betamethasone first dose given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

options.text: Complete the 48-hour steroid course and deliver electively at 48-72 hours, provided maternal and foetal status remain stable
options.rationale: Elective 48-72 hour delay is wrong because severe features (hepatic involvement, symptoms) mandate delivery within 24-48 hours of completing steroids, not deliberate elective delay; the anti-pattern is treating severe pre-eclampsia like late-preterm mild disease. Would be correct only with mild features not severe (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, as severe-features hypertensive disease with hepatic involvement mandates delivery regardless of gestational age
options.rationale: Deliver within 24 hours regardless is wrong because at 29 weeks, the 24-48 hour window for steroid completion is justified unless there is severe refractory hypertension, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema or abruption; the foetal benefit of steroid completion is substantial. Would be correct only with severe refractory features (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Continue expectant management with close monitoring until 34 weeks, as the CTG is reassuring and maternal status is stable on treatment
options.rationale: Expectancy to 34 weeks is wrong because expectant management at <34 weeks is reserved for the absence of severe features; she has hepatic involvement and symptoms, which mandate delivery after steroid completion. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without severe features (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and plan delivery for 48 hours after the first dose, with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: In severe pre-eclampsia between 24 and 34 weeks, delivery is recommended after the completion of a corticosteroid course (48 hours from first dose) if maternal and foetal conditions permit. This balances the benefits of foetal maturation against maternal risks. Delivery is expedited if deterioration occurs. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Deliver immediately by caesarean, as severe-features hypertension with abnormal LFTs and platelets <150 ×10⁹/L meets haemolysis-elevated-liver-enzymes-low-platelets criteria
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean for HELLP is wrong because HELLP requires haemolysis (LDH >600, low haptoglobin), ALT >70 and platelets <100; her platelets are 130, so HELLP is not fully met. Furthermore, severe pre-eclampsia after steroid course allows vaginal delivery route if appropriate. Would be correct only with full HELLP and rapidly deteriorating maternal status (SOMANZ Guideline).

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia at 29 weeks without immediate life-threatening complications (no eclamptic seizure, no pulmonary oedema, no abruption) should be managed by completing the corticosteroid course over 48 hours, then proceeding with delivery. This optimises neonatal outcomes while minimising maternal risk. Delivery can be expedited if deterioration occurs.
key_points: For severe pre-eclampsia <34 weeks, delivery is recommended after completion of the antenatal corticosteroid course (48 hours). (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

Indications for immediate delivery (within hours) include: uncontrolled hypertension despite multiple agents, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema, placental abruption, persistent severe headache/visual disturbance, deteriorating renal or liver function. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
HELLP syndrome diagnosis requires haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and thrombocytopenia <100 ×10⁹/L. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
Magnesium sulphate is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia, irrespective of delivery timing. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In severe pre-eclampsia, the clock starts with the first steroid dose; aim for delivery at 48 hours but be ready to deliver earlier if the mother or fetus deteriorates.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 underwent suction curettage for a suspected molar pregnancy at 10 weeks gestation after an ultrasound showed a 'snowstorm' appearance and no foetal pole. Histopathology confirms a complete hydatidiform mole. She is keen to conceive again as soon as possible. Her hCG level 2 weeks post-evacuation is 2500 IU/L, down from 120,000 IU/L pre-operatively. She has no vaginal bleeding, and pelvic ultrasound shows an empty uterus with normal adnexa. What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 115/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No vaginal bleeding, uterus well involuted, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: hCG 2500 IU/L (was 120,000); USS: empty uterus, normal adnexa; histology: complete mole

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

options.text: She may attempt pregnancy once hCG becomes undetectable, with no increased risk in subsequent pregnancies and no surveillance needed
options.rationale: No surveillance, conceive once hCG undetectable is wrong because monthly hCG surveillance is needed for at least 6 months after normalisation to detect persistent trophoblastic disease; conception during this window confounds interpretation. Would never be correct after complete mole (RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease).

options.text: Avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after hCG normalises, use reliable contraception, with monthly hCG surveillance throughout that period
options.rationale: Following evacuation of a complete mole, hCG is monitored fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6-12 months (Australian protocol: 6 months after first normal level). Pregnancy should be avoided during this surveillance period to prevent confusion with rising hCG from a new pregnancy vs. persistent gestational trophoblastic disease. Effective contraception (COCP, IUD, implant) is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease; KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: She should undergo a hysterectomy because complete moles have a 15-20% risk of progression to choriocarcinoma and future pregnancy is contraindicated
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is wrong because routine hysterectomy is not required for complete mole in a young woman who desires future fertility; surveillance with hCG manages persistent disease risk, and hysterectomy is reserved for older women or invasive mole. Would be correct only in older women with completed family or invasive disease (RANZCOG).

options.text: She requires a single dose of intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² immediately to prevent progression to persistent trophoblastic disease at any stage
options.rationale: Single-dose methotrexate prophylactically is wrong because prophylactic chemotherapy is not routine for complete mole; most patients resolve with evacuation alone. Would be correct only for selected high-risk profiles (very high hCG, age >40, very large uterus) (RANZCOG).

options.text: She should undergo CT chest and abdomen to stage the disease before pregnancy, as complete moles frequently metastasise to the lungs
options.rationale: CT staging routinely is wrong because routine CT is not performed for uncomplicated complete mole with falling hCG; staging is reserved for persistent/rising hCG or clinical suspicion of metastases. Would be correct only with persistent disease or metastatic symptoms (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Following evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole, hCG must be monitored until normalised, then monthly for at least 6 months to detect persistent trophoblastic disease (which occurs in 15-20% of complete moles). Pregnancy should be avoided during the surveillance period because a rising hCG from a new pregnancy would be indistinguishable from malignant transformation. Reliable contraception is advised. Routine chemotherapy, hysterectomy, and staging CT are not required unless hCG plateaus, rises, or metastases are suspected.
key_points: Complete moles require post-evacuation hCG surveillance: fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Pregnancy should be avoided for at least 6 months after the first normal hCG; effective contraception is recommended. (Source: KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)
Persistent trophoblastic disease (rising or plateaued hCG) requires chemotherapy (methotrexate or actinomycin D). (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Prophylactic chemotherapy is not routinely indicated for low-risk moles. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
explanation.pearls: hCG monitoring after a molar pregnancy is the single most important element of follow-up. Any missed test may delay detection of choriocarcinoma, which is highly curable if caught early.

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 22+5 weeks presents with painful regular contractions and cervical dilation 3 cm with bulging membranes. Foetal ultrasound: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal, estimated foetal weight 540 g (50th centile). She has been counselled with the neonatology team. After detailed discussion of likely outcomes at periviable gestation, the family has indicated they wish for active resuscitation if the infant is liveborn.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm, bulging membranes
data_table.Investigations: USS: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding antenatal corticosteroids?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, since active resuscitation is planned
options.rationale: RANZCOG and Australian/New Zealand neonatal consensus now support antenatal corticosteroids from 22+0 weeks when active neonatal resuscitation is planned, as ACS reduces neonatal mortality and major morbidity. Below 22 weeks ACS is not recommended (RANZCOG ACS; ANZNN periviable consensus).

options.text: Withhold ACS as gestation is below 23 weeks where benefit is uncertain
options.rationale: Withhold ACS at <23 weeks is wrong because updated RANZCOG/ANZNN guidance supports ACS from 22+0 weeks when active resuscitation is planned, given evidence of reduced neonatal mortality; the anti-pattern is using outdated 23+0 cutoff. Would be correct only at <22+0 weeks or when palliative neonatal care is chosen (RANZCOG; ANZNN).

options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM only
options.rationale: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; partial courses have reduced effect though some benefit if delivery is imminent. Would be correct only if delivery occurred before the second dose could be given (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 6 hours for four doses
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 6 mg q6h is wrong because the standard regimen is 6 mg IM 12-hourly for four doses; q6h is too frequent and not evidence-based. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Defer the decision until membranes rupture and only give if delivery is within 24 hours
options.rationale: Defer until membranes rupture is wrong because ACS is given when preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days; waiting for ROM loses the lead time required for steroid effect on lung maturation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids are recommended from 23 weeks gestation for women at risk of preterm delivery within 7 days, using betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids indicated at 23+0 to 34+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, 2 doses 24 hours apart. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
explanation.pearls: At 23 weeks, every hour gained in utero and every dose of steroids matters.

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents after her third consecutive miscarriage at 9 weeks. She had one previous live birth at term 4 years ago. She has no significant medical history. Which set of investigations is most appropriate?

data_table.Examination: Normal general and pelvic exam
data_table.Investigations: Products of conception: normal female karyotype

lead_in: What is the most appropriate set of investigations?

options.text: Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, plus pelvic ultrasound for anomalies
options.rationale: RANZCOG guidelines recommend testing for antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 glycoprotein I) and a pelvic ultrasound (3D if possible) to evaluate the uterine cavity. Parental karyotyping is not routinely recommended unless there is a family history or specific indication. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotypes, inherited thrombophilia screen, and outpatient diagnostic hysteroscopy
options.rationale: Karyotypes plus thrombophilia screen is wrong because parental karyotyping is not routine in Australia (offered only with family history of chromosomal disease) and inherited thrombophilia screening (other than APS) is not recommended because it does not alter management. Would be correct only with relevant family history (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: Full blood count, thyroid function tests, fasting glucose, and 75-gram oral glucose tolerance test
options.rationale: FBC, TFTs, glucose, OGTT is wrong because these are general health screens, not specific causes of recurrent miscarriage; while TFTs are often included, fasting glucose and OGTT are not standard recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only as general health check, not specific workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: Serum anti-Müllerian hormone, early follicular FSH, oestradiol level, and ovarian antral follicle count
options.rationale: Ovarian reserve markers is wrong because AMH, FSH, oestradiol and AFC assess reserve and IVF prognosis, not causes of recurrent loss; ovarian reserve does not explain her three losses. Would be correct only for fertility planning, not recurrent miscarriage workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: MTHFR mutation testing, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, and fasting homocysteine level
options.rationale: MTHFR and inherited thrombophilias is wrong because MTHFR testing is not recommended (no clinical management implications) and factor V Leiden/PT mutation testing is not routine in Australian recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only in selected research/case-specific contexts (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

explanation.summary: The recommended investigation for recurrent miscarriage (≥3 consecutive losses) includes antiphospholipid antibody testing (to diagnose APS) and a pelvic ultrasound (to detect uterine anomalies). Parental karyotyping and inherited thrombophilia testing are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Routine investigations for recurrent miscarriage: antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 GPI) and pelvic ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Parental karyotyping is only indicated if there is a family history of chromosomal rearrangements. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Inherited thrombophilias are not associated with recurrent miscarriage and should not be tested. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid syndrome is the only treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage; low-dose aspirin and heparin can significantly improve live birth rates.

stem: A 32-year-old woman at 40+2 weeks is attempting vaginal birth after one prior lower-segment caesarean (VBAC). She is contracting 4 in 10 minutes, and CTG is reassuring. She requests epidural analgesia. Her labour is augmented with oxytocin at 6 mU/min for slow progress. She suddenly develops constant severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. The CTG shows a prolonged foetal bradycardia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Acute constant abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, loss of contractions
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged bradycardia, previous LSCS

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Uterine rupture
options.rationale: The combination of previous caesarean, oxytocin augmentation, constant severe pain, bleeding, loss of contractions, and foetal bradycardia is classic for uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)

options.text: Placental abruption
options.rationale: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption typically causes pain plus woody-hard hypertonic uterus rather than LOSS of contractions; the prior caesarean, oxytocin and loss of contractions point to scar rupture. Would be correct only with hypertonic uterus and no prior surgery (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Cord prolapse
options.rationale: Cord prolapse is wrong because cord prolapse presents with a palpable cord protruding and variable decelerations, not constant pain or bleeding; the picture is wrong. Would be correct only with cord visible at the introitus and variable decelerations (RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse).

options.text: Normal labour pain with anxiety
options.rationale: Normal labour pain with anxiety is wrong because CTG bradycardia plus bleeding excludes normal labour; the anti-pattern is dismissing intrapartum red flags as anxiety. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline).

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism
options.rationale: AFE is wrong because AFE causes acute hypoxia, cardiovascular collapse and DIC, not isolated uterine pain and bleeding; the clinical picture is different. Would be correct only with the cardiovascular-respiratory-DIC triad (RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism).

explanation.summary: Uterine rupture is an obstetric emergency, especially in VBAC with oxytocin, presenting with constant abdominal pain, bleeding, loss of contractions, and foetal bradycardia. Immediate laparotomy is required.
key_points: Risk of uterine rupture in VBAC is higher with oxytocin augmentation (approximately 1.5-2%). (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)
Classic signs: constant pain, vaginal bleeding, loss of contractions, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In VBAC, the epidural may mask pain from early uterine dehiscence; CTG monitoring is essential for early detection.

stem: A 34-year-old woman who had diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks delivered a healthy infant at 39 weeks. She is now 8 weeks postpartum. What is the most appropriate follow-up test?

data_table.Examination: Normal postpartum exam
data_table.Investigations: GDM on OGTT: fasting 5.4, 1h 10.6, 2h 9.0; no postpartum glucose test yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up test?

options.text: 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with fasting, 1h, and 2h glucose
options.rationale: ADIPS and RANZCOG recommend a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to screen for persistent diabetes or prediabetes after GDM. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)

options.text: Fasting blood glucose only
options.rationale: Fasting glucose only is wrong because fasting alone misses impaired glucose tolerance (post-load hyperglycaemia); ADIPS specifies 75 g OGTT post-partum. Would be correct only as adjunct, not standalone (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: HbA1c
