options.rationale: HbA1c is wrong because HbA1c is less sensitive for detecting diabetes in the early postpartum period (red cell turnover, gestational haemodilution); OGTT is preferred. Would be correct only later as part of ongoing surveillance, not first postpartum screen (ADIPS).

options.text: No test required; she will be screened at her next routine health check at age 40
options.rationale: No test until age 40 is wrong because women with GDM carry a substantially elevated lifetime T2DM risk (up to 50% over 10 years) and require regular postpartum and ongoing screening; waiting to age 40 misses early diagnosis. Would never be correct (ADIPS).

options.text: Home glucose monitoring for 1 week
options.rationale: Home glucose monitoring is wrong because formal OGTT is the standard postpartum screening tool; home monitoring has no role in screening. Would never be correct as the screening test (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: All women with GDM should have a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to diagnose persistent diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance. Annual screening for type 2 diabetes is recommended thereafter.
key_points: Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks post-delivery for all women with GDM. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)
HbA1c is not recommended for initial postpartum screening. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)
explanation.pearls: GDM is one of the strongest predictors of future type 2 diabetes; up to 50% of women develop diabetes within 5-10 years.

stem: A 16-year-old girl reports 8 months of severe crampy lower abdominal pain with each period, missing school for 1-2 days each cycle. Her cycles are regular at 28 days with menarche at 13. She has tried regular paracetamol 1 g qid with minimal benefit. There is no pain outside of menses, no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no abnormal bleeding, no urinary or bowel symptoms and no fever. Examination is normal. Pregnancy test negative. She is otherwise well, has no contraindication to NSAIDs and no medication allergies. Her mother is concerned and asks whether laparoscopy is required to exclude endometriosis before further treatment.

data_table.Examination: Normal abdominal and external genital exam
data_table.Investigations: None

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence regular naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 3-5 days starting 1-2 days before menses, with review at 3 months
options.rationale: Primary dysmenorrhoea is diagnosed clinically; first-line is regular NSAIDs taken pre-emptively, not laparoscopy. NSAID failure after a 3-month trial, plus consideration of COCP, would precede invasive workup (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy now to exclude endometriosis
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy now is wrong because primary dysmenorrhoea is a clinical diagnosis and laparoscopy is reserved for refractory pain after NSAID and COCP trials, or features suggestive of secondary dysmenorrhoea. Would be correct only after failed medical management or red flags for endometriosis (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Continue paracetamol 1 g four times daily and add codeine 30 mg qid as needed
options.rationale: Paracetamol plus codeine is wrong because opioids are not first-line in adolescent dysmenorrhoea (dependence risk, no disease-modifying effect) and NSAIDs have stronger evidence. Would never be correct as first-line (eTG).

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (goserelin) to induce amenorrhoea
options.rationale: GnRH agonist is wrong because GnRH agonists in adolescents risk bone density loss and are not used without confirmed endometriosis with add-back HRT planning; NSAIDs are first-line. Would be correct only in confirmed refractory endometriosis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure and recommend transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation as first-line
options.rationale: TENS as first-line is wrong because TENS is a useful adjunct with modest evidence but not first-line pharmacotherapy; NSAIDs remain first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct (RANZCOG; Cochrane).

explanation.summary: Primary dysmenorrhoea is caused by prostaglandin release from the endometrium. NSAIDs like ibuprofen are first-line because they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis. Starting before menses improves efficacy. COCP is used if NSAIDs fail.
key_points: First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg TDS) started 1-2 days before menses. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
COCP is an effective second-line therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
explanation.pearls: Timing matters with NSAIDs for dysmenorrhoea: start before the pain begins.

stem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman has 2 years of severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to NSAIDs and the COCP, deep dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. Pelvic ultrasound is normal. What is the gold standard for diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Tender uterosacral ligaments, fixed retroverted uterus
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal; no cysts
data_table.Medications: Trial of COCP and NSAIDs failed

lead_in: What is the gold standard for diagnosis?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with visualisation of endometriotic lesions
options.rationale: Laparoscopy with direct visualisation is the gold standard for diagnosing endometriosis, and histological biopsy confirms the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: Transvaginal ultrasound is wrong because TVUS detects ovarian endometriomas and some deep infiltrating endometriosis but cannot exclude superficial peritoneal disease; the gold standard remains laparoscopy. Would be correct only as screening adjunct, not gold standard (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).

options.text: MRI pelvis
options.rationale: MRI pelvis is wrong because MRI is useful for mapping deep infiltrating endometriosis (bowel/bladder/rectovaginal) but is not the diagnostic gold standard; laparoscopy with visualisation and biopsy remains gold standard. Would be correct only as a pre-operative mapping tool (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).

options.text: Serum CA-125 level
options.rationale: CA-125 is wrong because CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis and can be elevated in many other conditions; it is not diagnostic. Would never be diagnostic (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).

options.text: Empiric treatment with a GnRH agonist for 3 months as a diagnostic test
options.rationale: Empirical GnRH agonist is wrong because GnRH agonist provides symptomatic improvement but does not give histological diagnosis and carries significant adverse effects; the anti-pattern is treating diagnostic uncertainty with hormonal suppression. Would be correct only as therapeutic trial after diagnosis, not as the diagnostic test (RANZCOG: Endometriosis).

explanation.summary: Laparoscopy remains the gold standard for diagnosing endometriosis, allowing direct visualisation and biopsy. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI can detect deep disease or endometriomas but cannot exclude superficial lesions.
key_points: Gold standard for endometriosis diagnosis: laparoscopy with histology. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
Ultrasound and MRI are helpful but do not replace laparoscopy. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
Empiric medical treatment without a diagnosis may delay definitive management. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
explanation.pearls: Negative imaging does not rule out endometriosis; up to 50% of women with chronic pelvic pain and normal ultrasound have endometriosis found at laparoscopy.

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation reports severe pain over the pubic symphysis that radiates to the inner thighs, worse with walking, climbing stairs, and turning in bed. Examination reveals tenderness over the symphysis pubis and positive FABER test. What is the most appropriate first-line management?

data_table.Examination: Tender symphysis pubis, positive FABER, no neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: USS: foetus well, cephalic

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer to a women's health physiotherapist for pelvic girdle exercises, support belt fitting, and analgesia
options.rationale: Symphysis pubis dysfunction (pelvic girdle pain) is managed conservatively with physiotherapy focusing on pelvic stabilisation exercises, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoiding aggravating movements (abduction, asymmetric loading) is key. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain in Pregnancy)

options.text: Induction of labour at 37 weeks gestation to minimise further musculoskeletal damage and ongoing maternal discomfort
options.rationale: Induction at 37 weeks for SPD is wrong because delivery does not improve outcomes and induces iatrogenic prematurity morbidity; SPD typically resolves postnatally without delivery escalation. Would be correct only for standard obstetric indications, not SPD itself (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Strict bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy with bedside toileting and meals provided
options.rationale: Strict bedrest is wrong because bedrest leads to deconditioning, VTE risk and does not improve pain; the anti-pattern is treating SPD as a structural injury requiring immobilisation. Would never be correct (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the symphysis pubis joint under image guidance in interventional radiology
options.rationale: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is wrong because injections are reserved for severe refractory cases after conservative measures have failed; not first-line. Would be correct only after physiotherapy and analgesia fail (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Prescribe regular oral opioid analgesia such as immediate-release oxycodone for severe ongoing pelvic pain
options.rationale: Regular oxycodone is wrong because opioids are avoided in pregnancy (foetal CNS depression, neonatal abstinence) and not first-line for SPD; paracetamol plus non-pharmacological measures are preferred. Would be correct only as short-course rescue for severe pain after conservative measures (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

explanation.summary: Pelvic girdle pain in pregnancy is managed with physiotherapy, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoidance of aggravating activities (stair climbing, asymmetric standing) is important. Bed rest and early delivery are not recommended.
key_points: First-line: physiotherapy, pelvic belt, paracetamol. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
Avoid bed rest and opioids. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
explanation.pearls: Symphysis pubis dysfunction does not typically affect mode of delivery; vaginal birth is usually possible with careful positioning.

stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with known fundal fibroid (8 cm at 20-week scan) presents with 36 hours of severe localised pain over the uterine fundus, mild nausea, and a low-grade temperature of 37.6. There is no vaginal bleeding, no leaking of fluid, no contractions and no urinary symptoms. Examination: tender over the fundal fibroid, no peritonism, normal bowel sounds, uterus otherwise soft. CTG reactive, foetus active. Ultrasound: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and central cystic change, no free fluid, normal placental position, normal Dopplers. White cell count mildly elevated 14, CRP 35, urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG appropriate for gestation.

data_table.Examination: Severe tenderness over the uterine fundus, no peritonism, bowel sounds present
data_table.Investigations: USS: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture, no free fluid, foetus well

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for analgesia (paracetamol +/- short-course oral or IV opioids), hydration, monitoring of foetal wellbeing and serial CTG, with avoidance of NSAIDs at this gestation
options.rationale: Red degeneration of a fibroid in pregnancy is treated conservatively: analgesia, rest, hydration and foetal monitoring. NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, indomethacin) are avoided after 30 weeks due to ductal closure risk but more importantly are best avoided across the third trimester; opioids are acceptable short-term. Surgery and tocolysis are not indicated unless complications develop (RANZCOG fibroids; eTG).

options.text: Commence indomethacin 50 mg orally tds for 7 days
options.rationale: Indomethacin 7 days is wrong because indomethacin risks foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios in the third trimester; while it has anti-inflammatory effect on degeneration, safer analgesia is preferred. Would be correct only before 30 weeks when third-trimester risks do not yet apply (eTG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed to elective myomectomy at this gestation
options.rationale: Antenatal myomectomy is wrong because it carries high risk of haemorrhage and foetal loss; reserved for refractory torted pedunculated fibroids. Would be correct only with torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or refractory necrotic lesion (RANZCOG).

options.text: Diagnose placental abruption and proceed to immediate caesarean section
options.rationale: Caesarean for abruption is wrong because abruption presents with bleeding, woody hypertonic uterus and foetal distress; her CTG is reassuring and pain is localised, not the abruption picture. Would be correct only with confirmed abruption (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed septic fibroid
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for septic fibroid is wrong because mild inflammatory marker rise is expected in red degeneration; without sepsis or chorioamnionitis features, antibiotics are not indicated. Would be correct only with sepsis or infection (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Red degeneration of a fibroid presents with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, and tenderness over the fibroid. Conservative management with analgesia and monitoring is the standard, as it resolves spontaneously.
key_points: Red degeneration: acute pain, vomiting, tenderness over fibroid in second/third trimester. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Management is conservative: analgesia, rest, foetal monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Surgery is only indicated for complications like torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or infarction. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: Red degeneration is common and benign – provide strong analgesia and reassure. Avoid unnecessary surgery.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 14+0 weeks attends a recurrent-pregnancy-loss clinic. She has had two previous second-trimester losses at 20 and 22 weeks, both with painless cervical dilation and rapid delivery of a liveborn infant who died shortly after delivery. No infection, no bleeding, no abruption identified at either loss. There are no other risk factors for preterm birth (no smoking, no LLETZ, no Mullerian anomaly, no DES exposure). Current TVUS cervical length 38 mm with closed internal os. She is otherwise well and asks specifically about the option of a prophylactic suture.

data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic exam, closed cervix
data_table.Investigations: TVS: cervical length 38 mm, closed os

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Offer a history-indicated cervical cerclage at 12-14 weeks now
options.rationale: Two or more previous second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency are the classical indication for history-indicated (prophylactic) cerclage placed at 12-14 weeks. Serial surveillance alone is less effective for this pattern (RANZCOG cerclage; RCOG Green-top 60).

options.text: Serial TVUS cervical length every 2 weeks, with ultrasound-indicated cerclage only if length falls below 25 mm before 24 weeks
options.rationale: Serial TVUS with ultrasound-indicated cerclage is wrong because that strategy is preferred when there is ONE prior preterm birth/loss; with two or more typical mid-trimester insufficiency losses, history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended. Would be correct only with one prior preterm birth (RANZCOG cerclage).

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks without cerclage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone alone is wrong because progesterone is indicated for asymptomatic short cervix on TVS or one prior preterm birth, but does not replace history-indicated cerclage in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as adjunct or alternative when cerclage is declined/contraindicated (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bed rest at home from 16 weeks with avoidance of intercourse and exercise
options.rationale: Bed rest is wrong because bedrest has no evidence of benefit and increases VTE risk; it cannot substitute for cerclage. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Await spontaneous cervical change and place emergency rescue cerclage only if dilation occurs
options.rationale: Rescue cerclage only when dilation occurs is wrong because rescue cerclage carries higher complication risk and lower success; planned history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is superior in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as salvage when history-indicated cerclage was missed or declined (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: A history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended for women with ≥2 second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency. This prevents recurrence and improves live birth rates.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: ≥2 prior mid-trimester losses without another cause. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
Prophylactic cerclage is placed at 12-14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
explanation.pearls: The best predictor of cervical insufficiency is a history of painless, progressive second-trimester cervical dilation – the current cervical length doesn't matter for the decision to place a history-indicated cerclage.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 weeks presents with reduced foetal movements for 12 hours. Bedside ultrasound confirms intrauterine foetal demise. She is haemodynamically stable, no bleeding, no contractions. The senior registrar has confirmed and broken the news, with bereavement midwifery support called. She is keen to take the baby home and is asking whether she could await spontaneous labour rather than be induced now.

data_table.Examination: No foetal heart auscultated, uterus size consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: USS: no cardiac activity, foetal demise confirmed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Counsel that delaying delivery beyond approximately 48 hours after foetal death increases the risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation, and offer induction of labour as the preferred option, while supporting her preferences
options.rationale: Prolonged retention of a dead foetus risks DIC (developing significantly after 4 weeks but rising from 2 days onward). Induction of labour is generally recommended; expectant management beyond a short window requires close haematological monitoring (RANZCOG stillbirth).

options.text: Proceed to category 1 caesarean section as soon as theatre is available
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is wrong because caesarean is reserved for maternal indications (e.g. praevia with bleeding, abruption with maternal compromise); after IUFD, vaginal birth is preferred and avoids surgical risk. Would be correct only with maternal indications for caesarean (RANZCOG stillbirth).

options.text: Discharge home and await spontaneous labour at term
options.rationale: Discharge and await spontaneous labour at term is wrong because awaiting term risks DIC from prolonged retention; induction is recommended. Would never be correct as default (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer betamethasone now and induce labour in 48 hours
options.rationale: Betamethasone and delay 48h is wrong because corticosteroids have no foetal benefit after demise; the delay raises DIC risk without benefit. Would never be correct after IUFD (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat ultrasound and CTG in 24 hours to reconfirm the diagnosis before discussing options
options.rationale: Repeat scan in 24h is wrong because diagnosis is already confirmed by ultrasound (typically by two clinicians per protocol); delay prolongs distress and DIC risk. Would never be correct once diagnosis is confirmed (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: When a stillbirth is diagnosed, the immediate priorities are sensitive communication, offering bereavement care, discussing delivery options (typically induction of labour), and offering investigations to determine cause.
key_points: After stillbirth diagnosis: empathetic communication, offer induction of labour within 24-48 hours, support. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
Investigations: maternal bloods, placental pathology, foetal autopsy if consent. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
explanation.pearls: The words used when telling a mother her baby has died will stay with her forever. Use clear language, not euphemisms, and allow silence.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 at 38+0 weeks presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and minimal vaginal bleeding. Examination: tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, fundal height larger than expected. HR 128, BP 88/54, RR 24, SpO2 97%, peripherally cool. CTG: foetal bradycardia 70 bpm with absent variability. She has chronic hypertension on labetalol and was a smoker until early pregnancy. Bedside ultrasound: posterior placenta, no praevia, retroplacental haematoma visible. Bloods sent: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 92, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L.

data_table.Examination: Tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, minimal PV loss
data_table.Investigations: CTG: bradycardia 90 bpm, reduced variability

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: This is placental abruption with maternal shock, foetal bradycardia and early DIC. Immediate category 1 caesarean section with MTP activation is required; both mother and foetus are in extremis (RANZCOG abruption; ANZCOR).

options.text: Commence tocolysis with nifedipine and observe with serial CTG
options.rationale: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in abruption with maternal compromise and foetal distress; immediate delivery is required. Would never be correct in this scenario (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert two large-bore IV cannulae, give 2 L crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Cannulae and wait 30 min is wrong because resuscitation runs concurrently with delivery preparation; waiting 30 minutes worsens outcomes for mother and foetus. Would never be correct as isolated step (RANZCOG).

options.text: Attempt artificial rupture of membranes to expedite vaginal delivery
options.rationale: ARM to expedite vaginal birth is wrong because vaginal birth in abruption requires stable mother and foetus and advanced labour; with shock and bradycardia, caesarean is mandatory. Would be correct only with stable mother, stable foetus and advanced labour, none of which apply (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transfer to tertiary centre for higher-level obstetric input
options.rationale: Transfer for tertiary input is wrong because she is haemodynamically unstable and the foetus is in extremis; transfer is unsafe and immediate on-site delivery is required. Would be correct only in a stable patient at non-tertiary site (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Concealed placental abruption presents with severe constant pain, a tense rigid uterus, and foetal distress with minimal external bleeding. Immediate delivery is indicated.
key_points: Concealed abruption: pain, rigid uterus, minimal vaginal bleeding, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
It is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate caesarean delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In abruption, the amount of vaginal bleeding does not reflect the severity – the worst haemorrhage is concealed.

stem: A 28-year-old nullipara at 41 weeks is in labour with oxytocin augmentation. The CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with normal variability but repetitive late decelerations after each contraction. Contractions are 5 in 10 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 100, BP 110/70
data_table.Examination: Cervix 6 cm, contractions 5:10
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 150, normal variability, late decelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease the oxytocin infusion, reposition the mother to left lateral, and consider acute tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC
options.rationale: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency. The first step is intrauterine resuscitation: cease oxytocin to reduce uterine activity, reposition, and consider tocolysis if contractions are hyperstimulated. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Continue oxytocin at the same rate and prepare for immediate vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Continue oxytocin is wrong because continuing oxytocin in late decelerations worsens uteroplacental insufficiency by increasing uterine activity and reducing perfusion; the anti-pattern is maintaining augmentation in the face of foetal distress. Would never be correct in late decelerations (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Perform an immediate caesarean section without further assessment
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean without resuscitation is wrong because normal variability and baseline mean there is time for intrauterine resuscitation; many tracings recover and avoid unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only if intrauterine resuscitation fails or with prolonged bradycardia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Apply a foetal scalp electrode and take a foetal scalp blood sample
options.rationale: Scalp electrode and FBS is wrong because scalp blood sampling is used if CTG remains pathological after resuscitation, not as first step; the immediate response is intrauterine resuscitation. Would be correct only as second step after failed resuscitation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer a maternal oxygen mask at 8 L/min and increase the oxytocin to achieve better contractions
options.rationale: Maternal oxygen and increased oxytocin is wrong because maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and is not recommended; increasing oxytocin would worsen the situation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Late decelerations reflect transient foetal hypoxia due to uteroplacental insufficiency. Immediate steps are to reduce uterine activity (cease oxytocin), improve placental perfusion (left lateral), and consider tocolysis. Escalation to delivery depends on response.
key_points: Late decelerations: cease oxytocin, reposition, consider tocolysis. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
If CTG normalises, oxytocin may be cautiously restarted at lower dose. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Late decelerations with normal variability = time for conservative measures; late decelerations with reduced variability = immediate delivery.

stem: A 28-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep when the baby sleeps, and feelings of guilt. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 14. She is breastfeeding. She has no suicidal ideation. What is the most appropriate initial management?

data_table.Examination: Normal physical examination
data_table.Investigations: EPDS score 14, TSH normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Refer for psychological therapy (CBT or interpersonal therapy) and consider sertraline 50 mg daily, with risk-benefit discussion in breastfeeding
options.rationale: Moderate postpartum depression (EPDS 13-19) should be managed with psychological therapy and considered for antidepressant medication. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding due to minimal excretion into breast milk. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health; Beyond Blue Guideline)

options.text: Reassurance that the 'baby blues' are normal and will resolve without any intervention or follow-up within a few weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance of baby blues is wrong because baby blues resolve within 2 weeks; symptoms beyond 2 weeks with functional impairment define postpartum depression. Would be correct only within the first 2 weeks postpartum without functional impact (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Immediate admission to a mother-baby psychiatric unit due to perceived risk of harm to the baby in the home setting
options.rationale: Immediate inpatient mother-baby admission is wrong because inpatient admission is reserved for severe depression, suicidal ideation or postpartum psychosis with safety concerns; this is moderate depression without active risk. Would be correct only with severe risk features (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise her to stop breastfeeding due to neonatal serotonergic risk concerns
options.rationale: Fluoxetine plus stop breastfeeding is wrong because fluoxetine has a long half-life and higher milk:plasma ratio, making sertraline preferred in breastfeeding; stopping breastfeeding is unnecessary on appropriate SSRI. Would be correct only when sertraline fails or is contraindicated (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Advise increased exercise and structured social engagement only, with reassessment in routine clinic in four weeks time
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures only is wrong because exercise and social engagement are adjuncts but insufficient for moderate depression (EPDS 13-19); active treatment with therapy and medication is recommended. Would be correct only for mild symptoms or as adjunct to active treatment (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

explanation.summary: Postpartum depression with an EPDS score of 14 is moderate. First-line treatment includes psychological therapy and consideration of an antidepressant. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI during breastfeeding. Untreated depression can affect maternal-infant bonding.
key_points: Postpartum depression: treat with CBT/interpersonal therapy ± SSRI (sertraline first-line in breastfeeding). (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
Baby blues resolve by day 10; symptoms persisting beyond 2 weeks are depression. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the SSRI of choice in breastfeeding due to minimal infant exposure. The mother's mental health is paramount – untreated depression also harms the infant.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramps. Speculum: cervical os is open with a small amount of tissue at the os. TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 8 mm, no retained products clearly visible, no adnexal mass, no free fluid. Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today (was 8500 IU/L two days ago). She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 122, blood group A Rh-positive, no allergies. She has had no prior surgery and is keen to be home with her partner. The team is choosing between expectant, medical and surgical management.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Open os, tissue in canal; minimal bleeding now
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 5 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Offer expectant, medical (misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) management options, and support her choice
options.rationale: This is an incomplete or completing miscarriage in a haemodynamically stable woman. Australian guidance (RANZCOG, eTG) supports shared decision-making between expectant, medical and surgical management with comparable safety; choice depends on patient preference, bleeding and availability.

options.text: Proceed directly to suction curettage in theatre today
options.rationale: Direct surgical curettage is wrong because surgical management is one option among three (expectant, medical with misoprostol, surgical) with comparable safety in stable women; mandating surgery ignores patient preference. Would be correct only when the patient prefers surgical management or with active heavy bleeding (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer single-dose methotrexate as the pregnancy may be ectopic
options.rationale: Methotrexate for possible ectopic is wrong because TVUS confirms intrauterine pregnancy products and beta-hCG is falling appropriately, making ectopic unlikely. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion to expedite expulsion of retained products
options.rationale: IV oxytocin to expedite expulsion is wrong because oxytocin is not standard for first-trimester miscarriage; misoprostol is the medical option of choice for first-trimester pregnancy loss. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester losses with viable uterine response (eTG).

options.text: Discharge home with no follow-up; the falling beta-hCG indicates resolution
options.rationale: Discharge with no follow-up is wrong because follow-up beta-hCG to negative confirms resolution and excludes retained products or molar pregnancy; the anti-pattern is treating falling hCG as complete resolution without confirmation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Incomplete miscarriage is diagnosed when there is an open cervical os with retained products of conception. Complete miscarriage shows a closed os and an empty uterus. Threatened miscarriage has a closed os and a viable pregnancy.
key_points: Incomplete miscarriage: open os, products of conception visible or retained. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
Management options include expectant, medical, or surgical evacuation depending on clinical stability. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
explanation.pearls: The appearance of the cervical os on speculum examination is the key to differentiating complete from incomplete miscarriage.

stem: A 24-year-old G2P1 at 29+4 weeks gestation presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid and ongoing trickling. She is afebrile and has no contractions. Speculum examination confirms pooling of clear fluid with positive nitrazine test. Cervix is closed and long. CTG is reassuring. She received a course of betamethasone at 27 weeks for a threatened preterm labour that resolved.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8°C, HR 85 bpm, BP 110/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pooling of fluid, closed cervix, no uterine tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Positive nitrazine, CTG reactive, WCC 11.2, CRP 8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: The ORACLE I trial demonstrated that erythromycin prolongs pregnancy and reduces neonatal morbidity in PPROM. Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. A single rescue course of betamethasone should also be given as the prior course was >7 days ago. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; ORACLE I Trial)

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin daily for 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin for 5 days
options.rationale: IV ampicillin/gentamicin for 48h is wrong because that regimen is for established chorioamnionitis or proven infection, not for uncomplicated PPROM latency; the question is about latency therapy. Would be correct only with clinical chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is wrong because the ORACLE I trial showed increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis with co-amoxiclav in PPROM; it is explicitly avoided. Would never be correct in PPROM latency (ORACLE I Trial).

options.text: No antibiotics are required as she is afebrile
options.rationale: No antibiotics is wrong because PPROM <34 weeks routinely receives latency antibiotics regardless of fever status; afebrile presentation does not exclude subclinical infection. Would be correct only at term PROM, not preterm (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days
options.rationale: IM ceftriaxone monotherapy is wrong because ceftriaxone is not the established Australian PPROM latency regimen; erythromycin is. Would be correct only when erythromycin is contraindicated and ceftriaxone is selected as alternative under specialist advice (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

explanation.summary: In PPROM, oral erythromycin for 10 days is recommended to prolong latency and reduce neonatal morbidity (ORACLE I trial). Co-amoxiclav is avoided. A rescue course of betamethasone is indicated if >7 days since the last course.
key_points: Antibiotic of choice for PPROM: erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days. (Source: ORACLE I Trial; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
Avoid co-amoxiclav due to NEC risk. (Source: ORACLE I Trial)
explanation.pearls: In PPROM, erythromycin is the magic bullet – cheap, effective, and safe.

stem: A 32-year-old primigravida with diet-controlled GDM and an EFW of 4.5 kg has just had the foetal head deliver with retraction and turtle-neck sign. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure (30 seconds), attempted delivery of the posterior arm (twice), and internal rotation manoeuvres (Rubin II and Woods' screw) have all failed. Time from delivery of the head: 4 minutes. CTG showed normal trace before delivery. The woman has an epidural in situ. The midwife asks the senior registrar if firm fundal pressure should now be tried while preparing for the next manoeuvre.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 115 bpm, BP 140/90 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Turtle-neck sign, failed McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, posterior arm delivery, internal rotations; foetal bradycardia 50 bpm
data_table.Investigations: GDM, macrosomia

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Roll the woman onto hands and knees (Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre) and reattempt delivery of the posterior arm
options.rationale: After failure of McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, posterior arm and internal rotation, the next recommended step in the RANZCOG algorithm is the all-fours (Gaskin) manoeuvre. Fundal pressure is contraindicated as it worsens impaction and risks uterine rupture and OASIS (RANZCOG shoulder dystocia).

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure to dislodge the impacted shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is wrong because it worsens impaction by driving the anterior shoulder onto the symphysis and risks uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury; the anti-pattern is replacing suprapubic pressure with fundal pressure. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Perform symphysiotomy to enlarge the pelvic outlet
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is wrong because it is a last-resort manoeuvre in settings where caesarean is unavailable; in Australian hospitals it precedes only Zavanelli, after all-fours and other manoeuvres have failed. Would be correct only in resource-limited settings without theatre access (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform Zavanelli cephalic replacement and proceed to caesarean section
options.rationale: Zavanelli is wrong because cephalic replacement plus caesarean is the absolute last resort after all other manoeuvres have failed, including all-fours, and carries high foetal mortality. Would be correct only after every other manoeuvre has failed (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat McRoberts and suprapubic pressure for another 60 seconds
options.rationale: Repeat failed manoeuvres is wrong because the algorithm proceeds to all-fours after the first internal manoeuvre sequence has failed; repeating wastes time during hypoxia. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: In severe shoulder dystocia refractory to McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, posterior arm delivery, and internal rotations, the next step is the all-fours (Gaskin) manoeuvre. Zavanelli and symphysiotomy are last resorts. Fundal pressure is contraindicated.
key_points: HELPERR sequence: after internal manoeuvres, Roll to all-fours (Gaskin). (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)
Zavanelli is a last resort with high morbidity. (Source: ALSO Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: Even with an epidural, the Gaskin manoeuvre can be performed with help; it often resolves the impaction by changing pelvic geometry.

stem: A 31-year-old woman with primary obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages; persistent lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG and anti-beta2GPI IgG on testing 12 weeks apart, no history of thromboembolic event) is now 6 weeks pregnant. She has been on warfarin 5 mg daily for the past 9 months as 'prevention' prescribed by another clinician. She has had no thrombosis. She has no other medical conditions and no allergies.

data_table.Examination: Normal
data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant positive, aCL IgG >40, anti-β2 GPI IgG >40; viable IUP 6 weeks

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication regimen for this pregnancy?

options.text: Continue warfarin 5 mg daily throughout pregnancy as she is already established on it
options.rationale: Continue warfarin is wrong because warfarin is teratogenic between 6 and 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy with nasal hypoplasia and chondrodysplasia punctata); furthermore she has obstetric APS without prior thrombosis, where the indication is aspirin plus LMWH, not warfarin. Would be correct only with mechanical heart valves where benefit may outweigh risk after counselling (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Stop warfarin immediately and commence low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic-dose enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, continuing through pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Obstetric APS (recurrent miscarriage, no prior thrombosis) is treated with low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH. Warfarin must be ceased before 6 weeks to avoid embryopathy; it is replaced by LMWH (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Continue warfarin until 12 weeks then switch to LMWH
options.rationale: Warfarin until 12 weeks then LMWH is wrong because warfarin embryopathy risk is concentrated in weeks 6-12; the switch must occur BEFORE 6 weeks, not after the teratogenic window. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Continue warfarin until 6 weeks then aspirin alone for the rest of the pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin to 6 weeks then aspirin alone is wrong because aspirin monotherapy is inferior to aspirin plus LMWH in obstetric APS with recurrent loss; LMWH must be added. Would be correct only for isolated APL antibodies without obstetric criteria (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Stop warfarin and commence intravenous immunoglobulin monthly as primary therapy
options.rationale: IVIG monthly is wrong because IVIG is not first-line for obstetric APS; aspirin plus LMWH is. IVIG is reserved for refractory cases or research protocols. Would be correct only in trial settings or refractory disease (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Obstetric APS without prior thrombosis is treated with low-dose aspirin and prophylactic-dose LMWH throughout pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum. This reduces pregnancy loss and prevents thromboembolism. Warfarin is reserved for those with prior thrombosis.
key_points: Obstetric APS: aspirin 100–150 mg + LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily) from early pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: APS)
Continue for 6 weeks postpartum to prevent maternal thrombosis. (Source: International APS Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: APS is the only treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage – low-dose aspirin and heparin can increase live birth rates from 20% to over 70%.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks with PROM 28 hours ago develops T 39.2, HR 124, RR 28, BP 88/52 (MAP 64), confused with GCS 14. Tender uterus, purulent vaginal discharge, foetal HR 175 bpm with reduced variability. Lactate 4.6, WCC 19. IV access obtained, bloods sent, blood cultures taken. The on-call team has called for theatre.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.2°C, HR 120, RR 28, BP 90/55, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Confused, tender uterus, purulent discharge
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.5, procalcitonin 22, blood cultures pending

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole) and a 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid bolus within the next hour, while preparing for emergency delivery
options.rationale: The Sepsis Six / Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle includes blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, lactate, fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg, oxygen and source control. In chorioamnionitis with maternal sepsis and foetal compromise, delivery is the source control and is performed in parallel with resuscitation (eTG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Transfer to ICU for central venous access before commencing antibiotics
options.rationale: ICU transfer before antibiotics is wrong because the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle prioritises antibiotics within 1 hour; CVC insertion must not delay antibiotic administration. Would be correct only if peripheral access is unobtainable AND antibiotics can be given via central line within the hour (Surviving Sepsis).

options.text: Proceed immediately to caesarean section as the primary intervention before fluids or antibiotics
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean before fluids/antibiotics is wrong because antibiotics and fluids run in parallel with theatre preparation, not sequentially; antibiotics within the first hour drive the mortality reduction. Would never be correct as a stepwise approach (Surviving Sepsis; RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer IV paracetamol 1 g and reassess vital signs in 30 minutes before further intervention
options.rationale: IV paracetamol and wait 30 min is wrong because temperature reduction does not treat sepsis; immediate antibiotics, fluids and source control are required. Would never be correct (Surviving Sepsis).

options.text: Order CT abdomen and pelvis to identify the source before starting antibiotics
options.rationale: CT abdomen first is wrong because the sepsis source is clinically obvious (chorioamnionitis), and CT delays antibiotics while exposing a critically unwell pregnant patient. Would be correct only when source is genuinely unclear and the patient is stable enough for imaging (Surviving Sepsis).

explanation.summary: Maternal sepsis requires immediate administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics and aggressive fluid resuscitation, along with lactate measurement and blood cultures. Delivery is considered but after initial stabilisation unless there is severe foetal distress or maternal deterioration.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures, lactate, antibiotics, crystalloid 30 mL/kg, vasopressors if needed. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
Maternal sepsis with chorioamnionitis: broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin, gentamicin, clindamycin). (Source: SOMANZ Maternal Sepsis)
explanation.pearls: In sepsis, every hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality. Do not wait for culture results or imaging.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 at 39+2 in spontaneous labour at 5 cm dilatation has a history of one lower-segment caesarean and one classical caesarean 1 year ago for preterm breech delivery. The midwifery team had counselled her against TOLAC due to the classical scar, but she insisted on attempting vaginal birth. Over the last 15 minutes she has developed constant severe suprapubic pain not relieved between contractions; the CTG now shows prolonged bradycardia 70 bpm; the foetal parts are easily felt abdominally, the uterine fundus is not palpable, and on VE the presenting part has receded from -1 to -3. Maternal HR 130, BP 88/52.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 120 bpm, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 24, SpO₂ 97%
data_table.Examination: Constant severe suprapubic pain, uterine fundus not palpable, foetal parts easily palpated, presenting part receded from -1 to -3, vaginal bleeding noted
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged foetal bradycardia 70 bpm, no variability

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section with anticipated laparotomy and possible hysterectomy
options.rationale: This is uterine rupture (very high risk with a prior classical scar in labour). Immediate operative delivery with MTP activation and senior consultant input is required; vaginal delivery, augmentation, tocolysis and further confirmatory tests are all unsafe (RANZCOG VBAC; RCOG).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin to augment contractions and expedite vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Augment with oxytocin is wrong because oxytocin augmentation in suspected rupture worsens haemorrhage and foetal compromise; the anti-pattern is treating rupture like inadequate labour. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to expedite vaginal delivery as the head was previously at -1
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is wrong because fundal pressure in a ruptured uterus causes catastrophic extension and foetal expulsion into the peritoneum; absolutely contraindicated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg tocolysis and reassess in 10 minutes
options.rationale: Terbutaline tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis applies to foetal distress with an intact uterus and a manageable cause (e.g. hyperstimulation); in rupture, delay worsens outcome and surgery is mandatory. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform speculum examination and foetal fibronectin to confirm membrane rupture before surgery
options.rationale: Speculum and fFN is wrong because confirmation in suspected rupture is clinical and intraoperative; further bedside testing delays surgery during a time-critical emergency. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Uterine rupture in a woman with a prior classical caesarean presenting with acute constant pain, loss of foetal station, and foetal bradycardia mandates an immediate category 1 caesarean with massive transfusion preparation and possible hysterectomy. Tocolysis, fundal pressure, and oxytocin are contraindicated. Vaginal examination for research purposes is dangerous.
key_points: Classic signs of uterine rupture: constant pain, loss of uterine contour, easily palpable foetal parts, recession of presenting part, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)
Risk of rupture: prior classical caesarean > prior lower-segment. (Source: KEMH Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In VBAC, a sudden change in pain character or loss of station demands immediate surgical intervention – do not delay for imaging.

stem: A 25-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation has been admitted three times over the past 4 weeks for severe hyperemesis gravidarum. She has lost 7 kg (12% of pre-pregnancy weight) and has persistent ketonuria, hypokalaemia, and hyponatraemia despite regular intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride and antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 100/60, HR 110, Temp 36.5°C, confused, ataxic, nystagmus
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosae, cachectic, nystagmus, ataxia, confusion
data_table.Investigations: Na⁺ 130, K⁺ 3.0, Cl⁻ 95, ketonuria, weight loss 12%

lead_in: What is the most critical vitamin to administer before starting dextrose-containing parenteral nutrition?

options.text: Intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily for 2-3 days, then 250 mg daily, to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with prolonged vomiting and weight loss leads to thiamine deficiency. Precipitating dextrose administration without thiamine can trigger Wernicke encephalopathy. High-dose parenteral thiamine must be given before carbohydrate loading. (Source: RANZCOG: Hyperemesis Gravidarum; RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 69)

options.text: Oral folic acid 5 mg once daily for the duration of the hyperemesis admission today
options.rationale: Oral folic acid is wrong because while folate is important antenatally for neural tube development, it does not prevent Wernicke encephalopathy when dextrose is administered to a thiamine-deficient patient. Would be correct only as routine antenatal supplementation, not as Wernicke prophylaxis (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).

options.text: Intravenous vitamin K 10 mg slowly over 30 minutes prior to commencing any dextrose containing fluids
options.rationale: IV vitamin K is wrong because vitamin K addresses warfarin reversal and vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency, not Wernicke; this is a wrong-vitamin error. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin B12 (hydroxocobalamin) 1000 mcg as a single dose now before glucose replacement
options.rationale: IM vitamin B12 is wrong because B12 deficiency is rare in pregnancy and is not the cause of Wernicke; thiamine (B1) is the critical vitamin. Would be correct only in confirmed B12 deficiency (RCOG Guideline 69).

options.text: No specific vitamin supplementation is needed before commencing dextrose containing intravenous fluid replacement therapy
options.rationale: No vitamin supplementation needed is wrong because dextrose without thiamine in a deficient hyperemesis patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy with potentially permanent neurological damage; the anti-pattern is treating dextrose as benign. Would never be correct in significant hyperemesis (RANZCOG: Hyperemesis).

explanation.summary: Prolonged vomiting in hyperemesis gravidarum can lead to thiamine deficiency. Before starting dextrose-containing parenteral nutrition, intravenous thiamine must be given to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, which presents with confusion, ataxia, and nystagmus.
key_points: Thiamine deficiency in hyperemesis: risk of Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: RCOG Guideline 69)
Always give IV thiamine before dextrose in at-risk patients. (Source: RANZCOG: Hyperemesis)
explanation.pearls: In hyperemesis with neurological signs, think thiamine. Dextrose without thiamine can be fatal.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 21+5 weeks presents with pelvic pressure and a bulging sensation. Speculum: cervix dilated 3 cm with exposed amniotic membranes visible at the os. No fever, no uterine tenderness; WCC 10.2, CRP 8, vaginal swab GBS negative, foetal fibronectin not applicable, no pooling of liquor. Ultrasound: viable foetus, cervical length 0 mm with funnelling, normal AFI, no foetal anomalies. Neonatology has counselled the woman and she wants every appropriate intervention to prolong the pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm, bulging membranes, no infection signs
data_table.Investigations: Cervical length 0 mm, funneling, foetal fibronectin negative, no infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Offer emergency McDonald cerclage, short-course indomethacin tocolysis (48 hours) given gestation under 32 weeks, antibiotic prophylaxis, and antenatal corticosteroids when delivery becomes likely
options.rationale: Australian/RANZCOG practice supports emergency rescue cerclage at 22-23+6 weeks for advanced cervical dilation with intact membranes and no infection, combined with short-course indomethacin tocolysis (limited to <32 weeks because of ductal closure risk above that gestation), antibiotic prophylaxis, and ACS when delivery is anticipated (RANZCOG cerclage; RCOG GT60).

options.text: Cerclage is contraindicated with bulging membranes; give only bed rest and corticosteroids
options.rationale: Bedrest and steroids only is wrong because bulging membranes are not an absolute contraindication to rescue cerclage when there is no infection or labour; bed rest alone has poor outcomes. Would be correct only with confirmed chorioamnionitis or established labour (RANZCOG).

options.text: Place cerclage and give corticosteroids but avoid indomethacin entirely due to ductal closure risk
options.rationale: Avoid indomethacin entirely is wrong because ductal closure risk applies primarily after 32 weeks; at 21+5 short-course indomethacin is acceptable and useful as tocolysis. Would be correct only at >32 weeks (RANZCOG).

options.text: Induce labour now as 21+5 is non-viable and ongoing pregnancy is futile
options.rationale: Induce labour as non-viable is wrong because at 22+0 weeks active resuscitation is increasingly offered in Australia and she wants intervention; inducing now is contrary to patient wishes and updated ANZNN guidance. Would be correct only at <22+0 weeks with palliative care chosen (ANZNN periviable consensus).

options.text: Place an abdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage today and commence IV magnesium sulfate
options.rationale: Abdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage is wrong because abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage in prior pregnancies, not first-line rescue; MgSO4 neuroprotection is for >=24+0 weeks, not 21+5. Would be correct only with prior failed transvaginal cerclage (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: For cervical insufficiency with bulging membranes and no infection, an emergency cerclage along with indomethacin and betamethasone can be considered. Strict asepsis and postoperative monitoring are essential. The goal is to prolong gestation to viability.
key_points: Emergency cerclage may be offered for bulging membranes up to 23–24 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
Indomethacin for 48 hours is used to reduce amniotic fluid and contractions. (Source: RCOG Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Bulging membranes are not an absolute contraindication to cerclage, but the risk of rupture and infection is high.

stem: A 37-year-old G5P4 with four prior lower-segment caesarean sections has anterior major placenta praevia. Ultrasound and MRI at 30 weeks confirm placenta accreta (loss of retroplacental clear zone, lacunae, hypervascularity at uterovesical interface), without percreta or bladder invasion. Foetal growth and Dopplers are normal. She has completed a course of betamethasone. She has been counselled about haemorrhage and hysterectomy and accepts a planned caesarean hysterectomy. The MDT is finalising timing and team composition.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 120/80, HR 78, fundal height 30 cm
data_table.Examination: Non-tender uterus
data_table.Investigations: USS + MRI: placenta accreta, no percreta, no bladder involvement; EFW 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35+6 weeks at a tertiary centre by a multidisciplinary team (obstetrician, gynae-oncology, interventional radiology, anaesthesia, haematology, neonatology), with cell salvage and MTP on standby and a vertical/midline abdominal incision
options.rationale: RANZCOG and SMFM consensus recommend planned delivery at 34-35+6 weeks for placenta accreta spectrum (before frequent late-preterm bleeding risk) in a tertiary centre with full multidisciplinary team and blood-product support. The placenta is generally left in situ during hysterectomy (RANZCOG PAS; SMFM).

options.text: Elective caesarean at 39 weeks under a single general obstetrician at her local hospital
options.rationale: 39 weeks at local hospital with single obstetrician is wrong because PAS warrants planned early delivery (34-35+6) in a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team and blood-product support; awaiting 39 weeks risks emergency haemorrhage in the late-preterm period. Would never be correct (RANZCOG; SMFM).

options.text: Plan vaginal delivery with active third-stage management and theatre on standby
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is wrong because placenta praevia with PAS is an absolute indication for caesarean; vaginal delivery would cause torrential haemorrhage from the placental site. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Plan delivery at 32 weeks to maximise prevention of any spontaneous labour
options.rationale: Deliver at 32 weeks is wrong because 32 weeks adds substantial prematurity morbidity without proportionate maternal benefit; the standard window is 34-35+6 unless acute indications arise. Would be correct only with antenatal bleeding or other acute indications (RANZCOG; SMFM).

options.text: Admit at 34 weeks for inpatient monitoring and deliver only when spontaneous labour or distress occurs
options.rationale: Reactive delivery only at labour or distress is wrong because reactive management compromises MDT availability and increases haemorrhage mortality; PAS requires planned delivery. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum demands meticulous antenatal planning with a multidisciplinary team. Delivery should be planned for 35–36+0 weeks in a tertiary centre with appropriate surgical, anaesthetic, and blood product support. A midline incision and high uterine incision are recommended. Interventional radiology may be an adjunct. Emergency delivery is avoided.
key_points: Planned delivery for PAS: 35–36+0 weeks, multidisciplinary team, tertiary centre. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS; FIGO PAS Guidelines)
Surgical approach: midline incision, high uterine incision avoiding the placenta, preparedness for hysterectomy. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS)
Massive transfusion protocol and cell salvage should be available. (Source: FIGO PAS Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: The single most important intervention in PAS is antenatal diagnosis – it transforms an emergency into a planned operation.
